A novel benchmark for evaluating audio-based reasoning and information retrieval in large audio-language models, designed to assess performance in real-world scenarios requiring external information grounding.
A reinforcement learning framework that enhances audio understanding in large audio language models by optimizing the use of external audio tools, improving fine-grained auditory perception.
A novel audio-only architecture designed for automatically segmenting long-form audio into coherent sections, leveraging learned audio representations.

A chronological record of system activities that provides documentary evidence of the sequence of activities affecting an operation, procedure, or event. Audit trails are essential for security, compliance, and forensics.

A framework for unsupervised discovery and clustering of auditory scene labels using a collaborative Human-Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) approach, enhancing label quality through zero-shot learning techniques.

A novel approach to combining unaided and aided AAC technologies, aiming to enhance communication for individuals with motor impairments by leveraging the naturalness of gestures with the intelligibility of speech-generated messages.

A novel kernel designed to enhance Gaussian splatting by explicitly modeling specular effects through view-dependent opacity, improving the representation of complex reflections in 3D scene reconstruction.
A technology that overlays digital information such as images, text, or sounds onto the real-world environment. AR is used in gaming, education, retail, and industrial applications.
An advanced modeling approach for energy markets that extends counterfactual reasoning frameworks to multivariate temporal data, incorporating learned causal structures for enhanced interpretability.
Aurchestra is a system that enables fine-grained, real-time soundscape control on resource-constrained hearables, allowing users to independently adjust multiple simultaneous sound sources.
A real-time soundscape control system designed for resource-constrained hearables, enabling fine-grained adjustments of multiple simultaneous sound sources.
A contrastive language-audio pre-training framework that repurposes multimodal large language models for audio-text retrieval, enhancing the understanding of complex queries.
A reinforcement learning framework that optimizes the invocation of external tools for audio reasoning tasks, using a sparse-feedback training strategy to manage information overload.
A cryptographic primitive that enables self-contained lineage verification of prompts in large language model workflows, enhancing security against prompt injection attacks.
A comprehensive security framework for agentic AI systems that enforces integrity and intent across four fundamental boundaries: prompts, tools, data, and context, utilizing cryptographic methods.

The process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system before granting access to resources. Common methods include passwords, biometrics, multi-factor authentication, and token-based systems.

The process of determining whether an authenticated user has permission to access specific resources or perform certain actions. Authorization is distinct from authentication, which verifies identity.

A cloud computing feature that automatically adjusts the number of compute resources based on current demand. It helps maintain performance during traffic spikes while minimizing costs during low usage.

A cloud computing feature that automatically adjusts the number of compute resources based on current demand. It maintains performance during traffic spikes while minimizing costs during low-usage periods.
A framework designed to automate the creation of security benchmarks for evaluating the correctness and security of LLM-generated code, addressing the limitations of manually-crafted benchmarks.

A type of neural network that learns to compress data into a lower-dimensional representation and then reconstruct it. Autoencoders are used for dimensionality reduction, denoising, and generative modeling.

AutoFFS is a data-driven framework that generates counterfactual skull morphologies for facial feminization surgery using adversarial free-form deformations, aiming to provide quantitative anatomical guidance in surgical planning.
An FPGA-based accelerator that enhances graph neural network performance by optimizing preprocessing tasks, utilizing specialized components to reduce inference latency.

Abstract machines used in theoretical computer science to study computation and formal languages. Types include finite automata, pushdown automata, and Turing machines, each recognizing different language classes.
A framework for evaluating the risks of large language models in mental health support by simulating therapy sessions with AI agents and assessing them against a quality of care ontology.

A.term: Automated Clinical AI Red Teaming Framework
A.definition: An evaluation framework for assessing the risks of large language models in mental health support, utilizing simulated patient agents and dynamic cognitive-affective models.
A machine learning-based framework that utilizes noninvasive acoustic sensing to detect swallowing abnormalities, enhancing early diagnosis of dysphagia.

This framework utilizes Large Language Models to automate the extraction of mechanical constitutive models from unstructured scientific literature, facilitating data-driven predictive maintenance in cultural heritage conservation.

A framework designed for evaluating document information extraction performance, leveraging large language models to conduct large-scale benchmarking and explore failure modes in MLLM-only pipelines versus traditional OCR+MLLM setups.
Automated Patch Correctness Assessment (APCA) is a method for evaluating the correctness of patches generated by Automated Program Repair, aiming to reduce the manual validation burden.
A framework inspired by psychiatric questionnaires that separates preprocessing from prediction to enhance the interpretability of predictive models in clinical settings.

An evolution of traditional red teaming that utilizes artificial intelligence and automation to conduct efficient and scalable security assessments, simulating real-world attacks to identify vulnerabilities.
The use of software tools to run tests on code automatically without manual intervention. It includes unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests that verify software functionality.

This prototype generates instruction-accurate processor models for electronic control units (ECUs) using a virtual test and integration environment, enabling early software testing before physical hardware availability.
A convolutional neural network model specifically designed to identify bugs in C code, trained on a combination of machine-labeled and human-labeled datasets.
An algorithm designed for the automatic quantification and uncertainty analysis of microglial cells in biological tissues, focusing on counting rather than detection.
A systematic approach that utilizes automatic differentiation to identify critical and uncritical elements in variables for efficient checkpointing in high-performance computing applications.
A technique for deriving parametric resource bounds in programs, extending traditional analysis to support non-local control transfers, such as exceptions and effects.

A framework designed for the automatic detection and analysis of singing mistakes in music pedagogy, utilizing a curated dataset of synchronized teacher-learner vocal recordings with annotated mistakes.

The use of technology to perform tasks with reduced human intervention. In software development, automation encompasses build processes, testing, deployment, infrastructure provisioning, and monitoring.
A library for building collaborative applications that implements conflict-free replicated data types. It allows multiple users to edit shared data concurrently without coordination.
Automated Machine Learning — tools and techniques that automate the process of applying machine learning, including feature engineering, model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment.
A framework that dynamically constructs and optimizes data processing pipelines using hierarchical agent orchestration, allowing for adaptive responses to evolving requirements.

The Autonomous Disclosure Regulator is a multi-node AI framework that audits the complexity and timing of regulatory disclosures, identifying a 'Strategic Gap' where companies obscure information to manipulate market perceptions.
A framework that allows robots to autonomously assemble stable structures without predefined plans, using reinforcement learning to adapt to environmental uncertainties.
Systems capable of performing tasks in complex environments without continuous human guidance. Examples include self-driving cars, drones, and robotic systems that make decisions independently.

AVS is a computational and hierarchical storage system designed for autonomous vehicles, co-designing computation with a hierarchical layout to efficiently manage the massive data generated by onboard sensors.

AutoPrunedRetriever is a graph-style retrieval-augmented generation system that maintains a minimal reasoning subgraph for efficient query processing, reducing redundancy in retrieval and reasoning tasks.
A framework that utilizes auxiliary variables to disentangle latent representations in deep generative models, improving the interpretability of generated samples.

AV-LMMDetect is a supervised fine-tuned large multimodal model that performs audio-visual deepfake detection by jointly analyzing audio and visual streams, framing the task as a yes/no classification problem.

An isolated location within a cloud data center region that has independent power, cooling, and networking. Distributing resources across availability zones provides fault tolerance and high availability.

A desirable property of cryptographic algorithms where a small change in input produces a significant and unpredictable change in output. Good hash functions and ciphers exhibit strong avalanche effects.
A morphable cache architecture that enhances security for last-level caches in multi-core processors by adapting to mitigate cross-core conflict and occupancy-based attacks.
A type of Markov Decision Process where the focus is on optimizing average rewards across multiple chains, with new policy gradient methods developed for this setting.
A self-balancing binary search tree where the heights of the two child subtrees of any node differ by at most one. AVL trees guarantee O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion operations.
A human-curated benchmark designed to assess large language models' understanding of audio-visual memes, incorporating contextual and cultural knowledge through a series of Q&A pairs.

The Audio-Visual Quality Assessment (AVQA) dataset is constructed through a crowdsourced subjective experiment framework, addressing the limitations of existing datasets by providing diverse content and quality annotations for model development.

A row-oriented remote procedure call and data serialization framework developed within Apache's Hadoop project. It uses JSON for defining data types and protocols, and serializes data in a compact binary format.

The AVS End-to-End Intelligent Video Coding Exploration Model (AVS-EEM) focuses on developing efficient video coding standards with low computational complexity, aimed at enhancing interoperability in video compression technologies.
An a trous wavelet diffusion model designed for lung ultrasound image synthesis that preserves fine-scale structures while avoiding resolution loss, enhancing diagnostic capabilities.

AWE is a memory-augmented multi-agent framework designed for autonomous web penetration testing, which incorporates structured, vulnerability-specific analysis to enhance adaptability and effectiveness in dynamic web environments.

Amazon Web Services — the world's largest cloud computing platform, offering over 200 services including compute, storage, databases, and machine learning. AWS pioneered the Infrastructure-as-a-Service model.

An Amazon Web Services service that gives developers and businesses an easy way to create a collection of related AWS resources and provision them in an orderly and predictable fashion using infrastructure as code.

An Amazon Web Services service that provides user authentication, authorization, and user management for web and mobile apps. It supports sign-up, sign-in, and access control with social and enterprise identity providers.

A serverless compute service from Amazon that runs code in response to events and automatically manages the underlying compute resources. It supports multiple programming languages and scales automatically.
A hardware-aware abstraction that maps logical tensor coordinates to a multi-axis physical space, unifying tiling, sharding, and offsets across device distributions.
A method for resolving dimensional ambiguity in ODRL policy constraints by classifying operands based on their value-domain structure, enhancing policy evaluation accuracy.

Microsoft's cloud computing platform that provides a wide range of cloud services including compute, analytics, storage, and networking. Azure supports multiple programming languages, tools, and frameworks.
A Microsoft product providing version control, reporting, requirements management, project management, automated builds, testing, and release management capabilities.

B.definition: B-DENSE is a novel framework for dense ensemble network learning that utilizes multi-branch trajectory alignment to enhance structural information retention during inference, addressing the limitations of existing distillation techniques.

An unsupervised speech emotion recognition method utilizing a modified Group Relative Policy Optimization to enhance sample selection during the learning process, addressing data sparsity and annotation bias.
A bounded variation of the posit number format designed for high-performance computing, offering improved efficiency over traditional floating-point formats by utilizing variable-length fields.
A self-balancing tree data structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time. B-trees are widely used in databases and file systems for indexing.
A crowd-sourced mapping pipeline that utilizes Bayesian B-spline fusion for high-definition map generation using consumer-grade sensors, enhancing localization and mapping accuracy.
Business-to-Business — commerce transactions between businesses rather than between a business and individual consumers. B2B companies provide products and services to other businesses.
Business-to-Consumer — commerce transactions where businesses sell products or services directly to individual consumers. E-commerce platforms and retail apps are common B2C channels.

A JavaScript compiler that converts modern JavaScript code into backwards-compatible versions for older browsers. Babel enables developers to use the latest language features without worrying about browser support.

A unified language-pivoted pretraining framework designed for heterogeneous multi-modal remote sensing object detection, which decouples modality alignment from downstream task learning to enhance optimization stability and generalization.
The primary algorithm for training neural networks that calculates the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by propagating errors backwards through the network.
An undirected graph derived from a directed graph where edges are defined based on a linear order of vertices, used to study the directed clique number.

The server-side portion of a web application that handles business logic, database interactions, and API responses. Backend systems process requests from the frontend and return data, typically built with languages like Python, Node.js, or Go.

A task that runs asynchronously outside the main request-response cycle of a web application. Background jobs handle time-consuming operations like sending emails, processing images, and generating reports.
An ordered list of work items that need to be completed, typically maintained in agile project management. The product backlog represents everything that might be needed in the product.

A mechanism for handling situations where a data producer generates data faster than the consumer can process it. Backpressure strategies include buffering, dropping, and signaling the producer to slow down.
The primary algorithm for training neural networks that calculates the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by propagating errors backwards through the network layers.
The process of creating copies of data so it can be restored in the event of data loss, corruption, or disaster. Backup strategies include full, incremental, and differential approaches.
The process of creating copies of data and systems to protect against data loss and enabling restoration of those copies in the event of a disaster, hardware failure, or accidental deletion.
The ability of a system or component to work with older versions of itself or with older data formats. Maintaining backward compatibility prevents breaking existing users when releasing updates.
A person or entity responsible for actions that are harmful or malicious in cyberspace. Bad actors include hackers, cybercriminals, nation-state attackers, and insider threats.

BadRSSD is a novel backdoor attack targeting the representation layer of self-supervised diffusion models, allowing for stealthy manipulation of semantic representations through poisoned samples with triggers in Principal Component Analysis (PCA) space.

Bootstrap Aggregating — an ensemble machine learning method that combines predictions from multiple models trained on random subsets of the training data. It reduces variance and helps prevent overfitting.

BALI is a branch-aware framework that integrates large language models to improve the inference and verification of loop invariants, enhancing the precision and scalability of reasoning about iterative algorithms.
A ranked preference aggregation method based on a modified PageRank algorithm that consistently identifies the Condorcet winner in ranked choice elections without damping.
A technique used to improve the upper bound for the Euclidean Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) by leveraging specific crossover strategies that enhance the efficiency of tour length estimations.

A module used in time-frequency domain dual-path models for source separation that compresses frequency information by encoding features for predefined subbands, emphasizing low-frequency parts to improve efficiency in high-sampling-rate tasks.

The maximum rate of data transfer across a network connection, typically measured in bits per second. Higher bandwidth allows more data to flow simultaneously, affecting download speeds, streaming quality, and application performance.

The Bandwidth Coloring Problem generalizes graph coloring by imposing minimum separation constraints between adjacent vertices, relevant in frequency assignment applications. This paper presents a systematic study of SAT encodings for BCP, proposing a unified framework with multiple encoding methods.

B.term: Bank Risk Interlinkage with Dynamic Graph and Event Simulations (BRIDGES)
B.definition: BRIDGES is a framework designed to analyze systemic risk in banking systems, particularly in BRICS countries, by utilizing dynamic graphs and event simulations to capture complex interdependencies among banks.
A machine-readable representation of data using varying widths and spacings of parallel lines or geometric patterns. Barcodes are used for product identification, inventory management, and tracking.
A physical server dedicated to a single tenant without any virtualization layer. Bare metal servers provide maximum performance and control for workloads that require direct hardware access.

A coordination primitive in parallel computing where threads or processes must all reach a designated point before any can proceed past it, ensuring that a phase of computation completes globally before the next begins.
A class from which other classes inherit methods and properties. Also known as a parent class or superclass, it defines common behavior shared by its derived classes.

A binary-to-text encoding scheme that represents binary data in an ASCII string format. It is commonly used for encoding email attachments, embedding images in HTML, and transmitting binary data over text-based protocols.
The Bourne Again Shell — a Unix shell and command language that serves as the default shell on most Linux distributions and macOS. It provides scripting capabilities for automating system tasks.
A Bayesian framework designed for decomposing time series data into trend and seasonal components, offering robust estimation even in the presence of abrupt changes.
A special-purpose computer on a network specifically designed and configured to withstand attacks. It serves as a gateway between trusted and untrusted networks, providing controlled access.

A technique used in neural networks that normalizes the inputs of each layer to have zero mean and unit variance. It speeds up training, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and acts as a regularizer.
The execution of a series of programs or jobs on a computer without manual intervention. Batch processing is used for large-scale data transformations, ETL jobs, and report generation.
The number of training examples used in one iteration of model training. Batch size affects training speed, memory usage, and model convergence, with common choices being 32, 64, or 128 samples.

Bauplan is a code-first lakehouse framework that employs typed table contracts, Git-like data versioning, and transactional runs to ensure safe and reproducible operations in data analytics and AI environments.
A probabilistic framework for signed distance function fusion that incorporates view planning on CPU, addressing the limitations of traditional volumetric fusion techniques in conveying uncertainty.

The Bayesian Adversarial Multi-Agent Framework is designed for AI for Science tasks, coordinating multiple LLM-based agents to enhance reliability and reduce error propagation in multi-agent workflows.
An equation that reformulates stochastic optimal control problems using Bayesian methods, incorporating observable side information to derive optimal policies.
A framework that utilizes Bayesian confidence propagation to enhance the performance of neuromorphic solvers, particularly in real-time applications.
A statistical method that uses Bayes' theorem to update the probability of a hypothesis as more evidence becomes available. It provides a principled framework for reasoning under uncertainty.

Bayesian Model Averaging is a statistical technique that combines predictions from multiple models to improve accuracy and account for model uncertainty, particularly useful in analyzing profitability in energy sectors.
A probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of variables and their conditional dependencies via a directed acyclic graph. It is used for reasoning under uncertainty and causal inference.
A study exploring the correspondence between Bayesian networks and graphical representations of proofs in linear logic, aiming to develop a proof-theoretical account of Bayesian inference.
A sequential design strategy for global optimization of black-box functions that uses a probabilistic surrogate model to guide the search for optimal hyperparameters or configurations.

A probabilistic model-based optimization technique that is particularly effective for hyperparameter tuning in machine learning models, enhancing performance through systematic exploration of parameter spaces.

A method utilizing Gaussian Processes to automate the search for optimal scheduling configurations in heterogeneous multi-core architectures, addressing multi-objective optimization between energy efficiency and latency.

B.term: Bayesian Optimization with Block Coordinate Descent
B.definition: This scalable framework for base station configuration utilizes Bayesian optimization combined with block coordinate descent to optimize parameters while mitigating the curse of dimensionality.

An approach for developing autonomous AI agents in mobile networks that focuses on real-time adaptation and transparency in decision-making, overcoming limitations of traditional deep reinforcement learning methods.
A statistical approach for constructing and rebalancing index-tracking portfolios that incorporates uncertainty quantification and operational constraints.

B.term: Bayesian Uncertainty Analysis Framework for Multi-Agent Debate (MAD)
B.definition: A framework that decomposes total predictive uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components, enhancing the understanding of information exchange in multi-agent debate scenarios.
A password-hashing function based on the Blowfish cipher that incorporates a salt and an adaptive cost factor to resist brute-force attacks. It is widely recommended for securely storing passwords.

Behavior-Driven Development — a software development methodology that extends TDD by writing tests in natural language that describe the expected behavior of the system. It bridges the gap between technical and non-technical stakeholders.
A small Bluetooth Low Energy device that broadcasts signals to nearby smartphones and tablets. Beacons are used for indoor navigation, proximity marketing, and asset tracking.
An approach to address the challenges posed by diverse user devices in beam-based communication, emphasizing the need for machine learning algorithms to consider hardware differences.
A parameter derived for generalized uniform rectangular arrays (URAs) that quantifies the spatial region where near-field beams can be focused, enabling enhanced spatial degrees of freedom.

BeamVLM is a generative model that leverages vision-language models to predict communication beams between unmanned aerial vehicles and ground stations, enhancing the semantic understanding of dynamic environments for improved generalization.
A type of access token that grants the bearer access to a protected resource. Anyone holding the token can access the resource, making secure token storage and transmission critical.

A novel machine learning framework that learns interpretable and identifiable optimization structures from data, facilitating the modeling of both single optimization problems and hierarchical compositions.

A framework that utilizes vision-language models to create 3D layouts that prioritize human behavior and spatial requirements, ensuring that generated scenes accommodate user-specific anthropometric data.
A software development process that encourages collaboration between developers, QA, and non-technical stakeholders. BDD uses natural language specifications to describe software behavior.

This technique focuses on identifying and correcting systematic behavioral biases exhibited by large language models (LLMs) in economic and financial decision-making tasks. It draws on insights from cognitive psychology and experimental economics to enhance model performance.
A framework designed to quantify how LLM-based agents update their expectations based on heterogeneous signals, integrating behavioral economics with Kalman filtering techniques.
A framework that utilizes large language models as calibrated measurement instruments to assess behavioral parameters like loss aversion and herding, providing insights into asset pricing models.
A category of design patterns that deal with communication and interaction between objects. Examples include Observer, Strategy, Command, and Iterator patterns.
A graph algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted directed graph. Unlike Dijkstra's algorithm, it can handle negative edge weights.

A comprehensive benchmark suite designed for evaluating algorithms in stationary Mean Field Games, focusing on discrete-time and discrete-space settings to assess robustness and generalization in multi-agent systems.
A comprehensive benchmarking and evaluation framework for assessing the performance of large language models in high-level synthesis code generation, facilitating the transition from RTL to HLS.
A standardized test or set of tests used to measure the performance of hardware or software components. Benchmarking helps compare different systems and identify performance bottlenecks.

This benchmark consists of 180 tasks designed to evaluate the performance of Large Language Models in generating syntactically correct and functional ABAP code, focusing on the effectiveness of compiler feedback for iterative improvement.
This benchmarking study evaluates various deep learning models for time-series anomaly detection across multiple heterogeneous cloud telemetry datasets to assess their generalizability.
A multistage approach developed for transcribing Bengali long-form speech, utilizing fine-tuned models and speaker diarization techniques to address challenges in low-resource language technology.

A set of community benchmarks for long-form Bangla automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speaker diarization, including a long-form ASR corpus and a speaker diarization corpus aimed at realistic multi-speaker content.

Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — a pre-trained natural language processing model developed by Google. BERT understands context by reading text bidirectionally and excels at tasks like question answering and sentiment analysis.

Bespoke OLAP refers to the synthesis of workload-specific database engines that eliminate structural overhead by optimizing for fixed analytical workloads, leveraging LLM-based code synthesis to reduce manual engineering efforts.
A method or technique that has been generally accepted as superior to alternatives because it produces better results. In technology, best practices evolve with industry standards and experience.
A machine learning-based framework for asset selection and portfolio construction that uses a sparse graphical model to identify conditionally independent or negatively correlated assets.
A phase of software testing where a near-complete version is released to a limited audience outside the development team. Beta testers provide feedback on bugs, usability, and features.
An advanced architecture for reconfigurable intelligent surfaces that improves beamforming gain by utilizing group-connected configurations in multi-user MIMO communication.
A comprehensive benchmark for evaluating code agents, focusing on cross-repository reasoning and domain-specialized problem solving.

A parametric curve defined by control points that determine its shape, used extensively in vector graphics, font design, CSS animations, and CAD. Cubic Bezier curves with four control points are the most common variant.

Border Gateway Protocol — the routing protocol used to exchange routing information between autonomous systems on the internet. BGP is fundamental to how data finds its path across the global internet.
An extension to the Border Gateway Protocol that facilitates the advertisement of Delay-Tolerant Network endpoint reachability information, aimed at simplifying configurations in DTN communication.

A hard physical constraint used in a neuro-fuzzy framework for quantum error attribution, preventing classifiers from attributing impossible output distributions to noise, thus improving diagnostic accuracy.

An advanced Monte Carlo simulation technique that enhances the valuation of corporate bonds in financial networks by effectively handling rare default events and capturing higher-order network effects through a two-level sampling strategy.

An innovative radar architecture that utilizes a bi-static configuration with Intelligent Reflective Surfaces (IRS) to improve target detection and parameter estimation in non-line-of-sight scenarios.

Bialgebraic Abstract GSOS is a categorical framework that models the operational and denotational semantics of programming and process languages, ensuring that bisimilarity is a congruence and coincides with denotational equivalence.
Systematic error in AI systems that produces unfair outcomes, often reflecting prejudices in training data or algorithm design. Addressing bias is essential for building fair and ethical AI systems.
A fundamental concept in machine learning that describes the tradeoff between a model's ability to fit training data and its ability to generalize to new data. Lower bias often means higher variance.
A speech-augmented benchmark dataset for assessing bias in multilingual audio language models, featuring balanced demographic attributes and extensive audio recordings.
A linear programming relaxation for the Steiner Tree problem that provides improved integrality gap bounds, recently shown to be at most 1.898.

A neural architecture that processes input sequences in both forward and backward directions simultaneously, capturing context from both sides of each token. BERT's masked language model is the most influential example.

Extremely large datasets that cannot be processed by traditional methods, characterized by volume, velocity, variety, and veracity. Big data technologies enable organizations to extract insights from massive amounts of structured and unstructured information.

A mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards infinity. In computer science, it is used to classify algorithms by their time and space complexity.

An ultra-lightweight neural network hardware accelerator inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks, designed to operate without multiplication by replacing nonlinear functions with binary, learnable thresholds.
A framework for modeling optimization problems where decisions are made sequentially by multiple players with conflicting objectives, extending beyond NP into the polynomial hierarchy or PSPACE.

A bilevel optimization approach that strategically reweights training domains to minimize validation loss, optimizing domain weights in an outer loop while adjusting model parameters in an inner loop.

A dual-level optimization approach that combines a soft actor-critic agent for real-time antenna positioning with zero-forcing precoding design, addressing the challenges of dynamic antenna placement in mobile user environments.
A framework that enhances the efficiency of querying large language models by selectively leveraging explanation features, reducing computational costs associated with LLM queries.
An interaction approach for teleoperation that specifies assembly tasks through hierarchies of relative and absolute object constraints, enhancing remote manipulation capabilities.

An interaction technique in extended reality (XR) that allows users to specify remote assembly tasks through hierarchical constraints, facilitating teleoperation with relative and absolute object positioning.

A base-2 number system using only 0 and 1, which forms the foundation of all digital computing. Every piece of data in a computer — text, images, video — is ultimately represented as sequences of binary digits (bits).
The examination of compiled binary executables to understand their behavior without access to source code. It is used in malware analysis, vulnerability research, and reverse engineering.
A machine learning task where the model predicts one of two possible classes for each input. Common examples include spam detection, fraud detection, and medical diagnosis.
A complete binary tree data structure where each node satisfies the heap property — the parent node is either greater than or equal to (max-heap) or less than or equal to (min-heap) its children.

An operator that takes two operands and performs an operation on them. Common binary operators include arithmetic operators like + and -, comparison operators like == and <, and logical operators like && and ||.
An efficient search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It has O(log n) time complexity.

A binary tree data structure where each node has at most two children, with left children containing smaller values and right children containing larger values. BSTs enable efficient searching, insertion, and deletion.
A tree data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. Binary trees form the basis for many advanced data structures and algorithms.
A neural network architecture designed for binaural personal sound zone rendering, utilizing simulated acoustic transfer functions to improve generalization in sound reproduction.
The association of a name with an entity such as a variable, function, or type in a programming language. Early binding occurs at compile time while late binding occurs at runtime.
A computational model that mimics the insect visual system to convert dense visual inputs into sparse, discriminative codes, employing a self-supervised contrastive learning objective.

BioEnvSense is a human-centered security framework that employs a hybrid CNN-LSTM model to analyze biometric and environmental data, enabling context-aware security decisions to prevent behavior-driven cyber incidents.
A security framework that combines a hybrid CNN-LSTM model to analyze biometric and environmental data for context-aware security decisions.
BioFabric Motifs are visual representations of complex networks that utilize effective vertex and edge ordering to expose salient patterns, allowing for clearer understanding of large graphs.

An interdisciplinary field that develops methods and software tools for understanding biological data. It combines biology, computer science, mathematics, and statistics to analyze genomic and proteomic data.
A neuro-inspired approach that uses living neural circuits as a computational substrate for reservoir computing, leveraging their spontaneous and stimulus-evoked activity for pattern recognition.

A learning rule that integrates neurobiological principles into neural network training to enhance generalization, adaptation, and learning efficiency, mimicking the mechanisms of biological neural systems.
A resource-efficient bioacoustic foundational model designed for Internet-of-Things (IoT) applications, focusing on reducing computational costs while maintaining performance in bioacoustic tasks.

This framework utilizes 3D human reconstruction from video data for gait analysis, extracting biomechanically meaningful markers and integrating them with OpenSim for accurate joint kinematic estimation.

Authentication methods that use unique physical or behavioral characteristics to verify identity, such as fingerprints, facial recognition, iris scans, or voice patterns. Biometrics offer convenience but raise privacy and security considerations.

A neuro-symbolic framework that integrates probabilistic planning with deterministic finite state machines, designed to enhance scientific planning in laboratory environments by ensuring a Design-Verify-Rectify workflow.
Basic Input/Output System — firmware that initializes hardware components during the boot process and provides runtime services for the operating system. BIOS has largely been replaced by UEFI.
A mathematical model used to represent spatially distributed supply and demand nodes, where edges form based on the proximity of demand nodes to supply nodes within a defined service range.

A method for analyzing quantization error in threshold level quantizers, establishing a relationship between uniform quantization and bipolar PFM to describe the spectral properties of quantized signals.
A measure of the shared information fraction between a system's observations, actions, and outcomes, essential for evaluating the effectiveness of complex systems under varying conditions.

BIRDTurk is the first Turkish adaptation of the BIRD Text-to-SQL benchmark, created through a controlled translation process that maintains the logical structure of SQL queries while adapting schema identifiers to Turkish.

BiScale is a two-tier energy optimization framework for disaggregated LLM serving that jointly optimizes placement and dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) to enhance energy efficiency while meeting strict service level objectives.

A concept used to separate polynomial complexity classes based on bisimulation-invariant queries, which are defined in the polyadic mu-calculus. This approach leverages model checking techniques to establish relationships between different complexity classes.
The most basic unit of information in computing, representing a single binary value of either 0 or 1. All digital data is ultimately composed of bits, with 8 bits forming a byte.
A design methodology that enables real-time few-shot learning on edge AI hardware with arbitrary fixed-point bit-widths, overcoming limitations of traditional design environments.

Bithoven is a high-level language designed to enhance the usability and security of Bitcoin smart contracts by bridging gaps in state management and resource liveness, addressing critical vulnerabilities in the UTXO architecture.

A compact integer representation where individual bits serve as boolean flags, manipulated via bitwise operations to test, set, or clear specific flags. Widely used for file permissions, feature flags, and capability sets.
A peer-to-peer file sharing protocol that distributes data across multiple nodes rather than relying on a central server. It reduces bandwidth costs and increases download speeds for popular files.
An operation that directly manipulates bits of a binary number. Bitwise operations include AND, OR, XOR, NOT, and shift operations and are used for low-level programming and optimization.

A hardware architecture that supports runtime-reconfigurable multi-precision quantized multiplication, enabling efficient computation in neural network accelerators by adapting precision dynamically across layers.
A system or component whose internal workings are hidden from the user, who interacts with it solely through its inputs and outputs. Black box design promotes encapsulation and modular architecture.
A software testing method that examines functionality without knowledge of internal code structure. Testers verify that inputs produce expected outputs without understanding implementation details.
A list of entities such as IP addresses, email addresses, or domains that are denied access or blocked. Blacklisting is used in spam filtering, network security, and content moderation.

The Blind Code Rate Recovery Metric is a new measure for evaluating the quality of code rate recovery in linear block codes, derived from rank-based estimation techniques, particularly useful in high noise conditions.

Blindfold is an automated attack framework targeting embodied Large Language Models (LLMs), exploiting the misalignment between linguistic instructions and physical outcomes to reveal vulnerabilities in real-world actuation.
A type of cloud storage optimized for storing massive amounts of unstructured data such as text, binary data, images, and documents. Azure Blob Storage and Amazon S3 are popular implementations.
A symmetric encryption algorithm that encrypts data in fixed-size blocks rather than one bit at a time. AES and DES are common block ciphers, with AES using 128-bit blocks.

A novel algorithm designed for monitoring the longitudinal power profile of fiber-optic links, which processes data in blocks to reduce complexity and latency compared to traditional least squares methods.
A tensor structure used in structure from motion that captures more information than essential matrices, enabling the recovery of multiple camera configurations through Tucker decomposition.
A set of optimization techniques that utilize block-coordinate strategies to reduce memory consumption in image restoration tasks while maintaining high performance.
A family of novel bounds that generalize PAC-Bayes bounds, providing a framework for estimating expected generalization error based on subsets of training data.
An NP-hard problem involving the arrangement of blocks of varying widths and masses to maximize overhang, with connections to other optimization problems.

A decentralized, distributed ledger technology that records transactions across multiple computers. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, creating an immutable chain that ensures data integrity without a central authority.
The mechanism by which nodes in a blockchain network agree on the current state of the distributed ledger. Common consensus algorithms include Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, and BFT.

This framework leverages blockchain technology to establish a resilient trust mechanism for cross-domain service orchestration in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) networks, facilitating secure and efficient collaboration among multiple UAVs.
A routing approach that utilizes blockchain technology to enhance security and stability in low-altitude intelligent networks, particularly for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).
A lightweight profiling tool that measures the Blocking Ratio to differentiate between genuine I/O wait and contention caused by Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) in edge AI systems.

A space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element is a member of a set. It may return false positives but never false negatives, making it useful for caching and filtering.

Bloom Filter Look-Up Tables are a mechanism for managing cryptographic keys securely in distributed databases within the Web3 ecosystem, enhancing data privacy and scalability in decentralized systems.
A variant of the QuasarRAT malware that utilizes DLL side-loading techniques to bypass security defenses during multi-stage infections targeting specific regions.

The defensive security team responsible for maintaining an organization's security posture, detecting threats, and responding to incidents. Blue teams work to defend against attacks simulated by red teams.

A release strategy that maintains two identical production environments (blue and green), where one serves live traffic while the other is updated. Traffic is switched instantly to the new version, enabling zero-downtime deployments and easy rollbacks.

A layout-aware multimodal retrieval system designed for large-scale engineering repositories, capable of detecting drawing regions and applying region-restricted OCR to produce structured metadata from legacy documents.
A wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances using UHF radio waves. Bluetooth connects devices like headphones, keyboards, and IoT sensors without cables.
A wireless personal area network technology designed for low power consumption while maintaining a similar communication range to classic Bluetooth. BLE is widely used in IoT devices and wearables.
A comprehensive benchmark for detecting false and synthetic content across 79 languages, combining human-written and LLM-generated samples to enhance detection tools for low-resource languages.
An end-to-end tool for verifying Timed Security Protocols using SMT-based bounded model checking and multi-agent modeling, facilitating the analysis of timed interactions in security protocols.

Brown-von Neumann-Nash (BNN) dynamics is a classical model in evolutionary game theory repurposed to achieve regularization-free last-iterate convergence in zero-sum games, simplifying the convergence process without the need for hyperparameter tuning.

A visual collaboration surface used in design tools like FigJam and Miro for brainstorming, wireframing, and planning. Boards support real-time collaboration with sticky notes, shapes, and connectors.

A three-layer architecture for decision-making in repeated games that integrates an echo state network for implicit inference, a cognitive filter for strategic tools, and a metacognitive governance layer.
Bayesian Optimization Genetic Algorithm, a framework that combines evolutionary search with Bayesian optimization to efficiently navigate the protein sequence space for desired characteristics.
A framework that integrates evolutionary search with Bayesian optimization to efficiently explore the sequence space for designing novel proteins with desired characteristics.

Sections of code that have to be included in many places with little or no alteration. Reducing boilerplate through abstractions, code generation, or language features improves developer productivity.
A transformer-based language model specifically fine-tuned on bond market news, designed to provide sentiment signals that integrate with financial forecasting models.
A data type with only two possible values: true or false. Booleans are fundamental to programming logic, used in conditional statements, loops, and logical operations to control program flow.
A branch of algebra dealing with variables that have two possible values: true and false. Boolean algebra forms the foundation of digital logic circuits and conditional expressions in programming.

A measure derived from Boolean entropy that quantifies the amount of information that can be obtained about a random variable through its Boolean representation, particularly in the context of the Boolean Central Limit Theorem.
A form of algebra in which all values are reduced to either true or false. Boolean logic forms the basis of digital circuit design and conditional expressions in programming.
An ensemble machine learning technique that builds models sequentially, with each new model correcting errors made by the previous ones. Popular boosting algorithms include AdaBoost and XGBoost.

The sequence of operations a computer performs when powered on, from initial hardware checks through loading the operating system. The boot process includes POST, bootloader execution, and kernel initialization.

A popular open-source CSS framework for building responsive, mobile-first websites. Bootstrap provides pre-built components, a flexible grid system, and JavaScript plugins that accelerate web development.
A data-driven online learning method that adaptively selects the look-back window size for financial risk forecasting based on historical data and realized scores.
A strategy for generating synthetic triplet data to optimize embedding models for low-resource languages, leveraging large language models to bridge the data gap.

The Boreas Road Trip dataset is a multi-sensor dataset for autonomous driving, comprising 60 sequences collected over 9 diverse real-world routes, aimed at evaluating algorithms under varying traffic and weather conditions.

Google's internal cluster management system that predates and inspired Kubernetes, scheduling hundreds of thousands of jobs across massive fleets by bin-packing workloads, enforcing priorities, and providing declarative job specifications.

A software application that runs automated tasks over the internet, typically performing simple and repetitive tasks much faster than a human could. Bots are used for web scraping, chatbots, and automation.

A network of compromised computers controlled remotely by an attacker, typically used to perform coordinated tasks like distributed denial-of-service attacks, spam distribution, and credential stuffing.
An architecture for an anthropomorphic social robot that utilizes a large language model to enhance human-robot interaction by conveying non-verbal social cues.
A point in a system where performance is limited, causing the overall throughput to be constrained. Identifying and resolving bottlenecks is key to optimizing system performance.

The process of identifying the slowest component or constraint in a system that limits overall throughput. Bottleneck analysis in software examines CPU, memory, I/O, and network to optimize performance.
An architecture designed for predicting the Short-Time Objective Intelligibility (STOI) metric, incorporating convolution blocks to enhance performance without requiring clean reference speech.
A novel Ising machine architecture that utilizes a tunable parameter to modulate spin dynamics, allowing for improved optimization by balancing escape from local minima and rapid convergence.

A technique that improves the efficiency of logic-based explanations for neural network decisions by tightening neuron bounds and eliminating unnecessary binary variables, significantly reducing explanation time.
A software testing technique that focuses on values at the boundaries of input ranges rather than the center. Boundary tests catch off-by-one errors and edge cases that are common sources of bugs.
A strategic mechanism used in metaheuristics to manage the exponential growth of non-dominated solutions in multi-objective optimization problems, enhancing solution diversity.
A central pattern in Domain-Driven Design that defines the boundary within which a particular domain model is defined and applicable. It helps manage complexity in large software systems.

A formal class of locality-preserving deterministic operators that act on graph-indexed state systems, allowing for incremental updates with a computational cost independent of the system's total dimension.
A novel logical framework that enhances traditional modal languages by incorporating intuitionistic and modal relations to better express resource availability and scoping in program semantics.
A rectangular border around an object in an image used in computer vision to define the location and extent of the object. Bounding boxes are fundamental to object detection tasks.
A pruning test for exact Euclidean AkNN joins that utilizes partitioned spatial datasets to optimize join evaluations by leveraging row group statistics.
The CSS box model describes the rectangular boxes generated for elements in the document tree. It consists of content, padding, border, and margin areas that determine element size and spacing.

The process of converting a value type to a reference type by wrapping it in an object. In languages like C# and Java, boxing allows primitive types to be treated as objects, though it incurs a performance overhead.
Business Process as a Service — a cloud computing model that delivers business process outsourcing through cloud services. It combines cloud infrastructure with business process management.

Berkeley Packet Filter — a virtual machine in the Linux kernel originally designed for packet filtering, now extended as eBPF to safely run sandboxed programs for observability, networking, and security without kernel module changes.
A task allocation algorithm for multi-core real-time vehicle control systems that integrates blocking time estimation and resource similarity analysis to minimize scheduling failures.

An omnifunctional brain foundation model that is jointly pretrained on fMRI, EEG, and MEG data, capable of processing both unimodal and multimodal inputs to leverage complementary spatiotemporal dynamics in neuroscience tasks.
BrainFuse is a unified infrastructure designed to integrate realistic biological modeling with core AI methodologies, addressing the incompatibility between AI frameworks and neural simulation tools.
A high-fidelity EEG foundation model that employs Dual-Domain Residual Vector Quantization for improved signal reconstruction, addressing the challenges of low signal-to-noise ratios in EEG data.
An independent line of development in a version control system. Branches allow developers to work on features or fixes in isolation before merging changes back to the main codebase.

An OS abstraction that enables AI agents to perform agentic exploration by providing copy-on-write state isolation and a structured lifecycle for process management, including atomic commit and rollback semantics.

A CPU pipeline optimization where the processor speculatively guesses the outcome of conditional branches and begins executing instructions along the predicted path, achieving near-zero branch penalty when predictions are correct.

An exact algorithm designed for solving the Moving Target Vehicle Routing Problem (MT-VRP) that incorporates a novel dominance criterion in its pricing subproblem to efficiently manage moving targets and time-dependent travel costs.
A model that generalizes the geometric rough path framework, allowing for the approximation of higher-level signatures through iterative compositions of lower-level signature maps.

A version control practice where developers create independent copies of code to work on features or fixes without affecting the main codebase. Git branching enables parallel development and is central to workflows like Git Flow and trunk-based development.
A convention for creating, using, and merging branches in version control systems. Strategies like Git Flow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development define how teams collaborate on code changes.

A multi-agent framework for seamless brand integration in text-to-video generation, addressing challenges such as maintaining prompt fidelity and ensuring brand recognizability while embedding advertiser brands into generated videos.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level. BFS is used for finding shortest paths and level-order traversal.
An exploratory prototype that spatially decomposes webpages into movable panels within a mixed-reality environment, allowing for mid-air and surface-attached interactions.

A virtual sub-meeting space within a video conference that allows participants to split into smaller groups for discussions. Breakout rooms are used for workshops, brainstorming, and parallel discussions.
A debugging tool that pauses program execution at a specified line of code. Breakpoints allow developers to inspect variables, evaluate expressions, and step through code line by line.
An audio deepfake detection framework that leverages fine-grained breath information to enhance generalization and detection accuracy against deepfake audio.

A self-supervised learning framework for extracting representations from CAD models to recognize machining features, utilizing a masked graph autoencoder based on geometric Attributed Adjacency Graphs.
A quantization module used for implementing fixed-point data processing in edge AI hardware, allowing for customizable bit widths during few-shot learning tasks.
A structural design pattern that separates an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently. It is useful when both the class and what it does need to vary.
A system designed to enhance parasport training by integrating a reconstruction pipeline for 3D play sequences and an embodiment-aware visualization framework for player tracking.

B.term: Broadband Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface Channel (BRISC) Dataset
B.definition: A dataset comprising channel state information (CSI) measurements collected at 5.53 GHz using a 256-element reconfigurable intelligent surface, aimed at facilitating research in wireless communication.
The transmission of data to all devices on a network simultaneously. Broadcasting is used for service discovery, network announcements, and distributing information to multiple recipients.
A data compression algorithm developed by Google that offers better compression ratios than gzip for web content. It is widely supported by modern browsers and reduces page load times.

A framework used in constructive mathematics to study decidability properties, allowing for nuanced classifications of propositions based on Brouwer ordinals, extending traditional notions of decidability.

A software development approach that involves working with existing systems, codebases, or infrastructure. Brownfield projects require dealing with legacy code, technical debt, and existing constraints.

Software development that involves working with existing legacy systems and infrastructure. Brownfield projects must account for existing code, data, and constraints unlike greenfield projects built from scratch.
A new stochastic activation function for LSTM networks that enhances gradient stability and learning performance in noisy financial time series by leveraging properties of Brownian motion.
A software application used to access and display web pages from the internet. Modern browsers like Chrome, Firefox, and Safari support HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and various web APIs.

Application programming interfaces provided by web browsers that allow JavaScript code to interact with browser features and the web platform. Examples include the DOM API, Fetch API, and Web Storage API.

The temporary storage of web resources like HTML pages, images, and scripts on a user's local device by the web browser. Caching reduces server load and improves page load times for returning visitors.

The ability of a website or web application to function correctly across different web browsers and versions. Ensuring compatibility requires testing and sometimes using polyfills or progressive enhancement.
The core component of a web browser that interprets HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to render web pages. Major browser engines include Blink (Chrome), Gecko (Firefox), and WebKit (Safari).
A technique that identifies and tracks users based on the unique configuration of their web browser and device properties such as screen resolution, installed fonts, and plugin versions.

An attack method that tries every possible combination of passwords or encryption keys until the correct one is found. Brute force attacks are computationally expensive but guaranteed to succeed given enough time.
Binary JSON — a binary-encoded serialization format used to store documents and make remote procedure calls in MongoDB. BSON extends JSON with additional data types like Date and BinData.

Binary Space Partitioning tree — a data structure that recursively subdivides space into convex sets using hyperplanes, used in 3D rendering for visibility determination and in computational geometry for point location.

A simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through a list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. It has O(n squared) time complexity and is mainly used for education.
A mathematical framework for parallel machine scheduling optimization that reduces computational complexity from exponential to polynomial by using adaptive temporal discretization.

A distribution-based sorting algorithm that distributes elements into a number of buckets, sorts each bucket individually, and then concatenates the results. It achieves O(n) time for uniformly distributed data.
A financial plan that estimates income and expenses over a specified period. In technology, budgets cover infrastructure costs, development resources, licensing, and operational expenses.
An automated notification triggered when cloud spending approaches or exceeds a predefined threshold. Budget alerts help organizations monitor and control their cloud infrastructure costs.

A type of uncertainty set in robust optimization where each parameter has an interval of possible values, and the total deviation from their lower bounds is constrained. This concept is particularly relevant in two-stage robust selection problems.

BuffCut is a buffered streaming graph partitioner that enhances the quality of partitioning in streaming scenarios by combining prioritized buffering with batch-wise multilevel assignment to reduce edge cuts.
A region of memory used to temporarily store data while it is being moved from one place to another. Buffers help smooth out differences in processing speed between components.
A software vulnerability that occurs when a program writes data beyond the boundaries of a pre-allocated buffer in memory. Buffer overflows can be exploited to execute arbitrary code.

An error, flaw, or unintended behavior in a software program that causes it to produce incorrect results or crash. The term originated from an actual moth found in a Harvard computer in 1947. Debugging is the process of finding and fixing bugs.

A program offered by organizations that rewards individuals for discovering and reporting software vulnerabilities. Bug bounty programs leverage crowdsourced security testing to find bugs before attackers do.
A software application that tracks reported software bugs and their resolution status. Bug trackers help teams prioritize fixes, assign work, and maintain a history of issues and solutions.
The process of logging, tracking, and managing software defects throughout their lifecycle from discovery to resolution. Bug tracking systems help teams prioritize and organize fix efforts.

The process of automating the creation of a software build and associated processes including compiling, packaging, and testing. Build tools like Make, Gradle, and Maven streamline development workflows.
A mechanism that stores the results of build steps so they can be reused in subsequent builds when inputs have not changed. Build caching significantly reduces build times in CI/CD pipelines.
A collection of tools and processes that automate the compilation, linking, and packaging of source code into executable programs or deployable artifacts. Examples include Make, Gradle, and Webpack.
Software that automates the process of compiling source code into binary code, packaging binary code, and running automated tests. Examples include Maven, Gradle, Make, and npm scripts.
A creational design pattern that separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations.

An advanced method for resource allocation in wireless systems that leverages machine learning to simultaneously allocate multiple resources, addressing the limitations of single-resource allocation in reliability-critical applications.
An architecture pattern that isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others continue to function. Named after ship compartments that prevent flooding from spreading.
A design pattern that isolates elements of an application into pools so that if one fails, the others will continue to function. It is named after the watertight compartments in a ship's hull.
A tool that combines multiple JavaScript, CSS, and asset files into optimized bundles for efficient delivery to web browsers. Popular bundlers include Webpack, Rollup, Vite, and esbuild.
A graphical representation of work remaining versus time in an agile sprint. It shows the amount of work left to complete and helps teams track progress toward sprint goals.
The rate at which a company spends its cash reserves or venture capital before generating positive cash flow. In cloud computing, it can refer to the rate of resource consumption.
A computer architecture where all components are connected through a shared communication pathway called a bus. It allows data transfer between the CPU, memory, and peripheral devices.
The minimum number of team members whose sudden loss would cripple a project due to lack of knowledge or capability. A low bus factor indicates high risk from key person dependency.

The organized effort of individuals to produce and sell goods or services for profit. In technology, business encompasses strategy, operations, marketing, finance, and the commercial aspects of software products.

The planning and preparation to ensure that an organization can continue to operate during and after a disaster. It encompasses disaster recovery, crisis management, and business resumption strategies.

Technologies, practices, and strategies for collecting, integrating, and analyzing business data to support better decision-making. BI tools create dashboards, reports, and visualizations that transform raw data into actionable insights.
The part of a software application that encodes the real-world business rules that determine how data can be created, stored, and changed. It represents the core functionality of the application.

A collection of related, structured activities that produce a specific outcome for a particular customer or market. Business process management involves analyzing, modeling, and optimizing these workflows.
The use of technology to execute recurring tasks or processes where manual effort can be replaced. BPA improves efficiency, reduces errors, and frees employees for higher-value work.
A synchronization technique where a process repeatedly checks a condition in a tight loop instead of sleeping or yielding. While simple, busy waiting wastes CPU cycles and is generally avoided.
A unit of digital information consisting of eight bits that can represent 256 different values. Bytes are the fundamental unit of data storage and processing in computing.

A form of instruction set designed for efficient execution by a software interpreter or virtual machine. Java bytecode and Python bytecode are compiled from source code and executed by their respective VMs.
A program that executes bytecode instructions one at a time, translating each bytecode operation into native machine operations. Interpreters trade execution speed for portability and simplicity.
A cloud-native OLAP engine designed for efficient multimodal retrieval and incremental computation, addressing challenges in data analytics infrastructures for modern enterprises.
A sequence of bytes transmitted between processes or over a network. TCP provides a reliable bytestream abstraction, while UDP provides datagram-based communication.
The ability of a distributed system to continue operating correctly even when some components fail or act maliciously. BFT consensus algorithms ensure agreement despite the presence of faulty nodes.

C.definition: A general-purpose, procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs in the 1970s. C provides low-level memory access and maps efficiently to machine instructions, making it foundational for operating systems and embedded systems.

A general-purpose procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs that has greatly influenced many other modern languages. C provides low-level access to memory and is widely used for system programming.
A cloud-native framework that automates the deployment and management of edge pods for remote access and sharing of wireless testbeds, facilitating over-the-air experimentation.
A programming language designed to express concepts captured by transformers, which facilitates the verification of programs through connections to synchronous dataflow languages.

A general-purpose programming language that extends C with object-oriented features, templates, and standard libraries. It is used for system software, game engines, embedded systems, and high-performance applications.

C$^{2}$TC is a training-free framework designed for efficient tabular data condensation, synthesizing small yet informative datasets to maintain data utility while minimizing storage and training costs.

CA-AFP is a unified framework for Cluster-Aware Adaptive Federated Pruning that addresses statistical and system heterogeneity in Federated Learning by performing model pruning specific to client clusters.
Customer Acquisition Cost — the total cost of acquiring a new customer, including marketing, sales, and related expenses. CAC is a key metric for evaluating the efficiency of growth strategies.

A high-speed data storage layer that stores frequently accessed data for rapid retrieval. Caching reduces latency and server load by keeping copies of data closer to where it's needed, used in browsers, CDNs, databases, and CPUs.
The process of removing or updating stale data from a cache when the underlying data changes. It is famously one of the hardest problems in computer science, requiring careful consistency management.

A conversational tool that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Large Language Models to enable semantic reasoning over cache traces, allowing architects to query cache performance issues in natural language.
A novel solution for optimizing CPU cache utilization in cloud virtual machines, providing accurate cache abstraction using eviction sets without requiring hardware support.
The practice of storing frequently accessed data in a fast-access storage layer to reduce latency and load on backend systems. Caching strategies include write-through, write-back, and write-around.

Continuous Actor-Critic with Trajectory Optimization (CACTO) is a framework that combines reinforcement learning and trajectory optimization to efficiently compute low-cost trajectories for optimal control problems.

C.term: CAD Profile Generation via Geometric Constructions
C.definition: A method for generating Computer Aided Design profiles through a sequence of geometric constructions, enhancing generation quality by incorporating construction steps.

CADDTO-PPO is a carbon-aware decentralized dynamic task offloading framework that utilizes multi-agent reinforcement learning to optimize task allocation in mobile edge computing (MEC) networks, integrating renewable energy sources for sustainable operations.
An evolution-based pipeline and dataset for creating realistic Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models, addressing the limitations of existing data by incorporating complex operations and design intent.

A framework that reformulates automated feature engineering as a causally-guided sequential decision process, integrating causal discovery with reinforcement learning to construct robust feature representations.
An evaluation framework designed to quantify audio reasoning errors in Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs), addressing the challenges of perception during reasoning tasks.
A technique for high-fidelity 3D head avatar reconstruction that uses cross-attention guidance to adaptively control Gaussian primitives based on distinct facial region dynamics.

Counterfactual Adaptive Kernel Execution (CAKE) is a system that learns to select the optimal physical implementation (kernel) for database operations based on the input data distribution using a microsecond-scale contextual multi-armed bandit approach.

C.term: Calibrated Federated Learning with Hardware-aware Fuzzy Clustering
C.definition: A novel intrusion detection method that combines Federated Learning with fuzzy clustering techniques, tailored to improve performance in heterogeneous IoT environments while addressing security vulnerabilities.

CaliCausalRank is a unified framework for ad ranking that optimizes multiple objectives through training-time scale calibration and robust counterfactual utility estimation, addressing challenges like score scale inconsistency and position bias.
A stack data structure that stores information about the active subroutines of a program. Each function call pushes a new frame onto the stack, and returning from a function pops the frame.

A function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed after an operation completes. Callbacks are a fundamental pattern in asynchronous programming, though modern code often uses Promises or async/await instead.

A novel ROS 2 Executor that establishes a persistent one-to-one correspondence between callbacks and OS threads, enabling direct application of OS scheduling parameters to enhance real-time scheduling.
An open-source tool for creating narrated e-books that synchronize audio with text, enhancing literacy and accessibility for readers, particularly those with reading challenges.

Cascaded Monotonic Bernstein operator, a modeling technique for recovering radiometric fidelity from unknown dynamic range compression by enforcing monotonicity as a hard architectural inductive bias.
An architecture designed to enhance cache efficiency for multi-tenant deep neural networks (DNNs) on integrated NPUs by supporting model-exclusive regions within shared cache to reduce contention.

Camel is a frame-level bandwidth estimation technique designed for low-latency live streaming, addressing the issue of stalling events caused by misestimations in conventional packet-level congestion control algorithms.
An online inspection algorithm that utilizes the fusion of camera and LiDAR data for real-time detection and localization of power line insulators without prior mapping.
A deployment strategy where a new version of software is gradually rolled out to a small subset of users before being deployed to the entire infrastructure. It reduces risk by limiting exposure.
A deployment strategy where a new version is gradually rolled out to a small subset of users before being deployed to the entire infrastructure. It reduces risk by detecting issues early.
A sound and complete solver for inhabitation and unification in dependent type theory, implemented in Lean, facilitating theorem proving and program synthesis.
An HTML element used to draw graphics on a web page via JavaScript. The Canvas API provides means for drawing shapes, text, images, and animations in both 2D and 3D contexts.
A theoretical framework stating that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide only two of three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.
A programming language paradigm that restricts access to resources and effects through capabilities, enhancing safety in AI agents' interactions with external tools.
The process of determining the production capacity needed by an organization to meet changing demands for its products or services. In IT, it ensures systems can handle expected workloads.
Algorithms designed to solve minimum-cost flow problems, including Orlin's algorithm, which is notable for its efficiency and formal correctness in executable implementations.

A safe and interpretable multimodal path planning method that allows decentralized agents to collaboratively adapt their paths in real-time to avoid collisions and ensure safety during physical collaboration.
Capital Expenditure — funds used by an organization to acquire, upgrade, or maintain physical assets such as servers, buildings, or equipment. Cloud computing shifts many CapEx costs to OpEx.
A capacity pricing framework that interprets capacity commitments as reliability options, addressing risk exposure in electricity markets amidst renewable energy challenges.
A power-capped pipelining technique for Coarse-Grained Reconfigurable Array (CGRA) compilers that maintains performance while adhering to strict power budgets.

Confusion-Aware Prompt Tuning (CAPT) is a framework designed to reduce misalignment in vision-language models by leveraging a Confusion Bank that models systematic misclassifications between visually and semantically similar categories.

A type of challenge-response test used to determine whether the user is human. CAPTCHAs prevent bots from accessing websites and services by requiring tasks that are easy for humans but difficult for machines.
A method for conducting membership inference attacks on latent diffusion models without requiring ground-truth captions, addressing privacy concerns in scenarios where only images are available.
An ML-guided framework for online tuning of client-side RPC and caching parameters in parallel file systems, aimed at enhancing scalability and adaptivity based on local metrics.
The practice of shifting computing workloads to times and locations where electricity generation produces the lowest carbon emissions. It is part of sustainable and green computing initiatives.
