C.term: Contact Modes for Tactile-Based Shape Reconstruction
C.definition: This concept explores different tactile interaction modes (grasp-releasing, sliding, rolling) to optimize shape reconstruction of objects using tactile sensors, enhancing the efficiency of data acquisition in robotic systems.
An odometry technique for legged robots that utilizes only IMU and motor measurements, treating contacting legs as kinematic anchors to improve pose and velocity estimation.

A lightweight, standalone package that includes everything needed to run a piece of software, including the code, runtime, libraries, and system tools. Containers provide consistent environments across development and production.
A repository for storing and distributing container images. Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, and Google Container Registry are popular registries for managing container artifacts.

A lightweight virtualization method that packages applications with their dependencies into isolated containers. Containers share the host OS kernel, making them faster and more efficient than traditional virtual machines.
A technique for identifying data contamination in small language models by measuring the peakedness of the model's sampled outputs, which can indicate the presence of verbatim memorization.
A geographically distributed group of servers that work together to provide fast delivery of internet content. CDNs cache content at edge locations close to end users to reduce latency.
The process of creating, managing, and modifying digital content. Content management systems like WordPress and Drupal provide tools for non-technical users to manage website content.
A strategic marketing approach focused on creating and distributing valuable, relevant content to attract and retain a target audience. It builds brand awareness and drives customer engagement.
The practice of monitoring and filtering user-generated content to ensure it complies with community guidelines and legal requirements. It uses a combination of AI and human review.

An HTTP response header that allows website administrators to control resources the user agent is allowed to load for a given page. CSP helps prevent cross-site scripting and other code injection attacks.
The planning, creation, delivery, and governance of content across all channels. A content strategy ensures that content supports business goals, meets user needs, and remains consistent.
A technique for rate control in video coding that predicts frame-level bit consumption based on content features, improving efficiency and quality stability compared to traditional methods.

Context Drift refers to the phenomenon where LLM agents deviate from project-specific coding conventions due to limitations in context or conflicting legacy code, leading to the accumulation of technical debt.
A method for managing access control in LLM-based computer-use agents by utilizing contextual information to enhance security and usability.
A platform for managing data streams in industrial IoT that integrates context-aware semantics to enhance interoperability and flexibility in handling heterogeneous data.
Context-Aware Windowing is a strategy used in automatic speech recognition to create context-preserving segments for decoding by incorporating vocal separation and voice activity detection.
A modeling approach for semantic communication that incorporates context as a state-dependent variable, influencing the communication process and addressing semantic noise.
A technique for inferring grammars of complex programming languages in black box settings, addressing the inefficiencies of existing tools when dealing with large datasets.
The challenge of using Transformers to recognize context-free languages, highlighting the limitations of standard architectures in processing grammatical syntax.

CD-MAB is a bilevel framework that optimizes adaptive modulation and transmission power in underwater acoustic networks by considering both channel state feedback and Age of Information (AoI) to maximize throughput.

A system that models the accumulated understanding of large language models as version-controlled state, using a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) to manage session history and enable efficient state management during reasoning tasks.

A system that models the accumulated understanding of large language models as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), enabling version-controlled state management and lossless trimming of context during extended reasoning tasks.

Contextual Metaprogramming integrates staged metaprogramming into session-typed message passing functional languages, enabling the transmission and application of contextual values across variable contexts.
An extension of the PARWiS algorithm that incorporates contextual information to enhance winner determination through active pairwise comparisons under budget constraints.
A proactive privacy defense paradigm where an instructor model generates context-aware privacy guidance during multi-step agent execution, enhancing user privacy management.
A technique for accelerating the prefill process in large language models (LLMs) through granularity-aligned key-value cache management, improving memory scalability and reducing I/O bottlenecks.

C.term: Continual Learning for Neural OFDM Receivers
C.definition: A framework for enabling deep neural networks to adaptively learn in real-time for orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) receivers, allowing for continual learning without the need for periodic retraining.

A technique that enhances the capabilities of multilingual LLMs by continuing the training process on a targeted dataset, improving performance for underrepresented languages while maintaining overall model quality.
A technique that improves the performance of multilingual large language models on underrepresented languages, such as Estonian, through continued pretraining with a diverse dataset.

A software development practice where code changes are automatically built, tested, and prepared for a release to production. It extends continuous integration by deploying all code changes after the build stage.

The T-score is a continuous dominance indicator that maps final scores in team sports to real values, facilitating real-time win probability evaluation and player assessment based on cumulative box-score data.
An end-to-end navigation policy that maps bird's-eye view observations and language instructions to continuous flow fields, allowing for smooth trajectory generation without discrete action sampling.
An innovative framework for uplink channel estimation in cell-free massive MIMO systems, utilizing Gaussian process regression to model spatially correlated wireless channels.
An ongoing effort to improve products, services, or processes through incremental and breakthrough improvements. In software, it involves iterative refinement based on feedback and metrics.
The ongoing process of observing system health, security, and compliance in real time. It enables rapid detection and response to performance issues, security threats, and policy violations.

An optimization problem focused on the placement of points in a compact subset of Euclidean space to maximize the minimum distance between points and boundaries, applicable in facility placement and communication network design.
A framework that addresses concept drift and catastrophic forgetting in evolving streaming time series by allowing neural networks to learn continuously from unbounded data streams.
A technique used to capture real-time audience engagement and reactions during presentations, allowing for immediate feedback on speaker performance.
A framework for integrated sensing and communications that utilizes continuous operators for signal modeling, enhancing performance under fading channels through various beamforming designs.
A reformulation of traditional marriage and divorce models using continuous time dynamics, improving computational efficiency in calculating key outcomes.
An approach to multi-agent reinforcement learning that operates in continuous time, addressing the limitations of discrete-time Markov Decision Processes in complex environments.
A framework for solving optimal switching problems across multiple regimes using reinforcement learning, characterized by an exploratory formulation that incorporates entropy regularization.
A framework for intent-based networking that translates operator intents into actionable policies while ensuring compliance with formal constraints through a closed-loop auditing process.
A technique used in multimodal large language models to refine visual-text fusion by performing systematic layer-wise masking analysis, revealing the dynamics of information integration.
Contrastive Concept-Tree Search (CCTS) is a method for LLM-assisted algorithm discovery that extracts hierarchical concept representations from generated programs to enhance optimization performance.

A learning paradigm that combines contrastive representation learning with continual adaptation, allowing IoT systems to robustly reuse features across different tasks while facing challenges from adversarial dynamics.
An approach that treats automatic word-naming recognition as an audio-text matching problem, leveraging text-audio alignment to improve recognition accuracy for post-stroke patients with aphasia.

A method for adjusting the generative behavior of large language models (LLMs) at inference time by using examples of prompt responses to identify a direction in an intermediate activation layer and shift activations in that subspace.

This technique enhances visual reasoning in vision language models by utilizing contrastive pairs of visually similar images and synonymous questions to refine reasoning paths and improve model performance.
A technique for tracking contributions in low-memory private algorithms, linking the memory efficiency of differential privacy to the necessity of limiting individual contributions.
A technique used in shared autonomy systems that enforces hard safety guarantees during control tasks by integrating safety constraints at the inverse kinematics layer.
The order in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls are executed in a program. Control flow is regulated by conditional statements, loops, and function calls.

Control Flow Auditing (CFAud) is a mechanism that ensures a remote verifier receives evidence about the control flow path taken on a Micro-Controller Unit (MCU), even in the event of software compromise.
The part of a network that carries signaling traffic and is responsible for routing. In Kubernetes, the control plane manages the overall state of the cluster and makes scheduling decisions.

C.term: Control Plane for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS)
C.definition: A proposed system-level control plane for integrating reconfigurable intelligent surfaces into wireless networks, focusing on spectral resource allocation and data plane rate selection.
A system that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of other systems or processes. Control systems are used in manufacturing, robotics, HVAC, and industrial automation.

Refers to the ability to induce any given solution as an optimum for a set of objective functions by controlling specific subsets of a finite ground set. This concept has applications in traffic flow optimization.

C.term: Controllable End-to-End Vector Floor Plan Generation
C.definition: An automated method for generating residential floor plans that balances topological, geometric, and aesthetic considerations without relying on a multi-step pipeline, allowing for greater flexibility in design outputs.
A software design paradigm that decreases the number of decisions developers need to make by providing sensible defaults. Frameworks like Ruby on Rails popularized this principle.

C.term: Convergence of Stochastic Gradient Descent with Perturbations
C.definition: A theoretical analysis of stochastic gradient descent that examines the effects of perturbations in both forward and backward passes on convergence behavior in composite optimization problems.
A method for proving complexity lower bounds for Boolean circuits through a convergent term graph rewriting system, facilitating circuit simplifications based on Boolean identities.

AI systems designed to engage in human-like dialogue, understanding natural language inputs and generating contextual responses. Conversational AI powers chatbots, virtual assistants, and customer service tools.
The use of conversational artificial intelligence by older adults for emotional expression, particularly in contexts where human interaction is unavailable or perceived as burdensome.
This framework evaluates conversational naturalness in two-speaker, multi-turn dialogues, addressing the limitations of existing naturalness estimators that focus on single-speaker utterances.
A task that generates NoSQL queries from natural language questions in a conversational context, addressing the challenges of multi-turn interactions in querying NoSQL databases.

The percentage of users who complete a desired action, such as making a purchase, signing up, or clicking a button. Optimizing conversion rates (CRO) is central to digital marketing and product design.

The Convex Block-Cholesky Approach is a method for designing robust trajectories in space missions under operational uncertainties, integrating navigation processes into the trajectory optimization framework.
A prototype-based method designed to enhance the evaluation of self-supervised learning embeddings in audio, minimizing the reliance on fine-tuning and improving the robustness of audio SSL models.

A mathematical operation used in neural networks, particularly CNNs, where a filter slides over input data to produce feature maps. Convolution is fundamental to image recognition and signal processing.
A numerical method for solving backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) that improves boundary error through modified damping and shifting schemes.

An innovative approach for pricing European options under the Heston model that utilizes convolution and Fast Fourier Transform techniques to achieve stable and accurate pricing without the need for empirical adjustments.
A class of deep neural networks most commonly applied to analyzing visual imagery. CNNs use convolution operations to automatically learn spatial hierarchies of features from input images.

An emulator designed to model consumer-grade zoned flash storage, incorporating architectural features and resource constraints typical of such systems to facilitate software and hardware design enhancements.
A small piece of data stored on the user's computer by a web browser that remembers information between sessions. Cookies are used for session management, personalization, and tracking.
A tool that combines reinforcement learning with probabilistic model checking and explainable AI to verify and explain neural policies in safety-critical domains like platelet inventory management.

C.term: Cooperative Game Theory Model for UN Financing
C.definition: A novel model that shifts from Nash equilibrium to a cooperative framework for funding mechanisms in the UN, aligning contributions with benefits derived from UN activities for sustainable financing.

A framework that enables multiple access points to collaboratively perform multistatic sensing while providing communication services in a cell-free MIMO system, enhancing localization and velocity estimation.
COOPERTRIM is a proactive data selection technique for cooperative perception in autonomous systems, designed to optimize bandwidth usage while maintaining situational awareness.
A strategic environment where competing entities collaborate in certain areas while competing in others, often seen in business ecosystems and service delivery models.
An algorithm developed for Bayesian Mixed Data Sampling (MIDAS) regression that utilizes a bilinear structure to improve the efficiency of variational updates.
An advanced multi-agent planning algorithm that employs a stochastic Boltzmann policy and a decaying entropy bonus to enhance exploration in decentralized Monte Carlo Tree Search environments.
This approach integrates truck and drone routing to enhance last-mile delivery efficiency by synchronizing the strengths of both vehicles, accommodating various operational constraints.
A latent diffusion transformer model designed for generating Earth observation data by parameterizing conditional mappings as data distributions to better capture uncertainty and variability.

COPIFTv2 enhances lightweight RISC-V cores by integrating lightweight queues for improved dual-issue execution, allowing concurrent processing of integer and floating-point instructions with increased IPC and energy efficiency.

A zero-shot 3D alignment framework that utilizes vision-language and geometric constraints to optimize the relative pose of two meshes based on a text prompt, enhancing content creation and scene assembly.
Legal protection granted to the creator of original works including software, documentation, and digital content. Copyright governs reproduction, distribution, and adaptation of creative works.
A novel framework for autoregressive image generation that introduces Constrained Reasoning to guide the overall structure of images, addressing spatial ambiguity and unrealistic object overlaps.

Common Object Request Broker Architecture — a standard defined by the Object Management Group for enabling communication between software components regardless of programming language or operating system.

A set of specific metrics defined by Google that measure user experience on web pages, including loading performance, interactivity, and visual stability. They influence search rankings and guide web optimization.
A component that generalizes subroutines for cooperative multitasking by allowing execution to be suspended and resumed. Coroutines are used in async/await patterns in modern programming languages.
Behavioral types that model interactions of coroutines, allowing for the representation of multiple receiving and yielding operations, enhancing the expressiveness of coroutine programming.
An analytical channel model for molecular communication systems that provides a closed-form description of trajectory-level arrival dynamics under time-varying drift conditions.
A statistical measure that describes the extent to which two variables change together. Correlation does not imply causation but helps identify relationships between variables in data analysis.

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a security mechanism that allows web pages to request resources from a different domain than the one serving the page. CORS headers control which origins, methods, and headers are permitted in cross-origin requests.
A resource-efficient mixed-precision vector processing engine designed for high-throughput AIoT applications, featuring a CORDIC-based MAC unit that adapts performance based on workload requirements.
A type of polymorphism defined for finite structures, characterized by operations that can generate cosets, with implications for computational complexity in decision problems.

CoShadow is a method for multi-object shadow generation in image compositing, utilizing diffusion models to create physically plausible shadows that maintain geometric consistency across multiple foreground objects.
The process of reducing cloud spending while maintaining performance and functionality. Cost optimization strategies include right-sizing resources, using reserved instances, and eliminating waste.
A non-autoregressive model for accent normalization that utilizes a source-synthesis methodology for training data construction, addressing issues of unnatural outputs and content distortion.

A sampling method that utilizes a time-varying Hamiltonian to improve the convergence of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo in multimodal distributions, effectively interpolating between distributions to enhance sampling efficiency.

CCL rewards are a novel approach to scoring exploration in multi-agent systems by isolating each agent's unique contribution to team exploration. This method aims to reduce redundancy and enhance coordinated strategies in open-ended domains.
A framework that tests user-specified microarchitectural models against performance counter data to identify inconsistencies and suggest plausible microarchitectural features.

CounterVid is a scalable framework for counterfactual video generation that mitigates hallucinations in video-language models by synthesizing videos with altered actions or temporal structures while maintaining scene context.

C.term: Coupled Dynamical System for Skill Evolution
C.definition: A mathematical model that describes the joint evolution of human skills and AI delegation, where skill improves with use and decays without, influenced by performance metrics.
The degree of interdependence between software modules. Low coupling is desirable as it makes systems more modular, maintainable, and testable by reducing dependencies between components.
A beamforming technique that incorporates a mutual coupling model in continuous aperture arrays (CAPAs) to enhance beamforming performance by addressing electromagnetic interactions between elements.
CASP is a two-stage repair operator for cardinality-constrained portfolio optimization that incorporates the covariance structure of asset returns to improve portfolio diversification.
CoVe is a post-training data synthesis framework for training interactive tool-use agents, ensuring data complexity and correctness through explicit task constraints that guide trajectory generation.

This technique focuses on uncertainty quantification in scientific machine learning by guiding models to internalize complex physical behaviors across multiple regimes, enhancing the understanding of system responses to varying conditions.
A framework that decomposes PAC sample complexity results into three factors: coverage, structure, and objective, providing insights into reinforcement learning guarantees.
An iterative framework for generating SystemVerilog assertions from natural language specifications, focusing on improving assertion quality through functional coverage-guided verification.

A novel framework for offline policy evaluation in partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) that utilizes the intrinsic metric structure of the belief space to derive error bounds, mitigating the exponential blow-up in horizon and memory.
A mathematical problem related to constructing arrays that can efficiently tolerate multiple failures in directed networks, improving routing decisions in communication networks.
A novel mechanism that exploits page faults to create covert communication channels, allowing unauthorized data leakage between processes by bypassing OS isolation mechanisms.

A communication strategy that utilizes direct sequence spread spectrum signaling to maintain covertness in the presence of adversarial cycle detectors, balancing latency and quality-of-service constraints.

An end-to-end large language model (LALM) that processes continuous audio inputs and generates outputs within a unified architecture, achieving state-of-the-art performance across various audio tasks.
The Chain-of-World Vision-Language-Action model that combines temporal reasoning with disentangled latent-action encoding to enhance predictive modeling of visual dynamics.

Central Processing Unit — the primary component of a computer that performs most of the data processing. The CPU executes instructions from programs by performing arithmetic, logic, control, and input/output operations.
The process of determining which process or thread gets to use the CPU at any given time. Scheduling algorithms like Round Robin, FIFO, and Priority Scheduling optimize system performance.
A compatibility-oriented approach that enhances communication in heterogeneous training environments by optimizing data transfer between CPUs and GPUs.
An MPI-based communication abstraction that eliminates CPU involvement in the communication path for GPU-based applications, enhancing performance and reducing synchronization burdens.
An architectural approach that eliminates the traditional CPU in favor of parallel execution of tasks specified in programming languages, leveraging the inherent parallelism of functional languages.
An innovative approach to executing functional programs directly in parallel digital logic without a CPU, leveraging the inherent parallelism of lambda calculus.
A hardware-aware embedding shaping technique for retrieval-augmented generation that optimizes Compute-in-Memory architectures to bridge the representation gap in high-dimensional embeddings.

Command Query Responsibility Segregation — a pattern that separates read and write operations into different models. CQRS can improve performance, scalability, and security in complex domain-driven applications.

Craig interpolation is a technique used in program verification to automate the inference of loop invariants and the computation of program abstractions. It involves deriving interpolants that can simplify the verification process across various theories and data types.
Crane is a neural sketching method for summarizing graph streams, designed to improve adaptability and accuracy in capturing dynamic relationships among entities within memory constraints.
CRANE-LLM is a novel approach that enhances large language models with structured runtime information from Jupyter notebook kernel states to detect and diagnose crashes in machine learning notebooks.

This technique involves implementing checkpoint installation protocols for AI training on macOS/APFS to ensure data integrity and recovery from failures, using various write modes for durability guarantees.
CrashFixer is an LLM-based software repair agent tailored for resolving Linux kernel bugs, leveraging a benchmark called kGym to evaluate its performance in system-level bug fixing.

Conflict-Free Replicated Data Type — a data structure that can be replicated across multiple computers in a network, where replicas can be updated independently and always converge to a consistent state.

This paradigm allows users to decompose story elements and link them coherently in human-AI co-creation tasks, ensuring that generated content aligns with user intent and narrative consistency. It enhances the collaborative storytelling process by enabling tailored prompts for AI-generated outputs.
A type of cyber attack where stolen username-password combinations from one breach are used to gain unauthorized access to other accounts. It exploits the common practice of password reuse.

A mechanism designed to reallocate influence in opinion aggregation systems by learning which agents and viewpoints consistently track evolving public evidence, addressing the brittleness of collective judgments under weak truth signals.
A social mechanism designed to improve collective self-correction in opinion aggregation systems by reallocating influence based on agents' reliability in tracking public evidence.
An allocation mechanism for shared resource pools that ensures fairness among agents with time-varying demands, satisfying strategyproofness and Pareto efficiency.
A dataset and case study focused on zero- and few-shot named-entity recognition in the crime domain, providing over 1.5k annotated documents to improve information extraction for law enforcement.
An XR framework designed for rapid reconstruction and analysis of dynamic crime scenes, facilitating offline analysis through user-friendly 3D modeling tools tailored for non-experts.

The longest sequence of dependent tasks in a project that determines the minimum project duration. Identifying the critical path helps project managers focus resources on tasks that affect the timeline.
Customer Relationship Management — a technology and strategy for managing all interactions with current and potential customers. CRM systems track sales, customer service, and marketing activities.

A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems that executes commands or scripts at specified intervals. Cron jobs are widely used for automated maintenance tasks, backups, and data processing.
A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems that allows users to schedule scripts or commands to run automatically at specified intervals. Cron is fundamental to system administration.
A statistical method for evaluating machine learning models by partitioning data into subsets, training on some and testing on others. K-fold cross validation is the most common technique.
A framework for managing AI-as-a-Service that integrates network and compute resources to ensure reliable and timely delivery of AI model inference.

A technique for training-free prompt compression in large language models that utilizes attention-based token importance estimation without relying on a draft model from the same family, enabling more flexible model deployment.
A methodology for evaluating and optimizing the resilience of embodied AI systems by analyzing interactions across different layers of hardware and software to enhance efficiency and reliability.
A framework for multimodal learning that organizes features into a three-level semantic hierarchy, addressing semantic misalignment and improving representation quality across modalities.
A novel channel estimation technique for multi-antenna OTFS receivers that utilizes superimposed pilot signals to effectively separate multipath components in the angular domain.
The ability of software to run on multiple operating systems or hardware platforms without modification. Cross-platform frameworks like Flutter, React Native, and Electron enable this capability.

A framework that integrates graph neural networks with transformer architectures to enhance sequential recommendation systems by explicitly modeling complex relationships between user interactions over time.
The practice of selling additional products or services to existing customers. In technology, cross-selling leverages data analytics and recommendation systems to suggest complementary offerings.
An attack that forces authenticated users to submit unwanted requests to a web application. CSRF exploits the trust that a site has in the user's browser.

A resampling technique used to evaluate machine learning models on limited data by splitting the dataset into multiple folds for iterative training and testing. It provides a more robust performance estimate.

CVGL is a technique for establishing spatial correspondences between images captured from different viewpoints, addressing challenges like geometric asymmetry and texture inconsistency in GNSS-denied environments.

A paradigm that integrates crowdsourcing platforms for data collection and expert opinion modeling to enhance the learning of causal structures from observational data, addressing challenges related to graph combinatorial explosion and ambiguities.
Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations of persistent storage. CRUD operations form the foundation of most database-driven applications and RESTful API designs.
Crypto-RV is a high-efficiency RISC-V cryptographic co-processor designed for IoT security, supporting a wide range of cryptographic algorithms within a single 64-bit datapath.
A digital or virtual currency that uses cryptography for security and operates on decentralized networks based on blockchain technology. Bitcoin and Ethereum are the most well-known cryptocurrencies.
A mathematical algorithm that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size output. Cryptographic hashes are one-way functions used for data integrity verification and password storage.
The practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of adversaries. Modern cryptography encompasses symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, and digital signatures.

The first RISC-V-based processor specifically designed for high-performance cryptographic computations, integrating power side-channel protection to mitigate vulnerabilities in cryptographic operations.

A framework that leverages micro-signals within Channel State Information (CSI) for Radio-Frequency Fingerprinting, enabling open-set authentication of commodity WiFi devices based on unique RF characteristics.
CSLib is a centralized library for formalized computer science and software in Lean, providing reusable semantic interfaces and proof automation to support compatibility with Mathlib.

Cross-Site Request Forgery — a web security vulnerability that tricks a user's browser into executing unwanted actions on a trusted site where they are authenticated. CSRF tokens are the primary defense.

Cascading Style Sheets — a stylesheet language used to describe the visual presentation of HTML documents. CSS controls layout, colors, fonts, animations, and responsive behavior, separating content structure from visual design.

The technique of animating HTML elements using CSS properties like transition, transform, and keyframes. CSS animations provide smooth visual effects without JavaScript and are hardware-accelerated by browsers.
A pre-built collection of CSS files providing consistent styling, layout systems, and UI components. Popular frameworks like Bootstrap, Tailwind CSS, and Bulma accelerate web development.
Custom properties defined in CSS using the -- prefix that store reusable values like colors, fonts, and spacing. CSS variables enable theming and reduce repetition in stylesheets.
A code similarity metric that utilizes program dependence graphs to model control dependencies and variable interactions, providing a deeper semantic analysis of code similarity.

Comma-Separated Values — a plain-text file format that stores tabular data with each line representing a row and commas separating column values. CSV is widely used for data exchange between applications.
Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) alignment is an optimization technique used in speech recognition to align sequences of varying lengths, enhancing model performance.
An innovative approach in Unified Speech Recognition that employs greedily decoded CTC pseudo-labels to enhance training efficiency and robustness against distribution shifts.
CTC-TTS is a dual-streaming text-to-speech system that utilizes connectionist temporal classification for improved text-speech alignment and low-latency synthesis.
A comprehensive dataset and method for detecting AI-generated CT images, addressing security risks associated with synthetic medical imaging and evaluating model generalization.

A Continuous-Time Markov Chain model used in a federated learning framework for assessing bridge deterioration, allowing municipalities to collaboratively train without sharing sensitive inspection data.
A low size, weight, power, and cost (SWaP-C) microwave spectrometer designed for atmospheric sensing, utilizing passive wave guide filter banks for water vapor radiometry.

CUCo is an agent-driven framework that automates the generation of high-performance CUDA kernels for large-scale distributed training and inference, focusing on optimizing both computation and communication.
A parallel computing platform and API model created by NVIDIA that allows developers to use GPUs for general-purpose processing. CUDA is extensively used in deep learning and scientific computing.

cuNRTO is a framework that utilizes CUDA for efficient nonlinear robust trajectory optimization, enabling autonomous systems to compute control policies under uncertainty with reduced computational costs.
A method for evaluating cultural biases in large vision-language models by generating counterfactual examples that highlight biases related to cultural differences beyond visual characteristics.

The phenomenon of erasing linguistic markers unique to non-native English varieties in large language model outputs, quantified using metrics like Identity Erasure Rate (IER) and Semantic Preservation Score (SPS).
A systematic approach to evaluate the cultural competence of AI systems across diverse cultural contexts, integrating measurement theory for comprehensive assessment.
A knowledge base created by extracting culturally relevant content from Wikipedia, tailored for diverse languages and cultures, enhancing the performance of retrieval augmented generation tasks.
A logical framework that extends propositional logic by incorporating dependence between propositions, allowing for more expressive entailments captured through cumulative models.

CUDA Nonlinear Robust Trajectory Optimization (cuNRTO) is a framework that introduces dynamic optimization architectures for robust trajectory optimization, enabling autonomous systems to operate safely under uncertainty using GPU acceleration.
A framework for counterfactual survival prediction that employs multimodal embedding and latent subgroup retrieval to optimize individualized survival outcomes.

cuRPQ is a high-performance GPU-optimized framework for processing regular and conjunctive regular path queries, addressing computational challenges through novel traversal algorithms and efficient management schemes.

A functional programming technique that transforms a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions, each taking a single argument. Currying enables partial application and function composition.

C.term: Curse of Unrolling in Algorithm Differentiation
C.definition: A phenomenon in machine learning where the process of unrolling iterative algorithms for hyperparameter optimization can lead to divergence from true Jacobians, necessitating a non-asymptotic analysis of its causes.

A database object used to retrieve and manipulate data one row at a time from a result set. Cursors are useful when row-by-row processing is needed but can be less efficient than set-based operations.

A framework for optimizing layer-wise capacity in large language models by applying the Minimum Description Length principle, enabling more efficient allocation and pruning decisions based on layer contribution.
A Web Components specification that allows developers to define new HTML elements with custom behavior and styling. Custom elements extend the browser's built-in element set with reusable components.

An extension of the Kubernetes API that allows users to define their own resource types. Custom resources, combined with custom controllers, enable operators to manage complex applications declaratively.
The overall perception a customer has of a brand based on all interactions throughout the customer journey. CX encompasses every touchpoint from awareness to purchase to support.

The complete path a customer takes when interacting with a product or service, from initial awareness through purchase and beyond. Mapping customer journeys helps identify opportunities for improvement.
A visual representation of every experience a customer has with a brand across all touchpoints. It helps organizations understand customer needs and identify opportunities for improvement.
The business methodology of ensuring customers achieve their desired outcomes while using a product or service. Customer success teams proactively guide customers to maximize value and reduce churn.
The range of services provided to help customers use a product or service effectively. Modern customer support combines human agents, self-service portals, chatbots, and knowledge bases.
A conceptual framework for describing and manipulating multi-dimensional tensor data on GPUs, grounded in categorical algebra, which includes operations like composition and logical product.
A mathematical specification for representing and manipulating tensor layouts in high-performance computing, introducing hierarchical layout representations for optimized execution.
CVA6-CFI is a design and integration of RISC-V extensions for Control-Flow Integrity, utilizing shadow stacks and landing pad primitives to protect against control-flow hijacking attacks.

Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures — a standardized system for identifying and cataloging publicly known cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Each CVE entry has a unique ID used for tracking and referencing.

CXLAimPod is a framework that leverages Compute Express Link (CXL) memory architecture to enhance performance for data-intensive applications by utilizing full-duplex channels for mixed read-write access patterns.
CXR-LT 2026 is a benchmark dataset for multi-label chest X-ray classification, containing approximately 143K images with 30 disease labels, aimed at addressing long-tailed class distributions.
Evidence-based knowledge about existing or emerging threats to an organization's assets. CTI helps security teams anticipate and prepare for potential cyber attacks.

The practice of protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks, unauthorized access, and damage. Cybersecurity encompasses everything from encryption and firewalls to security policies and incident response.
A structured set of guidelines and best practices for managing cybersecurity risk. Notable frameworks include NIST CSF, ISO 27001, and CIS Controls, which provide systematic approaches to security.
A method for constructing cyclic codes using a finite noncommutative matrix ring, specifically over the field F16, which enhances error correction capabilities in coding theory.
A spectral beamforming technique that exploits spectral correlations of cyclostationary noise to enhance speech signals in low signal-to-noise ratio scenarios.

An extension of traditional proof theory that allows for infinite proofs represented by finite graphs, facilitating the transformation of cyclic proofs into finite proofs while preserving their structure.
A method for mitigating judge bias in LLM-based evaluations by employing a round-robin assignment mechanism and variance decomposition to better understand and control evaluation variability.

A preprocessing technique that utilizes spectral correlations of cyclostationary noise to enhance speech signals in low-SNR scenarios, integrated with deep neural network-based denoising for improved performance.

D.definition: D-GVIO is a decentralized GNSS-Visual-Inertial Odometry framework that employs a buffer-driven strategy for efficient and robust state estimation in multi-agent systems, addressing challenges in cooperative localization.
A scalable many-core architecture specifically designed to accelerate matrix multiplication tasks in quantized large language models, utilizing adaptive-precision systolic array cores.
A differentiable real-to-sim-to-real engine that facilitates learning dexterous grasping by optimizing object mass identification from visual observations and control signals.

A problem formulation in temporal graphs that focuses on scheduling edge availability to optimize the worst-case temporal distance from a set of source nodes to all other vertices, applicable in logistics and multi-agent systems.
A structure for representing binary matrices that are organized based on twin-ordering, allowing for efficient queries and updates in logarithmic time.

A dual distillation framework for few-shot anomaly detection that enables the identification of anomalies in unseen tasks using a limited number of normal reference images, enhancing generalization in medical imaging contexts.
A JavaScript library for producing dynamic, interactive data visualizations in web browsers using SVG, HTML, and CSS. D3 binds data to the DOM and applies data-driven transformations.

D3PIA is a discrete diffusion-based model designed for generating piano accompaniments from lead sheets, utilizing Neighborhood Attention to enhance local contextual modeling in piano-roll representation.

The D4D Dataset is a paired dataset of endoscopic video and structured-light geometry for evaluating 3D reconstruction of non-rigid abdominal surgical scenes, designed to test algorithm robustness under various deformation conditions.
da Costian-Tarskianism is a novel approach for addressing ontological heterogeneity, utilizing consequence systems to extend traditional frameworks for reasoning about knowledge and inference.
Diffusion-Based Adaptive Contrast Transport, a zero-shot method for enhancing low-field MRI images by reconstructing high-field quality images from low-field data without paired training data.

Keyword-guided Target Speaker Extraction framework that identifies and isolates a target speaker's speech from mixtures by leveraging distinct keywords they utter, enhancing extraction capabilities in the absence of clean enrollment utterances.
A background process that runs continuously on a system without direct user interaction. Daemons handle tasks like scheduling, logging, network requests, and hardware device monitoring.
A library for verifying message-passing concurrent programs using Dafny, enabling formal proofs of properties such as deadlock freedom and functional equivalence without custom concurrency logics.
Directed Acyclic Graph — a finite directed graph with no directed cycles. DAGs are used in version control systems, task scheduling, data processing pipelines, and blockchain technology.
A dynamic action model-based framework that integrates vision-language reasoning with diffusion-based action models for improved robot manipulation in dynamic environments.

D.term: DANCE: Doubly Adaptive Neighborhood Conformal Estimation
D.definition: A conformal prediction method that adapts to the local structure of data, improving the efficiency of prediction sets for classification tasks by utilizing nearest-neighbor techniques.

Dandelion is a framework designed to enhance elasticity in cloud-native environments, enabling rapid resource allocation to match workload demands without the need for pre-provisioning idle resources.
A pointer that references a memory location that has been freed or deallocated. Accessing a dangling pointer leads to undefined behavior, crashes, or security vulnerabilities.
A technique for obtaining stronger relaxations in integer optimization problems, often used in conjunction with branch-and-bound methods to improve computational efficiency.

DAO-to-DAO metagovernance refers to the governance mechanisms where decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs) govern other DAOs, involving complex interactions of smart contracts and decision-making processes on the Ethereum blockchain.

Delay-Aware RIS Orchestrator (DARIO) is an O-RAN-compliant framework that dynamically assigns Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) to users based on their latency and reliability requirements, optimizing performance for 6G applications.

Information assets that organizations collect, process, and store during regular business operations but generally fail to use for any other purpose. Dark data represents untapped analytical potential.
A user interface design intentionally crafted to trick users into taking actions they did not intend, such as subscribing to services or sharing personal data. Dark patterns are considered unethical.

DARS is a dysarthria-aware rhythm-style synthesis framework that enhances automatic speech recognition by modeling the unique prosody and acoustic style of dysarthric speech through a multi-stage rhythm predictor.
A client-optimized programming language developed by Google for building mobile, desktop, server, and web applications. Dart is the primary language for the Flutter framework.
An independent worker in the Dart programming language that has its own memory heap and runs concurrently with other isolates. Isolates communicate through message passing without shared state.
An evolutionary GPT model that utilizes a genetic-algorithm-like optimization structure, allowing independent GPT agents to modify each other's training code to improve performance iteratively.
A visual display of the most important information needed to achieve one or more objectives, consolidated on a single screen. Dashboards provide at-a-glance views of key performance indicators.
DashengTokenizer is a continuous audio tokenizer that integrates acoustic information with frozen semantic features for improved performance in both understanding and generation tasks.

Data agents are a new paradigm that utilizes large language models and tool-using agents to automate data management tasks, with a proposed hierarchical taxonomy to clarify their capabilities and limitations.

The process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modeling data to discover useful information, draw conclusions, and support decision-making. It combines statistical methods with domain expertise.

The design of data systems including models, policies, rules, and standards that govern how data is collected, stored, managed, and used. Good data architecture ensures data quality and accessibility.
Data that is stored on a device or medium and is not actively being transferred or processed. Encrypting data at rest protects it from unauthorized access if the storage medium is compromised.
A technique used to increase the diversity of training data without collecting new data by applying transformations like rotation, flipping, and scaling to existing samples.
A technique that connects data sources to UI elements so that changes in the data are automatically reflected in the UI and vice versa. Data binding simplifies frontend development.
An incident where sensitive, protected, or confidential data is accessed, disclosed, or stolen by an unauthorized party. Data breaches can result in financial losses and reputational damage.
A centralized repository of metadata that helps organizations find, understand, and manage their data assets. Data catalogs improve data governance and enable self-service analytics.
A facility that houses computer systems and associated components such as telecommunications and storage systems. Modern data centers feature redundant power, cooling, and network connectivity.

A class primarily used to store data with minimal behavior, often with automatically generated methods like equality comparison, hashing, and string representation. Kotlin, Python, and Java support data classes.
The process of detecting and correcting corrupt, inaccurate, or irrelevant records from a dataset. Data cleaning is a critical step in data preparation that significantly impacts analysis quality.
The systematic process of gathering information from various sources for analysis. Data collection methods include surveys, web scraping, APIs, sensors, and database queries.
A formal agreement between data producers and consumers that defines the structure, format, semantics, and quality guarantees of shared data. Data contracts improve reliability in data pipelines.
A change in the statistical properties of production data compared to the training data over time. Data drift can degrade model performance and requires monitoring and retraining strategies.
A symmetric-key algorithm for the encryption of digital data that was widely used from the 1970s until being superseded by AES. DES uses a 56-bit key and processes 64-bit blocks.
The practice of designing, building, and maintaining systems that collect, store, and analyze data at scale. Data engineers create the infrastructure that enables data scientists to do their work.
An architecture approach that provides a unified data management framework across distributed environments. It simplifies data access and sharing while ensuring consistent governance and security.
A standardized structure for organizing and encoding data for storage or transmission. Common data formats include JSON, XML, CSV, Parquet, and Protocol Buffers.
The overall management of the availability, usability, integrity, and security of data used in an enterprise. Data governance includes policies, standards, and processes for managing data assets.
The process of combining data from different sources into a unified view. Data integration involves ETL pipelines, data mapping, and ensuring consistency across diverse data sources.
The maintenance of, and assurance of, data accuracy and consistency over its entire lifecycle. Data integrity ensures that information remains unaltered during storage, transfer, and retrieval.

A centralized repository that stores vast amounts of raw data in its native format until needed for analysis. Unlike data warehouses, data lakes accept structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
A data management architecture that combines the key benefits of data lakes and data warehouses. It provides the low-cost storage of a data lake with the management features of a data warehouse.

The stages data goes through from creation to archival or deletion, including collection, storage, processing, analysis, sharing, and disposal. Managing the data lifecycle ensures compliance and efficiency.
The process of tracking data from its origin through all transformations to its final destination. Data lineage helps organizations understand data flow, ensure compliance, and debug data issues.
The concept of moving computation close to where data resides rather than moving large amounts of data to where computation happens. Data locality is a key optimization in distributed computing.
A subset of a data warehouse focused on a specific business line, department, or subject area. Data marts provide faster query performance for targeted analytical needs.
The process of hiding original data with modified content while maintaining its format and usability. Data masking protects sensitive information in non-production environments.
A decentralized data architecture that organizes data by business domain with domain-oriented ownership. It treats data as a product and applies platform thinking to data infrastructure.
The process of discovering patterns, correlations, and anomalies in large datasets using statistical and computational techniques. Data mining extracts actionable knowledge from raw data.
An abstract representation of how data is organized, stored, and accessed within a system. Data models define entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints that structure information.
The process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity. Normalization involves dividing large tables into smaller tables and defining relationships between them.
A set of data processing elements connected in series where the output of one element is the input of the next. Data pipelines automate the flow of data from source systems to target destinations.
The part of a network that carries user traffic and forwards packets based on routing decisions made by the control plane. The data plane handles the actual movement of data.
The aspect of information technology that deals with the proper handling of personal data including consent, notice, and regulatory obligations. GDPR and CCPA are major data privacy regulations.
The collection, transformation, and organization of raw data into meaningful information. Data processing includes cleaning, filtering, aggregating, and enriching data for analysis or storage.
The process of examining data available in a source and collecting statistics and information about that data. Profiling helps assess data quality and understand data structure before analysis.

Practices and technologies for safeguarding data from unauthorized access, corruption, or loss throughout its lifecycle. Data protection encompasses encryption, access controls, backups, and compliance with privacy regulations.

The measure of data's fitness for its intended use, assessed across dimensions like accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity. High data quality is essential for reliable analysis and decision-making.

D.term: Data Quality Framework for EU Compliance
D.definition: A practical framework that aligns established data quality dimensions with specific EU regulatory requirements, aiding practitioners in ensuring compliance in machine learning systems.

This framework provides a structured approach for transparent reporting of data quality assessments throughout the clinical electronic health record data lifecycle, enhancing trust in clinical AI models.
A condition where two or more threads access shared data simultaneously and at least one modifies it, leading to unpredictable results. Data races are a common source of concurrency bugs.

The methods and formats used to encode information in computing systems, including binary, hexadecimal, floating-point, and character encodings. Data representation determines how computers store and process information.
Legal or regulatory requirements that data about a country's citizens or residents must be collected, processed, and stored within that country's borders.

An interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from structured and unstructured data. It combines statistics, machine learning, and domain expertise.

The practice of protecting digital data from unauthorized access, corruption, or theft throughout its lifecycle. Data security encompasses encryption, access controls, monitoring, and secure disposal.
The process of converting data structures or object states into a format that can be stored or transmitted. Common serialization formats include JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers, and Avro.
A horizontal scaling strategy that distributes data across multiple database instances based on a shard key. Sharding improves performance and scalability for large-scale databases.
The concept that data is subject to the laws and governance structures of the country in which it is collected or stored. It affects cloud architecture decisions for multinational organizations.
A role responsible for managing and overseeing an organization's data assets to ensure data quality, integrity, and compliance. Data stewards bridge the gap between technical and business teams.
The recording of information in a storage medium, including hard drives, SSDs, cloud storage, and distributed file systems. Data storage technologies vary in capacity, speed, cost, and durability.

A specialized format for organizing, processing, and storing data in a computer. Common data structures include arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, and stacks — each optimized for different operations and use cases.
A data lake that has become unmanageable due to lack of data quality, governance, or metadata. Data swamps make it difficult to find and trust data for analysis.
A classification that specifies which type of value a variable can hold, such as integer, string, boolean, or float. Data types determine the operations that can be performed on data.

The graphical representation of data and information using charts, graphs, maps, and dashboards. Effective visualization makes complex data patterns, trends, and outliers easily understandable for both technical and non-technical audiences.
A centralized repository that stores structured data from multiple sources for reporting and analysis. Data warehouses use ETL processes to transform raw data into a format optimized for querying.
The process of transforming and mapping raw data from one format into another to make it more appropriate for downstream analysis. Also known as data munging.
A technique that utilizes deep neural networks to improve symbol detection in multiuser MIMO systems, addressing challenges like inter-user interference and channel state information uncertainty.

A framework for discovering probabilistic laws underlying the Feynman-Kac formula using a stochastic SINDy method, allowing for the recovery of backward stochastic differential equations from stock and option trajectories without ergodicity assumptions.
A method for optimizing GPU resource allocation in distributed systems by computing adapter placements that minimize GPU usage while preventing request starvation and memory errors.

D.term: Data-driven Model-free Physical-informed Deep Operator Network (DeepOnet)
D.definition: A framework that learns nonlinear dynamic systems from limited experimental data without relying on extensive mathematical models, enhancing the applicability of deep operator networks.

An organized collection of structured data stored electronically, designed for efficient retrieval, insertion, and management. Databases range from simple relational systems (SQL) to distributed NoSQL solutions handling millions of operations per second.
A rule enforced on data columns in a database table to ensure data integrity. Constraints include primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, not-null constraints, and check constraints.
The process of defining the structure, relationships, and constraints of a database to efficiently store and retrieve data. It involves normalization, indexing strategies, and schema design.
The process of moving data and schema changes between database versions or systems. Migration tools track and apply incremental changes to database schemas in a controlled, repeatable manner.

The measures and technologies used to protect databases from unauthorized access, data breaches, and malicious attacks. It includes access controls, encryption, auditing, and vulnerability management.
A Python decorator that automatically generates special methods such as __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ for classes that primarily store data. Dataclasses reduce boilerplate in Python code.

A programming paradigm where the execution of operations is determined by data availability rather than explicit control flow. Dataflow architectures are used in parallel processing and stream computing.
A programming paradigm where the program is modeled as a directed graph of data flowing between operations. Execution is driven by data availability rather than explicit control flow.
A two-dimensional, tabular data structure with labeled rows and columns, commonly used in data analysis. DataFrames in libraries like pandas and Spark provide powerful data manipulation capabilities.

DataJoint 2.0 is a computational substrate for scientific workflows that employs a relational workflow model, allowing for structured data management and execution order through a unified schema for data integrity and provenance.

A structured collection of data organized for analysis or machine learning. Datasets can be tabular, image-based, text-based, or multimodal, and serve as the foundation for training and evaluating models.
An autonomous vehicle operating system designed to manage real-time driving and data-centric applications, facilitating the transition of vehicles into mobile computing platforms.
A capability within the DataBase Operating System (DBOS) that integrates web services and network sensing to enhance resilience and security through collaborative awareness of network environments.
A harness for the DataFusion engine that exposes physical plans through a compact serialized representation, allowing large language models to propose localized edits for query optimization.
A command-line tool that enables data analysts and engineers to transform data in their warehouse using SQL. dbt handles dependency management, testing, and documentation of data transformations.

DBTuneSuite is an experimental suite for testing the time performance of multi-layer tuning options on various database management systems, providing insights into optimal system configurations for different query types.
A unified framework for dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction that addresses robustness challenges in multimodal fusion by removing noise and reconstructing missing modalities.

A gradient-free framework that identifies minimal sufficient explanations in vision models by isolating the smallest subset of representational units whose joint activation preserves predictions, enhancing interpretability.

Distributed Denial of Service — a cyberattack that overwhelms a target server or network with a flood of traffic from multiple sources. DDoS attacks aim to make services unavailable to legitimate users and can be mitigated with CDNs and traffic filtering.
A technique used in 6G networks to compensate for packet delay variation by implementing virtual timeslots, ensuring reliable and time-sensitive communication.

Differential Evolution with Multi-Mutation Operator-Guided Communication, a multi-mutation optimization algorithm that enhances the performance and convergence of standard differential evolution in uncertain environments.
An interpretable algorithm designed to detect and break down Simpson's Paradox in observational datasets, aiding in the understanding of misleading empirical associations.
Source code that is executed but whose result is never used, or code that can never be reached during execution. Removing dead code improves maintainability, readability, and sometimes performance.
A queue that stores messages that cannot be processed successfully after a specified number of attempts. DLQs help debug message processing failures and prevent data loss.
Generative AI systems that simulate interactions with deceased individuals, allowing users to actively construct and reshape memories through selective input and ongoing adjustments.
A situation in concurrent programming where two or more processes are unable to proceed because each is waiting for the other to release a resource. Deadlock prevention is a critical concern.

The process of identifying circular wait conditions in concurrent systems where two or more processes are each waiting for resources held by the others. Detection algorithms use resource allocation graphs.
An approach in multi-party computation that utilizes a passive, non-colluding dealer to enhance the efficiency of the preprocessing phase in secure comparison protocols.
A software-defined testbed developed to measure and evaluate the resilience of Wi-Fi networks against deauthentication attacks under various security configurations.
A measure of the minimum number of bits a verifier must inspect to correctly decide a debate, revealing that PSPACE/poly can be efficiently decided with O(log n) queries.
An open-source distributed platform for change data capture that streams changes from databases into Apache Kafka. It enables real-time data pipelines from traditional databases.

A free and open-source Linux distribution known for its stability, extensive package repository, and strong commitment to free software principles. Debian serves as the foundation for Ubuntu and many other distributions.

A programming technique that limits how often a function is called by delaying its execution until a specified time has elapsed since the last invocation. Debouncing is commonly used for search inputs and resize events.
A programming technique that ensures a function is only executed after a specified time period has elapsed since its last invocation. It is commonly used for handling rapid user input events.

A software tool that helps developers find and fix bugs by allowing them to pause execution, inspect variables, step through code line by line, and set breakpoints. Common debuggers include GDB, Chrome DevTools, and IDE-integrated tools.

The process of identifying, analyzing, and removing errors (bugs) from software. Debugging tools include breakpoints, stack traces, logging, and specialized debuggers that let developers step through code execution line by line.

DeCEAT is a framework designed to evaluate the environmental and performance trade-offs of small language models (SLMs) during automated software testing, utilizing the HumanEval benchmark and adaptive prompt variants.

D.term: Decentralised Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP)
D.definition: A framework for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses challenges of partial observability and decentralized coordination among agents.

The distribution of authority, control, and data away from a single central entity. Decentralized systems like blockchain networks improve resilience, censorship resistance, and reduce single points of failure.
An application that runs on a decentralized network rather than a single computer, typically using blockchain or peer-to-peer technology. DApps operate without a central point of control.
A unified and standardized evaluation framework for large language models that operates in a decentralized manner, reducing risks of benchmark leakage and ensuring consistency in results.
An approach for selecting optimal equilibria in decentralized stochastic games under communication constraints, focusing on social welfare objectives while allowing for heterogeneous agent responses.
A set of gossip algorithms designed for decentralized consensus ranking, extending traditional ranking aggregation methods to environments where preference data is distributed across multiple agents.

A tree-like model of decisions and their possible consequences used for classification and regression tasks. Decision trees are interpretable and form the basis for ensemble methods like Random Forest.
A framework for evaluating AI weather prediction models that prioritizes local stakeholders' needs and decision-making processes over traditional aggregated meteorological metrics.

This framework formalizes steganography in Large Language Models (LLMs) using a decision-theoretic approach, enabling the detection and quantification of steganographic behaviors without relying on a known reference distribution.
An infrastructure that logs, replays, and audits decision-valued maps, which record the relationships between different data representations and their discrete outcomes.
A system that separates the specification of LLM agent workflows from their implementation, allowing for flexible execution across multiple programming languages and deployment environments.
A programming paradigm that expresses the logic of a computation without describing its control flow. SQL, HTML, and CSS are examples of declarative languages.

DeCo is a core calculus for incremental functional programming that facilitates the efficient reuse of previous results to speed up computations, while supporting a wide range of user-defined data types.
An architecture for automatic speech recognition that processes both speech and text in a single stack, utilizing a modality-aware sparse mixture of experts for efficient routing.

A tool that translates compiled machine code or bytecode back into a higher-level programming language. Decompilers are used in reverse engineering, security analysis, and understanding legacy software.

The Deconditional Gaussian Process framework enhances causal inference by providing reliable epistemic uncertainty quantification, recovering popular kernel estimators as the posterior mean while ensuring well-calibrated uncertainty.
A structural design pattern that allows adding new behaviors to objects dynamically by placing them inside special wrapper objects. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing.

D.term: Decorrelation Methods for Acoustic Feedback Cancellation
D.definition: A system enhancement that incorporates multiple decorrelation techniques into an acoustic feedback cancellation framework, improving performance through a combination of variable time delays, prediction, and distortion compensation.
An architectural paradigm that separates the refinement of implicit neural representations from their initial encoding, enhancing fidelity while maintaining computational efficiency.

D.term: Deep Balanced Multimodal Iterative Fusion Framework (DBMIF)
D.definition: An architecture designed for speech enhancement that integrates multiple modalities, specifically air and bone conduction, to improve performance in low signal-to-noise ratio environments.
A copy of an object that creates a new instance with copies of all nested objects, rather than references to the same nested objects. Deep copies are independent of the original.

Deep Data Research (DDR) is an open-ended task framework that enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to autonomously extract key insights from databases, emphasizing investigatory intelligence over executional intelligence.
A method that enhances handle-based mesh deformation by utilizing data-driven approaches to achieve semantically meaningful shape edits without requiring prior knowledge of handle placement.
An extension of the Black-Scholes-Merton model that employs a dual-network architecture within a deep Forward-Backward Stochastic Differential Equation framework for option pricing.
A mathematical framework for solving high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) using neural networks, focusing on the convergence of generalization error.
A biologically inspired mechanism that enhances Deep Q-Learning by dynamically scaling Q-updates based on latent-space surprise, improving convergence in sparse-reward environments.

Deep LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis) is a scale-invariant objective function designed to minimize intraclass variance and maximize interclass distance in deep learning architectures. It introduces a theoretical framework for understanding implicit regularization in discriminative metric-learning tasks.

A subset of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple layers to learn complex patterns from large amounts of data. Deep learning powers breakthroughs in image recognition, natural language processing, and generative AI.
A technique that enables supervised deep learning models to generalize across different but related domains, particularly in cancer histopathology, by addressing domain shift directly.
An optimization framework designed to minimize intraclass variance and maximize interclass distance, providing insights into implicit regularization effects in deep learning architectures.
A low degree of freedom deep neural network model designed to minimize Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) in loan portfolios by predicting default probabilities and timing.
A framework for approximating a broad class of probabilistic models using deep learning, establishing a universal approximation theorem under specific conditions.
A subfield of machine learning that combines deep neural networks with reinforcement learning. It has achieved superhuman performance in games and is applied to robotics and autonomous systems.

D.term: Deep Reinforcement Learning Based Block Coordinate Descent (DRL-based BCD)
D.definition: DRL-based BCD is an algorithm that integrates deep reinforcement learning with block coordinate descent methods to solve nonconvex weighted sum-rate maximization problems in AI-native wireless networks.

D.term: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Beam Management
D.definition: A method utilizing deep reinforcement learning to optimize user throughput and reduce latency in multi-panel mmWave radio access networks through adaptive beam management strategies.

D.term: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Order Execution
D.definition: An innovative application of Deep Reinforcement Learning to optimize order execution in financial markets, balancing risk and returns in trade execution.

A method that employs deep reinforcement learning to dynamically adjust Modulation and Coding Schemes (MCS) in cellular networks, enhancing throughput by learning from real-time environmental interactions.
A technique for optimizing energy consumption in Satellite IoT systems by determining optimal durations for deep-sleep states of devices, thereby extending battery life.
AI-generated synthetic media where a person's likeness is convincingly replaced with someone else's using deep learning techniques. Deepfakes raise concerns about misinformation, fraud, and privacy.
A method for detecting synthetic words in speech by fine-tuning a pre-trained Whisper model using next-token prediction, demonstrating effectiveness with partially vocoded utterances.
A scalable federated learning framework that accelerates the training of mixture-of-experts models by distilling knowledge from heterogeneous edge devices without requiring local model hosting.
A benchmark suite for evaluating reinforcement learning algorithms, providing a variety of continuous control tasks that challenge agents to learn effective policies.
A dual-view framework for molecular language modeling that integrates high-resolution molecular images with geometric invariants, enhancing the interpretation of molecular structures.
An LLM-powered agentic system that automates data preparation by transforming raw tables into analysis-ready data, allowing for iterative decision-making.

DeepRAHT is an end-to-end framework for lossy point cloud attribute compression that integrates the Regional Adaptive Hierarchical Transform (RAHT) within a learning reconstruction process, eliminating the need for manual preprocessing.
A content generation tool that utilizes large language models to produce responses in online discussions, particularly within the context of everyday digital feminism on social media.
A distributed training framework that enhances the scalability and performance of Vision Transformers by optimizing intra- and inter-node training efficiency across multiple GPU configurations.
DeepSVM is a physics-informed Deep Operator Network that learns the solution operator of stochastic volatility models without requiring labeled training data, focusing on real-time calibration.
A benchmark designed to evaluate large language model agents on complex tasks that require synthesizing information from multiple sources and structured reasoning.

An online generative modeling technique that produces outcomes indistinguishable based on observed data and predefined computational tests, enhancing the robustness of generative models against falsification.

A phenomenon observed in safety-tuned large language models where they refuse to assist in legitimate defensive cybersecurity tasks due to the similarity in language to offensive tasks, potentially hindering cybersecurity efforts.
The Deferred Local Ratio Technique is a new method introduced for solving the Tree Augmentation Problem, which focuses on achieving a minimum set of links to ensure 2-edge connectivity in a tree.

Decentralized Finance — financial services built on blockchain technology that operate without traditional intermediaries like banks. DeFi applications include lending, borrowing, trading, and insurance.
A framework that integrates blockchain technology and Explainable AI to enhance trustworthiness in decentralized financial decisions, particularly for small business loan underwriting.

DeGAS is a differentiable Gaussian approximate semantics for loopless probabilistic programs, allowing for sample-free, gradient-based optimization by evaluating programs under Gaussian-mixture semantics.

DAM is a technique for secure integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) that uses controlled per-path symbol delays to enhance signal power for intended users while creating misalignment for potential eavesdroppers.

A scheduling model that minimizes total flow time for jobs on a single machine without prior knowledge of job processing times. This model contrasts with clairvoyant scheduling, which assumes complete knowledge of job durations upon arrival.
An object-oriented design pattern where an object handles a request by delegating to a helper object rather than performing the task itself. Delegation promotes composition over inheritance.
An efficient log compression framework that utilizes pattern signature synthesis to improve log parsing accuracy and compression ratios, particularly for complex production logs.
