A label-free framework for quality assessment of evolving AI-generated images that constructs positive and negative pairs for evaluating visual quality and prompt-image alignment without human annotations.
A functional, concurrent programming language built on the Erlang VM designed for building scalable and maintainable applications. Elixir combines Erlang's reliability with modern language features.

A collection of three open-source tools — Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana — used together for searching, analyzing, and visualizing log data in real time. It is widely used for centralized logging and observability.
A mathematical curve defined by an equation of the form y squared equals x cubed plus ax plus b, used in cryptography to create smaller, faster, and more efficient cryptographic keys.
A public-key cryptography approach based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. ECC offers the same security as RSA with smaller key sizes, making it more efficient.

ELLMPEG is an edge-based processing tool for large language models that addresses the challenges of cloud deployment by enabling local processing, thereby reducing computational demands and enhancing privacy.
A functional programming language that compiles to JavaScript, designed for building reliable web applications. Elm is known for having no runtime exceptions and a friendly compiler.

A machine learning-driven framework for automated RF physical synthesis that converts circuit netlists into manufacturable GDSII layouts, addressing limitations in component modeling and routing capabilities.

Electronic mail — a method of exchanging digital messages between people using email clients and servers via protocols such as SMTP, IMAP, and POP3. Email remains a fundamental communication tool in business and personal use.
A set of techniques including SPF, DKIM, and DMARC that verify the identity of email senders and prevent email spoofing, phishing, and spam by authenticating the origin of messages.
A server or service that filters inbound and outbound email traffic for spam, malware, phishing attempts, and data loss. Email gateways are a critical layer of organizational cybersecurity.
The integration of analytical capabilities directly into business applications, dashboards, and workflows rather than requiring users to switch to separate analytics tools.
A computer system designed to perform a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electrical system. Embedded systems are found in everything from washing machines to aircraft controls.

Computer systems with dedicated functions within larger mechanical or electrical systems. They combine hardware and software designed for specific tasks, found in devices from microwaves to automotive control systems.
A learned representation that maps discrete objects like words, sentences, or images to continuous vectors in a lower-dimensional space. Embeddings capture semantic relationships and similarities.

A neural network layer that maps discrete categorical tokens — such as words, user IDs, or product codes — into dense continuous vector representations in a lower-dimensional space, capturing semantic relationships.
A novel approach to cross-robot policy learning that incorporates morphological information into transformer-based policies, enhancing robustness and performance across different robotic embodiments.

Dense vector representations of data such as words, sentences, or images in a continuous vector space. Embeddings capture semantic relationships and are foundational to modern NLP and recommendation systems.
A benchmark framework for analyzing embodied cooperation among large language model agents in dynamic environments, facilitating the study of collaborative task success.
A discipline-agnostic calculus that treats theories as fixed points of idempotent operators, integrating order-theoretic closure operators with dynamics to analyze composable processes.
Complex system behavior that arises from the interaction of simpler components, not explicitly programmed. In large language models, emergent abilities appear at certain scale thresholds.
A method that combines emission-aware Gaussian representations with path tracing to achieve physically based light transport in indoor scene reconstruction and editing.
Small digital images or icons used to express ideas, emotions, or concepts in electronic communication. Emoji are encoded in Unicode and supported across all major platforms and operating systems.
A unified framework for Omni-Modal Large Language Models that enhances emotional understanding and expression, addressing limitations in existing models' emotional responses.

A unified framework designed to enhance emotional understanding and expression in Omni-Modal Large Language Models (Omni-LLMs), addressing issues of contextually mismatched emotional responses in complex scenarios.
A branch of artificial intelligence that enables machines to detect, interpret, and simulate human emotions through analysis of facial expressions, voice tone, text, and physiological signals.
A hyperbolic hypergraph framework for multimodal emotion and sentiment modeling that utilizes Poincare-ball embeddings and contrastive learning in hyperbolic space to enhance class separation.
A technique for enhancing expressive speech synthesis by merging audios of emotionally congruent text, allowing for the generation of more natural and engaging speech outputs.
A design approach that considers the emotional responses of users and crafts experiences that evoke specific feelings such as delight, trust, or engagement through aesthetics and interaction.

A concept examining how the emotional tone expressed by users during interactions with AI, such as ChatGPT, influences the quality of AI responses, demonstrating that positive and negative emotions can enhance AI performance.

A neuro-symbolic dashboard designed to facilitate sustainable and dynamic knowledge exploration, synthesis, and reuse in software and requirements engineering, addressing challenges in literature reviews and data sharing.
A technique used to analyze time series data by decomposing it into intrinsic mode functions, which can then be transformed into graph representations for advanced modeling.

A framework for conditional independence tests that addresses inaccuracies in small samples and model misspecification, providing more reliable frequentist guarantees for causal discovery and feature selection.

EMS-FL is a federated learning approach that employs a mixture-of-experts (MoE) model to optimize performance in satellite-terrestrial networks, addressing challenges of data shortage and computational constraints in edge devices.
A compiler toolchain that compiles C and C++ code into WebAssembly, enabling high-performance applications to run in web browsers without native plugins.
A system that automatically generates music synchronized to the emotional and rhythmic content of videos, aiding content creators in enhancing their productions without manual composition.

Software or hardware that enables one system to behave like another, allowing programs designed for one platform to run on a different one. Emulators are essential for mobile app development, retro gaming, and cross-platform testing.

An object-oriented programming principle that bundles data and the methods that operate on it within a single unit (class), restricting direct access to internal state. Encapsulation protects data integrity and reduces coupling between components.
The process of converting data from one format to another for standardized communication or storage. Common encodings include UTF-8 for text, Base64 for binary, and URL encoding for web.
A model addressing the dynamics of information exchange among robots that can only share information upon meeting, focusing on the design of storage and routing algorithms for such interactions.

The process of converting readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using mathematical algorithms and keys. Encryption protects data in transit and at rest, with symmetric and asymmetric methods serving different security needs.
A problem concerning the computation of a finite set of solutions for first-order autonomous ordinary differential equations, with a complete validated algorithm presented for its resolution.

A framework that integrates non-sequential Monte Carlo polarization ray tracing with a differentiable transfer-matrix solver for optimizing geometric waveguide displays in augmented reality applications.
A communication system where only the communicating users can read the messages. No eavesdropper, including the service provider, can access the cryptographic keys needed to decrypt the conversation.

A framework for downlink non-orthogonal multiple access that integrates interference-aware and channel-adaptive super-constellations within an autoencoder architecture, directly embedding wireless channel characteristics into training and inference.
A testing methodology that verifies the complete flow of an application from start to finish. E2E tests simulate real user scenarios to ensure all integrated components work together correctly.

A computational technique that accelerates dynamic programming by inverting the Euler equation, specifically adapted for models with Epstein-Zin preferences. This method achieves significant speed and accuracy improvements over traditional value function iteration.

A specific URL or URI where an API can be accessed to perform operations on resources. Each endpoint represents a distinct function, such as retrieving user data or submitting a form, and responds to specific HTTP methods.
A cybersecurity technology that continuously monitors endpoint devices for suspicious activity and provides tools for investigation, containment, and remediation of threats.
A modeling technique for streaming real-time trajectory prediction that considers the influence of neighboring traffic agents on autonomous vehicle path planning and decision-making.
A method for optimizing data delivery in optical LEO satellite downlinks, balancing delay-sensitive and delay-tolerant service requirements to enhance communication efficiency.
A novel hydrogen electrolyzer design that integrates energy storage to provide grid-forming services, allowing for autonomous real and reactive power support in off-grid renewable systems.
A lightweight, programmable approach to manage CPU resources in softwarized Radio Access Networks (RANs), focusing on energy efficiency while meeting real-time constraints.

Energy-aware Design of Metaheuristics integrates machine learning surrogates into optimization algorithms to address real-world challenges while considering energy consumption, aiming to improve both performance and efficiency.
A framework for continual learning in spiking neural networks that optimizes both accuracy and energy efficiency by integrating experience replay and learnable parameters.
A class of numerical integrators for elastodynamic contact problems that balances energy conservation and stability, addressing limitations of traditional methods in dynamic simulations.
An FPGA architecture for stochastic simulated quantum annealing that utilizes probabilistic bits (p-bits) to optimize energy efficiency while supporting fully connected graphs.
An end-to-end framework for assessing the reliability of deep neural networks on systolic array architectures, designed to facilitate accurate transient fault analysis.

A measure of how actively users interact with a product, service, or content. Engagement metrics include time spent, clicks, shares, comments, and return visits, and are key indicators of product health.

Engineered simultaneity refers to a system design that necessitates the temporal comparison of events occurring at spatially separated locations, exemplified by the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) in U.S. equity markets.
A protocol for data dissemination in multi-hop wireless IoT networks that improves goodput by utilizing a distributed delay timer mechanism based on link quality.

The Enhanced Legendre Memory Unit is an advanced architecture for sequence modeling that provides stable performance with fewer recurrent parameters compared to traditional LSTMs, making it suitable for decoding short nonstationary signals.
An improved version of the projection pursuit tree classifier that allows for more flexible splits and class groupings, enhancing performance in complex multi-class classification problems.

A novel wireless architecture that employs intelligent metasurfaces to facilitate surface-wave propagation, significantly enhancing control and reducing attenuation compared to traditional space-wave transmission methods.

A framework that captures the wake-word segment during human-machine interaction to automatically serve as an enrollment reference for target speech extraction, facilitating seamless user experiences.

A theoretical framework for RNA design that bounds the folding probabilities of RNA structures using ensemble-based criteria, allowing for a more interpretable approach to RNA secondary structure design.

A method that combines multiple forecasting models to improve the accuracy of predictions for power quality parameters in transmission systems, particularly useful in managing data heterogeneity and seasonality.
A machine learning technique that combines multiple models to produce a single optimal predictive model. Ensemble methods like bagging, boosting, and stacking often outperform individual models.
A method in large-scale reinforcement learning that employs multiple policies to enhance exploration but requires careful management of inter-policy diversity to maintain learning efficiency.
A stock trading strategy that combines multiple deep reinforcement learning algorithms, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Advantage Actor Critic (A2C), to maximize investment returns.
A framework that synthesizes ensemble-based inference using the conditional Gaussian law, providing a finite-dimensional representation for Gaussian processes under conditioning.

Entanglement Learning is an information-theoretic framework that enhances Model Predictive Control (MPC) adaptability by utilizing an Information Digital Twin to monitor and optimize the information flow in real-time.
A method for constructing entanglement-assisted codes from arbitrary quantum codes, enabling efficient communication over erasure channels by utilizing correctable subsets of physical qubits.
A method that establishes a relationship between entanglement entropy and approximation errors in Hamiltonian simulation, improving resource estimation for quantum simulations.

Large-scale business software systems designed to support complex organizational operations. Enterprise software typically handles processes like resource planning, customer management, and supply chain coordination.
A discipline for proactively and holistically leading enterprise responses to disruptive forces by identifying and analyzing the execution of change toward desired business vision and outcomes.
A distinct object or concept in a domain that has a unique identity and lifecycle. In domain-driven design, entities are distinguished from value objects by their identity.
A visual representation of the relationships between entities in a database. ER diagrams are used in database design to model the logical structure of data.
The process of determining whether two or more records in a dataset refer to the same real-world entity. It is essential for data integration, deduplication, and master data management.
A measure of randomness or unpredictability in data, especially important in cryptography for generating secure keys and random numbers. Higher entropy means greater security.

A reservoir of environmental randomness collected by the operating system from hardware events like disk timings, mouse movements, and interrupt jitter, used to seed cryptographically secure random number generators.
This technique assesses understanding in heterogeneous LLM-based multi-agent systems by measuring entropy, facilitating guided collaboration among agents with varying capabilities.

A technique for neural audio codecs that incorporates an entropy-guided strategy to optimize the grouping of acoustic features, aiming to achieve high-quality speech reconstruction under ultra-low bitrate conditions.

Entropy-Guided Group Residual Vector Quantization (EG-GRVQ) is a technique for ultra-low bitrate neural speech codecs that enhances semantic modeling while ensuring high-fidelity audio reconstruction.

This framework applies entropy-regularization to American options pricing, utilizing a probabilistic approach based on the Doob-Meyer-Mertens decomposition and reflected backward stochastic differential equations to achieve monotone approximations of value functions.
A condition for the convergence of differential entropy under measure convergence of probability density functions, characterized by the uniform integrability of entropy integrands.
An enumeration type that defines a set of named constants representing a fixed group of possible values. Enums improve code readability and type safety by replacing magic numbers.
A configured system where software is deployed and executed, such as development, staging, and production environments. Each environment typically has different configurations and access controls.

A dynamic value stored outside of application code that configures behavior across different environments. Environment variables typically hold sensitive data like API keys, database URLs, and feature flags, keeping secrets out of source code.
A framework that optimizes generalized pinching-antenna systems by considering user mobility and localization errors, enhancing network performance through traffic-aware and geometry-aware metrics.

A temporary container injected into a running Kubernetes Pod for interactive debugging, enabling developers to inspect processes, filesystems, and network state without modifying the Pod's original spec or restarting it.
Large language models that autonomously pursue knowledge goals and shape information environments, raising concerns about reliability and alignment with epistemic norms.
A decision-making framework that estimates issue-specific signal reliability and assigns bounded voting weights, enhancing collective decision-making beyond traditional majority rule.
A method for breaking down mutual information into per-class contributions, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of a model's uncertainty in safety-critical classification tasks.
One complete pass through the entire training dataset during the training of a machine learning model. Multiple epochs are typically needed for the model to learn patterns in the data.
The number of seconds that have elapsed since January 1, 1970 (Unix epoch), used as a standard way to represent timestamps in computing. Also known as Unix time or POSIX time.

A benchmark dataset specifically designed for event stream-based visual place recognition, comprising 10K event sequences and 65K event frames to address challenges in low illumination and high-speed motion.

An exploration-exploitation balancing method in reinforcement learning that selects the action with the highest estimated reward with probability 1-epsilon and a random action with probability epsilon.
A software-level memory tiering solution designed for hyperscaler workloads using Compute Express Link (CXL), addressing multi-tenancy support and performance variability in memory management.
A physics-based training method for neural networks that leverages equilibrium states to optimize network performance, particularly in realistic architectures with connectivity constraints.

Equilibrium Propagation (EP) is a physics-inspired learning algorithm that extends traditional EP to non-conservative systems, enabling inference and learning in systems with non-reciprocal interactions.
A class of machine learning models that incorporate equivariant inductive bias to achieve high accuracy across diverse material systems, while addressing computational efficiency.

Equivariant machine learning interaction potentials (MLIPs) are models that incorporate equivariant inductive biases to achieve high accuracy in chemical and computational materials science benchmarks while addressing computational cost.

A data protection method that breaks data into fragments, expands and encodes them with redundant pieces, and distributes them across storage nodes. Unlike replication, it achieves fault tolerance with significantly less storage overhead.
A functional programming language designed for building massively scalable, fault-tolerant, distributed systems. Erlang is widely used in telecommunications, messaging systems, and databases.

Enterprise Resource Planning — integrated software systems that manage and automate core business processes including finance, HR, manufacturing, supply chain, and procurement. Major ERP vendors include SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics.
The maximum amount of time a service can be unavailable within a given period, derived from service level objectives. Error budgets balance reliability engineering with feature development velocity.

Techniques used to detect and correct errors in data transmission or storage. Error-correcting codes like Hamming codes and Reed-Solomon codes add redundancy to data to enable automatic recovery from bit errors.
A technique used in data storage and transmission that detects and corrects errors introduced during transfer or storage. ECC memory can detect and fix single-bit errors automatically.
The process of responding to and recovering from error conditions in a program. Proper error handling includes try-catch blocks, error codes, and graceful degradation strategies.

A technique for generating user diagnostics during the verification of probabilistic programs, focusing on slicing for error reporting, proof simplification, and preserving successful verification results.
An enhancement to active automata learning algorithms that leverages knowledge of observable errors to improve the efficiency and scalability of learning behavioral models from system interactions.
A technique introduced to enhance rendering quality in existing 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes by compensating for errors in the rendering process.
The official standard module system for JavaScript, using import and export statements to share code between files. ES Modules support static analysis, tree shaking, and asynchronous loading.

ECMAScript 2015 — a major update to the JavaScript language specification that introduced arrow functions, classes, template literals, destructuring, promises, and modules. It modernized JavaScript for large-scale development.
A compiler optimization technique that determines whether an object allocated inside a function can be accessed outside of it. Objects that do not escape can be allocated on the stack.
A static code analysis tool for identifying problematic patterns in JavaScript and TypeScript code. ESLint helps enforce coding conventions and catch potential bugs before runtime.
A programming language designed as a proof of concept, joke, or art form rather than for practical use. Examples include Brainfuck, Whitespace, and LOLCODE.
A family of low-cost microcontroller chips with integrated Wi-Fi and Bluetooth developed by Espressif Systems. ESP32 and ESP8266 are widely used in IoT projects and home automation.
A low-cost, low-power system-on-a-chip microcontroller with integrated Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, widely used in IoT projects. It supports multiple development frameworks including Arduino and MicroPython.
A novel framework for designing robust algorithms in insertion-deletion streams, combining multiple linear sketches to achieve sublinear space complexity while maintaining robustness.

An HTTP response header containing a validator token that uniquely identifies a specific version of a resource, enabling conditional requests for cache validation and optimistic concurrency control in REST APIs.
A decentralized, open-source blockchain platform that enables smart contracts and decentralized applications. Ethereum's native cryptocurrency Ether is the second-largest by market capitalization.

A family of wired networking technologies commonly used in local area networks. Ethernet defines wiring and signaling standards for the physical layer and data link layer, supporting speeds from 10 Mbps to 400 Gbps.

The practice of designing and deploying artificial intelligence systems that are fair, transparent, accountable, and aligned with human values. Ethical AI addresses bias, privacy, safety, and societal impact of AI systems.

Authorized security testing where professionals attempt to find vulnerabilities in systems using the same techniques as malicious hackers. Also called penetration testing, it helps organizations improve their security posture.

Extract, Transform, Load — a data integration process that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into a suitable format, and loads it into a destination database or data warehouse. ETL pipelines are foundational to business intelligence.
A workflow that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into a suitable format, and loads it into a target data store. ETL pipelines are fundamental to data warehousing.
A service discovery tool developed by Netflix that enables microservices to find and communicate with each other without hardcoding host names or port numbers.
A compact audio language model that achieves competitive performance against larger models across various audio understanding benchmarks, utilizing a unified end-to-end architecture.
A dataset comprising 81,000 embedding GeoTIFFs derived from three foundation models, designed to benchmark pooling strategies for geospatial data while preserving class-discriminative signals.

Eval4Sim is an evaluation framework designed for persona simulations in large language models, providing a more grounded assessment of human conversational behavior compared to traditional LLM-as-a-judge approaches.
A middleware component that receives events from publishers and routes them to interested subscribers. Event brokers decouple producers from consumers and enable asynchronous communication.
A communication pattern that allows different components to communicate with each other without being directly connected. Publishers emit events to the bus, and subscribers receive them.

An event-driven integration pattern where events carry all the data consumers need, eliminating the need for callbacks to the source system. It reduces coupling but increases event size and eventual consistency challenges.

An event-driven architecture pattern where multiple services collaborate by emitting and reacting to domain events without centralized orchestration, producing emergent system behavior from decoupled autonomous components.
A function or method that is called in response to a specific event such as a mouse click, key press, or network request completion. Event handlers are central to event-driven programming.

The mechanism by which a program responds to events such as user actions, sensor outputs, or messages from other programs. Event handlers are callback functions that execute when specific events occur.

A programming construct that continuously checks for and dispatches events or messages in a program. In JavaScript, the event loop manages the execution of callbacks, promises, and I/O operations, enabling non-blocking asynchronous behavior.
The process of reprocessing historical events stored in an event log to rebuild state, fix errors, or migrate systems. Event replay is a key benefit of event sourcing architectures.
A pattern where state changes are logged as a sequence of events. Instead of storing current state, the system stores all events that led to the current state, enabling full audit trails.
A workshop-based method to explore complex business domains by focusing on domain events. It brings together developers and domain experts to build a shared understanding of the system.

A strategy that integrates multimodal event knowledge to improve reasoning in Vision-Language Navigation tasks, addressing challenges related to coarse-grained instructions and long-horizon reasoning.

A programming paradigm where the flow of execution is determined by events such as user actions, sensor outputs, or message arrivals. Event-driven architectures decouple producers and consumers of information.

A software design pattern where the flow of the program is determined by events such as user actions, sensor outputs, or messages from other services. EDA enables loose coupling and real-time responsiveness in distributed systems.
Event-Graph Neural Networks are hardware-aware neural architectures implemented on FPGA for efficient audio classification and keyword spotting, utilizing sparse event data from neuromorphic devices.
A diffusion-based framework for text-to-motion synthesis that defines events as semantically self-contained actions, enabling better alignment between text prompts and motion segments.

An adaptive communication control mechanism for distributed learning that allows nodes to autonomously decide when to exchange model information based on local model deviations, reducing communication overhead.
A design paradigm for real-time systems that focuses on responding to events while maintaining safety and timeliness, particularly in environments that may violate operational assumptions.
EventCast is a modular forecasting framework that integrates future event knowledge into demand forecasting for e-commerce, improving accuracy during high-impact periods.
A consistency model used in distributed computing where the system guarantees that, given enough time without new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value. Used by many NoSQL databases.
A consistency model used in distributed systems where updates to a data item will eventually propagate to all replicas, but reads may temporarily return stale data.

A consistency model in distributed systems where, given enough time without new updates, all replicas of a piece of data will converge to the same value. It trades immediate consistency for availability.
A KV-cache management system that jointly optimizes eviction and compression decisions across all KV caches to minimize average generation latency in Large Language Model (LLM) inference systems.

EvoCorps is an evolutionary multi-agent framework designed to proactively depolarize online discourse by framing discourse governance as a dynamic social game, coordinating roles for monitoring and planning.

EoB is an automated benchmark designer for Black-Box Optimization that utilizes large language models to enhance diversity and objectivity in benchmark design, addressing biases inherent in human-crafted benchmarks.
The Evolution of Test Functions (EoTF) framework automatically generates diverse continuous optimization test functions to improve the evaluation of black-box optimization algorithms.
A framework that enhances language models' metacognitive abilities by aligning internal knowledge with explicit behaviors through a dual-prompt method.

A family of optimization algorithms inspired by biological evolution that use mechanisms like mutation, crossover, and selection to evolve solutions to problems. They are effective for complex search spaces.
A software architecture that supports guided, incremental change as a first principle across multiple dimensions. It uses fitness functions to evaluate architectural characteristics over time.

A subfield of artificial intelligence that uses biologically inspired computational methods such as genetic algorithms, genetic programming, and evolutionary strategies to solve optimization problems.

ECS is an evolutionary method that optimizes context combinations for large language models, improving knowledge acquisition without the need for retraining by leveraging accuracy on a small development set.

E.term: Evolutionary eXploration of Augmenting Quantum Circuits (EXAQC)
E.definition: An evolutionary approach for the automated design and training of quantum circuits, addressing the challenges of scalability and adaptability in quantum computing.

E.term: Evolutionary Game Theory in Agent-Based Modeling
E.definition: A framework used to model the decision-making behavior of household agents during self-evacuation in disaster scenarios, focusing on competition for limited resources.

An automated framework that utilizes black-box optimization to map computational graphs to spatial accelerators, enhancing the deployment of inference workloads with low latency and energy consumption.
A framework that applies multiple evolution strategies for LLM-based Verilog generation and optimization, demonstrating superior functional correctness through Monte Carlo Tree Search.

A framework for generating high-quality benchmarks for hardware model checking using reinforcement learning, addressing the benchmark gap in existing suites and facilitating rigorous evaluation of verification techniques.

EvoSkill is a self-evolving framework designed for automated skill discovery in multi-agent systems, enabling agents to autonomously develop reusable workflows and domain-specific capabilities without manual intervention.
A meta-evolution framework that combines LLM-driven optimization with evolutionary search, allowing for the reuse of previously evaluated solutions to guide the discovery of new candidate solutions.
A theoretical result demonstrating the ability to control two-dimensional hydroelastic wave systems precisely over short time intervals, given specific initial and final conditions.
A search or join operation that requires an identical match between values. In databases, exact match queries use equality operators and benefit from hash indexes.
An algorithm for mixed integer programming that generates strong valid inequalities for the binary knapsack set, optimizing storage and computation through a dominance list structure.
A deep learning framework designed for pricing American options by solving the Black-Scholes-Merton equations, ensuring compliance with terminal conditions and handling non-smooth price behaviors.
An event that occurs during program execution that disrupts the normal flow of instructions. Exceptions are typically handled using try-catch mechanisms to prevent application crashes.

A programming mechanism for responding to runtime errors or exceptional conditions using constructs like try-catch-finally blocks. Proper exception handling prevents program crashes and enables graceful error recovery.
A Gaussian process framework designed for causal inference in panel data, allowing for flexible nonlinear trends and ensuring exchangeability across treated and control units.
A file containing a program in machine code form that can be directly executed by the operating system. Executables are the output of the compilation and linking process.
The environment in which JavaScript code is evaluated and executed, containing variable bindings, the this keyword, and the scope chain. Each function call creates a new execution context.
Execution flow, represented as I = dV/dt, is identified as a fundamental driver of market dynamics, calculated using a numerical framework based on the Radon-Nikodym derivative.
A detailed description of the steps a database engine takes to execute a SQL query, including the order of operations, indexes used, and join strategies. Query plans guide optimization.
A component that manages and controls the execution of tasks, particularly in concurrent and parallel programming. Thread pool executors are common in Java and other languages.
The unauthorized transfer of data from a computer or network to an external location controlled by an attacker. Data exfiltration is often the final stage of a cyberattack after gaining access.
A Transformer-based architecture designed for forecasting daily exchange rate returns, featuring a multi-scale trend-aware self-attention mechanism to capture local and long-range dependencies.
A testing approach that tests all possible combinations of inputs and preconditions. While theoretically complete, exhaustive testing is impractical for most systems due to combinatorial explosion.
A numeric value returned by a process to its parent when it terminates, indicating success (typically zero) or failure (nonzero). Exit codes are used in shell scripting and CI/CD pipelines.

A simulation framework designed to optimize exoskeleton controllers by combining neuromechanical simulation with human-aligned exploration, addressing the challenges of human adaptation to external forces.

The expectation-realisation gap quantifies the discrepancies between anticipated productivity gains from AI tools and actual outcomes, highlighting the calibration errors experienced by developers and organizations.
A metric that quantifies the expected improvement of a solution relative to the energy consumed, guiding the adaptive selection of operators in energy-aware metaheuristics.

A computer system that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert by using a knowledge base of facts and rules. Expert systems were among the earliest successful forms of applied artificial intelligence.

A specialized guardrail model for large language models that ensures adherence to safety policies by moderating inputs and outputs in domain-specific contexts, particularly those rich in technical jargon.

The degree to which the internal mechanics of an AI or machine learning system can be understood by humans. Explainable AI (XAI) builds trust and helps meet regulatory requirements for automated decisions.
A framework for evaluating explainability solutions across different domains, demonstrating its applicability through case studies in employee attrition prediction and urban resource allocation.
An empirical evaluation approach that assesses the reliability, computational feasibility, and interpretability of transfer learning models for IoT DDoS detection under resource constraints.
A methodology for analyzing malware through memory analysis that captures volatile system state to recover transient artifacts, enhancing interpretability of automated malware detection models.
Explanation-Guided Learning is a method that incorporates human-provided explanations into the training of deep neural networks to enhance interpretability and robustness against adversarial inputs.

A framework for evaluating large language models in finance that identifies and addresses specific biases such as look-ahead and survivorship bias, ensuring more reliable performance metrics and deployment claims.

A piece of software, data, or sequence of commands that takes advantage of a vulnerability in a system to cause unintended behavior. Exploits are used in both offensive security testing and malicious attacks.

E.term: Exploratory Landscape Features for Multi-Objective Optimization (MO-ELA)
E.definition: A meta-algorithmic approach that rigorously expands exploratory landscape features to enhance automated algorithm selection in continuous multi-objective optimization problems.
A mechanism for policy search in reinforcement learning that incorporates user-specified Gaussian perturbations to baseline controls, facilitating exploration in risk-sensitive portfolio management.
The part of a floating-point number that represents the power of the base. In IEEE 754 format, the exponent determines the magnitude of the number along with the mantissa.
A retry strategy where the wait time between retries increases exponentially with each failed attempt. It is commonly used in network protocols and API clients to avoid overwhelming services.

E.term: Exponential Convergence in Stochastic Gradient Descent
E.definition: A phenomenon where stochastic gradient descent achieves faster convergence rates by leveraging instability near the edge of stability in optimization trajectories.
A language mechanism for making functions, classes, or variables available for use in other modules. Export and import statements are fundamental to modular programming in JavaScript and TypeScript.
A class of string-to-string functions characterized by exponential growth, extending the concept of polyregular functions, and exhibiting unique closure properties.

A minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework that provides a robust set of features for building web and mobile applications. Express is the most popular back-end framework in the Node.js ecosystem.
A minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework that provides a robust set of features for building web and mobile applications. Express is the most popular Node.js web framework.
A combination of values, variables, operators, and function calls that evaluates to a single value. Expressions are the building blocks of statements in most programming languages.
A method for generating cinematic portrait videos that maintains subject identity and expressions using personalized representations and improved disentanglement capabilities.
A mathematical property used in the analysis of nonconvex-nonconcave stochastic minimax optimization problems, enabling convergence without strong concavity assumptions.
Research methodologies that involve testing XR technologies in real-world settings, highlighting safety risks and the need for standardized safety protocols.
A domain-specific language used for generating executable simulation scenarios from multimodal crash report data, enhancing the safety validation process for autonomous driving systems.
A software engineering principle that allows a system to be extended with new functionality without modifying existing code. Plugins, hooks, and extension points enable extensibility.
A novel approach to integrate logic programming into typed functional programming languages, maintaining static typing while leveraging polymorphism and higher-order functions.
A software component that adds specific capabilities to an existing application or system. Browser extensions, IDE extensions, and language extensions customize and enhance functionality.
A Kubernetes add-on that automatically manages DNS records in external DNS providers based on Kubernetes resources like services and ingresses.
A database table whose data resides outside the database in external files or storage systems. External tables allow querying data in data lakes without importing it into the database.
A serverless computing model where data movement over the network is managed by the platform, allowing applications to specify data requirements at each computation stage.

A data integration process that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into a suitable format, and loads it into a destination data warehouse. ETL pipelines are fundamental to data engineering.
A data integration process that extracts data from source systems, transforms it into a suitable format, and loads it into a target database or data warehouse. ETL is fundamental to data engineering.

A computational problem that involves determining the relationship between two Boolean functions with minimal oracle queries, showcasing a significant difference in query complexity between quantum and classical approaches.
An optimized distributed gradient boosting library designed to be highly efficient, flexible, and portable. XGBoost is one of the most successful machine learning algorithms for structured data.

E.term: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) for UIT Detection
E.definition: A machine learning technique that employs XGBoost to detect and identify unlawful insider trading transactions by analyzing complex patterns in large-scale financial data.

A novel algorithm designed for classifying data into multiple labels simultaneously, particularly effective in analyzing job vacancies. It utilizes contrastive learning and LLM-driven data augmentation to enhance classification accuracy.

An agile software development methodology that emphasizes technical practices like pair programming, test-driven development, and continuous integration. XP focuses on delivering high-quality software.
ELAS is a method that utilizes very large effective array apertures in a monostatic wideband system for integrated sensing and communication, enabling operation in both near and far-field regimes.
XL-RIS refers to a large-scale intelligent surface that enhances communication and sensing capabilities in near-field systems, enabling efficient data transmission and target detection.
A phenomenon in cryptocurrency markets where extreme sentiment (fear or greed) predicts higher spreads, indicating excess uncertainty beyond realized volatility.
A novel method for discretizing tangent vector fields on triangle meshes, enabling the definition of continuous tangent vector fields and covariant derivatives for advanced mesh processing.
A technology that measures eye movements and gaze points to understand how users visually interact with interfaces. Eye tracking data informs layout decisions and identifies usability issues.

A lightweight attention-augmentation module that integrates human eye-gaze patterns into LLM-based code summarization, enhancing the model's ability to generate natural language descriptions of source code by modeling human attention during code reading.

F.definition: A computational problem related to graph isolation, which is NP-complete when the graph F is connected, expanding the classical graph domination problem.
A measure of a test's accuracy that considers both precision and recall. The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single metric for classification performance.

A metric that combines precision and recall into a single value using their harmonic mean. The F1 score ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates perfect precision and recall, making it useful for imbalanced datasets.

Function as a Service — a serverless computing model where cloud providers execute code in response to events without requiring server management. FaaS platforms like AWS Lambda and Cloudflare Workers charge only for actual compute time used.
A structural design pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. The facade hides the complexity of the subsystem and provides a cleaner, easier-to-use API.
A method for integrating diverse data formats into a unified access framework, allowing SPARQL queries to target various data structures beyond traditional RDF.
A computer technology that identifies human faces in digital images or video frames. Face detection is a prerequisite for face recognition and is used in cameras, social media, and security.
A search interface that allows users to filter results by multiple attributes or categories simultaneously. Faceted search is commonly used in e-commerce sites and content libraries.

A method for evaluating the quality of facial images captured under unconstrained conditions, focusing on both visual quality and identity-preserving features essential for reliable identity verification in surveillance.

F.term: Facial Restoration via Alignment, Entanglement, and Disentanglement
F.definition: A method aimed at optimizing facial identity consistency in multimodal editing models by addressing cross-source distribution bias and feature contamination.
An optimization problem that combines facility location and client matching, aiming to minimize costs while maximizing matched client pairs assigned to facilities.
A process utilizing large language models to verify the factual accuracy of knowledge graphs, addressing the challenges of manual verification at scale.

Fact-Flow is an innovative framework that separates the process of visual fact identification from the generation of medical reports using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), addressing challenges related to factual instability.

Factor Abstraction is a universal interface for manipulating probabilistic models that allows representation-agnostic probabilistic programming, enabling users to mix different model representations seamlessly.

An open-source Python library designed for the systematic computation and analysis of financial factors, featuring a modular API that allows users to define and integrate custom factors into their financial analysis workflows.
A self-evolving agent framework designed for financial alpha discovery, utilizing a modular skill architecture to systematically evaluate and execute investment strategies.
A creational design pattern that provides an interface for creating objects in a superclass but allows subclasses to alter the type of objects that will be created.
The process of automatically switching to a redundant system when the primary system fails. Failover mechanisms are essential for maintaining high availability in critical applications.

A clustering approach that ensures the distribution of sensitive attributes, such as gender or race, within clusters mirrors that of the overall dataset. This method modifies traditional clustering algorithms like K-means to adhere to fairness constraints.

This generalization of correlation clustering incorporates fairness constraints through the concept of fairlets, providing a framework to analyze clustering problems under various structural graph parameterizations.

FairGDiff is a graph diffusion model that mitigates topology biases in graph generation tasks by applying debiasing methods directly on the graph topology, enhancing fairness in synthetic graph outputs.

An optimization problem that involves selecting k centers from a set of data points in a metric space, ensuring a fair representation from different groups while minimizing the maximum distance to the nearest center.
A scheduling algorithm that allocates resources equally among all users or groups over time. Fair schedulers ensure that no single user monopolizes shared computing resources.
A method addressing biases in deep feature selection for recommender systems, utilizing trainable gate-based and sensitivity-based approaches to improve feature importance estimation.

The principle that AI systems should make predictions and decisions that do not discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected attributes. Fairness metrics and techniques help identify and mitigate algorithmic bias.
A method for adapting voice classification models to new cohorts or devices while addressing partial-label mismatches and demographic biases, enhancing fairness in model predictions.

A novel evaluation framework for multiparty multiobjective optimization that addresses biases in classical performance metrics by considering the fairness of solution sets across different decision-makers.
An alert that incorrectly indicates a security threat or anomaly when none exists. Minimizing false positives is crucial in security monitoring to prevent alert fatigue.
An approach to designing user-generated virtual worlds that enhances cooperative play experiences for families while addressing real-time harms and risks.
A messaging pattern where a single message is distributed to multiple recipients or queues simultaneously. Fan-out is used in notification systems, event broadcasting, and parallel processing.
A Linux-based far-memory system that employs deep learning to enhance memory prefetching by decoupling application semantics from runtime memory layout, enabling better prediction of access patterns.
A million-scale dataset that provides exhaustive annotations of visible fashion items within outfits, facilitating the development of generalist fashion intelligence across multiple tasks.
A framework for enabling secure collaboration between organizations on shared datasets while maintaining data confidentiality and high performance, utilizing fast datacenter networks.

An efficient algorithm for computing the discrete Fourier transform that reduces computation from O(n squared) to O(n log n). FFT is fundamental to signal processing, image analysis, and data compression.

FGFRFT is an optimized algorithm for computing the graph fractional Fourier transform, reducing computational complexity to O(2LN^2) while maintaining differentiability, thus enabling adaptive order learning.
A strategy for encoding high-quality 360-degree video for HTTP Adaptive Streaming that reduces encoding time by reusing encoder analysis across different resolutions and quantization parameters.
An algorithm designed for efficiently permuting sparse matrices arising from triangle meshes, focusing on fast partitioning and elimination-tree construction without strict balance requirements.

A technique for training surrogate models that efficiently map unordered vehicle positions to UAV counts and placements in multi-objective UAV placement problems for intelligent transportation systems.
Fast-MCS is a scalable open-source tool designed to evaluate Minimal Cut Sets (MCSs) in large, complex networks, helping to identify critical elements that impact network connectivity.
An FPGA-accelerated technique for optimizing sparse attention in long-context large language model inference, which reduces computational overhead by dynamically limiting token interactions.
A modern, fast web framework for building APIs with Python 3.7+ based on standard Python type hints. FastAPI provides automatic API documentation, validation, and high performance.

A framework that optimizes repository-scale code reasoning by separating exploration from content consumption, utilizing a structural scouting mechanism to improve efficiency and reduce computational costs.
An optimization framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting that consolidates and evaluates effective strategies to enhance performance while maintaining reconstruction quality.
A client in a client-server architecture that performs substantial data processing on the client side. Fat clients offer better offline capabilities but require more local resources.
A closed-loop, self-correcting framework for generating physically grounded robotic task curricula, addressing the limitations of traditional open-loop task generation methods.
The ability of a system to continue operating properly in the event of the failure of one or more of its components. Fault-tolerant systems use redundancy to maintain availability.
An enhanced Reduce operation that incorporates a correction phase to tolerate process failures, improving reliability in distributed computing environments.

A force-adaptive model that improves Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems by incorporating force/torque feedback to handle contact-rich manipulation tasks, addressing the challenges of mismatched sensor sampling rates.

A methodology for integrating thematic satellite allocations into core-satellite portfolio architectures, focusing on robustness-oriented allocation frameworks that account for fixed costs, irreversibility risk, and governance complexity.

Feasible pairings refer to the determination of suitable input-output configurations for decentralized integral controllability in non-square systems, particularly relevant in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) environments.
A version control workflow where each new feature is developed in a dedicated branch separate from the main codebase. Feature branches isolate work in progress and simplify code review.
The process of using domain knowledge to create input features that make machine learning algorithms work better. Good feature engineering can significantly improve model performance.
The process of transforming raw data into numerical features suitable for machine learning models. Feature extraction reduces data complexity while preserving the most informative aspects.

A software development technique that enables or disables features at runtime without deploying new code. Feature flags support gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and quick rollbacks, giving teams fine-grained control over what users see.
A technique where new features are deployed behind conditional checks that control which users can access them. Feature gating enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant rollbacks.

A dimensionality reduction technique that applies a hash function to map features into a fixed-size vector, avoiding the need for a pre-built dictionary. Also called the hashing trick, it trades exactness for memory efficiency.
A technique that measures the contribution of each feature to a machine learning model's predictions. Understanding feature importance aids model interpretation and feature selection.

The process of normalizing or standardizing the range of independent variables in a dataset. Feature scaling ensures that features contribute equally to model training and prevents bias toward larger values.
A centralized repository for storing and serving machine learning features that can be shared across teams and models. Feature stores ensure consistency between training and serving environments.
A technique that allows teams to modify system behavior without changing code by enabling or disabling features at runtime. Feature toggles enable trunk-based development and gradual rollouts.
A framework for personalized federated learning that utilizes a dynamic implicit graph for time-series forecasting, allowing for better handling of client heterogeneity.
A federated learning framework that enables collaborative fine-tuning of large language models across clients with heterogeneous resources using Low-Rank Adaptation.
A clustered federated learning approach that integrates global data and gradient information to enhance model performance in heterogeneous client environments.
A framework for federated cross-domain recommendation that utilizes large language models to enhance personalized recommendations while addressing data privacy concerns and overfitting risks.

A conjecture in constraint satisfaction problems stating that every finite-domain CSP is either in P or NP-complete, recently resolved for finite-domain cases but remains open for countably infinite first-order reducts.

An approach to artificial intelligence that emphasizes decentralized learning and collaboration among agents in wireless networks, addressing challenges of resource constraints and data heterogeneity.

A federated framework for causal representation learning in state-space systems that captures interdependencies among clients while preserving data privacy and enabling decentralized counterfactual reasoning.

A system that allows users to use the same identification data to obtain access to the networks and resources of all enterprises in a group. It enables single sign-on across organizational boundaries.

Federated Instruction Tuning (FIT) is a collaborative approach for tuning large language models across multiple organizations without sharing private data, addressing the risks of poisoned samples in training datasets.
An approach to mitigate latent space misalignment in multi-agent semantic communications by implementing a protocol that shares semantic equalizers between an access point and user devices.

A machine learning technique that trains models across multiple decentralized devices or servers holding local data, without exchanging raw data. It preserves privacy while enabling collaborative learning.

FedLoRA is a technique for communication-efficient and privacy-preserving fine-tuning of foundation models in federated learning, addressing challenges posed by client heterogeneity and preventing rank collapse.
A method for auditing demographic parity in federated learning environments, utilizing quantile sketches to measure disparities while maintaining privacy and data governance compliance.
A framework for federated learning that enables prompt-tuning across heterogeneous and incomplete multimodal client data, addressing the challenges of semantic alignment in distributed environments.

A recommendation paradigm that trains models across decentralized user devices, addressing privacy concerns while managing challenges related to sparse data and potential data poisoning from malicious clients.
This approach combines federated learning with decision transformers to optimize resource allocation in mobile edge computing environments, enhancing user experience in the metaverse.
A protocol for securely computing U-statistics of degree k in a federated learning setting, which allows for the computation of useful statistics while maintaining privacy.
An architecture pattern where multiple independent systems cooperate to provide a unified service while maintaining their autonomy. Federation is used in identity management and microservices.
A collection of federated social networking services that can communicate with each other using open protocols like ActivityPub. Mastodon, PeerTube, and Pixelfed are prominent fediverse platforms.
A method for stabilizing nonlinear partial differential equations using controllers based on finitely many parameters, ensuring computational feasibility for practical applications.

A mechanism where the output of a system is routed back as input to regulate future behavior. In software development, feedback loops from monitoring, testing, and user input drive continuous improvement.
A framework for generating high-quality test cases for programming tasks by leveraging external feedback to iteratively improve the diversity and effectiveness of test case synthesis.
A framework that captures the dynamics of user behavior and algorithmic recommendations over time, accounting for implicit feedback and retraining in recommender systems.

A bilingual (Korean-English) multimodal dataset specifically designed for training and evaluating jailbreak detectors in financial applications, addressing the vulnerabilities of Large Language Models and Vision Language Models.

A CPU instruction that enforces ordering constraints on memory operations, preventing the processor from reordering reads and writes across the fence. Essential for correct lock-free programming on modern out-of-order processors.

Fenchel-Young Estimators provide a unified estimation framework for the Perturbed Utility Model, addressing challenges in maximum likelihood estimation by leveraging the convex conjugate structure of the models.
A universal representation framework for many-body fermionic wavefunctions that enables the conditioning of predictions based on Hamiltonian parameters and particle numbers.

A modern JavaScript interface for making HTTP requests that replaces XMLHttpRequest. The Fetch API uses promises and provides a cleaner, more powerful way to handle network requests in web applications.

A machine learning approach where models learn to generalize from only a few labeled examples per class. Few-shot learning is critical for scenarios where labeled data is scarce or expensive to collect.

This novel attack method targets the reasoning process of medical large language models during supervised fine-tuning by injecting poisoned rationales into few-shot training data, leading to stealthy degradation of model performance.

Foreign Function Interface — a mechanism that allows code written in one programming language to call functions written in another language. FFI enables interoperability between languages like Python and C.

Fast Fourier Transform — an efficient algorithm for computing the discrete Fourier transform, which converts a signal from time domain to frequency domain. FFT is essential in signal processing and data analysis.

A reimagined GPU microarchitecture specifically designed to optimize performance for Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) tasks, addressing the computational and memory overheads typically associated with FHE.

A lightweight unit of execution that is cooperatively scheduled, meaning it explicitly yields control rather than being preempted. Fibers are used in Ruby, React, and some operating systems for concurrent tasks.
A data structure consisting of a collection of trees that provides better amortized running time for many operations than binary heaps. It is used in network optimization algorithms.
A high-speed network technology primarily used for storage area networks, delivering in-order lossless delivery of raw block data at speeds up to 128 Gbps over optical fiber or copper cables.
A metric that measures the freshness of entanglement in quantum repeater networks, linking slot-level success probability to long-run average fidelity age.

A high-fidelity and detail-preserving one-step diffusion super-resolution framework that utilizes a detail-aware weighting strategy during training to enhance visual quality in image super-resolution tasks.
An open authentication standard that enables passwordless sign-in using biometrics, mobile devices, or hardware security keys. FIDO2 combines WebAuthn and CTAP protocols to resist phishing attacks.
An integrated circuit that can be configured by a customer or designer after manufacturing. FPGAs offer hardware-level performance with software-like flexibility for specialized computing tasks.
First In, First Out — a method of organizing and manipulating data where the first element added is the first one removed. Queues and message brokers typically follow FIFO ordering.

A collaborative, browser-based design tool used for creating user interfaces, prototypes, and design systems. Figma's real-time collaboration features have made it the industry standard for product design teams working together.
An extension that adds custom functionality to the Figma design tool. Plugins can automate tasks, integrate external data, generate design assets, and enhance the design workflow.
An integer that uniquely identifies an open file or I/O resource within a process. File descriptors are used in Unix and Linux systems for reading, writing, and managing I/O operations.
An abstract reference to a file resource that provides an interface for reading, writing, and manipulating the file. File handles are managed by the operating system and must be properly closed.
Input and output operations for reading from and writing to files on a storage device. File I/O involves opening, reading, writing, seeking, and closing files using system calls or library functions.
Access rights assigned to files and directories that control who can read, write, or execute them. Unix-like systems use owner, group, and other permission levels with read, write, and execute flags.
The distribution of digital files over a network, enabling multiple users to access the same data. File sharing methods range from network drives and cloud storage to peer-to-peer protocols.
A method and data structure that an operating system uses to manage files on a disk or partition. File systems organize data into files and directories for storage and retrieval.

A system designed to automatically identify core filler-gap constructions in spoken English corpora, facilitating the analysis of children's language acquisition and the role of grammatical knowledge.

An advanced explicit surface reconstruction method that generates a surface mesh interpolating a given point cloud, ensuring preservation of sharp features and fine geometric details even with low-quality inputs.

F.term: Filtered Approximate k Nearest Neighbor (AKNN) Search
F.definition: A hybrid query approach that combines high-dimensional vector similarity with structured attribute filtering, requiring novel cost estimation techniques for optimization.
A search technique that enhances approximate nearest neighbor search by incorporating metadata constraints, enabling more relevant results in vector databases.
An algorithm that dynamically computes the finality guarantees of the Filecoin network, providing a more accurate assessment of tipset permanence based on live chain history.
A structured representation of financial data derived from SEC 10-K filings, used to enhance causal discovery algorithms by providing domain-specific constraints.

The process of creating mathematical representations of a company's financial performance for decision-making. Financial models use spreadsheets and software to forecast revenue, expenses, and valuations.

The process of producing statements that disclose an organization's financial status to stakeholders. Financial reports include balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow statements following accounting standards.

A computational representation that models financial structures like securitizations and insurance contracts by separating stochastic inflow generation from deterministic allocation rules, allowing for explicit mapping of inflows to payments.
A subfield of computational complexity theory that studies the precise run times of algorithms for NP-complete problems, aiming to identify the fastest possible algorithms beyond brute-force methods.
A technique for efficiently migrating memory in user-space across non-uniform memory access (NUMA) architectures, optimizing query performance by ensuring data locality.

Fine-Pruning is a biologically inspired algorithm that personalizes machine learning models by mimicking the brain's learning process through pruning, addressing the limitations of traditional backpropagation methods.

The process of taking a pre-trained machine learning model and training it further on a specific dataset to adapt it for a particular task. Fine-tuning enables transfer learning with less data and compute.

A purely functional data structure providing amortized O(1) access to both ends and O(log n) concatenation and splitting, used to implement sequences, priority queues, and interval trees in immutable settings.
A large language model designed for sentiment analysis in financial contexts, enabling the integration of sentiment signals into quantitative trading strategies.
An abstract machine that can be in exactly one of a finite number of states at any given time. Finite automata are used in lexical analysis, pattern matching, and protocol design.

A numerical technique for solving partial differential equations by dividing a problem domain into smaller elements. FEM is widely used in engineering simulation, physics modeling, and scientific computing.
A computational model consisting of a finite number of states, transitions between those states, and actions. FSMs are used in parsing, protocol design, game AI, and user interface logic.

An automaton with two tapes — input and output — that reads symbols from the input tape and writes to the output, used in natural language processing for morphological analysis, speech recognition, and text normalization.

A financial management discipline and cultural practice that brings financial accountability to the variable spend model of cloud computing. FinOps teams optimize cloud costs while maintaining performance.
Financial Technology — the application of technology to improve and automate financial services. FinTech encompasses mobile banking, cryptocurrency, insurtech, robo-advisors, and payment processing.
An analysis of how financial technology disrupts traditional banking by increasing competition for deposits and raising deposit rates, particularly affecting small financial institutions.
A financial text-paired time-series dataset designed to capture the complex interdependencies in financial markets by pairing textual information with numerical time-series data.
A software design pattern where a request is sent without waiting for or expecting a response. Fire-and-forget is used for non-critical operations like logging, analytics, and notifications.
Google's mobile and web application development platform that provides backend services like authentication, real-time database, cloud storage, and hosting. Firebase simplifies app development.
An open-source virtualization technology created by AWS that uses lightweight micro-virtual machines for serverless computing. Firecracker provides the security of VMs with the speed of containers.
An FPGA-based hardware accelerator that implements a novel plasticity algorithm for Spiking Neural Networks, enhancing real-time adaptive control through on-chip plasticity.

FireRed-OCR is a specialized framework that transforms general Vision-Language Models into high-performance Optical Character Recognition models, focusing on pixel-precise structural document parsing to enhance industrial OCR applications.

A network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on predetermined security rules. Firewalls establish a barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks like the internet.

A cloud-based network security service that provides firewall capabilities without on-premises hardware. FWaaS inspects and filters traffic at the cloud edge, integrating with SASE and zero-trust architectures.

Permanent software programmed into a hardware device's read-only memory that provides low-level control. Firmware bridges hardware and higher-level software, found in routers, IoT devices, and embedded systems.
A web performance metric that measures the time from page navigation to when the browser renders the first piece of DOM content such as text, images, or SVG elements.
A web performance metric that measures the time from when a user first interacts with a page to when the browser can respond to that interaction. Low FID indicates good interactivity.
A function that is treated as a first-class citizen in a programming language, meaning it can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
An advanced sampling technique for masked diffusion models that shows promise in generating samples more efficiently compared to traditional methods, particularly in high-dimensional spaces.

F.term: First-Order Logic of Here and There
F.definition: A logic system for automated theorem proving that combines sequent calculus and axiomatic embedding into intuitionistic logic, optimized for analytic proof search.

The FITO paradigm evaluates multimodal large language models by requiring them to construct explicit cross-modal evidence chains within scientific documents, promoting a deeper understanding of content.

A theoretical framework that characterizes the behavior of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) by modeling mini-batch noise as a loss-induced matrix. This approach provides insights into optimal rates and oracle complexity in the context of SGD.
An advanced theory of stochastic gradient descent that incorporates mini-batch noise as a loss-induced matrix, allowing for improved convergence rates and understanding of gradient covariance.
A framework that models mini-batch noise in stochastic gradient descent as an intrinsic matrix, allowing for a diffusion approximation with structured volatility.

A measure of system availability indicating 99.999% uptime, equating to approximately 5.26 minutes of downtime per year. Achieving five nines requires redundancy, failover, and rigorous operational practices.
A fixed state of a set of objects used as a baseline for running tests. Fixtures provide consistent test data and environment setup, ensuring test repeatability and isolation.

FL-HDC-DP is a federated learning framework that employs hyperdimensional computing and differential privacy to minimize energy consumption and enhance privacy in decentralized learning over wireless edge networks.
A test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails without any changes to the code. Flaky tests undermine confidence in the test suite and are often caused by timing issues or shared state.
This technique focuses on identifying flaky tests in software projects by analyzing source code identifiers, aiming to improve automated test execution quality in industrial settings.

A visualization of profiled software that shows which code paths consume the most resources. Flame graphs stack sampled call stacks horizontally by frequency, making performance bottlenecks easy to spot.

An IO-aware exact attention algorithm that computes scaled dot-product attention by tiling and recomputation, reducing GPU memory reads/writes from quadratic to linear and enabling longer sequence lengths in transformers.
A type of non-volatile computer memory that can be electrically erased and reprogrammed. Flash memory is used in USB drives, SSDs, memory cards, and embedded systems.

An accelerated implementation of Score-Debiasing Kernel Density Estimation (SD-KDE) that utilizes Tensor Cores to enhance performance significantly, achieving up to 47 times faster execution compared to traditional methods.
An advanced framework for evaluating sequences in tasks like recommender systems, which enhances accuracy and resource utilization by enabling parallel evaluation and cross-sequence comparison.
A memory streaming framework designed to optimize the execution of modern deep neural networks on mobile GPUs by dynamically managing memory usage rather than preloading all model parameters.

A lightweight Python web framework that provides the basics for building web applications without imposing a specific project structure. Flask is popular for small to medium-sized applications and APIs.
A database stored in a single file, typically containing records with no structural relationships. CSV and TSV files are common flat file formats used for data exchange.

A utility for software deployment and package management on Linux that provides a sandboxed environment for applications. Flatpak allows applications to run consistently across different Linux distributions.
The process of converting a nested data structure into a single-level structure. In arrays, flattening removes nested arrays; in databases, it denormalizes hierarchical data into flat tables.

FLCOA (Five Layers for Cooperation/Coordination among Autonomous Agents) is a framework that conceptualizes the emergence of cooperation and coordination in LLM-based multi-agent systems, focusing on the impact of computational and communication resources on agent interactions.
A unified framework for video generation that disentangles appearance and motion, utilizing a novel 3D control signal to enhance the control and precision of generated video content.
A CSS layout module that provides an efficient way to distribute space among items in a container, even when their size is unknown. Flexbox simplifies alignment and ordering of elements.

Flexi-NeurA is a configurable neuromorphic accelerator designed for spiking neural networks (SNNs) that allows users to customize neuron models, network structures, and precision levels, enhancing adaptability for diverse workloads.

An innovative array system that enhances wireless network capacity by allowing mechanical beamforming through the movement of passive couplers, improving communication coverage at a low hardware cost.
A composable High-Level Synthesis library that facilitates the rapid development of domain-specific LLM accelerators, allowing for customized inference architectures.
A fine-grained accelerator for 3D Gaussian Splatting that optimizes contribution-aware processing to enhance performance on resource-limited edge platforms.
A novel framework utilizing a generalized Hough transform on the unit sphere to estimate camera motion from monocular video, improving accuracy in high-noise conditions.
A monitoring tool that continuously captures application runtime data such as events, metrics, and thread states for post-mortem analysis. JDK Flight Recorder is a popular implementation in Java.
The minimum number of flips required to transform one triangulation of a convex polygon into another, a problem proven to be NP-hard.
