A data processing architecture that simplifies Lambda Architecture by using a single stream processing layer for both real-time and batch computation, treating all data as an immutable append-only log.
A method of simplifying Boolean algebra expressions using a visual diagram. Karnaugh maps are used in digital circuit design to minimize the number of logic gates required.
A coding exercise designed to improve programming skills through practice and repetition. Code katas help developers refine their problem-solving abilities and learn new techniques.
A measure of a node's influence in a network that accounts for both direct and indirect connections, useful in analyzing productivity in scientific and technological collaborations.
A curated dataset comprising 3,013 audio-text pairs from 195 Kazakh songs, segmented at the lyric-line level, used to improve automatic speech recognition for low-resource languages.

A novel knowledge distillation framework for large language models that features a decoupled architecture, improving training efficiency by optimizing the roles of student and teacher models during the distillation process.

An open-source Python framework for creating reproducible, maintainable, and modular data science code. Kedro provides a project template and pipeline abstraction for production-ready machine learning workflows.

KEEP is a KV-cache-centric memory management system designed to enhance the efficiency of memory-augmented Large Language Models (LLMs) in embodied planning by optimizing the storage and reuse of key-value caches.

An HTTP mechanism that reuses a single TCP connection for multiple requests and responses, avoiding the overhead of establishing new connections. Keep-alive connections reduce latency and server load.

A high-level neural network API written in Python that runs on top of TensorFlow, providing a user-friendly interface for building and training deep learning models. It emphasizes rapid experimentation.

A network authentication protocol that uses secret-key cryptography and a trusted third party to authenticate client-server applications. It is the default authentication protocol in Windows Active Directory.

The core component of an operating system that manages system resources and provides the lowest-level abstraction for hardware. The kernel handles memory management, process scheduling, device drivers, and system calls.

A networking technique that allows user-space applications to send and receive packets directly without traversing the kernel's network stack, dramatically reducing latency and increasing throughput for high-frequency trading and NFV.
A non-parametric method for estimating the probability density function of a continuous random variable. KDE is used in data visualization and statistical analysis to smooth distributions.
A new measure of statistical dependence that extends the integrated R^2 metric to multivariate and structured data using reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces.

A novel statistical measure of dependence that extends the integrated R² concept to multivariate and structured data using reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces, enhancing sensitivity to tail behavior and oscillatory structures.
A novel approach to measure performance interference and isolation in multi-tenant environments by analyzing kernel-level data structure access.
A piece of code that can be loaded into or removed from the kernel at runtime without rebooting the system. Kernel modules extend the functionality of the OS, such as adding device drivers.
A safety measure taken by an operating system's kernel upon detecting a fatal internal error from which it cannot safely recover. It is the Unix/Linux equivalent of the Windows Blue Screen of Death.

An optimal algorithm for estimating Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATEs) using kernel ridge regression in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, achieving minimax rates under specific structural conditions.

The protected memory area where the operating system kernel and its modules execute with full hardware access privileges. Kernel space is separated from user space to prevent applications from crashing the system.
A technique in machine learning that enables algorithms to operate in a high-dimensional feature space without explicitly computing the coordinates. It is fundamental to support vector machines.

A method that transforms Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations into convex semidefinite programs using kernel-based linear matrix inequalities, providing a robust framework for solving nonlinear optimal control problems.

A predictive model that utilizes deep learning techniques to forecast the task selection sequence of the Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) in the Linux kernel, aiming to enhance task scheduling for varied workloads.

A system that enables principled performance tunability in operating system kernels by allowing safe and efficient in-situ tuning of performance constants (perf-consts) without the need for kernel image rebuilding.

A formal procedure for generating, distributing, or destroying cryptographic keys under strict security controls with multiple authorized participants. Key ceremonies are used for root certificate creation and HSM initialization.
A cryptographic function that derives one or more secret keys from a master key, password, or passphrase. KDFs like PBKDF2, bcrypt, and Argon2 are used for secure password storage.

The administration of cryptographic keys throughout their lifecycle, including generation, storage, distribution, rotation, and destruction. Proper key management is essential for maintaining the security of encrypted data.
A system that manages the creation, storage, distribution, and retirement of cryptographic keys. Cloud providers like AWS KMS and Azure Key Vault offer managed key management solutions.
A set of two cryptographic keys — a public key and a private key — used in asymmetric encryption. The public key encrypts data that only the corresponding private key can decrypt.
A measurable value that demonstrates how effectively an organization is achieving key business objectives. KPIs are used at multiple levels to evaluate success at reaching targets.
The practice of periodically replacing cryptographic keys with new ones to limit the exposure if a key is compromised. Key rotation is a security best practice enforced by compliance frameworks.

A technique used to make a weak key, such as a short password, more secure by increasing the time needed to test each possible key. It is implemented through iterated hashing or key derivation functions.

A concept that explores the expressive capabilities of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) when node identifiers are present, focusing on how these identifiers impact the model's ability to differentiate between graph structures.
Key-Value Cache Compression is a method for optimizing memory usage in large language models by managing the retention and accessibility of key-value pairs in attention mechanisms.

A type of NoSQL database that stores data as a collection of key-value pairs, where each unique key maps to a value. Key-value stores like Redis and DynamoDB offer extremely fast lookups and are ideal for caching, session management, and real-time data.

An input device consisting of keys used to enter text, numbers, and commands into a computer. Modern keyboards include mechanical, membrane, and virtual variants, each with different key switch mechanisms.
A combination of keys pressed simultaneously to trigger a specific command or action in software. Well-designed keyboard shortcuts improve productivity and accessibility for power users.

Key combinations that trigger specific actions in software applications, enabling faster interaction than using menus or buttons. Well-designed keyboard shortcuts improve productivity and accessibility for power users.

An open-source identity and access management solution that provides single sign-on, identity brokering, and social login capabilities. It supports standard protocols like OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0, and SAML.
An animation technique where the designer specifies key states (keyframes) and the software interpolates the frames in between. CSS keyframe animations enable complex visual effects on the web.
A framework that models long-horizon dependencies in robot manipulation tasks by linking key historical frames, overcoming limitations of traditional VLA models.

A framework that automatically extracts kinematics-aware keyframes from demonstrations to generate structured rewards for reinforcement learning, addressing challenges of reward sparsity and procedural logic in long-horizon tasks.

A type of surveillance software or hardware that records keystrokes made by a user. While sometimes used legitimately for monitoring, keyloggers are frequently deployed as malware to steal credentials.

A secure repository for storing cryptographic keys, certificates, and sensitive credentials. Keystores protect encryption keys from unauthorized access and are used in SSL/TLS configurations, code signing, and application security.
The automated process of identifying the most relevant words or phrases in a document that best describe its content. Keyword extraction uses techniques like TF-IDF, RAKE, and transformer models.
A benchmarking framework tailored for evaluating NoSQL database paradigms in Knowledge Graph processing, addressing the complexity of real-world workloads.
A heterogeneous GPU architecture designed for graphics applications, optimizing performance and energy efficiency by adapting to varying computational intensity and memory bandwidth requirements.

KHI is an index for multi-attribute range-filtering approximate nearest neighbor search (RFANNS) that combines an attribute-space partitioning tree with HNSW graphs, optimizing performance for high-dimensional vector queries.

An open-source data visualization and exploration tool for Elasticsearch data. Kibana provides dashboards, charts, maps, and machine learning features for analyzing log data, monitoring infrastructure, and exploring large datasets in real time.

A cybersecurity model that describes the stages of a cyberattack from reconnaissance to data exfiltration. The Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain framework helps organizations identify and prevent intrusions.
A safety mechanism that immediately shuts down a system, feature, or process in case of emergency. In software, kill switches can disable features remotely without deploying new code.
A model for intertemporal utility maximization that incorporates a stochastic factor representing the excess return of risky assets, following an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process for mean reversion.
Kubernetes IN Docker — a tool for running local Kubernetes clusters using Docker containers as nodes. Kind is primarily used for testing Kubernetes itself and for local development workflows.
A differentiable volumetric body model that resolves Lie algebra ambiguities in large articulated motions, promoting smooth, bijective volumetric mappings for automated medical imaging analysis.
An optimization strategy for embodied Vision-Language-Action models that enhances inference speed while addressing token errors through careful adjustment of acceptance thresholds.
Amazon Kinesis — a platform on AWS for collecting, processing, and analyzing real-time streaming data at any scale. It enables developers to build applications that process data as it arrives.
An AWS service that processes and analyzes streaming data in real time using SQL or Apache Flink. Kinesis Analytics enables building real-time dashboards, metrics, and anomaly detection.
An AWS service for loading streaming data into data stores and analytics services. It can capture, transform, and deliver streaming data to Amazon S3, Redshift, Elasticsearch, and Splunk.

An AWS service for real-time data streaming that continuously captures gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources. It enables real-time analytics and event-driven applications.
A modeling technique for stochastic optimal control that treats the state of charge as a position and control as acceleration, enabling coordinated operation of residential energy storage devices.
An animation technique that combines motion and text to express ideas and evoke emotions. It is used in video titles, advertisements, and interactive web experiences.
AI-generated influencers that adopt kinship-based roles to engage older adults on social media platforms, raising questions about design strategies and user experience.
Keep It Simple, Stupid — a design principle stating that most systems work best when they are kept simple rather than made complex. KISS encourages avoiding unnecessary complexity in software design.

A versatile video editing framework that utilizes instruction-based and reference-guided editing techniques, enhanced by a scalable data generation pipeline for creating high-fidelity training quadruplets.
These are optimality conditions derived for optimization problems involving generalized invex fuzzy functions, extending the classical KKT framework to nonsmooth and fuzzy environments.
A reinforcement learning framework that utilizes KL-regularized objectives to achieve faster convergence rates and logarithmic regret in multi-armed bandit problems.
An algorithm for multi-armed bandits that utilizes KL-regularized objectives to achieve faster convergence rates and logarithmic regret, enhancing the efficiency of online learning.

A unary operator in formal language theory and regular expressions denoting zero or more repetitions of the preceding element. The Kleene closure of a set S, written S*, includes the empty string and all finite concatenations.
A DiT-based framework for character animation that utilizes a divide-and-conquer strategy to manage heterogeneous motion representations for body, face, and hands in a unified system.
A comprehensive K-8 Mathematical Pedagogical Benchmark designed to assess large language models (LLMs) from two perspectives: holistic pedagogical capabilities and multi-turn dialogue effectiveness.

A classic combinatorial optimization problem where given a set of items with weights and values, the goal is to determine which items to include in a collection so the total weight does not exceed a limit and the total value is maximized.
A knowledge-graph-driven framework that generates multiple-choice question datasets from external sources, allowing for adaptive difficulty calibration based on topic-specific knowledge graphs.

A graph structure where each node is connected to its k nearest neighbors in a feature space, used for approximate nearest neighbor search, manifold learning, community detection, and semi-supervised classification.
A method of filling in missing values by using the values of the k-nearest neighbors in feature space. KNN imputation considers the similarity between data points to estimate missing entries.
A centralized repository for information that is organized, searchable, and easily accessible. Knowledge bases are used for customer support, internal documentation, and AI-powered retrieval systems.
A self-service document that provides information about a product, service, or process to help users solve problems independently. Knowledge base articles reduce support ticket volume.

The process of transforming a knowledge representation into a form that supports efficient query answering. Compiled representations like decision diagrams trade preprocessing time for faster inference.

The process of extracting useful knowledge from large volumes of data through methods including data mining, machine learning, and statistical analysis. It encompasses the entire data analysis pipeline.

A model compression technique where a smaller student model is trained to replicate the behavior of a larger teacher model. It enables deployment of efficient models that retain much of the teacher's accuracy.

A structured representation of real-world entities and their relationships, stored as a network of interconnected nodes and edges. Knowledge graphs power search engines, recommendation systems, and AI assistants by encoding semantic relationships between concepts.

A reasoning framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) with structured knowledge graphs to enhance the analysis of Solid State Drive (SSD) operational data, improving diagnosis of performance and reliability issues.
A large language model enhanced with a structured knowledge graph to improve domain-specific question generation, particularly in medical contexts.
A framework that integrates diverse sources of drug-related data into a unified evidence-weighted bipartite network, facilitating improved prediction of adverse drug reactions.
The process of creating, sharing, using, and managing knowledge and information within an organization. Effective knowledge management improves decision-making and reduces redundant work.

The field of AI concerned with how to formally represent information about the world so that computers can use it to solve complex tasks. It encompasses ontologies, frames, semantic networks, and logic.

The process of finding and extracting relevant information from large knowledge bases or document collections. Retrieval-augmented generation combines knowledge retrieval with language models for improved accuracy.

Knowledge Tracing models are specialized, domain-specific temporal models that predict future student responses based on historical question-response data, optimized for high accuracy and fast inference in educational contexts.
The systematic process of sharing expertise, skills, and information from one part of an organization to another. It ensures continuity when team members change roles or leave the organization.
A model-based approach for audio segmentation that utilizes knowledge associated with data, such as music scores, to segment audio without relying on pre-segmented training data.

Knowledgeable Experience Learning is an approach aimed at aligning agentic world models with procedural grounding to prevent physical hallucinations in Large Language Models, enhancing their ability to operate within the laws of the physical world.

KnowTeX is a visualization tool that generates dependency graphs from LaTeX sources, enabling users to explore mathematical dependencies in a more accessible format than traditional formal proof systems.
An efficient string-matching algorithm that searches for occurrences of a pattern within a text by exploiting information about previous matches. KMP achieves linear time complexity.

The minimum length of a program that produces a given output string, measuring the string's inherent computational complexity. It provides a theoretical foundation for data compression and randomness.
Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) leverage B-spline locality for ultrafast online learning, enabling efficient adaptation in high-frequency systems with strict memory constraints.

A class of neural networks characterized by nonlinear, univariate activation functions typically implemented as piecewise polynomial splines or Gaussian processes. KANs utilize mathematical abstractions for property verification through Mixed Integer Linear Programming.

An open-source API gateway and microservices management layer that provides load balancing, authentication, rate limiting, and analytics. Kong is built on NGINX and supports plugin-based extensibility.
Functions that provide a representation of dynamical systems, extended to stochastic differential equations, facilitating the analysis of system behavior over time.
An approach that utilizes the Koopman operator defined on a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space to analyze the stability of nonlinear systems, enhancing control strategies based on Koopman theory.

Kubernetes Operations — a tool for deploying, upgrading, and managing production-grade Kubernetes clusters on cloud infrastructure. Kops automates the provisioning of compute, networking, and storage.

A modern, concise programming language developed by JetBrains that runs on the Java Virtual Machine. Kotlin is the preferred language for Android development and offers null safety, coroutines, and expressive syntax while maintaining full Java interoperability.
A Kotlin feature for writing asynchronous, non-blocking code in a sequential style. Coroutines simplify concurrent programming by providing structured concurrency primitives.

A Kotlin feature that allows sharing code between multiple platforms including JVM, JavaScript, iOS, and native targets. It enables writing business logic once and sharing it across mobile and web applications.

Key Performance Indicator — a measurable value that demonstrates how effectively an organization or individual is achieving key business objectives. KPIs vary by industry and role, from revenue growth and customer retention to deployment frequency and uptime.
A visual display that presents key performance indicators in an easily digestible format using charts, graphs, and gauges. Dashboards enable stakeholders to monitor business health at a glance.
Kusto Query Language — a read-only query language used in Azure Data Explorer and Azure Monitor for exploring data. KQL provides rich filtering, aggregation, and visualization capabilities.
A novel attack methodology that exploits higher-order electromagnetic side-channel information to extract parameters from deep neural networks, specifically targeting specialized GPU units.
A hierarchical anomaly detection framework that reconstructs execution hierarchies from flat logs to improve detection of dependencies and anomalies in system logs.
A family of flattening methods for tensors that generalizes Koszul flattenings, providing new border rank criteria through the vanishing of minors of these flattenings.

A distribution-adaptive variant of Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm that replaces global sorting with a three-phase procedure, enhancing efficiency in edge processing without the need for a full sort.
A greedy algorithm for finding the minimum spanning tree of a connected weighted graph. It works by sorting edges by weight and adding them to the tree if they do not create a cycle.

An asynchronous web framework built by JetBrains in Kotlin using coroutines, designed for building connected applications including HTTP servers, clients, WebSocket services, and microservices with a lightweight plugin architecture.
A network proxy that runs on each node in a Kubernetes cluster and maintains network rules for pod communication. It enables service discovery and load balancing within the cluster.
The command-line tool for interacting with Kubernetes clusters. It allows users to deploy applications, inspect and manage cluster resources, and view logs.
An open-source machine learning platform built on Kubernetes that makes it easy to deploy, scale, and manage ML workflows. It provides tools for model training, serving, and pipeline orchestration.

The primary node agent in Kubernetes that runs on each node and ensures containers are running in pods. It takes a set of pod specifications and ensures the containers described are healthy and running.

An open-source container orchestration platform that automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Originally developed by Google, Kubernetes (K8s) has become the industry standard for running containers in production.

A piece of code that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server before object persistence but after authentication and authorization. Admission controllers enforce policies on cluster resources.
A set of nodes that run containerized applications managed by Kubernetes. A cluster consists of a control plane that manages the cluster and worker nodes that run the application workloads.
An API object used to store non-confidential configuration data in key-value pairs. ConfigMaps allow decoupling environment-specific configuration from container images.
Custom Resource Definition — a way to extend the Kubernetes API by defining new resource types. CRDs allow users to create custom objects that Kubernetes manages like built-in resources.
A workload controller that ensures a copy of a pod runs on all or selected nodes in a Kubernetes cluster. DaemonSets are used for cluster-wide services like log collection and monitoring agents.
A distributed, reliable key-value store used as the backing store for all Kubernetes cluster data. etcd stores configuration data, state, and metadata that the cluster components rely on.

A mechanism that prevents deletion of a Kubernetes resource until specific cleanup operations are completed. Finalizers ensure that dependent resources are properly handled before an object is removed.
A package manager for Kubernetes that simplifies deploying and managing applications on Kubernetes clusters. Helm uses charts — pre-configured packages of Kubernetes resources.
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — a Kubernetes resource that automatically scales the number of pods in a deployment based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics.
An API object that manages external access to services in a Kubernetes cluster, typically HTTP and HTTPS. Ingress provides load balancing, SSL termination, and name-based virtual hosting.
The process management system within Kubernetes containers responsible for reaping zombie processes and forwarding signals. Using a proper init system like tini prevents resource leaks in containers.
A workload resource that creates one or more pods and ensures that a specified number of them successfully terminate. Jobs are used for batch processing and one-time computational tasks.

A configuration management tool built into kubectl that lets you customize Kubernetes YAML configurations without using templates. Kustomize uses overlays to modify base configurations for different environments.

A diagnostic check performed by the kubelet to determine if a container is still running. If a liveness probe fails, the kubelet kills the container and it is restarted according to its restart policy.
A mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single Kubernetes cluster. Namespaces provide a scope for names and can be used to divide cluster resources between multiple teams or projects.
A specification for how groups of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network policies provide fine-grained control over pod-level network traffic.

A method of packaging, deploying, and managing a Kubernetes application using custom resources and controllers. Operators encode operational knowledge to automate complex application lifecycle management.
A piece of storage in a Kubernetes cluster that has been provisioned by an administrator or dynamically provisioned using storage classes. PersistentVolumes decouple storage from pod lifecycle.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, consisting of one or more containers that share storage, network, and a specification for how to run. Pods are ephemeral by design.

Role-Based Access Control in Kubernetes — a method of regulating access to cluster resources based on the roles of individual users. RBAC policies define what actions users can perform on which resources.
A diagnostic check that determines whether a container is ready to accept traffic. Unlike liveness probes, a failing readiness probe removes the pod from service endpoints without killing it.
A deployment strategy in Kubernetes that incrementally replaces old pod instances with new ones. Rolling updates allow deployments to take place with zero downtime by updating a few pods at a time.
An object that stores sensitive information such as passwords, tokens, and keys in a Kubernetes cluster. Secrets are base64-encoded and can be mounted as volumes or exposed as environment variables.
An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of pods as a network service. Services provide stable endpoints for pod-to-pod communication even as pods are created and destroyed.

An infrastructure layer that manages service-to-service communication within a Kubernetes cluster using sidecar proxies. Service meshes like Istio and Linkerd provide traffic management and observability.
A container that runs alongside the main application container in a Kubernetes pod. Sidecars handle cross-cutting concerns like logging, monitoring, proxying, and configuration management.
A workload API object for managing stateful applications in Kubernetes. StatefulSets provide guarantees about the ordering and uniqueness of pods, essential for databases and distributed systems.

A property applied to a Kubernetes node that repels pods from being scheduled on it unless the pod has a matching toleration. Taints and tolerations work together to ensure pods are not scheduled on inappropriate nodes.

An abstraction that allows data to persist beyond the lifetime of a container and enables data sharing between containers in a pod. Kubernetes supports many volume types including local, NFS, and cloud storage.
Vertical Pod Autoscaler — a Kubernetes component that automatically adjusts CPU and memory resource requests for pods based on observed usage. VPA complements HPA by right-sizing individual pods.

KUDA stands for Knowledge Unlearning by Deviating Representation, a technique aimed at effectively removing specific knowledge from large language models without compromising their overall coherence and performance.

A mathematical tool used to analyze the convergence properties of optimization algorithms, particularly in the context of nonisolated local minima, without relying on gradient or Hessian computations.

An analytical framework that examines the limitations of kurtosis-based ICA in wide, balanced mixtures, establishing a redundancy law that relates excess kurtosis to effective width in statistical projections.
A management strategy for key-value caches in large language model (LLM) inference that optimizes cache retention during multi-turn interactions to enhance performance.

A key-value data store provided by edge computing platforms like Cloudflare Workers for storing persistent data at the edge. KV namespaces provide low-latency reads for globally distributed applications.

Kernel-based Virtual Machine — a virtualization module in the Linux kernel that turns the kernel into a hypervisor. KVM allows running multiple virtual machines with unmodified Linux or Windows images.

A theoretical framework for optimizing asymmetric key-value merging in large language models, addressing computational and memory demands by characterizing asymmetry through spectral energy distribution.

A post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism selected by NIST as a standard for quantum-resistant cryptography. Kyber is based on the learning with errors problem and is designed to replace RSA and ECC.

L.definition: L-systems are a formal language used to generate complex patterns and structures, which can be applied to create artificial programs for testing compiler and operating system performance across multiple programming languages.
The fastest and smallest cache memory located directly on the CPU die. L1 cache provides single-cycle access to frequently used data and instructions, significantly reducing memory latency.
A geometric invariant used to quantify divergence between Gaussian measures, leveraging the relationship between spherical squared-Hellinger distance and hyperbolic geometry.

A novel approach for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis that utilizes Large Language Model-generated annotations to fine-tune lightweight models, achieving competitive performance in low-resource scenarios.
A text element in a user interface that identifies or describes an associated form field, button, or other interactive element. Proper labeling is essential for usability and accessibility.

A technique that converts categorical variables into numerical values by assigning each unique category an integer label. Label encoding is simpler than one-hot encoding but may imply an ordinal relationship.

LACE is a lightweight concurrency testing framework for the Linux kernel that utilizes eBPF to enable controlled concurrency testing, making it easier to expose concurrency bugs through systematic exploration of thread interleavings.
A management framework for serverless computing that balances cold-start latency and carbon emissions by formulating pod retention as a sequential decision problem using reinforcement learning.
A generative framework that bridges language and trajectory planning in autonomous driving, addressing the challenges of representing multimodal driving behaviors.

LAER-MoE is an efficient training framework for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that employs Fully Sharded Expert Parallel (FSEP) to alleviate load imbalance among experts during parallel training, thereby enhancing overall training efficiency.

LAGO is a local-global optimization framework that integrates gradient-enhanced Bayesian optimization with trust region methods, allowing for adaptive competition between global exploration and local refinement in optimization tasks.

A system designed for distributed large-model training that optimizes the overlap of communication and computation by co-tuning communication parameters, reducing optimization complexity from exponential to linear.
A model adaptation technique for cross data lakes that minimizes the need for annotations by leveraging existing pre-trained language models to adapt to new data lakes.
A benchmark for evaluating large audio-language models in assessing safety in multi-turn spoken dialogues, generating synthetic dialogues to test for harmful content.

An anonymous function — a small, unnamed function defined inline without a formal function declaration. Lambda expressions are used in functional programming, callbacks, and collection operations across languages like Python, JavaScript, Java, and C#.

A data-processing architecture designed to handle massive quantities of data by using both batch and stream processing methods. It provides comprehensive and accurate views of batch data while using real-time stream processing.
A formal system in mathematical logic for expressing computation based on function abstraction and application. It forms the foundation of functional programming languages and type theory.

An anonymous function defined without a name, commonly used for short, throwaway functions in languages like Python, JavaScript, and Java. Lambda functions enable concise functional-style programming.
A reusable package of code and dependencies that can be shared across multiple AWS Lambda functions. Lambda layers reduce deployment package size and enable code sharing between serverless functions.

A parameter-efficient framework that integrates a Layer-Aware MixturE of LoRA Experts (Lamer) module with a replay strategy to enhance multilingual self-supervised models while preventing knowledge forgetting.

Laminar is a TCP stack that achieves ASIC-class performance and energy efficiency on programmable Reconfigurable Match-Action Table pipelines, introducing techniques like optimistic concurrency and pseudo-segment injection to handle TCP state updates.

A logical clock mechanism for ordering events in a distributed system without relying on synchronized physical clocks. Lamport timestamps assign a counter to each event to establish a partial ordering of events.

Local Area Network — a computer network that interconnects computers within a limited area such as a home, office, or building. LANs use Ethernet or Wi-Fi and provide high-speed, low-latency connectivity.

A correspondence-driven deformable registration framework for laparoscopic liver surgery that learns latent-grounded 2D-3D landmark correspondences to improve the stability and interpretability of intraoperative anatomical guidance.
A component of the LLandMark framework that detects cultural or spatial landmarks to enhance multimodal video retrieval by reformulating complex queries.
An innovative framework that separates algorithm discovery from high-cost evaluations, enhancing the efficiency of automated algorithm design in real-world optimization tasks.

A framework for developing applications powered by large language models that provides tools for prompt management, chains, agents, and memory. It simplifies building complex LLM-powered applications.
A benchmark designed to diagnose and close the language gap in Vision-Language-Action models, emphasizing the need for language understanding through systematic semantic perturbation diagnostics.

A framework built on LangChain for building stateful, multi-agent applications using large language models. LangGraph models agent workflows as graphs with nodes representing actions and edges representing transitions.

A developer platform for debugging, testing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM applications built with LangChain. LangSmith provides tracing, dataset management, and evaluation tools for AI application development.

The Language Extended Lexicon (LEL) is a glossary used to enhance the extraction of user stories from high-fidelity mockups by large language models, facilitating better understanding and generation of functional requirements.

A probabilistic model that assigns probabilities to sequences of words and can generate text. Large language models like GPT and Claude are trained on vast amounts of text data and power modern AI assistants.
A technique proposed to mitigate phoneme hallucinations in low-bitrate neural speech coding by utilizing language models to guide the training process in the absence of ground-truth transcripts.

An open protocol that defines the communication between a code editor and a language server that provides features like auto-completion, go-to-definition, and error checking. LSP enables language support across editors.

A technique for retrieving relevant local segments from large time-series datasets based on natural language queries, utilizing a Conformer-based contrastive retriever trained on segment-caption pairs.
An advanced approach to Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) that revisits the Laplace mechanism to improve its applicability in high-dimensional models.

LAPIS (Lightweight API Specification for Intelligent Systems) is a domain-specific format optimized for large language model consumption, reducing token overhead while preserving essential semantic information for API reasoning.

A PHP web application framework known for its elegant syntax and comprehensive feature set including routing, authentication, ORM, and templating. Laravel follows the MVC pattern and emphasizes developer productivity.

The Large Audio Language Model (LALM) architecture is designed for predicting semantic alignment between audio and text, employing a three-stage training pipeline that includes pretraining with CLAP pseudo-labels.

A single neural network model that produces variational wavefunctions of interacting electrons across the Hamiltonian parameter manifold, utilizing the Fermi Sets architecture to generalize predictions across various coupling strengths and particle numbers.

A neural network trained on vast amounts of text data that can generate, understand, and manipulate human language. LLMs like GPT-4, Claude, and Llama power chatbots, coding assistants, and content generation tools.

L.term: Large-Deviation Theory for Bayesian Neural Networks
L.definition: A theoretical framework that provides explicit variational objectives and rate functions for predictors in Bayesian neural networks, focusing on the complex fluctuations that influence posterior concentration beyond Gaussian-process limits.

LaSER is a self-distillation framework that internalizes explicit reasoning into latent space for dense retrieval, aiming to leverage the reasoning capabilities of large language models more effectively.
The final step of the delivery process where a product moves from a transportation hub to its final destination. In technology, it refers to connecting end users to network infrastructure.

A conflict resolution strategy in distributed systems where the most recent write operation overwrites any concurrent conflicting writes. LWW is simple but can cause data loss in high-concurrency scenarios.
A framework for Vision-Language-Action models in autonomous driving that operates in latent spatio-temporal space, addressing semantic-perceptual conflicts and enhancing reasoning capabilities.

The time delay between a request and its response, typically measured in milliseconds. Low latency is critical for real-time applications, gaming, video calls, and financial trading where even small delays significantly impact user experience.

An online, non-intrusive latency sculpting technique designed to guarantee service level objectives (SLO) during LLM inference, addressing latency spikes without requiring intrusive system modifications.
An interpretable regression-based model that employs vision transformers for masked flow reconstruction by partitioning flow snapshots into patches and applying dimensionality reduction.
A generalization of Latent Dirichlet Allocation that employs a Dirichlet-Tree distribution to model hierarchical relationships among topics, enhancing the expressiveness of topic proportions.
A method for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking that combines camera-based tracking with mask-based multi-object segmentation, enhancing spatiotemporal scene understanding.

An approach in multi-behavior sequential recommendation that captures dynamic user preferences by modeling the underlying decision-making processes, shifting from behavior-fixed to behavior-specific recommendations.
A compressed representation of data learned by a model, where similar data points are mapped to nearby points in the space. Latent spaces are central to autoencoders, GANs, and variational methods.

A conceptual framework that maps equational theories to a latent space based on their statistical behavior, facilitating the exploration of logical implications and relationships among various theories.
A theoretical framework suggesting that pretraining on large datasets encodes human values as directions in representation space, which can be elicited through constitutional prompts.

A re-examination of the empirical Phillips curve model that incorporates latent variables to enhance medium-term inflation forecasting accuracy. This model has been shown to outperform traditional models in predicting inflation six to eight quarters ahead.
A decision-theoretic framework that models human prosociality as a latent state, allowing robots to infer and influence this state through their actions in repeated interactions.

LatentTune is an efficient method for tuning high-dimensional database parameters using latent representation learning, aiming to enhance database performance while addressing the challenges of large training datasets.

The technique used by attackers to progressively move through a network after initial access, searching for sensitive data and high-value assets. Detection of lateral movement is key to preventing data breaches.

A scaling approach that distributes workload across multiple independent systems rather than scaling up a single system. Lateral scaling provides resilience through redundancy and geographic distribution.

A document preparation system widely used in academia and scientific publishing for producing high-quality typeset documents. LaTeX excels at rendering mathematical equations, bibliographies, and complex layouts.

A family of quasi-Monte Carlo methods applied to deep neural networks that utilize lattice rules for high-dimensional integration and function approximation, offering a simple implementation approach.

LatticeHashForest is an efficient data structure designed to manage repetitive data and operations, particularly in whole-program analysis and pointer analysis, minimizing the propagation of duplicate data.

A design guideline for developing software that states each unit should have limited knowledge about other units. Also known as the principle of least knowledge, it promotes loose coupling between components.

A theorem stating that the average of results from a large number of trials converges to the expected value as the number of trials increases. It underpins statistical sampling and Monte Carlo methods.
A normalization technique applied across the features of a single training example rather than across the batch. Layer normalization is preferred in transformer architectures and recurrent networks.

A technique for machine unlearning in large language models that identifies intervention depth as a critical factor for achieving multilingual knowledge erasure without collapsing capabilities in other languages.
A software architecture pattern that organizes components into horizontal layers, each providing services to the layer above it. Common layers include presentation, business logic, and data access.

Layered Modal ML is a metaprogramming language that guarantees strong type safety while allowing the storage and execution of open code, utilizing contextual modal types to manage free variables effectively.
A categorical framework that utilizes string diagrammatic algebra to study scientific theories at varying levels of abstraction, revealing connections to established categorical structures.

A framework that conceptualizes the dynamic and situational vulnerabilities experienced by operators of semi-autonomous vehicles, highlighting the interaction between human oversight and automated systems.

LayerGS is a framework for decomposing 3D human avatars into animatable multi-layered representations, utilizing 2D Gaussian encoding for accurate geometry and photorealistic rendering, while addressing occlusion challenges in traditional methods.

The arrangement of visual elements on a page or screen, including spacing, alignment, and hierarchy. Layout design uses grids, flexbox, and other systems to create organized and responsive interfaces.
An unexpected movement of visible page content that occurs when elements are dynamically resized or inserted. Cumulative Layout Shift is a Core Web Vital metric that measures visual stability.
A reinforcement learning framework that enhances large language models with spatial reasoning capabilities for content-aware graphic layout design, addressing opacity in design decision making.

An evaluation strategy that delays the computation of an expression until its value is needed. Lazy evaluation can improve performance by avoiding unnecessary calculations and supporting infinite data structures.
A design pattern that defers initialization of an object or loading of a resource until it is actually needed. On the web, lazy loading images and components improves initial page load performance.

Lightweight Directory Access Protocol — an open, vendor-neutral protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information services over a network. It is commonly used for authentication and user management.
LEAD-Drift is a framework for real-time intent drift detection in Intent-Based Networking, which enables proactive failure prevention by learning data-driven risk scores.

An algorithm that designates a single node as the leader responsible for coordinating tasks in a distributed system. Leader election protocols like Raft and Paxos ensure consensus in the face of failures.

A pipeline that converts 3D Gaussian Splatting of individual plants into editable, instanced mesh assets, facilitating integration into traditional game production workflows by addressing memory and topology issues.
An open-source memory leak detection library for Android applications. LeakCanary automatically detects memory leaks during development and provides detailed reports of the leak trace.

A novel evaluation framework in Generative Information Retrieval that separates the roles of supervision and evaluation to mitigate preference leakage and enhance the reliability of cultural relevance assessments.

A methodology for maximizing customer value while minimizing waste in product development and business processes. Lean principles emphasize continuous improvement, eliminating non-value-adding activities, and rapid iteration.
A centralized library of formalized computer science concepts and software, providing reusable semantic interfaces and proof automation for formal verification.

A software development methodology adapted from lean manufacturing principles that focuses on eliminating waste, delivering value quickly, and continuous improvement. It emphasizes efficiency and customer value.

A methodology for developing businesses and products that emphasizes rapid iteration, validated learning, and build-measure-learn feedback loops. It encourages testing hypotheses with minimum viable products.
A Lean package that automates the extraction and management of blueprint data, facilitating the integration of informal and formal mathematical components for large-scale formalization projects.
A framework that employs speculative execution to accelerate neural network training by predicting future model weights and validating them before acceptance.

LEAR is a dual-task learning framework designed for event-to-LiDAR localization, which jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to effectively bridge the sensing-modality divide in challenging environments.

A new paradigm in approximate membership testing where a model is trained to distinguish between members and non-members of a set, significantly improving false positive rates compared to traditional filters.
A query optimization approach that utilizes machine learning to improve query execution plans in distributed, multi-tenant data warehouses, addressing dynamic execution environments.
The rate at which a user gains proficiency with a new tool, interface, or system over time. Designing for a gentle learning curve improves adoption and reduces training costs.
A computational challenge critical to the security of post-quantum cryptographic primitives, which involves learning a secret binary vector from noisy linear equations.
A theoretically grounded representation of students' developing understanding, used to guide the generation of AI feedback in educational contexts.

A technique that enhances streaming algorithms by integrating machine learning models to improve space efficiency, particularly in scenarios where data items are weighted unequally, such as in sliding-window models.
An adaptive framework for matching systems that combines immediate and delayed matching strategies based on real-time data collection to improve market efficiency.

A framework that utilizes machine learning to dynamically select the most effective execution plan for filtered Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) queries, optimizing performance based on query characteristics.
A method for managing the execution of AI models in a network-exposed environment, allowing dynamic selection of model instances based on user intent and network conditions.

A security principle that requires giving users and processes only the minimum level of access necessary to perform their tasks. It limits the potential damage from accidents, errors, or unauthorized use.
A cache eviction policy that removes the item that has not been accessed for the longest time when the cache reaches capacity. LRU is one of the most common and effective caching strategies.

A database that provides a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log. AWS QLDB and Azure Confidential Ledger are examples used for audit trails and regulatory compliance.
A lock-free fault-tolerant resource sharing protocol designed for multicore real-time systems, addressing transient faults within critical sections.

An outdated computing system or application that continues to be used because it still serves business needs. Legacy systems pose challenges for maintenance, integration, and security but contain valuable business logic.
A benchmark and evaluation methodology for assessing the performance of legal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, featuring a dataset of 4,876 passages and 100 complex legal questions.

LeGend is a data-driven framework designed for lemma generation in hardware model checking, enhancing the inductive generalization step in formal verification processes by reducing overhead associated with per-clause graph analysis.
An advanced recurrent architecture that provides stable sequence modeling with fewer parameters compared to traditional LSTMs, enhancing performance in tasks such as infant cry classification.
An algorithmic approach to efficiently maintain communities in large dynamic graphs using the Leiden community detection algorithm, reducing the computational cost of updates.

LELA is a training-free framework leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) for hate video localization, utilizing modality-specific captioning to detect and temporally localize hateful content without requiring extensive human annotations.

A natural language processing technique that reduces words to their base or dictionary form called a lemma. Unlike stemming, lemmatization considers the context and part of speech to produce valid words.
A concept that provides guarantees on the ability of learning algorithms to generalize predictions for inputs of varying lengths, specifically related to a class of languages linked to transformers.

A two-stage optimization framework specifically designed for multimodal lens design, which partitions the design space into behavior descriptors to improve convergence and exploration of high-dimensional spaces.
LEO Topology Design refers to methods developed for optimizing network topology in large-scale Low-Earth-Orbit satellite networks, taking into account real-world deployment constraints.
A tool for managing JavaScript projects with multiple packages in a single repository, known as monorepos. Lerna optimizes the workflow around managing multi-package repositories with git and npm.

A project structure managed by Lerna where multiple related JavaScript packages coexist in a single repository. This approach simplifies dependency management and enables atomic cross-package changes.
A monorepo management tool that optimizes the workflow of managing multi-package JavaScript projects. Lerna handles versioning, publishing, and dependency linking across packages in a monorepo.
A CSS pre-processor that extends CSS with features like variables, mixins, functions, and nested rules. Less compiles into standard CSS and helps organize and maintain large stylesheets.
A free, automated, and open certificate authority that provides TLS certificates to enable HTTPS on websites. It has dramatically increased the adoption of encrypted web traffic.

Latent Ensemble Variational Data Assimilation (LEVDA) is an ensemble-space variational smoother designed to improve long-range geophysical forecasts by operating in a low-dimensional latent space of a pretrained differentiable neural network.

A technique in 3D graphics that reduces the complexity of rendered objects as they move farther from the camera. LOD improves rendering performance while maintaining visual quality where it matters most.

A string metric that measures the minimum number of single-character edits — insertions, deletions, or substitutions — required to change one word into another. It is used in spell checkers and fuzzy matching.
A method that enhances traditional economic growth models by incorporating Lévy noise to account for non-Gaussian stochastic perturbations affecting capital stock and output.
A model that describes liquid-asset returns exhibiting Levy-stable scaling, allowing for the derivation of horizon-correct formulas for various risk metrics like Value-at-Risk and Sharpe ratios.
An agentic framework for structured event timeline extraction from legal documents, employing a dual-agent architecture to identify and score candidate events in judicial outcomes.

A program that performs lexical analysis by converting a sequence of characters into a sequence of tokens. The lexer is the first stage of a compiler or interpreter, categorizing input into meaningful symbols.

The process of converting a sequence of characters into a sequence of tokens during compilation. The lexer or tokenizer is the first phase of a compiler that breaks source code into meaningful symbols.
A variable scoping mechanism employed in R that resolves variable references based on the structure in which functions are defined, rather than at runtime.
A large-scale dataset for long-form question answering that includes 1.3 million human pairwise preference annotations, aimed at improving evaluation metrics for LFQA systems.
An acronym for Looks Good To Me used in code reviews to indicate approval of proposed changes. LGTM signifies that the reviewer has examined the code and finds it acceptable for merging.

LiaisonAgent is an autonomous multi-agent framework that enhances risk investigation and governance in Security Operations Centers by integrating specialized agents to bridge technical risk detection with business-level governance.
A collection of pre-written code that developers can use to perform common tasks, avoiding the need to write code from scratch. Libraries provide reusable functions, classes, and modules.

A legal instrument governing the use, redistribution, and modification of software. Common software licenses include MIT, Apache 2.0, GPL, and proprietary licenses, each with different rights and obligations.
A Lightweight Complex Question Answering system that operates unsupervised to address complex questions across multiple documents without the need for extensive training data.

Light Detection and Ranging — a remote sensing technology that measures distances by illuminating a target with laser light and analyzing the reflected light. It is used in autonomous vehicles and mapping.
A set of 3D data points generated by a LIDAR sensor that captures the spatial coordinates of surfaces and objects. Point clouds are used in autonomous vehicles, surveying, and geospatial mapping.

The series of stages a software component goes through from creation to destruction. In frameworks like React, Vue, and Android, lifecycle methods allow developers to execute code at specific stages such as initialization, rendering, updating, and cleanup.
A callback function that allows developers to execute code at specific stages of a component's lifecycle in frameworks like Angular, React, and Vue. Common hooks include mount, update, and destroy.
A method that is automatically called at specific stages of a component's existence in frameworks like React and Vue. Lifecycle methods handle mounting, updating, unmounting, and error handling.

A framework that synthesizes AI governance and cloud security standards into a lifecycle-staged threat taxonomy, addressing vulnerabilities in hybrid AI-cloud deployments that could lead to cross-layer attacks.

A migration strategy that involves moving an application and its associated data to the cloud with minimal or no changes. While quick to implement, it may not fully leverage cloud-native capabilities.
A metric in predictive modeling that measures how much better a model performs compared to random selection. A lift of 2.0 means the model is twice as effective as random chance.
A paradigm for 3D avatar animation that completes sparse monocular observations in kinematic space, utilizing a video diffusion transformer to synthesize high-fidelity expression sequences.
An open-source automated tool by Google for improving the quality of web pages. It audits performance, accessibility, progressive web apps, SEO, and provides actionable recommendations.
Large language models designed to generate secure scripting languages with minimal resource requirements, enhancing automation while maintaining security.
An architectural enhancement for Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs) that reduces CPU overhead by implementing a lightweight caching mechanism to accelerate data retrieval and processing.

A thread managed by the runtime environment rather than the operating system, offering lower overhead and higher scalability. Green threads, goroutines, and virtual threads are examples of lightweight threads.
An automated evolutionary method designed to enhance the accuracy and robustness of Skull-Face Overlay (SFO) in craniofacial superimposition by aligning 3D skull models with 2D facial images.

The branch of mathematics dealing with vectors, matrices, and linear transformations. It is fundamental to machine learning, computer graphics, and data science for representing and manipulating data.
A class of models that utilize linear attention mechanisms, providing a theoretical framework for analyzing the effects of fine-tuning on in-context learning performance.
An oracle used in constrained convex optimization that provides solutions for linear constraints, facilitating the development of algorithms such as Frank-Wolfe methods.

A collision resolution technique in hash tables where, upon collision, the algorithm checks the next sequential slot until an empty one is found. Linear probing is simple but can suffer from clustering.

A statistical method that models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables using a straight line. It is one of the most basic and widely used predictive analysis techniques.

A diagonalization-based optimization technique for Linear Echo State Networks that reduces computational complexity of reservoir state updates from O(N^2) to O(N) by reformulating dynamics in the eigenbasis of the recurrent matrix.
Models trained to reconstruct linear spectrograms from neural representations of vocalized, mimed, and imagined speech, demonstrating generalization across different speech conditions.

An expressive extension of classical temporal logic that allows for the specification of non-Markovian rewards in Markov Decision Processes, utilizing first-order predicates instead of simple Boolean variables. This framework promotes complex task specification over unstructured data domains.
A formulation of mean-field games that incorporates rough common noise, characterized by a Volterra-type formulation for optimal control and state characterization.

A large-scale multi-agent benchmark for evaluating LLMs in psychiatric consultation and diagnosis, providing realistic patient simulations and clinician-verified diagnostic labels for dynamic multi-turn consultations.
A technique for combining multiple network connections in parallel to increase throughput beyond what a single connection could sustain. It also provides redundancy in case one link fails.

A compiler optimization technique that performs interprocedural analysis and optimization across all translation units at link time. LTO enables optimizations that are impossible within a single source file.
A linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, each pointing to the next node. Unlike arrays, linked lists allow efficient insertion and deletion but do not support random access.
A program that combines object files generated by a compiler into a single executable program. The linker resolves symbol references between modules and handles library dependencies.

LinkVLA is a novel architecture designed to enhance alignment and efficiency in Vision-Language-Action models for autonomous driving by establishing a structural link between language instructions and action outputs.
A specific code quality check defined in a linter's configuration that flags patterns considered errors or bad practices. Teams customize lint rules to enforce consistent coding standards.
A static code analysis tool that flags programming errors, bugs, stylistic errors, and suspicious constructs. Popular linters include ESLint for JavaScript, Pylint for Python, and RuboCop for Ruby.

The automated analysis of source code to flag programming errors, bugs, stylistic inconsistencies, and suspicious constructs. Linters like ESLint, Pylint, and RuboCop enforce coding standards and catch issues before code reaches production.

LintMe is a design probe that utilizes a lightweight domain-specific language (DSL) to create context-specific checks for improving README files, addressing both style and content issues while maintaining authorial agency.

An open-source, Unix-like operating system kernel that powers the majority of servers, cloud infrastructure, and Android devices worldwide. Linux distributions like Ubuntu, Fedora, and Arch provide complete operating systems for diverse computing needs.

An operating-system-level virtualization method that allows running multiple isolated Linux systems on a single host. LXC containers share the host kernel but have isolated process and network spaces.
A complete operating system built on the Linux kernel that includes system utilities, libraries, and application software. Popular distributions include Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, and Arch Linux.
A comprehensive 882-hour multi-speaker Bengali dataset designed to enhance Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and speaker diarization for long-duration audio.
A data optimization technique in data lakehouse platforms that automatically organizes data for optimal query performance without requiring manual partitioning or file compaction.
A type of neural network that incorporates learnable time constants to adaptively model temporal dynamics, particularly applied in emotion recognition from physiological signals like EEG.
A template language created by Shopify for rendering dynamic content in web pages. Liquid is safe, stateless, and widely used in e-commerce platforms and static site generators like Jekyll.
LiSFC is a lifelong planner for optimizing service function chaining (SFC) in non-stationary networks, utilizing Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) statistics to adapt to changing network configurations.

The L in SOLID — a principle stating that objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without affecting the correctness of the program. It ensures proper inheritance hierarchies.
A type that represents a specific, exact value rather than a general type category. In TypeScript, literal types allow variables to hold only specific string, number, or boolean values.

The process of moving a running virtual machine or container from one physical host to another without downtime. Live migration enables hardware maintenance and load balancing without service interruption.
A development feature that automatically refreshes the browser or application when source code files are modified. It speeds up the development feedback loop by eliminating manual refresh cycles.

A comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate agentic systems across 104 real-world challenges, constructed using the Social Perception-Driven Data Generation method to ensure relevance to actual user tasks.
LiveCultureBench is a multi-agent, multi-cultural benchmark for evaluating large language models in dynamic social simulations, focusing on task completion and adherence to socio-cultural norms.
An active-structure neural re-ranking framework designed for exercise recommendation, utilizing graph-based representation to enhance personalized educational content delivery.

The practice of managing and updating a live software product or service after launch, including content updates, events, and real-time monitoring. It is especially common in game development and SaaS.
A family of open-source large language models developed by Meta AI. Llama models are available for research and commercial use, enabling widespread experimentation with and deployment of LLMs.
A framework that integrates large language models into an evolutionary loop to generate and benchmark continuous optimization problems with controllable high-level properties.

LLaMEA-SAGE is a mechanism for guiding automated algorithm design using structural feedback from explainable AI, enhancing the exploration of algorithm design spaces by utilizing graph-theoretic features.

The process of further training a pre-trained large language model on a specific dataset to adapt it for a particular task or domain. Fine-tuning requires less data and compute than training from scratch.

A phenomenon where a large language model generates text that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect or fabricated. Hallucinations are a significant challenge in deploying LLMs for production applications.

Theoretical and empirical mechanisms identified that affect the diversity of ideas generated by large language models, highlighting fixation at the individual level and aggregation at the collective level.
A reference framework aimed at accurately measuring the carbon footprint of large language model inference at the prompt level, facilitating sustainability-focused decision-making.
A system that utilizes multiple large language models (LLMs) to collaboratively review and validate code generated from natural language queries, enhancing the reliability of automated coding tools.
A concept evaluating the performance enhancement for novice users utilizing large language models (LLMs) compared to traditional internet resources in biosecurity-related tasks.

A paradigm of Automated Algorithm Design that utilizes Large Language Models to autonomously generate code implementations of algorithms, emphasizing the importance of assessing algorithmic similarity beyond mere syntactic variation.

L.term: LLM-Assisted Multimodal Emotion Data Annotation Toolkit
L.definition: A toolkit that facilitates the annotation of multimodal emotion data by preprocessing and aligning heterogeneous recordings, visualizing them on an interactive timeline, and detecting candidate events for annotation.

An approach that utilizes large language models to detect user experience (UX) smells in Integrated Development Environments by analyzing user-reported issues and classifying them based on a validated taxonomy.
This system leverages Large Language Models to automate the remediation of accessibility issues in web applications, particularly focusing on the challenges posed by dynamic Single-Page Applications.
A framework that reformulates channel state information compression as a masked token prediction task, leveraging large language models for efficient feedback in massive MIMO systems.
A system that uses a Large Language Model to autonomously manage cooperative edge caching decisions across multiple base stations, enhancing content replacement strategies.

A systematic evaluation of four architectures for automated patching using large language models: fixed workflow, single-agent system, multi-agent system, and general-purpose code agents, focusing on their effectiveness in patch correctness and failure modes.
The use of large language models (LLMs) as proxies for human judges in evaluating relevance, exploring their reliability and potential overrating behavior across various evaluation paradigms.

LLM-Derived Control Policies leverage Large Language Models as intelligent mutation operators in an iterative framework for optimizing residential electric vehicle energy management, improving interpretability in vehicle-to-grid systems.

A novel approach that utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to facilitate the hardware-software co-design process for post-quantum cryptography, focusing on optimizing the implementation of cryptographic algorithms like FALCON.
A comprehensive taxonomy categorizing the integration of large language models in compiler development based on design philosophy, methodology, code abstraction level, and task type.

A natural-language-guided scenario generation framework that allows users to generate diverse and controllable power system scenarios by leveraging large language models and frequency-aware flow diffusion techniques.
Components that integrate large language models into relational data processing tasks, enhancing operations like semantic filtering and entity matching while preserving a relational interface.

LLM-FSM is a benchmark designed to evaluate the ability of large language models to recover finite-state machine behavior from natural-language specifications and translate it into register transfer-level implementations.
A method that augments app store ranking systems by integrating large language model-generated relevance judgments, addressing the scarcity of expert-provided textual relevance labels.
A multi-agent architecture that facilitates natural interaction between human planners and AI systems by providing explanations that enhance understanding and trust in automated planning solutions.

The LLM-MLFFN (Multi-Level Autonomous Driving Behavior Feature Fusion via Large Language Model) is a framework that enhances the classification of autonomous vehicle driving behaviors by integrating features from large language models with multi-dimensional driving data.
An interactive tool embedded in virtual reality applications that utilizes large language models to assist users in understanding and navigating privacy policies during app selection.
A formal framework for modeling conversations with large language models (LLMs), focusing on the flow of information and the interactions between AI agents and human prompts.

LLMServingSim 2.0 is a unified simulator designed for heterogeneous and disaggregated large language model serving infrastructures, focusing on the interaction between diverse hardware and software components to enhance scalability and efficiency.

LLMTailor is a tool that optimizes the checkpointing process for large language models by selectively saving only the layers that experience significant updates, thereby reducing storage overhead and resource contention.
An algorithm that modifies n-dimensional Poisson disc sampling for parallel execution in meshless numerical methods, optimizing node generation for adaptive analysis in complex geometries.

A device or service that distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server bears too much load. Load balancers improve application availability, reliability, and performance through intelligent traffic distribution.

The process of distributing network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed. Load balancers improve application availability, reliability, and performance through various distribution algorithms.
The ratio of the number of stored entries to the total number of slots in a hash table. When the load factor exceeds a threshold, the hash table is resized to maintain performance.
A technique where a system intentionally drops some requests during periods of extreme load to maintain performance for remaining requests. It prevents cascading failures in distributed systems.
A type of performance testing that evaluates how a system behaves under expected and peak load conditions. It identifies performance bottlenecks before they affect end users in production.

A model-native local conformal prediction method for gradient-boosted trees that enhances uncertainty quantification by adapting to heteroscedasticity without the need for auxiliary models or excessive data splits.
A technique for normalizing scores in anomalous sound detection by adapting neighborhood size based on locality preservation, enhancing detection accuracy across varying data densities.

L.term: Local Differential Privacy (LDP) in Molecular Communication
L.definition: LDP is integrated into molecular communication networks to ensure privacy during data exchange, allowing for secure aggregate data analysis in sensitive applications like health monitoring.
A problem that involves enumerating satisfying assignments of a k-CNF formula with specific Hamming weight constraints, leading to new algorithms for k-SAT.

A discussion on the computability of algorithms within the LOCAL model, emphasizing the importance of whether nodes update their state using arbitrary or computable functions in locally checkable labeling problems.
LNDP is a model for achieving differential privacy in graphs, where each node releases outputs based on its local edge list, allowing for accurate aggregation of queries while maintaining privacy.

L.term: Local Second-Order Limit Dynamics for ADMM
L.definition: This framework analyzes the local second-order dynamics of the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) for semidefinite programming, particularly in regions of slow convergence.

A web browser feature that allows websites to store key-value pairs persistently on the user's device. Unlike cookies, local storage data is not sent with HTTP requests and has a larger storage capacity.
The tendency of a processor to access the same set of memory locations repetitively over a short period. Temporal and spatial locality are exploited by caching systems to improve performance.
The process of adapting software for a specific region or language, including translating text, adjusting date and number formats, and handling right-to-left scripts. Often abbreviated as l10n.
A method for controlling time-dependent acoustic and electromagnetic waves in a bounded domain, allowing for strong localization of wave energy in specified regions.
A class of codes that allows for efficient local list decoding with high rate and error tolerance, achieving near-optimal performance in the context of hardness amplification.
A method for detecting distributional changes in time series data while preserving local differential privacy, utilizing randomized response and binary mechanisms for privacy guarantees.

Technologies and APIs that determine the geographic position of a device using GPS, cell towers, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth signals. Location services power mapping, navigation, and location-based applications.
A synchronization mechanism for enforcing limits on access to a shared resource in a concurrent environment. Locks prevent race conditions but can lead to deadlocks if not managed carefully.

A file that records the exact versions of all dependencies installed in a project, ensuring consistent installations across environments. Examples include package-lock.json, yarn.lock, and Gemfile.lock.

A concurrent programming approach where threads make progress without using mutual exclusion locks. Lock-free algorithms use atomic operations like compare-and-swap to ensure thread safety without blocking.
A file that records the exact versions of all dependencies installed in a project, ensuring reproducible builds. Examples include package-lock.json for npm and Pipfile.lock for Python.
A distribution-free loss-quantile score that provides a per-input reliability score for predictions, allowing for better risk-aware decision-making in machine learning models.

The process of collecting, normalizing, and consolidating log data from multiple sources into a centralized location. Tools like the ELK Stack, Splunk, and Datadog facilitate log aggregation at scale.

The practice of collecting, storing, analyzing, and retaining log data generated by applications and infrastructure. Log management tools help with debugging, compliance, security analysis, and operational monitoring.

A data structure used in databases that provides high write throughput by sequentially writing data to append-only logs before merging into sorted structures. It powers databases like RocksDB and Cassandra.

A widely used Java-based logging framework that provides flexible and configurable logging capabilities. The Log4Shell vulnerability in December 2021 highlighted the critical importance of supply chain security.
