A creational design pattern that maintains a set of initialized objects ready for use rather than creating and destroying them on demand. Object pools improve performance for expensive object creation.
A technique that lets you query and manipulate data from a database using an object-oriented paradigm. ORM libraries like Hibernate, SQLAlchemy, and ActiveRecord map database tables to classes.

A storage architecture that manages data as objects rather than file hierarchies or block addresses. Each object includes the data, metadata, and a unique identifier, making it ideal for unstructured data at scale.

A programming paradigm based on the concept of objects that contain data (attributes) and code (methods). OOP principles — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction — promote code organization, reuse, and maintainability.
A mathematical function that a machine learning algorithm seeks to minimize or maximize during training. The objective function, also called a loss or cost function, quantifies model performance.

The ability to understand the internal state of a system by examining its external outputs — metrics, logs, and traces. Observability goes beyond monitoring to help teams debug complex distributed systems and understand why problems occur.
A data source that emits values over time and can be subscribed to by observers. Observables are the foundation of reactive programming libraries like RxJS and are used extensively in Angular.

A design pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents that are automatically notified of state changes. The observable pattern is widely used in reactive programming and event-driven systems.
A behavioral design pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them automatically of any state changes. It is widely used in event-driven systems and GUI frameworks.

A unified mathematical structure that allows autonomous agents to reason about truths from multiple perspectives, integrating the reasoning of different agents in various contexts to improve Theory of Mind capabilities.
A type of probabilistic loop invariant used in programming to mathematically infer a program's output distribution from its initial distribution, particularly in the context of loops.
A decentralized data exchange protocol that enables sharing and monetization of data while preserving privacy. Ocean Protocol uses blockchain technology to create data marketplaces.
A high-performance, scalable cloud-native shared storage architecture that utilizes an LSM-tree design to efficiently handle high-concurrency read/write scenarios in multi-cloud environments.
An innovative OLAP system designed for petabyte-scale data that supports real-time transaction processing and advanced analytical capabilities.

Open Container Initiative — an open governance structure for creating industry standards around container formats and runtimes. OCI specifications ensure container portability across different platforms.

Optical Character Recognition — technology that converts different types of documents such as scanned paper documents, PDFs, or images into editable and searchable data by recognizing text characters.
An end-to-end model for stress signaling in plants using Green Leaf Volatiles, demonstrating how biochemical networks can interpret chemical signals for defensive responses.

A framework for real-time expression control of photorealistic Gaussian head avatars in VR, utilizing blendshape signals from VR headsets to recover occluded facial expressions without additional sensors.
A logic error where a loop iterates one time too many or too few, or an index is shifted by one from the correct value. Off-by-one errors are among the most common programming mistakes.
A design approach where applications are built to work without an internet connection, syncing data when connectivity is restored. Offline-first architecture uses service workers and local storage.
A value used to specify a position within a data structure or result set. In database queries, offset is used with limit for pagination to skip a specified number of rows.

Objectives and Key Results — a goal-setting framework that defines clear objectives and measurable key results to track progress. Popularized by Google, OKRs align team efforts, promote transparency, and focus organizations on outcomes rather than outputs.
A cloud-based identity and access management platform that provides single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and lifecycle management for applications and users.

Online Analytical Processing — a category of software that allows users to analyze information from multiple database systems simultaneously. OLAP tools enable complex analytical queries with fast execution times.
A multidimensional data structure optimized for fast analysis along multiple dimensions. OLAP cubes pre-aggregate data to enable rapid slicing, dicing, and pivoting in business intelligence queries.

The OLAP Intent Signature is a unified key space that canonicalizes SQL and natural language queries for analytical workloads, enabling better reuse of cached entries by capturing intent across various BI tools.

Online Transaction Processing — a class of systems that manages transaction-oriented applications, typically for data entry and retrieval. OLTP systems are optimized for fast query processing and maintaining data integrity.
A mathematical notation that describes the lower bound of an algorithm's growth rate. While Big O describes the worst case, Omega notation describes the best-case time complexity.
The first feed-forward framework capable of generating generalizable, 360°-complete, and animatable 3D Gaussian avatars from a single image, addressing the challenges of avatar generation.
A joint audio-video generation model that synchronously customizes both video identity and audio timbre based on reference images and audio, enhancing multimedia content creation.

OmniGAIA is a benchmark for evaluating omni-modal AI agents, designed to assess their capabilities in deep reasoning and multi-turn tool execution across diverse modalities including video, audio, and images.

OmniLottie is a framework that generates high-quality vector animations from multi-modal instructions, focusing on the Lottie format. It includes a tokenizer that transforms Lottie JSON files into structured sequences for better control over animation generation.

A multimodal retrieval model capable of processing complex queries that span text, vision, and audio, advancing beyond traditional models limited to two modalities and enhancing universal retrieval capabilities.
A motion tracking system that leverages physics-consistent references to mitigate artifacts in humanoid robot motion, enhancing stability and generalizability during training and execution.

OmniTune is a framework for refining arbitrary SQL queries using LLM-based optimization by prompting (OPRO), employing a two-step scheme to explore refinement subspaces and sample candidates effectively.
A scalable framework designed to overcome the generality barrier in high-dynamic humanoid control by addressing learning bottlenecks and physical executability constraints in motion tracking.
A unified and lightweight lossless compressor designed for multi-modal data, addressing the challenges of varying formats and statistics in data types.

A rotation where engineers are designated to respond to production incidents outside normal working hours. On-call responsibilities include monitoring alerts, diagnosing issues, and restoring service availability.

This analytical framework examines both on-chain and off-chain factors that influence cryptocurrency prices, particularly Bitcoin. It utilizes an Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) model to assess the differential impacts of these factors on market dynamics.

O.term: On-Cursor Visual Context for Low-Vision Chart Accessibility
O.definition: An interaction method that enhances chart accessibility for low-vision individuals by providing critical contextual elements through pointer-based interactions, improving data exploration.

A novel approach for personalized keyword spotting that combines on-device training with online structured channel pruning to adapt models to user-specific and environmental changes while maintaining efficiency.

Computing infrastructure that is physically located within an organization's facilities, as opposed to being hosted in the cloud. On-premises deployments give organizations full control over hardware and data but require significant capital investment and maintenance.
On-device Channel Pruning technique that combines weight adaptation with architectural adaptation for efficient personalized keyword spotting, enabling real-time user-specific model adjustments.
OSMF is a unified framework that aligns diverse group-wise click preferences in large-scale advertising image generation, optimizing for specific user group preferences.
A decision problem involving the allocation of tasks among one fast machine and many slow machines, focusing on minimizing overhead in online algorithms.
A representation method that converts categorical variables into binary vectors where only one element is active. It is commonly used in machine learning to handle categorical features.
A novel theorem in quantum information theory that provides a bound on decoupling error using quantum relative entropy distance and sandwiched Rényi conditional entropies.
A federated learning framework designed to handle incremental data in a communication-efficient manner while mitigating the challenges of catastrophic forgetting.
A password that is valid for only one login session or transaction, generated by a time-based or counter-based algorithm. OTPs provide an additional factor of authentication beyond static passwords.

One-Time Programs (OTPs) are cryptographic constructs that allow a user to evaluate a program on a single input without revealing any additional information, addressing security challenges posed by gentle-measurement attacks in quantum settings.
A relationship type in database design where a single record in one table can be associated with multiple records in another table. It is the most common relationship type in relational databases.
An optimization technique for key-value cache management in multi-tenant large language model serving that remaps memory to avoid costly KV cache swapping.
A unified model for advertising recommendation that integrates generation and ranking processes to address misalignment between user interests and business value.

A software architecture pattern that places the domain model at the center with layers of application logic around it. Dependencies always point inward, promoting separation of concerns and testability.
A technique for anonymous communication over a network where messages are encrypted in layers, each peeled by successive relay nodes. Tor is the most widely known implementation of onion routing.
A new model for online decision-making that separates predictive and algorithmic components, analyzing algorithms through request-answer games with potentially unreliable guidance.

A category of software tools that enable analysis of data stored in databases in real time for complex queries and multidimensional analysis. OLAP provides fast query response times for business intelligence.
An online algorithmic problem focused on maintaining a dynamic partition of nodes into two clusters based on incoming requests, modeling resource allocation in data centers with migration costs.

O.term: Online Convex Optimization for SINR Estimation
O.definition: This framework applies online convex optimization techniques to estimate the Signal-to-Interference-Plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) using limited feedback mechanisms, enhancing resource allocation in wireless systems.

O.term: Online Job Scheduler for Quantum Multiprogramming
O.definition: An online scheduling algorithm designed for fault-tolerant quantum computing systems, capable of dynamically handling multiple quantum jobs while ensuring efficient resource utilization.

A machine learning approach where the model is updated incrementally as new data arrives rather than being retrained on the entire dataset. Online learning is suited for streaming data and evolving distributions.
An optimization framework that adapts the choice of mirror maps to improve performance in online convex optimization, particularly useful for constrained sets and various loss functions.
Online registers are learnable tokens used in dual-mode self-supervised speech models to mitigate attention mismatches by acting as placeholders for unseen future frames, enhancing context capture.
An algorithm that estimates the speed of sound in real-time during multichannel audio playback using a single observation microphone, enhancing spatial audio control.

OnlineX is a feed-forward framework for real-time 3D scene reconstruction that integrates visual appearance and language fields using streaming images, overcoming the limitations of offline reconstruction methods.
A formal representation of knowledge as a set of concepts within a domain and the relationships between those concepts. In AI, ontologies are used for knowledge representation and semantic reasoning.

An out-of-distribution benchmark for evaluating the performance of Large Vision-Language Models, focusing on the challenges posed by real-world data that may not be independent and identically distributed.
Object-Oriented Programming — a paradigm that organizes software design around data, or objects, rather than functions and logic. OOP languages include Java, Python, C++, and C#.

Open Policy Agent — a general-purpose policy engine that enables unified policy enforcement across the stack. OPA uses a declarative language called Rego to define policies for Kubernetes, APIs, and microservices.
A Pythonic reinterpretation of the PIMPL idiom, allowing for modularity and encapsulation by delegating behavior to a separate implementation while maintaining a stable public API.
A data type whose concrete implementation is hidden from the user, exposing only its interface. Opaque types enforce encapsulation by preventing external code from depending on internal structure.

The OPC UA PubSub model is a communication mechanism that enables efficient interoperability among heterogeneous devices in industrial automation systems, facilitating resource sharing and communication configuration.
The portion of a machine language instruction that specifies the operation to be performed. Opcodes are fundamental to how processors execute programs at the hardware level.

A specification for building and documenting RESTful APIs that provides a standard, language-agnostic interface description. Formerly known as Swagger, it enables both humans and machines to understand API capabilities.

A proposed system that circumvents the Fischer–Lynch–Paterson (FLP) impossibility result by rejecting asynchronous system models, allowing for deterministic consensus protocols even in the presence of faulty processes.

An engineering approach that modifies the traditional CAP theorem framework by introducing bounded-time bilateral reliability in message delivery, allowing for improved consistency and availability in distributed systems.
A class of audio language models that apply next-token prediction to audio data, integrating semantic content, acoustic details, and text for enhanced audio generation and cross-modal capabilities.
Platforms that facilitate the creation and distribution of generative AI models, raising concerns about the production of harmful content due to varying levels of openness and transparency.
A web security vulnerability that occurs when a web application redirects users to an external URL specified by user input without proper validation. Attackers exploit it for phishing attacks.

Software whose source code is freely available for anyone to view, modify, and distribute. Open source powers the majority of the internet's infrastructure, from Linux and Kubernetes to React and TensorFlow, fostering collaboration and innovation.

A publicly available specification that is developed and maintained through a collaborative process and is freely implementable. Open standards like HTTP, HTML, and JSON promote interoperability and prevent vendor lock-in.

An open-source observability framework for cloud-native software that provides a collection of tools, APIs, and SDKs for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data such as traces, metrics, and logs.

A benchmarking framework for evaluating diffusion-based motion planners in closed-loop autonomous driving, addressing communication latency and real-time scheduling constraints through modular decomposition.

A variant of network protocol fingerprinting that operates under the assumption that not all protocol models are known in advance, addressing the limitations of traditional closed-world approaches and reducing misclassifications.

An artificial intelligence research organization that develops and deploys AI models including GPT and DALL-E. OpenAI provides APIs that enable developers to integrate AI capabilities into applications.

A specification for describing RESTful APIs in a machine-readable format (formerly known as Swagger). OpenAPI definitions enable automatic documentation generation, client SDK creation, testing, and API gateway configuration.
A tool that automatically generates client SDKs, server stubs, and documentation from OpenAPI specifications. It supports over 40 programming languages and frameworks, accelerating API integration.

An open-source automated machine learning library designed for natural language understanding tasks, featuring data-aware training regime selection, integrated data quality diagnostics, and configurable out-of-distribution detection.
An open-source computer vision and machine learning software library that provides a common infrastructure for computer vision applications. It includes over 2500 optimized algorithms.

A hybrid evaluation framework for Compute Express Link (CXL) SSDs that combines real-device guidance with simulation to assess performance in the absence of native hardware support for CXL.mem protocol.
A cross-language, cross-platform API for rendering 2D and 3D vector graphics. It is widely used in CAD, virtual reality, scientific visualization, and video games.
An identity layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol that allows clients to verify the identity of end-users and obtain basic profile information. It is widely used for single sign-on.
The largest multimodal dataset for human action recognition in industrial environments, utilizing data from wearables and cameras to monitor worker activities and improve safety and efficiency.
A standardized, open-access repository that aggregates AI models for radiology, enhancing discoverability and reproducibility by providing details on pretrained weights and applications.

OpenSage is an agent development kit that enables large language models to automatically create agents with self-generated topology and toolsets, enhancing the generalizability and performance of these agents.
An open-source search and analytics suite derived from Elasticsearch. It provides full-text search, log analytics, and visualization capabilities through its Dashboards interface.

Red Hat's enterprise Kubernetes platform that provides a consistent application platform for managing hybrid cloud and multicloud deployments. It adds developer and operations-centric tools on top of Kubernetes.
A widely used open-source software library for applications that secure communications over computer networks. It implements the SSL and TLS protocols and provides cryptographic functions.
A free and open-source cloud computing infrastructure software project that provides IaaS. It controls large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources managed through APIs or a dashboard.
A vendor-agnostic component that receives, processes, and exports telemetry data including traces, metrics, and logs. The collector decouples data generation from backend-specific export formats.
A fork of Terraform created after HashiCorp changed Terraform's license. Now known as OpenTofu, it is a community-driven open-source infrastructure as code tool under the Linux Foundation.
A value or variable upon which an operation is performed by an operator in a programming expression. Operands can be constants, variables, function calls, or sub-expressions.
System software that manages computer hardware and software resources and provides common services for computer programs. Examples include Linux, Windows, and macOS.

Operation-R1 is a framework that generates table manipulation pipelines for Table Question Answering using lightweight large language models, trained via a novel reinforcement learning approach with verifiable rewards to reduce latency and computational costs.
A metric introduced to evaluate the efficiency of AI agent inference workloads, focusing on the balance between computation and memory usage, particularly in high-speed interconnect scenarios.

O.term: Operational Modal Analysis via Tangential Interpolation
O.definition: A method for operational modal analysis that employs tangential interpolation techniques to enhance the identification of modal parameters in large structural systems, improving efficiency and accuracy.
A symbol or keyword that performs an operation on one or more operands, such as arithmetic operators (+, -, *), comparison operators (==, <), logical operators (&&, ||), and assignment operators (=).
A feature that allows operators like +, -, and == to be redefined for user-defined types. It enables intuitive syntax for custom classes in languages like C++, Python, and Kotlin.

A Kubernetes design pattern that uses custom controllers to encode operational knowledge for managing complex stateful applications. Operators automate deployment, scaling, and recovery of applications.
A centralized directory of Kubernetes operators that extends the Kubernetes API to manage complex applications. It provides a standard way to discover, install, and manage operators on clusters.
Operating Expenditure — ongoing costs for running a business or system, as opposed to capital expenditure. Cloud computing shifts IT spending from CapEx to OpEx through pay-as-you-go models.

A method that enables large language models to adapt their strategies in real-time during repeated negotiations with dynamic opponents, enhancing their decision-making capabilities beyond offline training.
Short for Operations — the discipline responsible for managing and maintaining IT infrastructure, ensuring system availability, and supporting production environments.
A design pattern requiring users to explicitly choose to participate in a feature, service, or data collection. Opt-in respects user autonomy and is required by many privacy regulations like GDPR.
Technology that converts images of typed, handwritten, or printed text into machine-readable text data. Modern OCR systems use deep learning to achieve high accuracy across fonts and languages.

A computing approach that uses photons (light) instead of electrons for data processing. Optical computing promises higher bandwidth, lower power consumption, and faster speeds for specific computational tasks.
A flexible, transparent fiber made of glass or plastic that transmits data as pulses of light. Fiber optic cables provide high bandwidth and long-distance data transmission with minimal signal loss.

OptiLeak is a reinforcement learning-enhanced framework that improves the efficiency of prompt reconstruction in multi-tenant LLM services, addressing side-channel vulnerabilities associated with shared Key-Value caches.

O.term: Optimal Control of Adversarial Swarm Engagements
O.definition: A modeling framework for controlling large-scale autonomous systems in adversarial environments, accounting for agent attrition and spatial dynamics during interactions.

This approach addresses the NP-hard problem of optimizing integer matrices for Integer-Forcing precoding by revealing a geometric structure in the solution space, allowing for polynomial-time algorithms.
An efficient algorithm designed for partitioning the edges of subcubic and almost-subcubic graphs into the minimum number of paths, solving a problem previously considered NP-hard.

O.term: Optimal Program Synthesis via Abstract Interpretation
O.definition: A framework for synthesizing programs with numerical constants that optimize quantitative objectives, utilizing A* search and abstract interpretation to enhance scalability and ensure optimality guarantees.

A novel approach to over-the-air computation (OAC) that studies optimal quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) constellations under heavy-tailed channel noise, improving computation performance in distributed networks.
This technique employs a Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) solver to achieve optimal software pipelining, enhancing instruction-level parallelism in loops beyond traditional heuristic methods.

O.term: Optimal Transport for Multilingual News Alignment
O.definition: A method that aligns sentences in multilingual news articles using optimal transport techniques, enhancing the semantic similarity and interpretability of financial news data.

O.term: Optimal Variable Order in Autoregressive Ising Models
O.definition: A method for optimizing variable orderings in autoregressive Ising models by learning the underlying Markov random field structure, which enhances sampling efficiency.

Optimal-Horizon MPC is a navigation strategy for social robots that adapts to dynamic crowds by addressing the limitations of fixed prediction horizons and treating pedestrians as heterogeneous agents rather than homogeneous obstacles.
A concurrency control method that allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources, checking for conflicts only at commit time. It works well when conflicts are rare.
A concurrency control strategy that allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources, checking for conflicts only at commit time. It works well when collisions are infrequent.

A user interface pattern that immediately reflects changes as if they succeeded before receiving server confirmation. It provides a snappier feel but requires rollback handling if the server rejects the change.
The process of modifying a system to make it work more efficiently or use fewer resources. In software, optimization targets execution speed, memory usage, network bandwidth, or energy consumption.
A phenomenon observed in autonomous workflows where continuous improvement leads to degraded performance, particularly in clinical symptom detection tasks.

A modeling approach for mixers in frequency comb OFDM radar processing, which incorporates polynomial nonlinearities and spectrum-domain fitting to accurately capture the behavior of multi-tone local oscillator signals.
A framework for user scheduling in multiuser MIMO systems that approximates a nonconvex optimization problem to improve resource allocation and mitigate inter-user interference.
An advanced parametrization technique for Multi-Level Monte Carlo methods that improves efficiency in estimating risk measures, particularly for irregular functions.

A concept demonstrating how different optimizer choices can shape the learning dynamics and response profiles of neural networks in financial time series forecasting, despite similar out-of-sample errors.

A problem that extends the Maximize Phylogenetic Diversity challenge by incorporating dependencies in species selection based on predator-prey relationships, aiming to maximize conservation efforts under budget constraints.
An extension of the Maximize Phylogenetic Diversity problem that incorporates dependencies between species, represented as a food web, to ensure effective conservation strategies.

OptiML is an end-to-end framework for program synthesis and optimization of CUDA kernels, transforming natural-language intent or input CUDA code into performance-optimized kernels through systematic exploration and verification.
A type that represents a value that may or may not be present, replacing null references with explicit handling. Option types (Maybe in Haskell, Optional in Java) prevent null pointer exceptions.

OptiRepair is a framework that utilizes large language model agents for the diagnosis and repair of supply chain optimization models, addressing modeling errors through iterative repairs and rationality checks.

A configurable multi-agent research framework designed for automated exploration in experimental environments, organizing research as a structured tree-based workflow that facilitates hypothesis generation and systematic backtracking.
A multi-model relational database management system produced by Oracle Corporation. It is widely used in enterprise environments for transaction processing, data warehousing, and mixed workloads.
A specification in type theory that captures the computational intuition of oracles, allowing reasoning about queries and their answers through a defined predicate.

The challenge in reinforcement learning and testing where no perfect reference exists to evaluate the correctness of a system's output. The oracle problem makes validation of AI systems fundamentally difficult.
A bi-level reinforcement learning problem focused on aligning large language models under misspecified preference feedback, utilizing the SAIL framework for improved tractability.

A semantic framework that transforms spoken content into an interactive node-link diagram, aiding users in organizing and clarifying their thoughts through verbalization rather than traditional transcripts.
Optimized Row Access Prefetching is a technique designed to mitigate Rowhammer vulnerabilities in memory systems by improving the efficiency of memory access patterns.
Object Request Broker — middleware that manages communication between distributed objects, allowing programs to make requests to objects located on other machines in a network.

This method focuses on recovering a three-dimensional band-limited signal from noisy observations that are randomly rotated, utilizing the first and second moments to jointly identify the signal orbit and rotation distribution.
A multi-agent framework designed for Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) that enhances reasoning capabilities through collaborative agent coordination and iterative refinement of answers.

The automated configuration, coordination, and management of complex computer systems, services, and workflows. Container orchestration (Kubernetes), workflow orchestration (Airflow), and service orchestration coordinate multiple components working together.
A system that manages the deployment, scaling, and operation of containerized applications across clusters of machines. Kubernetes is the most widely adopted container orchestrator.

ORCHID is a framework for fair orchestration in mission-critical Air-Ground Integrated Networks, addressing challenges of instability and energy efficiency through hybrid intelligent deployment strategies.
A framework for orchestrated reasoning with multi-collaborative heterogeneous agents, designed to improve reasoning flexibility and reliability in scientific and knowledge-intensive domains.
A property of a messaging system that ensures messages are delivered in the same order they were sent. Kafka provides order guarantees within a partition, while some systems offer global ordering.
Software that tracks sales, orders, inventory, and fulfillment across channels. OMS platforms coordinate the entire order lifecycle from placement to delivery and returns.
The set of rules that defines the sequence in which operations are evaluated in an expression. Understanding operator precedence is essential for writing correct arithmetic and logical expressions.
A method for extracting and selecting features from the orderbook to enhance probabilistic forecasting of intraday electricity prices.
Business expansion achieved through increasing output, customer base, or revenue internally rather than through mergers or acquisitions. Organic growth indicates sustainable business development.
Website visitors that arrive through unpaid search engine results rather than paid advertisements. Growing organic traffic requires effective SEO strategies and quality content.
A CDN configuration where the edge server fetches content from the origin server on a cache miss rather than having content pushed to it. Origin pull simplifies CDN configuration for dynamic content.
The server that hosts the original, authoritative version of web content. CDNs cache content from origin servers to deliver it faster from edge locations closer to end users.

Object-Relational Mapping — a technique that converts data between incompatible type systems in object-oriented programming languages and relational databases. ORMs like Prisma, SQLAlchemy, and Hibernate let developers interact with databases using their programming language.
A process whose parent process has terminated while the child is still running. In Unix systems, orphan processes are adopted by the init process to ensure proper cleanup.
OSSDM is a semantic-aware waveform design framework for AI-native 6G networks that optimizes physical layer resource usage while preserving semantically significant content.
A technique for formula enumeration in first-order logic that partitions the search space into manageable sections, facilitating incremental candidate pruning.
A design principle where components are independent and changes to one do not affect others. Orthogonal systems are easier to design, test, and maintain because they minimize unexpected interactions.

A framework that leverages large language models to create benchmarks capturing permissible orthographic variations in speech recognition systems for Indian languages, addressing challenges in traditional evaluation metrics.

An agentic Linux kernel tuning framework that utilizes rule-based reinforcement learning to optimize kernel performance by abstracting the configuration space into an RL environment for efficient exploration.

A novel framework for space-time video super-resolution that utilizes diffusion models to enhance both visual quality and frame rate by recovering realistic visual content and improving temporal coherence.
The Open Systems Interconnection model — a conceptual framework that standardizes communication functions into seven abstraction layers. It guides the design and understanding of network protocols.

Open Source Intelligence — the collection and analysis of information gathered from publicly available sources for intelligence purposes. OSINT techniques are used in threat intelligence, investigations, and security assessments.
Open Source Software — software released under a license that grants users the rights to use, study, modify, and distribute the software and its source code to anyone for any purpose.

OSVBench is a benchmark specifically designed for evaluating large language models on the task of generating formal specifications for verifying the functional correctness of operating system kernels, featuring 245 complex tasks.

OSWorld is a benchmark designed to assess the efficiency of computer-use agents in performing desktop application tasks, focusing on understanding the temporal performance and latency issues in generative AI systems.

One-Time Password — a password that is valid for only one login session or transaction. OTPs are commonly delivered via SMS, email, or authenticator apps as part of multi-factor authentication schemes.
A wafer-scale SRAM-based Computing-in-Memory architecture designed to optimize large language model inference by executing operations in situ, minimizing data movement.

Communication that occurs outside the primary communication channel. In networking, out-of-band management provides a separate channel for managing network devices when the main network is unavailable.
Techniques for processing datasets that are too large to fit in main memory by streaming data from disk. Libraries like Dask and Vaex enable out-of-core computation on very large datasets.
An error condition that occurs when a program requests more memory than the system can allocate. OOM errors can crash applications and are addressed through memory profiling and optimization.

A computational task involving the membership of a word in a formal language where the letters are revealed in an adversarial order, requiring efficient algorithms to determine membership based on the revealed information.
An unplanned interruption in service availability that prevents users from accessing a system or application. Outage management involves incident response, communication, and post-mortem analysis.
Outer Diversity refers to a measure of the diversity of preference orders within an ordinal preference domain, which can impact the outcomes of elections and decision-making processes.
A SQL join operation that returns all rows from one or both tables, filling in nulls where no matching row exists in the other table. Outer joins include LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL variants.
A data point that differs significantly from other observations in a dataset. Outliers can indicate measurement errors, data entry mistakes, or genuinely unusual phenomena that warrant investigation.
The process of identifying data points that deviate significantly from the expected pattern. Methods include statistical tests, clustering-based approaches, and isolation forests.
The practice of temporarily storing output data in memory before writing it to the destination. Output buffering improves I/O performance by reducing the number of write operations.
The process of converting data into a safe format before rendering it in a web page or other output context. It prevents cross-site scripting attacks by neutralizing potentially dangerous characters.
A component of an LSTM neural network cell that controls which parts of the cell state are output. The output gate uses a sigmoid function to determine what information passes through.
The final layer of a neural network that produces the result for a given input. The output layer's activation function depends on the task — softmax for classification or linear for regression.
An abstraction that represents a destination for writing data sequentially, such as a file, network connection, or console. Output streams are part of the I/O model in most programming languages.
Open-vocabulary keyword spotting, a technique that enables personalized device control through arbitrary voice commands, addressing prefix bias in enrollment.
OAC is a method that enables low-latency aggregation over multiple-access channels by exploiting the superposition property of the wireless medium to compute functions in distributed networks.
A novel implementation of Extreme Learning Machines (ELM) in the physical layer of MIMO systems, utilizing programmable MetaSurfaces for binary classification tasks in wireless communications.

A machine learning problem where a model performs exceptionally well on training data but poorly on new, unseen data. Overfitting occurs when a model learns noise and details specific to the training set rather than general patterns.
A UI pattern that hides less frequently used actions behind a three-dot or hamburger icon to keep the interface clean. Overflow menus balance discoverability with visual simplicity.
The additional time, memory, or other resources required beyond what is strictly necessary to perform a computation. Reducing overhead is a key goal in system optimization.
A virtual network built on top of an existing physical network infrastructure. Overlay networks are used in SDN, VPNs, and container networking to provide flexible connectivity.
The ability to create multiple methods or functions with the same name but different parameter lists. Method overloading allows a class to have multiple implementations of the same logical operation.
The practice of allocating more resources than currently needed to handle unexpected spikes in demand. While it ensures availability, overprovisioning increases costs and wastes resources.
A feature that allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. Method overriding is a key mechanism for achieving polymorphism.

A technique for addressing class imbalance in training data by creating more instances of the minority class. Methods like SMOTE generate synthetic samples to improve model performance on rare classes.
A phenomenon in machine learning where multiple platforms competing for the same user data can lead to poor global model performance due to feedback-induced mechanisms.
A method for computing least fixed points in parallel and distributed systems, allowing non-atomic updates and focusing on changes to improve efficiency in synchronization.

The Open Web Application Security Project — a nonprofit foundation that works to improve the security of software. OWASP is known for its Top 10 list of the most critical web application security risks.
A regularly updated list of the ten most critical web application security risks. It includes threats like injection, broken authentication, cross-site scripting, and security misconfiguration.
A technique for detecting a user's own voice in hearing aids using a single microphone, enhanced by a data augmentation strategy based on simulated acoustic transfer functions.

In software development, the person or team responsible for a particular product, feature, or service. Product owners define requirements, prioritize backlogs, and make decisions about the product direction.
A memory management concept in Rust where each value has a single owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope. Ownership eliminates data races and memory leaks at compile time.
A memory management approach used in Rust where each value has a single owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope. Ownership eliminates memory leaks without garbage collection.

A novel method for long-wave infrared (LWIR) absorption-based ranging that utilizes characteristic features from ozone absorption to estimate the contribution of reflected downwelling radiance, improving distance estimation accuracy in low temperature variations.

P.definition: One of the most important unsolved problems in computer science: whether every problem whose solution can be quickly verified can also be quickly solved. If P equals NP, many currently intractable problems would become solvable.
A regression technique that minimizes the p-adic distance between observed and predicted values, proven to be NP-hard, indicating significant computational challenges in its optimization.

The probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed results, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (typically below 0.05) suggests evidence against the null hypothesis.

Peer-to-Peer — a distributed network architecture where participants share resources directly with each other without a central server. P2P is used in file sharing, blockchain, and decentralized applications.
A point-to-point passive optical network architecture proposed as an energy-efficient and low-latency backhaul solution for visible light communication systems in indoor fog computing environments.
The 99th percentile latency, meaning 99% of requests are served faster than this value. P99 is a more meaningful performance metric than averages because it captures the worst-case user experience.

P.term: PA$^2$CO (Process Algebra for Ant Colony Optimization)
P.definition: A process algebra framework designed to specify and represent parallelized Ant Colony Optimization algorithms in detail, allowing for various implementations with different parallel execution schemes.

Platform as a Service — a cloud computing model that provides a complete development and deployment platform without managing underlying infrastructure. PaaS providers like Heroku, Railway, and Google App Engine handle servers, storage, and networking automatically.

A collection of related code modules bundled together for distribution and reuse. Package managers like npm, pip, and cargo handle downloading, installing, and managing dependencies in software projects.
A formal model for dependency resolution that unifies the semantics of various package managers, addressing issues of dependency expression and security vulnerabilities in multilingual projects.
A file (package-lock.json or yarn.lock) that records the exact versions of every installed dependency. Lock files ensure consistent, reproducible installs across different environments and machines.

A tool that automates the process of installing, updating, configuring, and removing software packages and their dependencies. Package managers like npm, pip, cargo, and apt ensure consistent, reproducible environments across development teams.

The process of capturing and examining network packets to diagnose problems, detect intrusions, or understand network behavior. Tools like Wireshark and tcpdump provide detailed packet inspection capabilities.
The interception and logging of network traffic for later analysis. Packet capture files (pcap) record complete network conversations and are essential for forensic investigation and troubleshooting.
A technique used in industrial multi-hop IAB networks to enhance reliability by providing path diversity, though it increases traffic load and can lead to congestion.

The failure of data packets to reach their destination in a network transmission. Packet loss degrades voice calls, video streams, and real-time applications and is caused by congestion or hardware issues.

A method of data transmission where messages are broken into small packets that are sent independently across the network and reassembled at the destination. The internet is fundamentally a packet-switched network.
A design framework for device-to-device (D2D) coded caching schemes that reduces subpacketization levels by introducing asymmetry in file splitting and cache placement.
A technique that monitors decoding time in large language models to eliminate package hallucinations, enhancing the reliability of dependency recommendations in software development.

A perceptually-aware color management framework for holographic displays that addresses system-level distortions to achieve accurate color reproduction essential for visual realism in augmented and virtual reality applications.
The space between an element's content and its border in CSS and UI design. Proper padding improves readability and visual hierarchy by providing breathing room around content.
A data structure used by the operating system to map virtual addresses to physical memory addresses. Page tables are essential for implementing virtual memory and process isolation.
A practice of assigning on-call engineers to respond to production alerts and incidents outside business hours. Pager duty rotations ensure continuous system monitoring and rapid incident response.
An algorithm used by Google Search to rank web pages in search engine results. It measures the importance of a page based on the number and quality of links pointing to it.
A technique for dividing large sets of data into discrete pages for display. Pagination improves performance and usability by loading only a subset of results at a time.
An agile software development technique where two programmers work together at one workstation. One writes code while the other reviews each line, promoting knowledge sharing and reducing bugs.
A sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. Palindrome detection is a common coding interview question and has applications in DNA sequence analysis and string processing.

PAM (Processing Across Memory Hierarchy) is a serving system designed for efficient key-value (KV) operations in Large Language Models (LLMs), addressing memory bandwidth and capacity challenges in memory-intensive operations.

A hierarchical memory management system for multi-agent LLM serving that combines multi-level index caching, coordinated index management, and collaborative GPU-CPU acceleration to address challenges in agentic memory.

PAND (Prompt-Aware Neighborhood Distillation) is a two-stage framework for lightweight fine-grained visual classification that decouples semantic calibration from structural transfer, enhancing model efficiency.
A Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. Pandas introduces DataFrame and Series objects that simplify tabular data manipulation.
A hierarchical multi-agent framework designed for autonomous data discovery and analysis in geoscientific data archives, featuring a Supervisor-Worker topology and data-type-aware routing.

A node-labeled directed graph representation of a pangenome that captures genetic diversity across multiple individuals, facilitating more comprehensive genome analysis compared to linear genome representations.
A text rendering library used in GNOME and GTK applications that supports internationalization and complex text layouts. Pango handles bidirectional text, ligatures, and multiple scripts.

PaperTrail is a claim-evidence interface designed to improve the grounding of provenance in LLM-based scholarly question-answering systems. It decomposes LLM outputs and source documents into discrete claims and evidence, facilitating rigorous verification of information.
Algorithms designed to maintain low out-degree orientations in undirected graphs, capable of processing edge insertions and deletions in parallel with polylogarithmic depth.
A type of computation where many calculations are carried out simultaneously, operating on the principle that large problems can be divided into smaller ones solved concurrently.
This approach allows for the parallelization of fixpoint iteration algorithms at varying granularities, optimizing static analysis performance by adapting task granularity dynamically.
Efficient algorithms designed for computing maximal matchings in hypergraphs, achieving logarithmic time complexity in parallel computing models.

A dual-branch architecture designed for multi-channel speech separation that processes spectral and spatial features through separate parallel streams, enhancing modeling efficiency in dynamic environments.
A novel approach to designing spiking neurons that enhances parallel computation capabilities by removing the reset mechanism, which complicates temporal dependencies during training.
A Java Stream API feature that automatically partitions data and processes elements concurrently across multiple threads. Parallel streams simplify parallelism but require awareness of thread safety.

The simultaneous execution of multiple computations, typically across multiple processor cores or machines. Unlike concurrency, which is about structure, parallelism is about actual simultaneous execution for speed.

P-NTM is a simplified version of the Neural Turing Machine that enables efficient scan-based parallel execution, enhancing performance on algorithmic tasks such as state tracking and basic arithmetic.
A variable listed in a function definition that represents a value the function expects to receive when called. Parameters define the interface of a function and enable code reuse.
PEFT is a technique for adapting large language models to downstream tasks with minimal parameter adjustments, leveraging geometric spaces to capture complex structures in language data.

Parameterized algebraic theories provide a concrete syntax for strong monads on functor categories, facilitating the axiomatization of dynamic threads and enhancing the expressiveness of programming constructs like 'fork' and 'wait'.
Variables declared in a function definition that receive values (arguments) when the function is called. Parameters define the input interface of a function and enable code reuse with different data.

A design approach that uses parameters and rules to define and manipulate the geometry of a design. Changes to parameters automatically propagate through the design, enabling rapid exploration of variations.
An exact algorithm for solving convex quadratic minimization problems involving binary indicator variables, with computational complexity dependent on the treewidth of the Hessian's support graph.
A form of polymorphism where code is written generically so it can handle values of different types uniformly. Generics in Java and templates in C++ are implementations of parametric polymorphism.
A new proof system for safety verification of infinite-state parameterized programs that utilizes local symmetry to reuse proof arguments across isomorphic neighborhoods.
A novel concept in graph reasoning that generalizes traditional path search by incorporating gap transitions, enabling a multi-dimensional evaluation of incomplete infrastructure networks.
A framework designed for efficient downloading of AI models in mobile networks, which prioritizes the transmission of parameter packets based on their sensitivity to model inference accuracy.

ParamMem is a parametric memory module that enhances language agents by encoding cross-sample reflection patterns into model parameters, promoting diverse reflection signals to improve reasoning performance.
A zero-configuration web application bundler that automatically detects and transforms assets including JavaScript, CSS, HTML, and images. Parcel provides fast builds using multicore processing.
PARCER is an operational contract designed to mitigate variance, cost, and risk in Large Language Model systems by ensuring consistency and auditability in their outputs.
ParEVO is a framework that synthesizes code for irregular data structures, facilitating high-performance parallelism and addressing challenges in concurrent programming for complex data dependencies.
A GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework designed for long-context LLM inference, featuring drift-robust candidate selection and quantized inner-product reranking.
An error detection method that adds an extra bit to data to make the total number of 1-bits either even or odd. Parity checks detect single-bit errors in data storage and transmission.
An open-source columnar storage file format designed for efficient data storage and retrieval. Parquet provides excellent compression and encoding schemes, making it ideal for big data analytics.
A tree structure that represents the syntactic structure of an input string according to a formal grammar. Parse trees are constructed by parsers and converted to ASTs for further processing.
A software component that takes input data and builds a data structure such as a parse tree or abstract syntax tree. Parsers are essential in compilers, interpreters, and data processing.
A technique in functional programming where a function is applied to some of its arguments, producing a new function that takes the remaining arguments. It enables function composition and reuse.
A memory model that maintains hardware cache coherence only within subsets of cores, allowing for large-scale memory sharing while compromising global cache coherence.
This method focuses on learning the causal structure from data in interventional settings, enabling selective conformal prediction by identifying exchangeable calibration examples.

A variant of rewriting in logically constrained term rewrite systems that allows for partial application of constraints, enhancing the flexibility and power of term rewriting in programming languages.
A program optimization technique that specializes a program with respect to some of its inputs at compile time. Partial evaluation generates faster residual programs by pre-computing known values.
An approach that enhances Kleene algebra by adding hypotheses to prove program-specific equivalences, allowing for more expressive reasoning about program behaviors.
An extension of the soft-matching distance metric that allows for partial unit correspondence in neural representations, improving robustness against noise and outliers.
A bidirectional transformation technique that allows for managing updates across multiple views of a shared source state, ensuring correspondence and compositional integrity.

A novel waveform design for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems that combines linear frequency modulation (LFM) pulse signals with orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) for enhanced communication and sensing performance.

A method of implementing program transformations in functional logic programming by defining transformations that may not cover all cases, allowing for more compact and comprehensible implementations.
A technique for mining episode rules from event sequences that relaxes strict ordering constraints, allowing for unordered events in rule antecedents and consequents.
A design principle for community projects that emphasizes simple, visible routines to facilitate participation and collaboration among members.
The division of a database or dataset into distinct independent parts. Partitioning improves query performance, enables parallel processing, and simplifies data management.

An improved branch-and-bound strategy for enumerating maximal bicliques in bipartite graphs, achieving better worst-case time complexity than existing methods. This approach is particularly useful in applications like E-commerce and transaction networks.

The division of a database or dataset into distinct, manageable segments. Partitioning strategies include horizontal partitioning (sharding), vertical partitioning, and range-based or hash-based partitioning.

The PARWiS algorithm determines a winner among items using active pairwise comparisons under budget constraints, employing spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection. It includes variants like Contextual PARWiS and RL PARWiS for enhanced performance.

PASAR, or Parametric-Sensitivity Aware Retransmission, is a framework designed to optimize AI model downloading in mobile networks by prioritizing the transmission of parameter packets based on their impact on inference accuracy.
A phase-aware scheduling algorithm designed to optimize the serving of reasoning-based Large Language Models by distinguishing between reasoning and answering phases to improve Time-To-First-Token.

The process of gathering information about a target without directly interacting with the target system. It involves collecting publicly available data from DNS records, social media, and public databases.

A passwordless authentication credential based on FIDO2/WebAuthn standards that uses public-key cryptography stored on user devices. Passkeys replace passwords with biometric or device-based authentication.
A secret string of characters used to authenticate a user's identity. Strong passwords use a mix of character types, sufficient length, and uniqueness to resist brute-force and dictionary attacks.

The process of transforming a password into a fixed-length string using a cryptographic hash function. Algorithms like bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2 are designed specifically for secure password storage.

Practices and technologies for protecting passwords, including hashing, salting, key stretching, password managers, and policies enforcing complexity and rotation. Modern approaches favor passphrases and passwordless authentication.

PASTA (Program AnalysiS Tool Framework for Accelerators) is a modular framework that abstracts low-level profiling APIs and deep learning frameworks, providing a unified interface for runtime event analysis in heterogeneous computing environments.
A piece of software designed to update, fix, or improve a computer program. Patches address security vulnerabilities, fix bugs, and occasionally add features to existing software.

The process of distributing and applying updates to software to fix vulnerabilities, bugs, or performance issues. Effective patch management balances security urgency with stability and testing requirements.

Patchwork is a unified framework for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) serving that addresses efficiency bottlenecks in deploying heterogeneous computational pipelines involving large language models and external knowledge sources.

A legal right granted to an inventor that provides exclusive use of an invention for a limited period. Software patents cover novel technical processes, algorithms, or systems and can be contentious in the industry.
An optimal-control-based particle filtering method for state estimation in hybrid systems, utilizing saltation matrices to manage uncertainty during contact events.
A technique for determining the maximum transmission unit size that can be sent across a network path without fragmentation. PMTUD optimizes packet sizes for efficient network communication.
A web security vulnerability that allows attackers to access files and directories outside the intended directory. Also known as directory traversal, it exploits insufficient input validation.

A linear programming relaxation for the Directed Steiner Tree problem in layered graphs, achieving an integrality gap of O(l log k). This method simplifies the approach to obtaining state-of-the-art bounds without complex hierarchy machinery.
A technique that leverages rescaling symmetries in ReLU neural networks to improve training dynamics by aligning kernel parameters through a geometrically motivated criterion.
A training strategy that leverages rescaling symmetries in ReLU neural networks, providing a geometrically motivated criterion to improve training dynamics and align kernel functions.

Algorithms that find the shortest or most efficient route between two points in a graph or grid. Common algorithms include A*, Dijkstra's, and breadth-first search, widely used in games and navigation.

PathSpace is a method for efficient SLAM that utilizes B-Splines for rapid continuous map approximation in constrained environments, enhancing the semantic reasoning capabilities of autonomous vehicles.
A visualization tool that encodes the interaction dynamics between therapists and patients during rehabilitation therapy, supporting the development of a Synthetic Therapist model.
A collection of reusable UI design components with documentation on their usage, behavior, and implementation. Pattern libraries promote consistency across products and accelerate development.
A mechanism for checking a value against a pattern. Modern languages like Rust, Scala, and Python use pattern matching for destructuring data and controlling program flow based on data shapes.

The automated identification of patterns, regularities, and anomalies in data using algorithms. Pattern recognition is fundamental to machine learning applications including image recognition, speech processing, medical diagnosis, and fraud detection.
A technique for generating real-time video commentary that considers both the content and timing of commentary to enhance viewer engagement and accessibility.
The actual data being carried by a network packet or message, excluding headers and metadata. In API communication, the payload typically contains the request body or response data.

PBLean is a method for importing pseudo-Boolean proof certificates into Lean 4, leveraging reflection to ensure soundness and scalability for large proofs. This integration allows for efficient verification of complex logical statements within the Lean theorem prover.

P.term: PC-LLM (Power Control using Large Language Models)
P.definition: PC-LLM repurposes pre-trained large language models to address power control in wireless networks, utilizing a physics-informed framework to enhance decision-making in high-interference environments.

P.term: PC-LLM (Power Control with Large Language Models)
P.definition: A physics-informed framework that repurposes pre-trained large language models for power control in wireless networks, utilizing an interference-aware attention bias to improve computational efficiency in high-interference scenarios.

Principal Component Analysis — a statistical technique that reduces the dimensionality of data by transforming it into a new set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered by the amount of variance they capture.

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard — a set of security standards designed to ensure that all companies that accept, process, store, or transmit credit card information maintain a secure environment.
An enhanced first-order solver for large-scale convex quadratic programming problems, incorporating algorithmic innovations like Halpern-type acceleration and adaptive updates.

PDP, or Prototype-guided Dual-pool Prompting, is a novel framework for Incremental Object Detection that decouples prompts into shared and task-specific pools to mitigate prompt degradation during continual learning.
A bug localization framework that reconstructs causal chains in sequential hardware designs, addressing challenges in timing misalignment and error propagation.

PedaCo-Gen is a human-AI collaborative video authoring system that incorporates pedagogical principles to enhance instructional video creation, featuring an Intermediate Representation phase for interactive refinement.
A distributed application architecture that partitions tasks among equally privileged participants. P2P networks allow direct sharing of resources without a central server.

A pre-trained model by Google for abstractive text summarization that uses gap-sentence generation as a pre-training objective. PEGASUS achieves state-of-the-art results on many summarization benchmarks.
A novel framework for solving non-self-adjoint topology optimization problems by incorporating a convex nonlocal perimeter approximation scheme, ensuring solution consistency across scales.

A benchmark framework designed for evaluating the reasoning capabilities of large language models through a set of constraint-satisfaction problems known as pencil puzzles, which are closely related to NP-complete problems.

An authorized simulated cyberattack performed to evaluate the security of a system. Pen testers use the same tools and techniques as malicious hackers to discover vulnerabilities before they can be exploited, providing actionable remediation guidance.
A statistical measure indicating the value below which a given percentage of observations fall. Percentiles are used in performance monitoring (P50, P95, P99) and standardized testing.
A novel objective function introduced to enhance the robustness of Autoencoders against anomalies by focusing on the distribution of loss values across samples.
The ability of an AI system to interpret sensory data such as images, sounds, or touch to understand its environment. Computer vision and speech recognition are key perception capabilities in AI.

The simplest type of artificial neural network, consisting of a single neuron that computes a weighted sum of inputs and applies an activation function. Perceptrons are the foundational building block of deep learning.
A property of permutation polynomials that ensures optimal resistance to c-differential attacks, characterized using difference distribution tables.

The concept of a Perfect Hypervisor refers to a theoretical hypervisor that can replicate the behavior of bare metal execution without any observable overhead, which has been proven to be impossible in practical scenarios.

The speed, efficiency, and resource utilization of a software system. Performance engineering involves profiling, benchmarking, and optimizing code, algorithms, and infrastructure to meet response time and throughput goals.
A set of isolation mechanisms, including NVIDIA's MPS, MIG, and Green Contexts, that ensure predictable inference times in safety-critical applications using deep learning models on edge GPU systems.
Testing conducted to evaluate the speed, responsiveness, and stability of a system under various workload conditions. It includes load testing, stress testing, and endurance testing.
A method to optimize QUIC performance by enabling in-network assistance while maintaining end-to-end encryption, addressing the limitations of traditional TCP-based Performance-Enhancing Proxies.
A concept in which algorithmic predictions influence the data distributions they are trained on, leading to a feedback loop that can stabilize model performance in dynamic environments.
An online performance troubleshooting system designed for large-scale model training, utilizing fine-grained profiling to diagnose performance issues in production environments.
An external device connected to a computer that provides input, output, or storage functionality. Common peripherals include keyboards, mice, monitors, printers, and external storage drives.
A permanent URL that points to a specific web page or piece of content, designed to remain unchanged over time. Permalinks ensure that links to content remain valid indefinitely.
An IAM feature that sets the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant to an IAM entity. Permission boundaries limit the scope of privileges without directly granting any permissions.
A methodology for allocating a specific percentage of battery-based solar systems within residential areas to mitigate power quality issues caused by distributed energy resources.
A decoding method that utilizes the permutation automorphism group of linear codes to correct errors in received messages, particularly effective for algebraic geometry codes.

This framework transforms representational similarity metrics into calibrated scores with statistical guarantees, addressing confounding effects due to network scale in neural network representations.

A multi-agent system designed for the discovery of perovskite materials, facilitating closed-loop workflows that integrate literature retrieval, data integration, experimental design, and synthesis while maintaining physical constraints.
A network connection that remains open for multiple request-response cycles rather than being closed after each exchange. HTTP keep-alive and WebSockets are examples of persistent connections.
A method in Topological Data Analysis (TDA) that studies the shape of data by analyzing the connectivity and features of data points across multiple scales.
A set of strategies and tools designed to enhance the performance and reliability of programs utilizing persistent memory, addressing challenges such as cache volatility and store reordering.

PerSoMed is the first large-scale, balanced dataset for Persian social media text classification, containing 36,000 posts across nine categories, aimed at addressing the lack of resources in this domain.
A fictional character created to represent a user type that might use a product or service. Personas help design teams understand user goals, behaviors, and pain points.

A framework that generates and validates conversational personas for AI agents by analyzing social media interactions, utilizing techniques such as k-means clustering and retrieval-augmented generation.
A recommendation framework that employs offline reasoning to enhance user-item relevance understanding using large language models, reducing latency in real-world applications.
These agents facilitate negotiation between user preferences and automated driving behaviors in traffic, addressing conflicts that arise in automated mobility systems.

P.term: Personalized Calibration Strategies in Conformal Prediction
P.definition: Personalized calibration strategies enhance conformal prediction methods in healthcare settings, addressing distribution shifts and improving coverage guarantees for clinical predictions.

A method for estimating the Personalized PageRank (PPR) in undirected graphs, which calculates the probability of a random walk starting from a source node terminating at a target node, with a focus on time complexity and accuracy.
A method in stochastic gradient descent (SGD) that accounts for perturbations in both forward and backward passes, leading to a geometric amplification of errors through sequential operators.

A concurrency control strategy that locks a resource for exclusive use by one transaction, preventing other transactions from accessing it until the lock is released. It avoids conflicts but can reduce throughput.
A unit of digital information storage equal to 1,000 terabytes or approximately one quadrillion bytes. Petabyte-scale storage is common in large enterprises, scientific research, and cloud providers.
A pose-free diffusion model for generating music aligned with dance movements, utilizing visual features from dance videos to enhance performance across diverse scenarios.

Property Graph Constraints (PG-Constraints) are a subset of constraints designed for graph databases, focusing on denial constraints and recursion, enabling automata-based structural analyses of errors in property graphs.
Pretty Good Privacy — an encryption program that provides cryptographic privacy and authentication for data communication. PGP is used for signing, encrypting, and decrypting emails and files.

PhantomRun is an automated repair tool for compilation errors in embedded open-source software, focusing on resolving hardware dependencies, syntax errors, and build-script issues to streamline Continuous Integration processes.
A concept describing how the introduction of AI into workflows alters the dynamics of task delegation and verification, impacting worker efficiency and institutional evaluation.
An extension of Variational Autoencoders that uses a latent-conditioned Phase-Type distribution as its decoder, enabling the modeling of heavy-tailed data distributions.
An algorithm that implements the phenotype-first framework, allowing organisms to adapt through genetic innovation and learning, emphasizing the role of organismal agency in evolution.

A social engineering attack where attackers disguise themselves as trustworthy entities to trick victims into revealing sensitive information. Phishing is commonly conducted through email and fake websites.

An algorithm that enhances the Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) for approximate nearest neighbor search by applying Principal Component Analysis (PCA) filtering to reduce dimensionality and improve search efficiency.

Phoenix is a modular pointer analysis framework for C/C++ that integrates various alias analysis algorithms into a single interface, facilitating easy comparison and composition of analyses while highlighting precision-performance trade-offs.
A web framework written in Elixir that provides high performance and fault tolerance through the Erlang VM. Phoenix is known for real-time features through channels and LiveView.
An Elixir library for building real-time interactive web interfaces without writing client-side JavaScript. LiveView uses WebSockets to sync server-rendered HTML to the browser dynamically.

A phenomenon observed in self-supervised speech models where phonological features can be represented as linear directions within the model's representation space, revealing insights into the structure of phonetic information.
The application of editing techniques to alter photographs digitally, including retouching, compositing, and color correction. Professional photo manipulation uses tools like Photoshop and GIMP.
The first benchmark designed for personalized intent-driven photo retrieval, constructed from authentic personal albums to evaluate retrieval systems beyond visual matching.

A reimagined network fabric for machine learning datacenters that utilizes optical circuit switches to optimize communication, overcoming limitations of traditional electrical switches in high-radix environments.
A widely-used open-source scripting language especially suited for web development. PHP powers many major platforms including WordPress, Laravel-based applications, and Facebook's early codebase.
A physics-aware framework for novel-view acoustic synthesis that integrates spatial geometry modeling with vision-language semantic priors to enhance spatial audio fidelity.

PhysFusion is a dual-stream radar and vision fusion framework designed for detecting water-surface objects, utilizing physics-informed techniques to enhance perception in challenging maritime environments.

PhysGraph is a physically-grounded graph transformer policy that models bimanual dexterous manipulation as a kinematic graph, allowing for more effective handling of the complex dynamics involved in tool use.
An architecture that implements Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks using reconfigurable nonlinear-processing units to enable efficient hardware realization of learnable nonlinear edge functions.

The lowest layer of the OSI model that handles the transmission of raw bitstreams over a physical medium. The physical layer defines electrical, optical, and radio specifications for network hardware.

An approach that emphasizes the importance of choosing appropriate physical representations for relations in Datalog evaluation, moving away from one-size-fits-all solutions to optimize execution plans.
A methodology that incorporates physical laws into neural network training, used here to estimate coolant velocity for effective thermal management in MOSFETs.
A reinforcement learning approach that incorporates physical and geometric constraints into value function estimation, enhancing the learning process in complex environments.

A framework designed to enhance the quality of motion data captured through video-based methods, focusing on physical realism and production readiness by addressing artifacts introduced during capture.
An approach that employs physics-informed probabilistic representations for lossless dataset compression, ensuring distributional fidelity in scientific analysis.
An interpolation method for exterior sound field estimation that utilizes Gaussian processes with a point source reproducing kernel, allowing flexible estimators optimized from recordings.

An unsupervised sound source tracking method that combines a variational encoder with a physics-based decoder, incorporating geometric constraints into the latent space through pairwise time-delay likelihoods.
A unified approach for reconstructing and simulating dynamic 3D scenes that integrates visual rendering with physical simulation using convex primitives governed by continuum mechanics.

PIGTN is a novel detection model that utilizes graph-based representations to enhance attack detection in smart grids, optimized through a multi-objective genetic algorithm for strategic sensor placement.
A neural network architecture that reconstructs local sound fields from sparse acoustic data to infer complex surface impedance, enabling accurate acoustic characterization.
A framework that integrates physical laws into neural networks through soft constraints in the loss function, enabling the modeling of complex systems governed by partial differential equations.

A neural network model that integrates physics principles to estimate vocal-tract area functions and radiation coefficients for singing voice synthesis, ensuring consistency with physical laws during training.
A model for speech emotion recognition that integrates physiological insights into vocal amplitude and phase dynamics to enhance interpretability and performance in humanoid robots.
A novel method for sparse-view Gaussian splatting that enhances view synthesis by utilizing dense π^3 initialization to improve rendering quality and speed in real-time applications.
