A proportional-integral-derivative controller used in industrial control systems to maintain a desired output by adjusting inputs based on error measurement. PIDs are fundamental in automation and robotics.

A control strategy for wireless networked control systems that generates control commands based on plant states and control-aided channel predictions, eliminating the need for real-time channel state information.
A novel framework that replaces explicit pilots in short packet transmission with coded pilots, enhancing reliability and efficiency in ultra-reliable low-latency communication.
A novel architecture for high-frequency wireless communications that utilizes position-adjustable antennas and waveguides to enhance signal coverage and localization.
A type of antenna used in multigroup multicast communications that optimizes placement and resource allocation to enhance max-min fairness in multiple access schemes.

An innovative system designed to mitigate line-of-sight blockages in wireless communications by dynamically repositioning pinching antennas along a dielectric waveguide, optimizing user signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio.

PINEAPPLE is a framework that combines physics-informed neural networks with an evolutionary search algorithm for rapid and interpretable parameter inference in lithium-ion battery electrodes, aimed at improving battery management.
A physics-informed neural network framework designed for accurate wireless channel estimation under pilot constraints, integrating physical interpretability with data-driven approaches.

A three-phase pipeline that constructs schema-specific natural language SPARQL benchmarks for RDF knowledge graphs, utilizing techniques like reverse querying and retrieval-augmented prompting to improve query performance.

An automated sequence of steps that code goes through from development to production, including building, testing, security scanning, and deployment. CI/CD pipelines ensure consistent, reliable software delivery with minimal manual intervention.

The practice of defining continuous integration and deployment pipelines in version-controlled configuration files rather than through manual UI configuration. Jenkinsfile, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI use this approach.
A framework designed for the automation of reusable high-throughput network applications on FPGA, enhancing the agility of network infrastructure in response to evolving demands.

A model parallelism technique that distributes different layers of a neural network across multiple devices, processing different micro-batches simultaneously. Pipeline parallelism enables training very large models.

PiPNN (Pick-in-Partitions Nearest Neighbors) is an ultra-scalable graph construction algorithm that utilizes a novel online pruning technique called HashPrune to enhance the efficiency of approximate nearest neighbor indexing.
An ultra-scalable graph construction algorithm for Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search that utilizes a novel online pruning method to enhance query performance while minimizing construction time.
A large-scale dataset consisting of over 23 million statements in propositional logic along with their corresponding proofs, designed to benchmark reasoning models focusing on length generalization.
The smallest addressable element in a digital image or display screen. Pixel density and resolution directly affect the quality and clarity of digital visual output.
The number of pixels per unit of physical length on a display, typically measured in pixels per inch. Higher pixel density produces sharper images and is a key specification in retina displays.
A lightweight network used for extracting compact, task-relevant semantics from raw sensory data in multi-robot systems, enhancing communication efficiency in 3D environments.

Public Key Infrastructure — a framework of policies, procedures, and technologies for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption. PKI enables secure communication, digital signatures, and identity verification.
A knowledge distillation framework for large audio-language models that aligns audio embeddings and output logits, enhancing the efficiency of speech emotion recognition models.

A novel algorithm that utilizes Message Passing Networks to create latent node embeddings for telemetry-aware greedy routing, improving network routing efficiency without solving the all-pairs shortest paths problem.
A computational problem involving counting weighted homomorphisms of planar graphs, with a dichotomy theorem that classifies the problem as either polynomial-time tractable or #P-hard.

An extended Actor-Critic architecture designed for creative 3D modeling, where a Planner coordinates tasks, an Actor executes them, and a Critic provides feedback, integrating human supervision for enhanced modeling processes.

A novel algorithm that enables agents to learn tasks through observation and interaction without predefined rewards or demonstrator actions, focusing on world modeling in data-constrained environments.
A mobile application that employs conversational AI to assist users in plant care, promoting well-being through interactive engagement with their plants.

A foundational technology or service upon which other applications, processes, or technologies are built. Platforms range from operating systems and cloud services to developer tools and marketplace ecosystems.

A cloud computing model that provides a platform allowing customers to develop, run, and manage applications without managing the underlying infrastructure. Heroku and Google App Engine are PaaS examples.
The discipline of designing and building toolchains and workflows that enable self-service capabilities for software engineering organizations. Platform teams create internal developer platforms.
A cross-browser automation framework by Microsoft for reliable end-to-end testing of web applications. Playwright supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit with a single API.
A multimodal system that enables AI-supported narrative co-authoring in mixed reality, allowing users to manipulate virtual characters and props to create stories.
A method for visual pragmatic repair in natural language database querying that addresses input ambiguity by utilizing incremental clarification through minimal interaction.
A method for restoring Landsat images that facilitates the analysis of river dynamics over time, enabling the uncovering of historical surface water changes.
A software component that adds a specific feature to an existing computer program. Plugins enable customization without modifying the core application, following the open-closed principle.

PluRel is a framework designed to synthesize multi-tabular relational databases from scratch, incorporating schema structure and primary-foreign key connectivity to facilitate the training of Relational Foundation Models.
A simulation tool within the PLUTO code framework designed for predicting non-thermal emissions from shock-accelerated particles in astrophysical flows, leveraging Lagrangian particle methods.
PocketGS is a mobile scene modeling paradigm that enables on-device training of 3D Gaussian Splatting, addressing resource constraints while maintaining high perceptual fidelity in 3D scene modeling.

PoCo is a technique that enhances coverage-based seed selection methods in fuzzing by systematically removing conditional statements that obstruct deeper seed selection, thereby improving fuzzing effectiveness.
The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes that can contain one or more containers that share storage and network resources. Pods represent a single instance of a running process in a cluster.

P.term: POEM (Proximal Policy Optimization with Evolutionary Mutations)
P.definition: A modified version of the Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm that incorporates an adaptive exploration mechanism inspired by evolutionary algorithms to enhance policy diversity.

P.term: Point Vortex Dynamics on Closed Surfaces
P.definition: An extension of point vortex dynamics theory to closed surfaces, providing a comprehensive guide for computational fluid simulations with improved speed and accuracy.

PointCoT is a benchmark designed for explicit 3D geometric reasoning in multimodal large language models, addressing the challenges of geometric hallucinations by emphasizing intermediate logical steps in 3D point cloud understanding.
A variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Pointers enable dynamic memory allocation, efficient array handling, and data structure implementation in languages like C and C++.

PointSLAM++ is an advanced RGB-D SLAM system that employs a hierarchically constrained neural Gaussian representation to enhance structural consistency and localization accuracy in real-time 3D reconstruction, particularly in noisy environments.
An analysis of the convergence behavior of optimal transport mappings and potential functions when approximating target measures with unbounded support.

This concept explores the vulnerabilities of learned indexes to poisoning attacks, where maliciously injected keys can degrade model accuracy and performance, particularly in linear regression models over cumulative distribution functions.
A mathematical function synthesized from an occupancy map to enhance dynamic collision avoidance in robotic systems by serving as a control barrier function.
A multi-architecture, containerized ephemeral malware detonation sandbox that allows dynamic malware analysis in isolated environments, enhancing portability and automation.

Polar is an algebraic analyzer designed for automating the analysis of classical and probabilistic loops using algebraic reasoning, capable of handling recurrences, invariants, and loop sensitivities.
An innovative antenna architecture that maintains polarization orientation while enabling 3D rotation, enhancing directional gain for wireless communications.
A tool designed to enforce complex authorization policies in LLM-based agents by modeling system states as dependency graphs to ensure deterministic policy enforcement.
This benchmark consists of annotated user requests that assess compliance with diverse organizational policies, aimed at ensuring safe and reliable interactions with AI systems.

A policy-parametric type system is a framework developed for choreographic programming that enforces non-interference by preventing information leaks from high-security data to low-security observers. It manages both explicit and implicit flows through a program-counter discipline.

PolicySmith is a framework that utilizes large language models (LLMs) to automate the design of instance-optimal heuristics for systems controllers, replacing manual policy design with automated code generation.
A technique where a client repeatedly checks a server for new data at regular intervals. While simple to implement, polling is less efficient than event-driven approaches like WebSockets.

A uniform complete equational theory for quantum circuits over d-level systems (qudits), utilizing control functors as primitive categorical constructors to establish soundness and completeness for unitary d-level circuits.
A piece of code that provides the functionality of a newer web feature on older browsers that do not natively support it. Polyfills enable developers to use modern APIs across all browsers.

The ability of objects of different types to be treated as objects of a common base type. Polymorphism enables code flexibility and extensibility through method overriding and interface implementation.

P.term: Polynomial Kernel for Face Cover Number
P.definition: A polynomial kernel that reduces the Face Cover Number problem for planar graphs to a smaller instance, facilitating efficient computation without a fixed embedding.

A novel token-mixing mechanism that serves as a drop-in replacement for multi-head self-attention in speech-to-text models, achieving linear complexity in computation with respect to input sequence length.

A training method for Differentiable Ternary Logic Gate Networks that uses polynomial representations to manage the complexity of potential gates, enabling efficient gradient-based learning in the presence of ternary logic states.

This method generates polynomial inequalities to serve as symmetry breaking constraints in integer programming, reducing redundant searches. It utilizes an arbitrary base polynomial and a specific group of permutations to create effective constraints.

P.term: Polynomial Time Algorithm for Noisy Character Recovery
P.definition: An algorithm that recovers polynomials over finite fields from noisy character values, achieving polynomial time complexity even with a constant fraction of errors in the data.
A mathematical property established for optimal control problems involving infinite-dimensional oscillating systems, providing a polynomial estimate for convergence around steady states.
A function defined over L2 spaces that can be computed in polynomial time, with two natural definitions shown to be incomparable unless complexity class FP_1 includes #P_1.

P.term: Polynomial-time Isomorphism Test for k-generated Extensions
P.definition: An algorithm that determines the isomorphism of certain extensions of abelian groups in polynomial time, improving upon previous algorithms for arbitrary groups.
A computational problem that investigates the feasibility of covering a stain with non-overlapping stickers of identical shapes, classified under polyomino constraints.
A polytopological version of Propositional Dynamic Logic (PDL) that can express common knowledge and its generalizations, demonstrating the finite model property over closure spaces.

Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) is a framework used for modeling decision-making in environments where the state is not fully observable, applied in remote monitoring with energy-harvesting sensors.
A pretraining framework that implements token-wise adaptive pondering, allowing models to allocate additional computation selectively at inference based on self-supervised learning objectives.

This framework develops a Poisson Hamiltonian formulation for optimal control of mechanical systems on Lie groupoids, providing a natural reduced phase space for Pontryagin dynamics through symplectic leaves.

A set of maximum principles established for optimal control of partially observed coupled forward stochastic partial differential equations and backward stochastic differential equations, facilitating control in stochastic environments.
A technique used in continuous-time portfolio optimization that aggregates costate variables across uncertain parameters to maximize investment objectives.
An investigation into the space-time trade-offs for population protocols in sparse interaction graphs, revealing the limitations of existing lower bound techniques in bounded-degree trees.
A virtual point where network connections start and end. Ports are identified by numbers and help computers sort network traffic — for example, HTTP uses port 80 and HTTPS uses port 443.
A method of determining which ports on a network are open and could be receiving or sending data. Port scanning is used by administrators for security auditing and by attackers for reconnaissance.
A technique for rendering portals in virtual reality that aims to make transitions through portals seamless for users while optimizing performance in VR environments.

A pose-guided Gaussian Splatting framework for high-fidelity human novel view synthesis, integrating pose signals into both geometric and temporal stages for enhanced depth estimation and temporal consistency.
Techniques aimed at reducing computational overhead in multi-condition Diffusion Transformers by eliminating redundant attention mechanisms through spatial and semantic alignment.
A module introduced to normalize engagement in video ranking systems, addressing position bias through quantile-based distributions for robust long-term value estimation.

An advanced attention mechanism that incorporates positional information more effectively into sequential models, overcoming the limitations of traditional additive positional embeddings in capturing temporal order.
Novel click models designed for carousel interfaces that account for complex user browsing behaviors, optimizing recommendations based on positional information in swipeable lists.
A data structure designed for efficiently representing and querying large collections of sequences, particularly useful in genomics for haplotype matching and related analyses.
A CSS mechanism that determines how an element is placed in the document flow. Values include static, relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky, each offering different layout control capabilities.
An active learning strategy used to curate a large dataset of acoustic data from marine mammals, employing weakly-supervised methods to identify instances effectively.

P.term: Post Hoc Extraction of Pareto Fronts
P.definition: A method for extracting Pareto front solutions in continuous control tasks, allowing agents to balance multiple objectives after training on single-objective policies.
A research paradigm that seeks to align artificial intelligence development with sustainable and equitable economic practices, advocating for satisficing over optimization.

A process conducted after an incident or project completion to identify what went wrong, what went right, and how to improve. Blameless post-mortems focus on systemic issues rather than individual fault.

Cryptographic algorithms designed to be secure against attacks by quantum computers. As quantum computing advances, post-quantum algorithms are being standardized to replace vulnerable RSA and ECC schemes.

This novel signature scheme utilizes a chameleon hash function based on the McEliece cryptosystem, allowing authorized modifications of messages while ensuring immutability and security against quantum attacks.
An online calibration approach for complex social simulations that formulates the calibration task as a dynamic optimization problem, continuously updating parameters to match real system changes.

A spatial database extension for PostgreSQL that adds support for geographic objects, allowing location queries to be run in SQL. PostGIS enables geographic information systems and spatial analysis in relational databases.

A powerful, open-source relational database system known for its reliability, feature richness, and standards compliance. PostgreSQL supports advanced data types, full-text search, JSON, and extensions, making it suitable for everything from small apps to enterprise systems.
An API development platform for building, testing, and documenting APIs. Postman provides tools for sending HTTP requests, writing test scripts, and collaborating on API development.

A structured review conducted after an incident to identify root causes, document lessons learned, and implement preventive measures. Blameless postmortems focus on system improvements rather than individual fault.
An algorithm that transforms normal form games into approximating games with ordinal potential functions, enabling efficient multi-agent learning through a surrogate reward structure.
A business analytics service by Microsoft that provides interactive visualizations and business intelligence capabilities. It enables users to create reports and dashboards from various data sources.

Power Particles is a method for fluid simulation based on partial optimal transport that utilizes analytically constructed generalized Laguerre cells to improve computational efficiency and accuracy in free-surface fluid simulations.
A model that characterizes complex market dynamics through power-law scaling of covariance matrices, integrated with deep residual networks for improved estimation in noisy environments.
A framework for the automated discovery of superior reconfigurable power converters, utilizing an analytical approach to explore a vast design space for optimal circuit topologies.

The Partially Ordered Workflow Language 2.0 (POWL 2.0) is an evolution of POWL that introduces choice graphs for modeling non-block-structured decisions and cycles in process modeling, enhancing expressiveness and quality guarantees.

The Physically-Plausible ISP (PPISP) correction module disentangles camera-intrinsic and capture-dependent effects in multi-view 3D reconstruction, providing a physically grounded approach to mitigate photometric inconsistencies.

PPO-LTL integrates Linear Temporal Logic constraints into Proximal Policy Optimization, enabling the systematic monitoring of safety requirements in reinforcement learning applications, particularly in robotics.

PR2 (Personalized Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning) is a reinforcement learning framework designed to enhance personalization in question answering by integrating reasoning with retrieval of personal context from user profiles.
This technique enhances session types by enabling type inference with arithmetic refinements, reducing the annotation burden on programmers while ensuring safe communication in concurrent systems.

An established method or custom in a professional discipline. In software engineering, best practices represent proven approaches to common problems, though they should be adapted to specific contexts.
A script that runs automatically before a git commit is finalized. Pre-commit hooks enforce code quality by running linters, formatters, and tests before code enters the repository.
A technique used in scientific data compression to remove sequential dependencies and enable high parallelism, though it may introduce artifacts that degrade data quality under certain error bounds.

A method for improving data quality in crowdsourced GUI experiments by screening participants through a preliminary task that assesses their attention and care, thereby enhancing the validity of performance data.

PSUM is the first standardized specification for uncertainty modeling in Model-Based Systems Engineering, providing formally defined semantics for representing and reasoning about uncertainty in engineered systems.

A classification metric that measures the proportion of true positive predictions among all positive predictions. High precision means few false positives, which is critical in applications like spam detection.
A technique that optimizes flow matching and score-based diffusion by reshaping the geometry of intermediate distributions to mitigate optimization bias caused by ill-conditioned covariance.

A unified framework for search relevance matching and click-through rate prediction that incorporates cross-user preference mining and exposure bias correction to improve personalized relevance modeling.
A function that returns a boolean value, used to test whether something meets a specific condition. Predicates are commonly used in filtering, searching, and conditional logic.

A framework introduced for beamforming design in amplify-and-forward relay MIMO systems that utilizes predictive models to optimize beamform design while addressing challenges in channel state information acquisition.
The use of statistical algorithms and machine learning techniques to identify the likelihood of future outcomes based on historical data. It is used in marketing, finance, healthcare, and operations.
An architecture that enhances memory retrieval by utilizing temporal co-occurrence rather than similarity, allowing for more nuanced associations in neural systems.

Predictive Coding Networks (PCNs) are a class of neural networks that minimize an energy function based on network activities, offering a biologically plausible alternative to traditional backpropagation methods.
A technique used for coverage verification in UAV trajectory planning that estimates the likelihood of rare failure events more efficiently than traditional uniform sampling methods.
A domain-specific language designed for predictive modeling on relational databases, facilitating the prediction of future or missing values using machine learning methods.

A communication paradigm where the transmitter and receiver maintain a shared predictive state, allowing the physical channel to primarily convey innovations or corrective information rather than raw data.
A standardized benchmarking framework for evaluating task scheduling and preemption techniques in FPGAs, aimed at improving reproducibility and comparison across studies.
A technique that loads resources before they are needed to improve perceived performance. Browser prefetching downloads pages, scripts, or data that the user is likely to request next.

Prefill-Decode Disaggregation is a paradigm that separates the compute-intensive prefill phase from the memory-intensive decode phase in serving large language models, optimizing resource utilization for multi-round inference.

PrefillShare is a shared prefill module designed for key-value (KV) reuse in multi-LLM disaggregated serving, which reduces prefill load and tail latency by optimizing the execution of common prompt prefixes across multiple language models.
A generalization of clustering and partial ordering that seeks to maximize the sum of values for ordered pairs in a finite set, with applications in bioinformatics and social network analysis.

A program that processes source code before it is passed to the compiler, performing transformations like macro expansion, file inclusion, and conditional compilation. C/C++ preprocessors and CSS preprocessors like Sass are common examples.
A technique that renders pages on the server or at build time to improve initial load performance. Prerendering generates static HTML that can be served immediately to users.

A control technique ensuring convergence to equilibrium of a one-dimensional heat equation within a specified time frame, independent of initial conditions, enhancing transient performance in applications.
A processing-in-memory (PIM) based inference accelerator for large language models (LLMs) that incorporates low-rank adaptation techniques to enhance efficiency and reduce power consumption.
A column or set of columns in a relational database table that uniquely identifies each row. Primary keys enforce entity integrity and serve as the main reference for relationships between tables.
A statistical technique for dimensionality reduction that transforms a set of correlated variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated components. PCA is widely used in exploratory data analysis.
A novel framework for pricing complex data products in data-poor markets, leveraging prior predictive distributions to address challenges in transaction data scarcity.
A data structure where each element has a priority, and elements are served according to their priority rather than insertion order. Priority queues are typically implemented as binary heaps.

PRISM-FCP (Partial shaRing and robust calIbration with Statistical Margins for Federated Conformal Prediction) is a Byzantine-resilient federated conformal prediction framework that enhances robustness against Byzantine attacks during model training and calibration through partial model sharing.

PRISM-XR is a framework that enhances privacy-aware collaboration in Extended Reality (XR) environments by utilizing multimodal large language models to create objects and animations while safeguarding sensitive user information.

The right of individuals to control how their personal information is collected, used, and shared. Privacy regulations like GDPR and CCPA mandate data protection practices and give users rights over their data.

An approach to systems engineering that takes privacy into account throughout the entire development process. It ensures privacy is embedded into IT systems, business practices, and networked infrastructure.

A tool used to surface mental models and explore privacy concerns, particularly in voice-first ambient interfaces, by customizing interview protocols based on user interactions and privacy considerations.
A challenge in Differentially Private Federated Learning (DP-FL) where clients have varying privacy requirements, complicating the selection of clients for collaborative model training.

A system that enables privacy-aware inference of large language models by splitting the model between a trusted local GPU and an untrusted cloud GPU, ensuring that sensitive data remains on the local device.
A real-time conversational assistant that uses audio and inertial measurement unit (IMU) data to provide step-by-step guidance for procedural tasks while maintaining user privacy.

A method that quantifies the contribution of individual training examples to a model's behavior while ensuring that sensitive information about the data contributors remains confidential, addressing the tension between privacy and utility under differential privacy.
A framework for enabling direct trades among prosumers and consumers in local energy markets while ensuring participant data privacy and considering network-related costs.
A cloud computing environment dedicated exclusively to a single organization. Private clouds offer greater control, security, and customization compared to public clouds.

An attack that exploits a vulnerability to gain elevated access to resources that are normally protected. It can be vertical (gaining higher privileges) or horizontal (accessing another user's resources).
An approach that enhances Vision-Language-Action models by leveraging privileged 4D information during training to improve understanding of world dynamics and physical interactions.

PrivMedChat is an end-to-end framework for differentially private reinforcement learning from human feedback (DP-RLHF) tailored for medical dialogue systems, designed to mitigate risks of sensitive information leakage.
A phenomenon where pro-China influencers on social media platforms like TikTok achieve higher engagement rates than traditional state media, impacting public perceptions of authoritarian regimes.
A framework for perceptive humanoid-object interaction that optimizes control policies based on desired root trajectories and real-world deployment conditions.
A dual-system framework for proactive embodied social agents, allowing for synchronized speech and gestures while managing the latency constraints of real-time interactions.
A method for enhancing item-side fairness in interactive recommendation systems by actively guiding user preferences towards long-tail items while maintaining user satisfaction.
A technique that combines probabilistic amplitude shaping with quadrature amplitude modulation formats to enhance continuous variable quantum key distribution systems over linear quantum channels.
An innovative optimization algorithm that treats the optimization landscape as variational, learning circuit parameters iteratively from samples to solve combinatorial optimization problems.
A consensus protocol that operates under a probabilistic failure model, allowing for more nuanced handling of faults in distributed systems compared to traditional deterministic models.

Probabilistic consensus protocols extend traditional consensus mechanisms by incorporating a probabilistic failure model, allowing systems to optimize for cost and performance in the face of complex real-world faults.
A method for achieving fair ordering of events in distributed systems by embracing synchronization errors and using statistical models to compare timestamps.
A framework that simulates simultaneous or future matches to compute qualifying probabilities for outcomes in sports tournaments, addressing limitations in existing match classification models.
A novel sensing paradigm that utilizes probabilistic decision-making for data sampling, inspired by the autonomous nervous system, to improve energy efficiency in sensor networks.

A programming paradigm where probability distributions are first-class citizens and programs can include random variables. It is used for Bayesian inference, generative modeling, and uncertainty quantification.
This technique involves the automated formalization of probabilistic requirements from structured natural language, addressing challenges in specifying uncertainties in software-intensive systems.

Probabilistic Retrofitting is a method that transforms pre-trained deterministic models into probabilistic ones using a proper scoring rule, allowing for efficient training while leveraging existing model strengths.
A probabilistic extension of Strategic Timed CTL (STCTL) that is applicable to stochastic multi-agent systems, enhancing the expressiveness of temporal logic in strategic settings.
A mathematical framework for quantifying uncertainty and the likelihood of events. Probability theory underpins statistics, machine learning, risk assessment, and decision-making under uncertainty.

A mathematical function that describes the likelihood of different outcomes in a random experiment. Common distributions include normal, binomial, Poisson, and uniform, each modeling different types of random phenomena.

A plug-in module for text-to-image diffusion models that enables early quality assessment of generated images, improving efficiency by allowing for the selection of high-quality images without extensive post-hoc evaluation.
A system for procedural 3D generation and parametric editing that produces editable 3D models in real-time using a procedural compact graph representation.

A phenomenon observed in large language models where they fail to execute a verifiable, prompt-grounded specification despite having the correct value in context, often manifesting as readout-stage routing failures.
A framework for evaluating Large Language Model-based agents that formalizes agent procedures and assesses their performance across multiple dimensions, including utility and procedural integrity.
An instance of a computer program that is being executed, containing the program code and its current activity. Each process has its own memory space, resources, and execution context.
A guiding framework for inference in DEEPTHINK systems that utilizes process rewards to enhance the correctness of candidate solutions during reasoning tasks.

PIM architectures enable computation directly within DRAM, addressing the memory wall problem by allowing operations like bit-shifting to be performed without moving data off-chip. This approach enhances performance for various applications, including cryptography.

The process of acquiring goods, services, or technology from external sources. In IT, procurement involves vendor evaluation, contract negotiation, and managing supplier relationships for hardware and software.
A phenomenon where college enrollment rates increase during economic upturns and decrease during downturns, influenced by labor market conditions at the time of high school graduation.

A component that generates and sends data or messages to a queue, stream, or topic for consumption by other components. Producers are one half of the producer-consumer pattern used in event-driven architectures.

The practice of strategically guiding the development, launch, and continuous improvement of a product. Product managers define the vision, prioritize features, and align stakeholders around user needs and business goals.
A role in Scrum responsible for maximizing the value of the product by managing the product backlog. The product owner defines user stories, prioritizes features, and makes scope decisions.

The state where a product fully satisfies market demand, indicated by strong organic growth, high retention, and customers who would be very disappointed without the product. It is a key milestone for startups.
A structured, multi-stage AI coding system designed for client-side development that addresses the complexities of real-world engineering constraints and heterogeneous inputs.
The efficiency of converting inputs into useful outputs, or tools and practices that help individuals and teams accomplish more. Productivity tools automate repetitive tasks and streamline workflows.

The practice of measuring the performance characteristics of a program, including execution time, memory usage, and function call frequency. Profiling identifies bottlenecks and guides optimization efforts.

This technique allows users to define executable specifications in a flexible manner, enabling customization of testing processes beyond the limitations of traditional property-based testing frameworks.
The automated buying and selling of online advertising using algorithms and real-time bidding. Programmatic platforms use data to target specific audiences and optimize ad placement and pricing.
The process of writing instructions that a computer can execute to perform specific tasks. Programming involves problem-solving, algorithm design, coding in a language, testing, and debugging.
Jokes, memes, and cultural references shared among programmers about common coding experiences. Themes include off-by-one errors, naming things, and the eternal debate between tabs and spaces.

A formal system of notation for writing instructions that can be translated into machine code for execution. Languages vary in paradigm, type system, and abstraction level, from assembly to high-level languages.
A fundamental style or approach to programming that provides a way of structuring and organizing code. Major paradigms include imperative, object-oriented, functional, and declarative programming.
A web design strategy that starts with basic content and functionality accessible to all browsers, then adds enhanced features for browsers that support them. It ensures broad accessibility.
A lightweight codec for generative image compression that utilizes residual vector quantization to progressively encode image residuals, improving flexibility for low-bitrate transmission.
An interactive approach to visualizing data with large magnitudes by using motion to allow viewers to gradually experience differences in scale, enhancing comprehension.

A web application that uses modern web technologies to deliver app-like experiences, including offline functionality, push notifications, and home screen installation. PWAs bridge the gap between web and native apps without requiring app store distribution.
A mixed-method framework that evaluates the social impact of community cooperation projects by analyzing participation architecture and collaboration networks.

The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet requirements. In software, common frameworks include Agile, Scrum, Kanban, and traditional waterfall approaches.

A logic programming language associated with artificial intelligence and computational linguistics. Prolog programs define relations and query them, making it well-suited for expert systems and symbolic reasoning.

An open-source systems monitoring and alerting toolkit designed for reliability and scalability. It features a multi-dimensional data model, a flexible query language, and pull-based metrics collection.

An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. Promises provide a cleaner alternative to callbacks for handling async code, and form the basis for the async/await syntax.

A class of computational problems where the goal is to satisfy certain constraints under specific promise conditions, with implications for robust satisfiability and hardness results in computational complexity.

A class of constraint satisfaction problems where the instance is guaranteed to satisfy at least a certain fraction of constraints, allowing for the study of robust satisfiability under specific conditions.

The practice of designing and refining input prompts to effectively communicate with and elicit desired outputs from large language models. It involves techniques like few-shot learning and chain-of-thought.
Types of vulnerabilities in large language models where malicious prompts can manipulate model behavior, necessitating robust security evaluations to safeguard against such risks.
Metrics developed to evaluate the consistency and accuracy of small open-source language models in clinical question answering, focusing on their performance across various prompt styles.
A non-parametric approach to model reasoning that extracts reasoning patterns from a teacher model and organizes them into structured instructions for a student model's system prompt.

This paradigm allows users to express their immediate intent through natural language prompts, enabling recommendation systems to adapt dynamically to user goals rather than relying solely on historical behavior.

PromptPream is a computation-powered preloading paradigm that reduces bandwidth demand by sending compact semantic prompts instead of pixel-level video chunks, effectively minimizing playback stalls in short video platforms.
The first proof calculus designed for reasoning about liveness properties in concurrent programs executed on weak memory models, extending existing proof rules to incorporate memory fairness.
A collaborative workflow for rapid draft generation and verification of mathematical claims, utilizing adversarial verification and explicit provenance to enhance the quality of research outputs.
A testing approach where tests are defined by properties that should hold true for all valid inputs, and the testing framework generates random inputs to verify those properties.

Variables introduced in a verification framework to predict the number of steps until certain temporal properties hold, aiding in the verification of first-order temporal properties in infinite-state systems.
A technique that predicts future execution behavior of programs to facilitate backwards data-flow analysis, enabling program transformations and optimizations.

Proportion Loss is a regularization term introduced in semi-supervised learning that aligns model predictions with the global class distribution, effectively mitigating bias in class-imbalanced datasets.

A methodology for constructing an index composed of risky and risk-free assets that achieves a fixed target volatility using proportional control, demonstrating improved performance over traditional methods.

A general model for selecting candidates based on voter preferences under constraints, which generalizes committee elections and participatory budgeting, incorporating axioms of proportionality for diverse decision-making.

Proscenium is a dual-layer, large transparent display workspace setup that allows for adjustable separation between layers, enabling innovative interaction techniques through layered information experiences.

A U-Net variant that employs decoder conditioning for proximal-sparse skip feature selection, enhancing medical image segmentation by filtering out irrelevant information while preserving fine spatial details.

The Protean Compiler is an agile framework designed to address the phase ordering problem in compiler optimization by utilizing machine learning to adaptively optimize the compilation phases based on varying benchmarks.
An optimization framework designed to enhance the security resilience of cell-free massive MIMO networks against active eavesdropping by employing protective partial zero-forcing techniques.

Proto is an instructional operating system designed to run on commodity hardware, featuring modern capabilities such as per-app address spaces and multicore support, aimed at teaching OS concepts through engaging applications.
Proto-ML is an integrated development environment (IDE) designed to enhance machine learning solution prototyping by facilitating collaboration and knowledge reuse among stakeholders.
A set of rules and conventions that govern how data is transmitted and received over a network. Common protocols include HTTP, TCP, UDP, and SMTP.
Google's language-neutral, platform-neutral mechanism for serializing structured data. Protobufs are smaller, faster, and simpler than XML and JSON for inter-service communication.

Google's language-neutral, platform-neutral serialization format for structured data. Protocol Buffers (protobuf) are smaller, faster, and more strongly typed than JSON or XML, commonly used with gRPC.
A method for network traffic representation that addresses challenges posed by encryption and evolving protocols by leveraging redundancy across network layers for more efficient classification.
A prototype-based disentanglement text-to-speech framework that separates speaker identity from dysarthric articulation within a unified latent space.
An object-oriented programming model where objects inherit directly from other objects rather than from classes. JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance as its primary inheritance mechanism.
An early model or release of a product built to test a concept or process. In design, prototypes range from low-fidelity wireframes to interactive high-fidelity mockups.
ProtRLSearch is a multi-round multimodal protein search agent that utilizes large language models trained via reinforcement learning to enhance protein analysis tasks in healthcare.
A framework that tracks correction lineage in OCR processes, capturing details such as edit type and confidence to enhance the interpretability of digital humanities texts.
A memory management approach for agents that retains raw logs while allowing for efficient summaries, addressing the challenges of unverified omissions in long-horizon interactions.
An algorithm that minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence between steady state and target Gaussian distributions in linear dynamical systems, promoting sparsity through L1 regularization.

A concept where a collection of sets satisfies a proximity gap with respect to a property if members are either close to the property in Hamming distance or only a small fraction are. This is relevant in the context of Reed-Solomon codes and list decoding.

An intermediary server that sits between clients and destination servers, forwarding requests on behalf of clients. Proxies serve various purposes including caching, load balancing, security filtering, and anonymizing client connections.
A structural design pattern that provides a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it. Proxy patterns are used for lazy loading, access control, and logging.

The Prune-then-Merge framework is a two-stage approach for efficient visual document retrieval that combines pruning and merging techniques to optimize the trade-off between compression rate and feature fidelity.
A lightweight spiking neural network architecture derived from SqueezeNet, designed to benchmark accuracy-energy tradeoffs, particularly for edge intelligence applications.

A dual-branch parallel spectral-spatial architecture designed for multi-channel speech separation, processing spectral and spatial features through separate streams to improve performance in dynamic environments.
A randomized algorithm that outputs a fixed canonical solution for the global minimum cut problem, utilizing a graph-theoretic tie-breaking mechanism to enhance performance.

Quantum algorithms that consistently output a canonical solution with high probability for any input, characterized by unique complexities that differentiate them from classical randomized algorithms.
A technique in automatic chord recognition that generates labels for unlabeled audio data using a pre-trained model, facilitating the training of a student model in a two-stage pipeline.

P.term: Pseudo-Labeling and Knowledge Distillation for ACR
P.definition: This two-stage training pipeline enhances automatic chord recognition by generating pseudo-labels from pre-trained models and training a student model on unlabeled audio data.
An informal high-level description of a computer program's operating principle using structural conventions of a programming language but intended for human reading rather than machine execution.

Algorithms that generate sequences of numbers that approximate the properties of random numbers, particularly useful in derandomization and circuit lower bounds, with optimal constructions over polynomial-size fields.
A framework for Pseudo Seed Quality Enhancement in Unsupervised Multimodal Entity Alignment, addressing challenges in obtaining labeled seed pairs by utilizing pseudo-alignment seeds.
A methodology for defining and characterizing surrogate models in deep learning, focusing on analyzing their Precision, Time, Memory, and Energy consumption to enhance optimization processes.
A Prompt-Tuned Temporal Shift Spiking Neural Network designed for efficient speech emotion recognition, addressing distribution mismatch in self-supervised learning representations.

Publish/Subscribe — a messaging pattern where senders (publishers) broadcast messages to topics without knowledge of receivers (subscribers). Pub/Sub decouples components and enables scalable event-driven architectures.
A cloud computing model where services are delivered over the public internet and shared across organizations. Major providers include AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud Platform.
An experimental economic game that studies cooperation among individuals in a group setting, where participants decide how much to contribute to a common pool that benefits all.

The openly shared component of an asymmetric cryptographic key pair, used for encrypting data or verifying digital signatures. The corresponding private key is kept secret and used for decryption or signing.
A framework for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption that enables secure electronic transfer of information. PKI is the foundation of HTTPS and secure email communication.

Public-moment-guided Truncation (PMT) is a technique that applies differential privacy to unbounded data by leveraging second-moment information from public datasets to transform private data while maintaining privacy guarantees.

A messaging pattern where senders publish messages to topics without knowledge of receivers, and subscribers receive only messages from topics they have subscribed to. It decouples message producers and consumers.

The process of making software, content, or data available to users. In software, publishing includes deploying applications, releasing packages to registries, and distributing content through platforms.

This framework integrates Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) into deep neural network (DNN) models to provide post-theft accountability and traceability, enhancing security against model theft during knowledge distillation.
A mechanism in version control systems where a developer requests that their code changes be reviewed and merged into a target branch. Pull requests facilitate code review and collaboration.
A traditional South Indian craft practice that utilizes structured geometric rules for physical data representation, integrating cultural practices with data visualization.
A neural architecture that incorporates learnable oscillatory dynamics for robust continuous-time sequence processing, enhancing state evolution independently of external inputs.
An open-source configuration management tool that automates the provisioning and management of infrastructure. Puppet uses a declarative language to describe system configuration.
A dyadic messaging prototype that enhances intimate communication through embodied interaction and reciprocity-aware recommendations.
A Node.js library that provides a high-level API to control headless Chrome or Chromium browsers. Puppeteer is used for web scraping, automated testing, screenshot generation, and PDF creation.

PURE is a preference-aware reasoning framework for explainable recommendation systems that improves the selection of evidence to generate more consistent and convincing explanations aligned with user preferences.
A function that always produces the same output for the same input and has no side effects. Pure functions are a cornerstone of functional programming and enable easier testing and reasoning.
A message that pops up on a user's device from an application, even when the app is not actively in use. Push notifications are used for real-time alerts, updates, and engagement.
A decentralized optimization method that facilitates learning over directed communication networks, addressing stability and generalization issues through a uniform-stability framework.
A decentralized learning framework that utilizes push-sum methods for optimization over directed communication networks, addressing stability and generalization issues.
A scalable orchestration framework for zero-knowledge proof generation in blockchain systems, addressing timing and reliability constraints to ensure timely proof completion.

PVT-GDLA is a decoder-centric transformer model that employs gated differential linear attention to achieve linear-time complexity while preserving sharp, long-range dependencies in medical image segmentation.
Progressive Web App — a web application that uses modern web capabilities to deliver an app-like experience to users. PWAs work offline, can be installed on devices, and support push notifications.
A physics and data-informed model that predicts battery state of charge (SOC) depletion in robots based on time and pulse-width modulation (PWM) duty cycle.

A novel distributed Gaussian Process framework tailored for large-scale multi-robot systems, utilizing sparse variational inference to create compact pseudo-representations for efficient modeling under computational constraints.

A fusion power-plant costing framework developed by the Clean Air Task Force, which incorporates a standards-aligned chart-of-accounts and a physics-to-economics workflow for analyzing costs in Magnetic Fusion Energy, Inertial Fusion Energy, and Magneto-Inertial Fusion Energy.

PyFi is a framework designed for pyramid-like financial image understanding, enabling vision language models to process financial questions in a structured manner, enhancing their reasoning capabilities.
PymooLab is an open-source visual analytics framework that simplifies multi-objective optimization by providing a user-friendly interface for configuration, execution monitoring, and analysis.

A new functional element type introduced for dynamic adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) in three-dimensional mixed-element meshes, designed to connect tetrahedral and hexahedral elements without creating hanging edges.

The Python API for Apache Spark that enables Python developers to use the Spark framework for large-scale data processing. PySpark combines Python's simplicity with Spark's distributed computing power.

A versatile, high-level programming language known for its readable syntax and extensive ecosystem. Python dominates data science, AI/ML, web development (Django, Flask), automation, and scientific computing, making it one of the world's most popular languages.

An open-source machine learning framework developed by Facebook's AI Research lab. PyTorch provides tensor computation with GPU acceleration and a dynamic computational graph for building neural networks.

Q.definition: A model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns the value of actions in states without requiring a model of the environment. It builds a Q-table mapping state-action pairs to expected cumulative rewards.

A lookup table used in Q-learning where rows represent states and columns represent actions, storing expected future rewards. As the agent explores, Q-values converge to optimal action-value estimates.
An extensible quantum network simulator built on ns-3, designed to evaluate quantum network architectures and protocols while emulating quantum dynamics on classical platforms.

Quality Assurance — the systematic process of ensuring that software products meet specified requirements and quality standards. QA encompasses test planning, test execution, defect tracking, and process improvement.

The use of specialized software tools to execute pre-scripted tests on a software application before it is released into production. QA automation increases test coverage and reduces manual testing effort.

An automated sequence of quality assurance stages that code changes pass through before deployment. It typically includes linting, unit tests, integration tests, and acceptance tests to ensure code quality.
A tokenization method that incorporates data reliability into vocabulary construction, optimizing vocabulary and downstream performance through a bilevel optimization formulation.

A self-attention mechanism designed for predicting video quality of experience (QoE) in encrypted traffic, enhancing performance in video conferencing applications by analyzing encrypted media streams.
A web-based platform for automated essay scoring specifically tailored for Arabic, providing tools for assignment creation, essay upload, and scoring configuration.
A novel framework for unitary synthesis in quantum computing that combines Generative Flow Networks with Transformers to efficiently learn from sparse reward signals.
The first general-purpose static analysis framework for Verilog, aimed at enhancing bug detection and program understanding in hardware design through sophisticated static analysis techniques.

An ontology-grounded framework for constructing interpretable medical text embeddings, where each dimension corresponds to clinically meaningful yes/no questions, enhancing the usability of embeddings in clinical decision-making.
Amazon Quantum Ledger Database — a fully managed ledger database providing a transparent, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable transaction log owned by a central trusted authority.
A business intelligence platform that uses an in-memory associative data model to let users explore data relationships dynamically without predefined queries or hierarchies.

QMAVIS (Q Team-Multimodal Audio Video Intelligent Sensemaking) is a novel long video-audio understanding pipeline that utilizes late fusion of Large Multimodal Models, Large Language Models, and speech recognition models to analyze long-form video content, enhancing applications in sensemaking and video content analysis.
An open-source firmware for custom mechanical keyboards and input devices, supporting programmable key layers, macros, tap-dance keys, and RGB lighting through a C-based configuration system.

Quality of Service — a set of technologies and policies that manage network traffic to ensure reliable performance for critical applications. QoS prioritizes certain types of traffic, guaranteeing bandwidth, reducing latency, and minimizing packet loss.
A performance modeling method that partitions the execution configuration space of distributed workflows into regions with similar behavior to ensure Quality of Service (QoS) guarantees.
Quantum Processing Unit — a specialized processor that performs quantum computations using qubits. QPUs are the core hardware component of quantum computers, distinct from classical CPUs.

Quick Response Code — a two-dimensional barcode that stores data in a matrix of black and white squares. QR codes can encode URLs, text, contact information, and more, readable by smartphone cameras for instant access to digital content.

A matrix factorization technique that decomposes a matrix into an orthogonal matrix Q and an upper triangular matrix R. It is fundamental to solving linear least-squares problems and eigenvalue computations.
QSIM is a framework for mitigating Q-value overestimation in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning by employing action similarity to reconstruct temporal-difference targets, enhancing policy stability.

QSpy is a proof-of-concept Quantum Remote Access Trojan that intercepts quantum circuits in transit, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks by installing a rogue certificate authority and proxying outgoing API traffic.

A cross-platform application development framework for creating graphical user interfaces and applications that run on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. It uses C++ and offers QML for declarative UI design.
A hybrid lossless compression technique that combines the advantages of Huffman and universal codes to optimize decoding speed and efficiency, particularly for large language models.
A technique for reformulating 0-1 quadratic programming problems, enhancing solver flexibility by incorporating nonconvex quadratic constraints.
An algorithm that achieves a quadratic speedup in finding ε-fixed points of contraction maps, improving the running time from O(log^k(1/ε)) to O(log^{⌈k/2⌉}(1/ε)) for both ℓ₁ and ℓ₊ norms.

Q.term: Quadratic Speedup for Contraction Fixed Points
Q.definition: An algorithmic advancement that finds ε-fixed points of contraction maps with a running time improvement, achieving O(log^(⌈k/2⌉)(1/ε)) for both ℓ₁ and ℓ₊ norms.
This method introduces a novel representation and optimization framework for computing squared geodesic distances on triangle meshes, enhancing accuracy on uneven and coarse discretizations.

A tree data structure in which each internal node has exactly four children, used to partition a two-dimensional space by recursively subdividing it into four quadrants. Quadtrees are used in spatial indexing and collision detection.
A method for learning high-quality Semantic IDs that addresses the collision problem in multimodal item features, focusing on the varying impacts of collisions on recommendation quality.
Non-numerical information that describes qualities, characteristics, or categories. In tech research, qualitative data includes user interviews, open-ended survey responses, and observational notes.

A systematic process of ensuring that software products and services meet specified requirements and quality standards. QA encompasses test planning, test execution, bug tracking, and process improvement throughout the software development lifecycle.
A discipline that focuses on building quality into the software development process rather than testing it in after the fact. It encompasses test automation, observability, and process improvement.

A set of predefined quality criteria that a software project must meet before it can proceed to the next stage of development or deployment. Quality gates enforce minimum standards for code coverage, bugs, and vulnerabilities.
Quantitative measures used to assess the quality of software products and development processes. Common metrics include code coverage, defect density, mean time to failure, and cyclomatic complexity.
A measure of the delight or annoyance of a user's experiences with a service or application. QoE goes beyond technical metrics to capture subjective user satisfaction and perceived performance.
The description or measurement of the overall performance of a service, particularly network performance metrics such as latency, bandwidth, throughput, and error rate.
A small graph that preserves multicommodity flows between a set of terminal vertices in a larger graph, maintaining a specified quality factor, which is essential for graph compression tasks.
An optimization paradigm aimed at discovering a diverse set of high-performing solutions within a specified behavior space, applicable in various domains such as robotics and creative design.
An evolutionary framework for alpha mining that improves automation through trajectory-level mutation and crossover operations, addressing noise sensitivity in financial markets.

The inverse of a cumulative distribution function that returns the value below which a given percentage of data falls. Also called the percent-point function, it is used in statistical analysis and risk modeling.

A type of regression analysis that estimates the conditional quantiles of a response variable distribution, rather than the conditional mean. It provides a more complete view of the relationship between variables.
A statistical method used to analyze systemic risk transmission across stablecoin markets, allowing for the examination of tail-risk behavior and spillover effects across different quantiles.
Numerical information that can be measured and expressed as numbers, enabling statistical analysis. Metrics like page load time, conversion rate, and error count are quantitative data.

Research methods that collect numerical data about user behavior and preferences through surveys, analytics, A/B testing, and task completion metrics. It complements qualitative methods for evidence-based design decisions.

The process of reducing the precision of a neural network's weights and activations from floating-point to lower-bit representations. Quantization reduces model size and inference time while maintaining acceptable accuracy.
A unified framework that optimizes dataset compactness and precision simultaneously by integrating a differentiable quantization module within the dataset distillation process.

A technique that simulates the effects of quantization during model training so the model learns to maintain accuracy despite reduced numerical precision. It produces models better suited for edge deployment.

Quantized precoding is a framework for maximizing sum rate in multi-user MIMO systems, addressing the challenges posed by limited-capacity fronthaul by modeling quantization effects in the precoding design.

A sum rate maximization framework for MU-MIMO systems that incorporates quantization constraints into the precoding design, addressing the non-convex mixed-integer programming problem with a new iterative algorithm.
A method that transforms high-dimensional problems, such as the d-asset Black-Scholes PDE, into tractable forms using tensor networks, allowing for efficient computation on personal computers.
A class of constant-depth quantum circuits that utilize single-qubit gates and generalized Toffoli gates, representing a fundamental model of quantum computation.
A security vulnerability in quantum computing systems where classical access control mechanisms fail due to the effects of quantum entanglement.
The point at which a quantum computer can solve a problem faster or more efficiently than any classical computer. It differs from quantum supremacy by focusing on practical, useful computations.

An algorithm designed to run on a quantum computer, exploiting quantum phenomena like superposition and entanglement. Notable examples include Shor's algorithm for factoring and Grover's search algorithm.
A quantum algorithm designed to implement the diffusion step of grid-based Bayesian filters, utilizing quantum Fourier transform to enhance efficiency over classical methods.
A quantum computing approach that uses quantum fluctuations to find the global minimum of a function. D-Wave systems use quantum annealing to solve optimization problems.

A hybrid quantum-classical variational algorithm designed to find approximate solutions to combinatorial optimization problems by alternating between problem-specific and mixer unitaries on a quantum circuit.

A hybrid quantum-classical algorithm designed for solving combinatorial optimization problems, particularly effective in portfolio optimization under strict constraints, leveraging quantum mechanics to explore solution spaces more efficiently than classical methods.

A metric that quantifies the error rate in quantum communication channels, measuring the fraction of bits that are incorrectly received. QBER is critical for assessing the security and reliability of quantum key distribution.

A communication channel that transmits quantum information, typically encoded in the quantum states of photons. Quantum channels are the backbone of quantum networking and quantum key distribution systems.
The maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a quantum channel, with recent findings suggesting that computing this capacity may be QMA-hard.
A model for quantum computation in which a computation is a sequence of quantum gates applied to qubits. Quantum circuits are the most commonly used model for describing quantum algorithms.

The property of quantum systems where particles maintain a definite phase relationship, enabling superposition and entanglement. Maintaining coherence is a central challenge in building practical quantum computers.

A type of computing that uses quantum mechanical phenomena — superposition, entanglement, and interference — to process information. Quantum computers can potentially solve certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers, impacting cryptography and optimization.
A construction method for quantum convolutional codes based on difference triangle sets, focusing on maintaining sparse stabilizers and small encoding memory while ensuring commutation properties.
The science of exploiting quantum mechanical properties to perform cryptographic tasks. Quantum key distribution allows two parties to produce a shared random secret key known only to them.
The process by which a quantum system loses its coherence and quantum behavior due to interaction with its environment. Decoherence is a primary obstacle to building large-scale quantum computers.
A nanoscale semiconductor particle with quantum mechanical properties used in displays, solar cells, and quantum computing. Quantum dots emit light of specific wavelengths depending on their size.

A quantum mechanical phenomenon where two or more particles become interconnected so that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently. It is a key resource in quantum computing and communication.

This framework introduces a novel method for training multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) agents to utilize shared quantum entanglement as a coordination resource, allowing for communication-free correlated policies.
A set of techniques used to protect quantum information from errors due to decoherence and other quantum noise. Quantum error correction is essential for building reliable quantum computers.

Quantum Federated Learning (QFL) is a framework that enables distributed training of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) models by sharing model gradients instead of raw data, enhancing privacy in the process.
A hybrid classical-quantum method for estimating statistical distributions using Fourier series, specifically applied to the pricing of financial derivatives.
The quantum analogue of the classical discrete Fourier transform, operating on quantum states. It is a key subroutine in many quantum algorithms, including Shor's factoring algorithm.
A basic quantum circuit operating on a small number of qubits, analogous to classical logic gates. Common quantum gates include the Hadamard gate, CNOT gate, and Pauli gates.
Restricted sets of quantum gates represented as symmetric monoidal categories, facilitating equational reasoning for quantum circuit optimization and verification through finite equational theories.

A quantum algorithmic approach to Integer Linear Programs (ILPs) that exploits local structures in the objective and feasible set, providing super-quadratic speedups for solving certain classes of ILPs.
A framework that utilizes time-resolved measurement data to infer gene regulatory networks through a quantum Hamiltonian-based model, providing finite-sample recovery guarantees.
A perfectly-secure encryption scheme that allows for computations on encrypted data, enabling secure training and inference in quantum neural networks.
The quantum indel distance is a metric proposed to evaluate the insertion and deletion error-correcting capability of quantum codes, establishing a framework for quantum error correction.

A model that generalizes quantum Probabilistically Checkable Proofs and Interactive Proofs, allowing multiple rounds of quantum interaction between a prover and a verifier with limited quantum resources.
A proposed network that would use quantum signals and entanglement to connect quantum devices over long distances. It would enable ultra-secure communication and distributed quantum computing.
An asymptotically optimal quantum algorithm for matrix multiplication that achieves a computational complexity of O(N^2 log_2 N), outperforming classical algorithms in efficiency.

A secure communication method that uses quantum mechanics to enable two parties to produce a shared random secret key. Any attempt to eavesdrop on the key exchange can be detected due to quantum properties.

An emerging field that explores the intersection of quantum computing and machine learning. It investigates how quantum algorithms can speed up machine learning tasks and how classical ML can be applied to quantum systems.
A device that stores quantum information in the form of quantum states for later retrieval. Quantum memories are essential components for quantum repeaters and scalable quantum networks.
A framework for optimizing resource allocation in quantum networks, focusing on identifying optimal routes that maximize network utility under varying user demands.
The field of connecting quantum processors and devices using quantum communication channels. It enables distributed quantum computing, secure communication, and the future quantum internet.

Random fluctuations in quantum systems that arise from the inherent uncertainty of quantum mechanics. Quantum noise is a major challenge in quantum computing, affecting qubit fidelity and gate accuracy.
A quantum communication task that allows one party to send information to another without revealing which piece of information was sent, implemented alongside quantum key distribution.

The ability of a quantum computer to evaluate a function on many different inputs simultaneously by exploiting superposition. It is one of the key advantages that gives quantum computers their computational power.

This approach applies quantum computing techniques to the problem of query containment for conjunctive queries, presenting a novel optimization formulation that leverages quantum algorithms to address computational complexity.
A system of multiple qubits that together represent quantum information. Quantum registers are the quantum analogue of classical processor registers and form the working memory of quantum algorithms.
A framework for analyzing communication complexity in distributed quantum algorithms, facilitating optimal solutions for problems like leader election and broadcast in arbitrary networks.

The use of quantum systems to measure physical quantities with higher sensitivity and precision than classical sensors. Applications include magnetic field detection, gravitational sensing, and medical imaging.

A novel paradigm for quantum computing that utilizes quantum transistors and symmetry-protected topological junctions to implement quantum gates, enabling feedback loops analogous to classical sequential circuits.
A quantum device purpose-built to simulate the behavior of other quantum systems. Quantum simulators help study complex quantum phenomena like superconductivity and molecular behavior.
A framework for encoding datasets into quantum states, allowing for approximate nearest neighbor queries while ruling out certain compression possibilities in quantum computing.
The mathematical description of a quantum system that encodes all information about the system's properties. Quantum states can exist in superposition, representing multiple values simultaneously.

The point at which a quantum computer can perform a calculation that no classical computer can complete in a feasible amount of time. Google claimed quantum supremacy in 2019 with their Sycamore processor.

A technique for transferring quantum information from one qubit to another distant qubit using entanglement and classical communication. It does not transfer matter or energy, only quantum state information.

A metric that measures the overall capability of a quantum computer, accounting for qubit count, connectivity, gate fidelity, and error rates. Higher quantum volume indicates a more capable quantum processor.
The quantum mechanical analog of a classical random walk, where a particle moves in superposition along multiple paths simultaneously. Quantum walks form the basis of several quantum algorithms.
A computational problem that involves estimating the quantum α-Rényi entropy, which is a generalization of the von Neumann entropy, under specific bounds.
A method that utilizes quantum computing to enhance the efficiency of numerical integration in multi-asset option pricing, addressing high-dimensional computational challenges.
A proposed architecture for tighter integration of quantum computers with classical systems to achieve lower latencies and improved determinism in high-performance computing environments.

Cloud-based access to quantum computing resources and simulators. QaaS platforms from providers like IBM, Amazon, and Google allow developers to experiment with quantum algorithms without owning quantum hardware.

A method for optimizing the Holevo bound in quantum communication, utilizing a unified projected gradient ascent approach to enhance the amount of classical information transmitted over quantum channels.
An optimization framework that integrates quantum-inspired mechanisms within a multi-objective salp swarm optimization process to enhance global search capabilities in complex decision spaces.
A framework for multi-user angle-of-arrival estimation using Rydberg Atomic Receivers, which employs a radio-frequency lens to enhance spatial power profile measurements.

Cryptographic algorithms designed to be secure against attacks by both classical and quantum computers. Also known as post-quantum cryptography, these algorithms are being standardized to prepare for the quantum era.

The practice of isolating suspicious files, emails, or network traffic to prevent potential threats from spreading. Quarantined items are held in a secure location for analysis before being deleted or restored.

An isolated network segment where suspicious files, emails, or devices are held for analysis before being allowed into the production environment. It prevents potentially malicious content from spreading.
A technique for accelerating large language model inference through quantized self-speculative acceleration, focusing on memory-efficient verification during the inference process.
A Vue.js-based framework for building responsive single-page apps, server-side rendered apps, PWAs, and mobile applications from a single codebase using a rich component library.

An iterative optimization method that approximates the Newton's method Hessian matrix using gradient evaluations rather than computing second derivatives directly. L-BFGS is the most widely used variant in machine learning.
A classical algorithm that approximates thermal expectations of local observables in quantum systems with a quasi-polynomial cost, applicable to the SYK model at any constant temperature.

A QPTAS is an algorithmic framework that provides approximate solutions to optimization problems within a quasipolynomial time complexity, particularly useful for problems like Maximum Independent Set in specific graph classes.
An algorithm designed for deep learning-enhanced beam alignment in mmWave massive MIMO systems, which improves angular resolution and reduces training overhead by leveraging monotonic power ratios.
A number system extending complex numbers, widely used in 3D computer graphics for representing rotations. Quaternions avoid gimbal lock problems associated with Euler angles.

The basic unit of quantum information, analogous to a classical bit but capable of existing in a superposition of both 0 and 1 states simultaneously. Qubits are the building blocks of quantum computers.
