An iterative framework for training convolutional neural network classifiers using Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) via quantum annealing, avoiding gradient-based optimization.

A request for data or information from a database, search engine, or API. In SQL, queries use structured syntax to select, filter, join, and aggregate data. In search, queries are the terms users enter to find relevant results.

A software component that provides a programmatic interface for constructing database queries without writing raw query strings. Query builders improve safety by preventing injection attacks and simplifying complex query construction.
A mechanism that stores the results of database queries so that identical future queries can be served faster without re-executing. Query caches reduce database load for read-heavy workloads.

The practice of storing the results of database queries in memory so that identical future queries can be served faster without re-executing the query. Query caching significantly reduces database load.
A measure of the computational resources required to execute a database or API query. In GraphQL APIs, query complexity analysis prevents clients from submitting excessively expensive queries.

The component of a database or analytics system responsible for parsing, optimizing, and executing queries. Modern query engines support distributed execution across multiple nodes for parallel processing.

A technique that allows a single query to access and combine data from multiple heterogeneous data sources as if they were a single database. Federated queries simplify analytics across distributed data systems.
A directive embedded in a SQL query that instructs the database optimizer to override its default execution plan, forcing specific join strategies, index usage, or parallelism levels.

A specialized language designed for requesting and manipulating data from databases and information systems. SQL, GraphQL, SPARQL, and Cypher are query languages, each optimized for different data models and use cases.
The process of computing and storing query results as a physical table to avoid repeated expensive computations. Materialized views are automatically or manually refreshed as underlying data changes.

The process of improving database query performance by selecting the most efficient execution plan. Query optimizers analyze indexes, table statistics, and join strategies to minimize response time and resource consumption.

An ordered set of steps used to access data in a relational database management system. The query plan shows how the database engine will execute a query, including which indexes and join methods it will use.
An optimization technique where filter conditions are pushed as close to the data source as possible, reducing the amount of data read from storage before further processing occurs.
The process of transforming a query into an equivalent but more efficient form before execution. Query rewriting is performed by database optimizers to improve performance without changing results.

The part of a URL that contains data to be passed to a web application, appearing after the question mark. Query strings consist of key-value pairs separated by ampersands and are commonly used for search parameters.
A formal framework for analyzing the capabilities of agentic systems, with abstractions that model reasoning techniques and their computational power, including equivalences to Turing machines.

A subfield of natural language processing focused on building systems that automatically answer questions posed in natural language. Modern QA systems use transformer models to extract or generate answers from text.

A data structure that follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle, where the first element added is the first one removed. Queues are used in task scheduling, message passing, breadth-first search, and buffer management.

A queue data structure where each element has an associated priority and elements with higher priority are dequeued first regardless of insertion order. Priority queues are used in scheduling and graph algorithms.
A design pattern that uses a queue as a buffer between a task and a service to smooth intermittent heavy loads. This prevents service overload and improves reliability under variable demand.

The mathematical study of waiting lines and service systems, used to model network traffic, server load balancing, and resource allocation. It predicts queue lengths, wait times, and system utilization.

A general-purpose transport layer network protocol developed by Google that provides built-in encryption, reduced connection establishment latency, and multiplexed streams over UDP. QUIC is the basis of HTTP/3.

A highly efficient divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that selects a pivot element and partitions the array around it. Quick sort has an average time complexity of O(n log n) and is widely used in practice.
The programming interface for integrating with Intuit QuickBooks accounting software. It enables developers to automate invoicing, expense tracking, and financial reporting in business applications.

A property-based testing framework originally developed for Haskell that generates random test cases to verify that specified properties hold. QuickCheck-style testing has been adopted in many programming languages.

QuickGrasp is a responsive video-language querying service that utilizes a local-first architecture to provide quality of service (QoS)-aware video-language model deployment, optimizing for both speed and accuracy.

A highly efficient divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that selects a pivot element and partitions the array into elements less than and greater than the pivot. QuickSort averages O(n log n) time complexity and is one of the most widely used sorting algorithms.
A protective method against acoustic side-channel attacks targeting 3D printers, aimed at safeguarding intellectual property during the printing process.
A modern rich text editor built for compatibility and extensibility. Quill provides a clean API, custom content and formatting, and works consistently across browsers for web-based editing.
A data package manager that versions, discovers, and reproduces datasets by creating immutable snapshots with content-addressable hashes, enabling reproducible data science workflows.
A computer program that takes no input and produces a copy of its own source code as its only output. Quines are a classic exercise in self-referential programming and computability theory.
Mathematical representations used for generating kinematically feasible, jerk-limited trajectories from arbitrary geometric paths, allowing for real-time adaptation in dynamic environments.

A framework designed to synthesize trajectory queries from examples, incorporating novel techniques for parameter space pruning and quantitative semantics to enhance query generation for machine learning predictions.

The minimum number of nodes in a distributed system that must agree on a value before it is considered committed. Quorum-based consensus ensures data consistency across replicas and is fundamental to distributed databases and blockchain systems.

The practice of setting and enforcing limits on resource consumption in cloud computing environments. Quotas prevent individual users or applications from consuming excessive resources and ensure fair distribution.
A quantum-native database that compiles SQL into quantum circuits, employs a hybrid optimizer for execution plans, and introduces selective quantum indexing for improved performance.

QVCache is a query-aware vector cache designed to optimize approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in vector databases, addressing memory capacity and I/O bandwidth constraints while maintaining high recall under strict latency service level objectives (SLOs).
A lightweight Python toolkit for unified language metadata management, addressing challenges in reporting language identifiers across multilingual NLP datasets.

R.definition: A programming language and software environment for statistical computing and graphics widely used by statisticians and data scientists. R provides a vast ecosystem of packages for data analysis, visualization, and machine learning.
A complexity measurement system that assesses the performance and asymptotic behavior of algorithms, characterized by properties such as reflexivity and symmetry.
An active reconstruction framework that enables an embodied agent to efficiently select views for high-quality novel-view rendering without relying on heavy representation-specific mechanisms.

R3D (Regional-guided Residual Radar Diffusion) is a framework that enhances millimeter-wave radar perception by integrating regional-guided residual diffusion modeling, focusing on critical structures in sparse and noisy point clouds.
A novel method for relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting that models scene illumination, enabling effective relighting of outdoor scenes captured in diverse lighting conditions.

An open-source message broker that implements the Advanced Message Queuing Protocol. RabbitMQ enables applications to communicate asynchronously through message queues, supporting patterns like pub/sub and work queues.

A situation where the behavior of a system depends on the relative timing of events such as thread execution order. Race conditions can lead to unpredictable bugs and are a common challenge in concurrent programming.
A model for vehicle movement in discrete space with acceleration constraints, where the position changes by vectors in Z^2 with specific limitations on consecutive vectors.

Robust Adaptive Denoising Direct Preference Optimization (RAD-DPO) is a method for aligning generative retrieval systems with user preferences, addressing challenges related to noisy feedback and hierarchical prefix conflicts.
A Radar-Aware Dual-conditioned Generative Adversarial Network designed for speech reconstruction from mmWave radar captures, particularly effective in low signal-to-noise ratio conditions.

A log-adversary-aware framework that enhances digital forensics by cross-referencing low-level storage artifacts with high-level application logs to derive forensic ground truth in NoSQL environments.
A method that combines radar and inertial measurement units to achieve reliable odometry in challenging subterranean environments, addressing IMU drift issues.
A rendering technique optimized for many-core processors that focuses on local ray traversal and structured communication to efficiently handle volumetric rendering in distributed systems.
Radiometric consistency is a novel approach in inverse rendering that ensures accurate disentangling of material properties from global illumination effects using Gaussian primitives.

A consensus algorithm designed to be more understandable than Paxos while providing the same fault tolerance guarantees. Raft is used in distributed systems like etcd and HashiCorp Consul for leader election and log replication.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation — an AI framework that enhances large language model outputs by first retrieving relevant documents from a knowledge base and incorporating them into the generation process. RAG improves factual accuracy and reduces hallucinations.
A technique used in Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems that combines multiple retrieval fusion methods to improve document recall and answer quality in production environments.
An interpretable framework for robotic ultrasound examinations that integrates retrieval-augmented learning to enhance decision-making processes in clinical settings.

Redundant Array of Independent Disks — a data storage virtualization technology that combines multiple physical disk drives into one or more logical units for data redundancy, performance improvement, or both.

Region-Aware Incremental Preference Editing is a technique for updating large language model-based recommendation systems that addresses non-stationary user-item interactions by balancing global updates and pointwise edits.

Resource Acquisition Is Initialization — a programming idiom where resource allocation is tied to object lifetime. When an object is created it acquires resources, and when destroyed it releases them, preventing resource leaks.
A multi-design and architecture-aware framework for repository-level feature addition that mitigates the risks of architectural blindness in code generation processes.
A precomputed table for reversing cryptographic hash functions, used to crack password hashes. Rainbow tables trade storage space for computation time, making them effective against unsalted hashes.

Random Access Memory — a form of volatile computer memory that stores data and machine code currently being used. RAM allows data to be read and written in roughly the same amount of time regardless of the physical location of data.

RAMSeS is a robust and adaptive model selection framework for time-series anomaly detection, utilizing a stacking ensemble optimized with a genetic algorithm to adaptively leverage complementary detectors across varying data contexts.

An ensemble machine learning method that operates by constructing multiple decision trees during training and outputting the mode of their predictions. Random forests reduce overfitting and improve generalization.

R.term: Random Geometric Graphs with Smooth Kernels
R.definition: A study on random geometric graphs that establishes a sharp detection threshold for distinguishing them from Erdős–Rényi graphs based on smooth kernel functions.

An algorithm or device that produces a sequence of numbers that lack any pattern. Cryptographically secure random number generators are essential for encryption, authentication, and security protocols.
A flexible metaheuristic framework for solving Mixed-Integer Programs (MIPs) that utilizes a random-key representation to compute high-quality solutions efficiently.

An algorithm that uses random numbers to decide what to do next at one or more points during its execution. Randomized algorithms can provide simpler and more efficient solutions for problems like sorting, searching, and optimization.
A parallel Bayesian optimization method that selects diverse input sets for evaluating expensive black-box functions, improving practical performance while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

RRDS is a cellphone-based adaptation of Respondent Driven Sampling (RDS) that incorporates researcher-controlled randomization into each recruitment wave, addressing biases introduced by non-random peer recruitment, especially in contexts of high homophily.
A novel algorithm designed for solving nonconvex nonsmooth potential games under uncertainty, achieving optimal sample complexity of O(N²ε⁻⁴).

A technique for randomized corpus reduction in fuzzing seed scheduling, aimed at addressing seed explosion by efficiently selecting a subset of seeds while maintaining diversity and minimizing runtime overhead.

A database query that retrieves all records where a specified field falls within a given range of values. Range queries are optimized using B-tree indexes and are fundamental to time-series and geospatial databases.
An automated system for discovering improved retrieval algorithms using large language models, employing an evolutionary search approach to optimize ranking performance.
A real-valued function defined over states of a dynamical system that aids in verifying the system against ω-regular properties by ensuring certain invariants.

A type of malicious software designed to block access to a computer system or data until a sum of money is paid. Ransomware attacks encrypt files and demand cryptocurrency payments for decryption keys.

A software development methodology that emphasizes rapid prototyping and iterative delivery over extensive planning and testing. RAD focuses on quick user feedback and adapting to changing requirements.

The practice of quickly creating a working model of a product or feature to test concepts and gather feedback early in the development process. It reduces risk by validating ideas before full implementation.
A memory-efficient indexing technique for cloud block storage that indexes block ranges instead of individual blocks, significantly reducing memory consumption during I/O operations.

Digital images composed of a grid of pixels, where each pixel stores color information. Raster formats like PNG, JPEG, and BMP are resolution-dependent and lose quality when scaled up, unlike vector graphics.

The process of converting vector graphics or 3D models into a raster image composed of pixels for display on a screen. Rasterization is the primary rendering technique used in real-time graphics and gaming.

The practice of controlling the rate of requests that clients can make to a server within a specified time window. Rate limiting protects services from abuse, prevents resource exhaustion, and ensures fair usage.
A scalable architecture for implementing rate limiting in distributed systems, utilizing in-memory databases like Redis to maintain accuracy and low latency.

This framework utilizes rate-distortion theory to analyze the trade-offs between accuracy and robustness in visual recognition systems, providing geometric signatures that characterize these trade-offs.
A design consideration in quantum switches that balances the simplicity of operation with the use of quantum memories, impacting the efficiency of entanglement generation in quantum networks.
An AI meta-operating system that integrates a decentralized MLOps protocol, enabling unified AI model development and deployment across heterogeneous edge devices using a blockchain-based framework.

A computational technique that expands around selected time constants rather than zero frequency, enhancing the efficiency of solving stress-governing partial differential equations in electromigration analysis.

A network socket that provides direct access to lower-level network protocols, bypassing the transport layer. Raw sockets are used for network diagnostics, custom protocol implementation, and security tools.

A rendering technique that simulates the physical behavior of light by tracing rays from the camera through each pixel and calculating their interactions with objects. It produces highly realistic lighting, shadows, and reflections.
A framework used in extremal graph theory for deriving asymptotic inequalities between induced subgraph densities, providing a logical perspective on proofs and inequalities.

Role-Based Access Control — a security model that restricts system access based on the roles assigned to individual users within an organization. RBAC simplifies permission management by grouping permissions into roles like admin, editor, and viewer.
A method for in-context learning over relational databases that enables predictive modeling without the need for retraining, by automatically featurizing target rows using relational aggregations.

Remote Desktop Protocol — a proprietary protocol developed by Microsoft that provides a user with a graphical interface to connect to another computer over a network. RDP is widely used for remote administration and remote work.
A speech-based large language model that integrates dimensional emotion embeddings to enhance empathetic responses in human-AI interactions, significantly improving empathy metrics.

A JavaScript library for building user interfaces, developed by Meta (Facebook). React uses a component-based architecture and virtual DOM to efficiently render and update UI elements, powering millions of web and mobile applications worldwide.

A framework for building native mobile applications using React and JavaScript. React Native allows developers to write code once and deploy it on both iOS and Android platforms with near-native performance.
A simulation framework that models market dynamics and order flow, allowing for the evaluation of reinforcement learning strategies for optimal execution in trading.

A programming paradigm oriented around data flows and the propagation of change. Reactive systems automatically propagate changes through the data flow, making it easier to build responsive, resilient applications.

A standard for asynchronous stream processing with non-blocking back pressure. Reactive Streams defines a minimal set of interfaces for interoperable reactive stream processing across libraries and frameworks.

A database isolation level that guarantees a transaction only sees data that has been committed before it began, preventing dirty reads. It is the default isolation level in many relational databases like PostgreSQL.

A copy of a primary database that receives and serves read-only queries to distribute database load. Read replicas improve performance by offloading read traffic from the primary database and enabling horizontal scaling.

A synchronization mechanism that allows readers to access shared data without locking while writers create updated copies. RCU is widely used in the Linux kernel for high-performance concurrent data access.

READ-Net is an adaptive feature recalibration model designed to enhance audio-visual depression detection by addressing emotional ambiguity, which can lead to misinterpretation of transient emotional expressions as stable depressive symptoms.

READY is a framework for Reward Discovery in Meta-Black-Box Optimization that employs large language models to automate the design of reward functions, reducing expert bias and improving optimization performance.

A passive monitoring technique that captures and analyzes every transaction by real users of a website or application. RUM provides insight into actual user experience including page load times and errors.
A novel error correction code designed for neural networks that detects and corrects memory and computational errors using real-number-based linear constraints on weights.
A technique for accurately estimating food portion sizes from monocular images by reconstructing 3D models that maintain real-world scale, addressing challenges in dietary assessment.

A system or process that responds to input or events within a guaranteed time constraint. Real-time systems are critical in applications where delayed responses could cause failures, such as flight control and industrial automation.
This framework models heat-induced air turbulence in real-time, integrating thermal transport with optical refraction to create accurate 3D simulations.

The process of analyzing data as it is generated or received, enabling immediate insights and decision-making. Real-time analytics powers dashboards, fraud detection, and operational monitoring systems.

The ability for multiple users to work on the same document or resource simultaneously, with changes visible to all participants instantly. Technologies like CRDTs and operational transformation enable this functionality.
A visualization tool for N-body simulations that supports real-time feedback across various operating systems and architectures without external dependencies.
A technique that utilizes biometric technology to capture and display facial expressions in real-time, influencing cooperation in social dilemmas and strategic decision-making.
A method utilizing acoustic signal analysis for the real-time detection of mechanical faults in 3D printers, enhancing reliability and reducing the need for costly visual inspections.

An operating system intended for real-time applications that processes data and events within guaranteed time constraints. RTOS systems are used in embedded devices, robotics, and industrial control systems.
The processing of data as soon as it arrives, producing immediate results with minimal latency. Real-time processing is essential for fraud detection, live analytics, and IoT sensor data streams.
A reinforcement learning algorithm designed for online adaptation of pretrained policies in dynamic environments, particularly for autonomous driving applications.
A bilingual benchmark for evaluating financial reasoning by systematically removing essential premises from exam-style questions, assessing models' ability to handle implicit assumptions.
A framework for accelerating probabilistic logical reasoning in neuro-symbolic AI systems, addressing inefficiencies in symbolic and probabilistic inference.

Reasoning Core is a scalable procedural data generation suite that creates verifiable symbolic reasoning data across various formal domains, including PDDL planning and first-order logic, aimed at enhancing the reasoning capabilities of language models.
A communication paradigm designed for 6G networks that focuses on aligning the internal reasoning processes of autonomous agents to prevent belief divergence.
Statistical tools designed to infer the operations of Boolean circuits by sampling internal computation nodes, providing a probability distribution over possible gate executions.
An optimized system architecture designed to enhance memory bandwidth for reasoning applications in LLMs, addressing the performance bottlenecks associated with memory-bound workloads.
A communication paradigm for 6G networks that addresses belief divergence among autonomous agents by designing communication protocols that account for internal reasoning processes.

A metric in classification that measures the proportion of actual positive instances that were correctly identified by a model. High recall means the model misses few positive instances, which is critical in applications like medical diagnosis.
A memory-efficient framework for encoding and predicting acoustic parameters of sound propagation in virtual scenes, utilizing precomputed impulse responses to facilitate real-time applications.
A residual-conditioned diffusion framework for sparse-view CT reconstruction that employs self-guided sampling based on observation residuals to improve reconstruction accuracy.

A system that predicts and suggests items or content a user might be interested in based on their behavior, preferences, or similarities to other users. Recommendation systems power features on platforms like Netflix, Amazon, and Spotify.

The computational core of a recommender system that implements algorithms to generate personalized suggestions. Recommender engines use techniques like matrix factorization, deep learning, and graph-based methods.

A system that predicts the preference a user would give to an item based on historical data. Recommender systems use collaborative filtering, content-based filtering, or hybrid approaches to personalize suggestions.
Antennas that can adapt their radiation patterns or physical placement, providing additional degrees of freedom for improved performance in wireless communication and sensing systems.
An innovative method for designing sparse antenna arrays in near-field multi-user MIMO systems, allowing dynamic adaptation to user distributions while maintaining performance with fewer elements.
A technology that enhances target localization in integrated sensing and communication systems by utilizing reflected paths to improve positioning accuracy.

The task of identifying records in one or more data sources that refer to the same real-world entity. Record linkage is essential for data integration, deduplication, and building unified customer profiles.

A composite data type that groups related data fields together under a single name. Records are fundamental to structured programming and are implemented as structs in C, dataclasses in Python, and records in Java.

A class of neural networks where connections between nodes form directed cycles, allowing the network to maintain internal state and process sequences of inputs. RNNs are used for time series, natural language processing, and speech recognition.

A programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller, identical subproblems. Recursion elegantly solves problems like tree traversal, factorial computation, and fractal generation, requiring a base case to prevent infinite loops.

A Common Table Expression that references itself, enabling hierarchical or iterative queries in SQL. Recursive CTEs are used to traverse tree structures, generate sequences, and process graph data in relational databases.
The Recursive Gradient Profile Function assigns grayscale values in cellular automata to reveal latent self-similarity and fractal structures, enhancing the visualization of complex systems.

A framework that allows large language models to process extensive contexts by offloading prompts into an external REPL environment, with the potential for deeper recursion to enhance context handling.

Recursive Models leverage recursion to enable long-horizon reasoning in language models, allowing them to invoke themselves for solving subtasks in isolated contexts, surpassing traditional context management approaches.
A specification framework for recursive mutexes in separation logic, allowing for uniform treatment of mutex acquisition and release across multiple threads in object-oriented programming languages.
A method for aggregating multiple zero-knowledge proofs in a scalable manner to facilitate admission control in decentralized services while maintaining user privacy.
A framework that enables recommendation models to enhance their performance autonomously by generating plausible user interaction sequences without relying on external data or teacher models.

An iterative reasoning technique for large language models that enhances answer accuracy by allowing models to engage in multiple reasoning cycles, supported by a confidence generator to assess response certainty.

A group of security professionals who simulate real-world attacks on an organization to test its defenses. Red teaming identifies vulnerabilities before malicious actors can exploit them and improves incident response.

A self-balancing binary search tree where each node stores an extra bit representing its color, either red or black. The tree maintains balance through color constraints, ensuring O(log n) operations.

An HTTP mechanism that automatically forwards a client from one URL to another using status codes like 301 (permanent) or 302 (temporary). Redirects are used for URL changes, authentication flows, and load distribution.

An open-source, in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, message broker, and queue. Redis supports various data structures and delivers sub-millisecond response times, making it ideal for real-time applications and caching layers.

A higher-order function that applies a binary operation to the elements of a collection, accumulating them into a single result. Also called fold, it is fundamental to functional programming and MapReduce frameworks.

The rCDL model is an advanced channel model designed to enhance the accuracy of MIMO performance evaluations by capturing spatial propagation characteristics more effectively than the traditional tapped delay line model.
A novel reduction technique that applies commutativity reasoning to structured concurrent programs, enabling sound verification by preserving interleavings of commutative actions.

The duplication of critical components or functions of a system to increase reliability. In computing, redundancy is applied to hardware, data storage, network paths, and power supplies to prevent single points of failure.
A configuration of multiple storage devices organized to provide improved performance, reliability, or both through data distribution and redundancy techniques such as mirroring and striping.

A predictable state management library for JavaScript applications, commonly used with React. Redux provides a centralized store for application state and enforces unidirectional data flow through actions and reducers.
A lightweight error-correction code encoder designed for superconducting digital electronics, capable of detecting and correcting bit errors in data transmission between SFQ and CMOS circuits.

The process of restructuring existing computer code without changing its external behavior. Refactoring improves code readability, reduces complexity, and makes the software easier to maintain and extend.
A retrieval feedback-driven dataset construction framework that identifies failed retrieval cases and generates datasets that account for stylistic characteristics in query rewriting.

A value that points to or refers to another value or object in memory without copying it. References enable efficient data sharing between different parts of a program and are fundamental to languages like C++, Rust, and Java.

A template solution architecture for a particular domain that provides a common vocabulary and reusable design patterns. Reference architectures accelerate solution design and ensure alignment with best practices.
A technique in reinforcement learning that utilizes human-written reference solutions to guide the fine-tuning of models, addressing reward sparsity in complex mathematical reasoning tasks.

A database concept that ensures relationships between tables remain consistent. Foreign key constraints enforce referential integrity by ensuring that references from one table to another always point to existing records.
A method for refining an approximate Control Barrier Function (CBF) using warm-started Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, enabling online adaptation of safety measures in dynamic environments.

A robust imaging system that utilizes multispectral imaging techniques to estimate soil composition and classify soil texture, enhancing field deployment capabilities compared to traditional laboratory methods.
The ability of a program to examine, introspect, and modify its own structure and behavior at runtime. Reflection is used in frameworks, serialization libraries, and dependency injection containers.
Innovative surfaces designed to enhance data transmission for vehicular communication by overcoming throughput limitations in bandwidth-constrained environments.

A project focused on scalable, GPU-enabled multi-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations for urban airflow prediction, emphasizing performance portability across heterogeneous supercomputing architectures.
A registry-driven architecture that facilitates the deterministic grounding of agentic AI systems in enterprise telemetry, addressing challenges related to model context and evolving metrics.

Regular Expression — a sequence of characters that defines a search pattern, primarily used for string matching and manipulation. Regex is supported in virtually all programming languages and is essential for text processing tasks.

The software component that matches text against regular expression patterns. Regex engines use algorithms like NFA and DFA to process patterns and are embedded in most programming languages and text editors.

A geographical area containing one or more data centers where cloud services are deployed and operated. Selecting the right region affects latency, compliance, and data residency requirements for cloud applications.

A memory management technique that allocates objects in regions or arenas and deallocates them all at once when the region is freed. It reduces fragmentation and allocation overhead compared to individual object deallocation.
A metric derived from functional MRI data used to classify states of advanced concentration absorption meditation, highlighting changes in consciousness and cognitive processing.
A design pattern that provides a global point of access to objects or services, acting as a well-known lookup mechanism. Registries are used in dependency injection containers and service locators.

A statistical method for modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables. Regression analysis is used for prediction, forecasting, and understanding variable relationships.

A set of statistical methods for estimating the relationships between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables. Regression analysis is fundamental to prediction, forecasting, and causal inference.

A type of software testing that verifies existing functionality still works correctly after code changes, bug fixes, or new feature additions. Automated regression test suites catch unintended side effects and prevent previously fixed bugs from reappearing.

A framework utilizing deep learning to approximate optimal multi-item auctions while addressing incentive compatibility through ex post regret measurement, though it faces challenges in accurately estimating true regret.

A sequence of characters that defines a search pattern used for string matching and manipulation. Regular expressions provide a concise and flexible syntax for text processing across programming languages.
Advanced features added to regular expressions, such as backreference and squaring, which enhance their matching capabilities but increase computational complexity.
A technique for simulating nonstationary Gaussian processes by using regular Fourier features to avoid the limitations of traditional spectral methods.
A set of techniques used to prevent overfitting in machine learning models by adding a penalty term to the loss function. Common regularization methods include L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge), and dropout.
A modified algorithm that utilizes regularization techniques combined with denoising methods to enhance convergence properties under specific mathematical conditions.
A model designed for covert semantic entropy control in Low-Altitude Economy Networks, prioritizing high-level semantic understanding over traditional bit-level communication.
An innovative ensemble forecasting technique that combines current forecasts and historical performances to optimize weight assignment, minimizing variance while incorporating a regularization term.
REV-INR is an approach that extends traditional Implicit Neural Representations by incorporating uncertainty awareness, enabling better volume visualization and interpretation of volumetric datasets.

An iterative pseudo-label refinement framework that integrates an audio-aware large language model into the self-training loop for semi-supervised speech recognition, improving transcript accuracy from noisy supervision.

A machine learning paradigm where an agent learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards or penalties. RL powers game-playing AI, robotics, recommendation systems, and autonomous vehicles.

A novel reinforcement learning method that optimizes a generator using rewards derived from an evaluator's answers to natural-language meta-questions, enabling training of language models without requiring ground-truth labels.
A method that employs reinforcement learning to develop adaptive and generalizable policies for handling constraints in complex optimization problems, improving overall optimization performance.

A communication mechanism designed for decentralized Multi-Robot Path Planning that incorporates pairwise Manhattan distances into attention weight computations, allowing robots to prioritize messages from spatially relevant neighbors for improved coordination.
RAC is an online cache eviction strategy designed for large language models that utilizes semantic relationships among requests to improve caching efficiency and reduce latency.
Everyday household devices designed as embodied social actors that engage users through personalized interactions, particularly in the context of health promotion and dietary decision-making.
A framework for understanding how firms adjust their trade relationships during disruptions, emphasizing the role of relational contracts in maintaining partnerships despite temporary setbacks.

A type of database that stores and provides access to data points that are related to one another using tables with rows and columns. Relational databases use SQL for querying and are the most widely deployed database type.
A formal framework that enables robust and transparent transformations in high-level synthesis (HLS) for hardware accelerators, addressing the challenges of manual tuning.
A framework for predicting the perceptual difference between two utterances from the same speaker, focusing on quantifying shifts along an antonymic axis based on subjective evaluations.

R.term: Relative-error framework for Boolean function property testing
R.definition: A testing framework that allows for the evaluation of Boolean functions with a focus on maintaining a relative error, enabling efficient testing of functions with a super-constant number of variables.

A variant of locally decodable codes that allows for efficient decoding with constant query complexity, yet has codeword lengths that are super-polynomial in the message length. This concept is significant for error-correcting codes in theoretical computer science.
The process of planning, scheduling, and controlling the build, test, and deployment of releases. Release management ensures that new features and fixes are delivered reliably and on schedule.

The ability of a system to consistently perform its intended function without failure under stated conditions for a specified period. Reliability engineering focuses on designing systems that maintain uptime and correctness.

An engineering discipline focused on ensuring systems perform their required functions under stated conditions for a specified time. It encompasses fault tolerance, redundancy, testing, and monitoring.

This framework addresses task allocation challenges in edge-hub-cloud architectures by balancing reliability and latency for critical workflow applications, ensuring efficient execution despite device limitations.
A new extract-function and verification toolchain for Rust that operates atop rust-analyzer, providing low-latency refactorings and automated equivalence proofs.
A diffusion-based framework for controllable image generation that utilizes Riemannian geometry to navigate latent space, improving semantic fidelity and inference speed.
The ability to access a computer or network from a remote location, typically through VPN, SSH, or remote desktop protocols. Remote access enables telecommuting and remote administration of systems.

A software or operating system feature that allows a personal computer's desktop environment to be run remotely on one system while being displayed on a separate client device. It enables remote work and support.

A proprietary protocol developed by Microsoft that provides a graphical interface for connecting to another computer over a network. RDP is widely used for remote administration and virtual desktop access.

A protocol that allows a program to execute a procedure on a remote server as if it were a local call. RPC abstracts the complexity of network communication and is the foundation of many distributed systems.

The acquisition of information about an object or phenomenon without making physical contact, typically using satellite or aircraft-based sensor technologies. Remote sensing is used in geography, environmental monitoring, and agriculture.

Resources like CSS and JavaScript files that prevent a browser from rendering page content until they are fully downloaded and processed. Eliminating render-blocking resources is key to improving page load performance.

A tree structure constructed by the browser that combines the DOM tree and CSSOM tree, containing only the nodes required to render the visible content of a page. The render tree drives the layout and painting phases.
The process of generating visual output from code, data, or markup. In web development, rendering refers to converting HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into the visual representation displayed in a browser.

The sequence of steps a graphics system follows to transform 3D scene data into a 2D image on screen. Modern rendering pipelines include vertex processing, rasterization, fragment shading, and compositing.

Rendezvous synchrony is a communication model for distributed processes, allowing for synchronous realizability of global protocol specifications, which has been shown to be decidable under certain conditions.

The process of migrating an application to a new platform with some level of optimization but without changing its core architecture. Replatforming balances modernization benefits with migration risk and effort.

A group of database instances that maintain the same data set, providing redundancy and high availability. In MongoDB, a replica set consists of a primary node and multiple secondary nodes that replicate data.
A learning framework that explores the sample complexity of learning problems, focusing on the dependence of complexity on the size of the hypothesis class and utilizing Cayley graphs for analysis.

The process of sharing data across multiple servers or data centers to ensure consistency, reliability, and fault tolerance. Database replication can be synchronous or asynchronous and is essential for high availability.

A novel approach for automated program repair that enhances fault localization by generating hierarchical code documentation, allowing for better understanding and context-aware repairs across entire code repositories.

The automated process of creating formatted documents that present data and analysis in a structured, readable format. Report generation tools pull data from databases and produce reports for business intelligence.
A central location where data, code, or artifacts are stored and managed. In software development, repositories typically refer to version control systems like Git that track changes to source code.

A design pattern that mediates between the domain and data mapping layers, acting as an in-memory collection of domain objects. It abstracts the data access logic and provides a cleaner separation of concerns.

An architectural style for designing networked applications that relies on stateless, client-server communication over HTTP. REST APIs use standard HTTP methods and are the most common style for web service design.

The ability to obtain consistent results using the same data and methods, which is a fundamental principle of scientific research and machine learning experimentation. Reproducibility requires documenting data, code, and environment configurations.

A build process that produces identical outputs given the same source code, build environment, and instructions. Reproducible builds enable verification that no vulnerabilities or backdoors were introduced during compilation.

ReqElicitGym is an interactive evaluation environment designed for assessing the interview competence of large language models in conversational requirements elicitation, facilitating systematic and quantitative comparisons.
An HTTP message sent by a client to a server to retrieve a resource or trigger an action. Requests include a method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), headers, and optionally a body containing data.

A buffer that holds incoming requests when a server cannot process them immediately. Request queues prevent request loss during traffic spikes and enable backpressure management in distributed systems.
A message exchange pattern where a client sends a request to a server and waits for a corresponding response. It is the fundamental communication pattern of HTTP and most web APIs.
The process of defining, documenting, and maintaining the requirements for a software system. It includes elicitation, analysis, specification, and validation of stakeholder needs.
A technique in online independent component analysis that formalizes the injection of features into a reservoir to improve performance without displacing important passthrough directions.

A method in online independent component analysis (ICA) that formalizes the injection of features into a reservoir while preserving passthrough directions, addressing the bottleneck caused by top-n whitening.

A technique introduced to enhance the efficiency of marginal estimates in differential privacy by integrating residual queries into adaptive mechanisms, improving reconstruction in batch query answering.
An algebraic invariant for free convolutional codes over finite local rings, serving as an intrinsic characteristic of the code that is invariant under equivalent reduced internal degree matrices.

The ability of a system to gracefully handle and recover from failures while maintaining acceptable service levels. Resilience engineering encompasses strategies like circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks, and redundancy.

The discipline of designing systems that can absorb and adapt to unexpected failures rather than simply preventing them. Resilience engineering focuses on graceful degradation, recovery, and learning from incidents.
A complexity problem that determines whether a specified number of edges can be removed from a directed multigraph to prevent it from satisfying a union of conjunctive digraph queries.

The Resilient Federated Chain integrates Blockchain technology with Federated Learning to create an active defense layer that enhances model integrity and data confidentiality against adversarial attacks in decentralized environments.

Strategies designed for decision-making in stochastic environments that maintain robustness against disturbances, ensuring that an agent's decisions remain valid despite potential malfunctions or uncertainties.
The number of pixels displayed on a screen, typically expressed as width by height. Higher resolution provides more detail and sharper images but requires more processing power and bandwidth.

An architecture that utilizes self-organizing sparse hierarchical connectivity in hyperbolic space, achieving dynamic sparsity and reducing computational complexity compared to traditional dense attention mechanisms.

RTDs are semiconductor devices that enable terahertz oscillation and nonlinear mixing, allowing for compact radar sensors that can measure micrometer-scale displacements and thicknesses using self-mixing techniques.
A conflict that arises when multiple processes or threads compete for the same limited resource, such as CPU, memory, or a lock. Resource contention can cause performance degradation and deadlocks.
A W3C standard model for data interchange on the web that represents information as subject-predicate-object triples. RDF is a foundational technology for the Semantic Web and linked data.

The process of planning, allocating, and monitoring computing resources such as CPU, memory, storage, and network bandwidth. Effective resource management ensures optimal performance and cost efficiency.

A collection of pre-allocated computing resources such as threads, connections, or memory blocks that can be reused by multiple consumers. Resource pooling reduces the overhead of creating and destroying resources.

R.term: Resource-Rational Framework for Visual Attention Control
R.definition: A simulation-based framework that models visual attention as a sequential decision-making process under constraints, formalizing visual tasks as bounded-optimal control problems using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes.

A simulation-based framework that models visual attention as a sequential decision-making process under constraints, formalizing tasks as bounded-optimal control problems using Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes.
Resp-Agent is an autonomous multimodal system for respiratory sound generation and disease diagnosis, utilizing an Active Adversarial Curriculum Agent to enhance diagnostic capabilities.

An HTTP message sent by a server back to a client in reply to a request. Responses include a status code indicating success or failure, headers with metadata, and typically a body containing the requested content.
The total time it takes for a system to respond to a request, measured from when the request is sent to when the response is received. Response time is a key performance metric for user experience.

A web design approach that ensures pages look and function well across all screen sizes and devices. Responsive design uses fluid grids, flexible images, and CSS media queries to adapt layouts from mobile phones to desktop monitors.
A method for enhancing trust in human-robot collaboration by enabling robots to proactively adapt their interaction strategies based on user feedback and situational context.

ResQ is a framework designed for generating realistic user workloads for database benchmarking, focusing on performance-aware query generation while addressing privacy concerns associated with real-world SQL queries.

Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for designing networked applications using standard HTTP methods. RESTful APIs are stateless, use predictable URLs for resources, and return data in formats like JSON, making them the most common API design pattern.

An API that conforms to the constraints of REST architectural style, using HTTP methods to perform CRUD operations on resources identified by URLs. REST APIs are stateless and use JSON or XML for data exchange.

A brand name used by Apple for screens with a pixel density high enough that the human eye cannot discern individual pixels at a typical viewing distance. Retina displays require higher-resolution assets in web and app development.
A novel framework for multimodal information retrieval that operates in a training- and data-free manner by formulating retrieval as a similarity score generation task using large language models.
A geometric framework for optimizing quantum circuits, formulated as energy minimization over the unitary group, ensuring compatibility with quantum hardware implementations.

The process of resending data packets that were lost, corrupted, or unacknowledged during network transmission. TCP uses retransmission mechanisms with exponential backoff to ensure reliable data delivery.
A structural risk in information retrieval characterized by the dominance of AI-generated content, leading to decreased source diversity and potential quality degradation in search results.

An AI framework that enhances language model outputs by retrieving relevant information from external knowledge sources before generating responses. RAG combines the strengths of retrieval systems and generative models.
A framework that combines retrieval mechanisms with generative models to enhance the performance of natural language processing tasks by providing relevant context during generation.
A technique that combines retrieval methods like multi-query retrieval and reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) to enhance document recall in RAG systems, particularly in production environments.
An inference-time safety mechanism for code generation in Large Language Models that utilizes retrieval techniques to adaptively revise generated code based on evolving security standards.
An approach for enabling robots to actively retrieve information before executing tasks, addressing the information gap in zero-shot settings where prior demonstrations are unavailable.

Retrodictive forecasting is a paradigm that identifies the future best explaining the observed present through inverse MAP optimization over a Conditional Variational Autoencoder, leveraging temporal asymmetry in time series prediction.

A strategy for handling transient failures by automatically re-attempting a failed operation after a delay. Retry patterns often include exponential backoff and jitter to avoid overwhelming the target service.

A design pattern for handling transient failures in distributed systems by automatically retrying failed operations with configurable delays and backoff strategies. The retry pattern improves reliability without manual intervention.
A system that employs OS-driven hash-based speculative address translation to enhance data fetching speed by predicting physical addresses early in the pipeline.

The total income generated by a business from its normal operations, typically from the sale of goods and services. Revenue analysis helps businesses track growth, profitability, and market performance.

The practice of maximizing revenue through strategic pricing, product bundling, customer segmentation, and demand forecasting. Revenue optimization uses data analytics to identify opportunities for increased income.

An evaluation framework designed to test large language models' susceptibility to invisible Unicode-encoded instructions embedded in normal text, exploiting the models' ability to perceive Unicode control characters that are not visible to human readers.

The process of resolving an IP address back to its associated domain name, the opposite of standard DNS lookup. Reverse DNS is used for network troubleshooting, email verification, and security logging.

The process of analyzing a system or software to identify its components and their interrelationships and to extract design information. Reverse engineering is used in malware analysis, security research, and interoperability.

This programming language introduces a novel way to construct neural networks using algebraic effects and handlers, allowing for high-level network descriptions while enabling flexible implementation assignments like backpropagation.

A server that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them, appearing to clients as the origin server. Reverse proxies provide load balancing, caching, SSL termination, and security, commonly implemented with Nginx or Cloudflare.
A novel small-step semantics for the Janus programming language that maintains reversibility, ensuring that every reduction has an inverse, which is crucial for debugging and concurrency extensions.

A new semantics for reversible programming languages that maintains information during execution, allowing for both forward and backward execution of programs, enhancing debugging and concurrency capabilities.

A method for rapid failure recovery in large-scale large language model (LLM) deployments that avoids the costly process of restarting serving instances, thus minimizing delays in model-as-a-service (MaaS) inference settings.
A sampling method from a diffusion model that incorporates a reward function to influence the sampling process, allowing for fine-grained control over the generated outputs.

A framework that constructs instance-level graphs from tabular data using proximity measures induced by random forests, enabling effective modeling of complex relationships in datasets lacking inherent graph structures.

Request for Comments — a formal document published by the Internet Engineering Task Force that describes standards, protocols, and conventions for the internet. RFCs define core protocols like HTTP, TCP, and DNS.
A benchmark designed for evaluating large language models on financial misinformation detection, focusing on contextual complexity in financial news.

Radio-Frequency Identification — a technology that uses electromagnetic fields to automatically identify and track tags attached to objects. RFID is used in inventory management, access control, and asset tracking.
A cloud-offloaded ransomware defense system that employs format-aware validation to detect privilege-escalated evasive ransomware attacks, enhancing data security.

Rhythm is a unified framework for interactive whole-body control of dual humanoid robots, designed to facilitate complex collaborative interactions in shared environments by addressing kinematic mismatches and contact dynamics.

A web component that allows users to create and edit formatted text with features like bold, italics, links, and images. Rich text editors use contentEditable or custom renderers to provide WYSIWYG editing.
An end-to-end pipeline that utilizes a three-camera rig to capture videos of vehicle undercarriages, producing interactive 3D models for inspection and analysis of structural integrity.
A principle advocating for a tamper-evident and verifiable record of actions performed by AI agents on behalf of individuals, addressing regulatory requirements for accountability in AI systems.

A method for protein design that incorporates rigidity-aware geometric representations during pretraining, enabling the model to learn both geometric and design tasks effectively. This approach addresses limitations in current generative models for protein structures.

RigMo is a unified generative framework that simultaneously learns rig and motion from raw mesh sequences, eliminating the need for human-provided rig annotations and enhancing scalability and interpretability in animation generation.
A robust incremental and distributed back-end for Collaborative Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (C-SLAM) that is resilient to outliers and effective in real-world deployments.

A fixed-size circular data structure that uses a single, fixed-size buffer as if it were connected end-to-end. Ring buffers are efficient for buffering data streams and are widely used in audio processing and networking.
A system designed to manage high-throughput host OS system calls from TrustZone Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), ensuring timely processing of sensor inputs in safety-critical applications.
A change-driven testing system that explores the broader impacts of code changes through the GUI, leveraging LLMs to identify change-induced issues.
A web-based tool developed to support assessment authoring for educators, featuring multilevel reusable interactions that streamline the creation of formative assessments.

A coordinate-guided transformer model for the continuous reconstruction of room impulse responses, utilizing sinusoidal encoding and a segmented multi-branch decoder for improved acoustic signal processing.

RIRFlow is a training-free Bayesian framework for room impulse response (RIR) inverse problems that utilizes flow matching and an analytic Wiener denoiser, eliminating the need for data-driven priors.
A communication system that utilizes reconfigurable intelligent surfaces to enhance the secrecy rate in multi-input multi-output setups, accounting for non-ideal effects of reflecting elements.

Reduced Instruction Set Computer — a CPU design philosophy that uses a small, highly optimized set of instructions rather than a large set of specialized instructions. ARM and RISC-V are prominent RISC architectures.

An open-standard instruction set architecture based on reduced instruction set computer principles. RISC-V is royalty-free and has gained adoption in embedded systems, research, and increasingly in commercial processors.

The potential for loss or negative outcome associated with a decision, investment, or action. In technology, risk management involves identifying, assessing, and mitigating threats to systems and projects.

The process of identifying, analyzing, and evaluating potential risks to an organization's information assets. Risk assessments help prioritize security investments and determine appropriate controls for identified threats.

The systematic process of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and treating potential risks that could affect an organization's objectives. In IT, risk management covers security threats, project risks, and operational vulnerabilities.

A visual tool that plots the likelihood of a risk occurring against its potential impact to prioritize risk response. Risk matrices are used in cybersecurity, project management, and compliance to guide decision-making.
A novel risk assessment metric that aggregates instantaneous risk intensity during occlusions, providing a comprehensive profile of risk for connected and automated vehicles.
A stochastic optimal design approach for elliptic partial differential equations that minimizes a generalized risk measure of system response under uncertainty in loading conditions.

A solution concept in multi-agent reinforcement learning that incorporates risk aversion and bounded rationality, addressing the challenge of learning stationary policies in infinite-horizon general-sum Markov games.

An integrated framework for wildfire monitoring that optimizes UAV route planning, fleet sizing, and edge service provisioning, focusing on minimizing response time while considering energy and capacity constraints.
A control strategy for end-to-end autonomous driving that incorporates risk assessment to improve decision-making in rare or unseen scenarios, enhancing model generalization.
A neural operator designed to learn joint SPX-VIX term structures under no-arbitrage constraints, incorporating static arbitrage enforcement and Lipschitz bounds.

A framework leveraging large language model agents for the reliable detection of configuration drift in Infrastructure as Code (IaC) systems, automating the analysis of telemetry data to ensure consistency with IaC specifications.

This approach employs reinforcement learning for coverage path planning of deformable objects on 3D surfaces, addressing challenges in manipulation tasks that require both vision and tactile feedback.

An agent-driven framework for reinforcement learning that dynamically assigns tailored reward functions to individual queries, enhancing the alignment of large language models through adaptive reward acquisition.

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — a technique for training AI models by incorporating human preferences into the reinforcement learning process. RLHF is used to align language models with human values and improve response quality.

A serverless computing framework designed to enhance the efficiency of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) by dynamically managing resource demands during training and inference processes.

An end-to-end framework that utilizes reinforcement learning to optimize mesh grid points in simulations of parametric partial differential equations, focusing computational resources on regions critical for accuracy.
A specialized finetuning technique for the Qwen3-8B model, optimized for efficient performance in financial text classification tasks, enhancing instruction-following and multilingual capabilities.
A reinforcement learning framework designed for multi-agent cyber defense in financial systems, focusing on real-time response orchestration and modeling attack-surface dynamics.

A benchmark designed to evaluate memory-dependent robotic manipulation policies, focusing on the relationship between architectural design choices and memory performance in real-world manipulation tasks.

Root Mean Square Error — a commonly used metric for measuring the differences between predicted and observed values. RMSE gives a relatively high weight to large errors and is widely used in regression model evaluation.
An advanced snapshot algorithm for asynchronous concurrent shared-memory systems that allows processes to perform complex read-modify-write operations atomically.
A reinforcement learning framework that utilizes structured coarse-grained sparsity to enhance training efficiency while minimizing performance loss in deep reinforcement learning models.

Recurrent Neural Network — a class of neural networks where connections between nodes form a directed cycle, allowing the network to maintain internal state. RNNs are designed for sequential data processing.
RO-N3WS is a benchmark dataset for Romanian speech designed to enhance generalization in automatic speech recognition, featuring over 126 hours of diverse transcribed audio from various domains.
An optimal control method applied to the Boussinesq equations, where control is introduced through a Robin boundary condition on temperature, ensuring well-posedness and optimality conditions.

RobMRAG is a framework for zero-shot robotic manipulation that utilizes 3D Gaussian splatting to enhance multimodal retrieval-augmented generation, improving generalization to unseen objects and tasks.
A motion generation system for virtual reality games that simulates player movements based on in-game object arrangements, trained on a large dataset to reflect diverse player behaviors.
A framework designed to accelerate GPU-based collision detection for autonomous robots, enhancing real-time motion planning capabilities.

A scalable reward modeling framework for general-purpose robotics that combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preference supervision, enhancing the training process with a dual objective.
An intelligent multi-agent framework that integrates large language models and visual language models to solve robotic manipulation problems using both textual and visual inputs.

The use of software robots to automate repetitive, rule-based tasks typically performed by humans. RPA bots interact with applications through their user interfaces to process transactions and manipulate data.

The interdisciplinary field that integrates computer science, engineering, and AI to design, build, and operate robots. Robotics applications range from manufacturing automation to surgical systems and autonomous vehicles.
A framework designed for learning control policies that maintain inverted poses in miniature blimp robots, utilizing a high-fidelity simulation environment calibrated with real-world data.

A fragment of differential dynamic logic designed to specify and reason about robust hybrid systems, ensuring that definable properties remain topologically open and robust against infinitesimal perturbations.
Sets that characterize the domains of attraction for nonlinear systems, allowing for the estimation of safe and robust domains under uncertainties and state constraints.
An extension of the Kaczmarz method designed to handle nearly singular linear systems, improving convergence rates in ill-conditioned scenarios.
A method that enhances the sensing range in OFDM-based radar systems by mitigating intersymbol interference and intercarrier interference through interference cleaning techniques.
An extension of Markov Decision Processes that incorporates uncertainty in transition probabilities, focusing on optimizing decisions against worst-case scenarios.
An extension of mean-field game theory that incorporates risk aversion and bounded rationality to model agent behavior under distributional uncertainty and cognitive constraints.
A framework for verifying strategies in stochastic multi-agent systems with imperfect information, focusing on coalition compliance with temporal specifications amidst uncertain transitions.

R.term: Robust Optimal Portfolio with Partial Ambiguity
R.definition: A robust portfolio optimization approach that manages financial risk in scenarios where some risk distributions are known and others are ambiguous, utilizing a projected subgradient descent algorithm.
A technique that improves informed down-sampling by excluding potential outliers from the sampling process, enhancing performance in symbolic regression tasks.
A problem in scheduling where job processing times may deviate under budgeted uncertainty, solvable in polynomial time for two machines and approximable for any fixed number of machines.
A private inference protocol that addresses privacy concerns by allowing secure inference without revealing sensitive data, specifically designed to handle malicious client scenarios.

A novel algorithm designed for robust distributed nonconvex optimization that enhances security by introducing random noise to obfuscate local variable transmissions among agents in a multi-agent network.

A study on the robust satisfiability of promise constraint satisfaction problems (PCSPs), demonstrating hardness results under the Unique Games conjecture and exploring the limits of constraint satisfaction.
A framework for maximizing the total value in tournaments with probabilistic match outcomes, focusing on player popularity and the dynamics of match results.

The ability of a system or model to maintain performance when faced with unexpected inputs, adversarial attacks, or changing conditions. Robust models generalize well and resist perturbations in input data.

Return on Investment — a performance measure used to evaluate the efficiency of an investment by comparing the gain from the investment relative to its cost. ROI is a key metric for business decision-making.

A region-of-interest driven extraction framework for optimizing X-ray Computed Tomography imaging strategies, addressing challenges in data reduction and reconstruction in high-performance computing environments.

The process of analyzing existing user permissions and access patterns to discover optimal roles for role-based access control. Role mining uses data mining techniques to identify natural groupings of permissions.

The process of reverting a system or database to a previous state after a failed deployment or erroneous change. Rollback mechanisms are essential for maintaining system stability and minimizing downtime.

A deployment strategy that gradually replaces instances of the previous version with the new version one at a time. Rolling deployments minimize downtime and allow monitoring for issues during the rollout.
Read-Only Memory — a type of non-volatile memory that retains its contents even when powered off. ROM stores firmware and boot instructions that are essential for starting up computer systems.
A benchmarking framework that utilizes the Roofline model to characterize the performance of on-device Large Language Models, focusing on operational intensity and resource constraints.
A method of problem-solving used to identify the fundamental reason for a fault or failure. RCA goes beyond treating symptoms to find and address the underlying cause, preventing recurrence.

A public key certificate issued by a trusted certificate authority that sits at the top of a certificate chain of trust. Root certificates are pre-installed in browsers and operating systems to validate SSL/TLS connections.

A collection of malicious software designed to enable unauthorized access to a computer while actively hiding its presence. Rootkits can modify the operating system to conceal files, processes, and network connections.

ROS 2 Agnocast is a middleware solution that supports true zero-copy inter-process communication for unsized message types in robot applications, enhancing efficiency and real-time performance in large-scale systems.
ROSA (Roundabout Optimized Speed Advisory) is a system that integrates multi-agent trajectory prediction with coordinated speed guidance for mixed traffic scenarios at roundabouts.
A few-shot robotic sequence retrieval method that extracts reusable, task-centric segments from unlabeled logs, addressing the challenge of scarce labeled training data.

RosettaStone 2.0 is an open benchmark translation and evaluation framework that provides RTL-to-GDS reference flows for both 2D and 3D designs, facilitating standardized comparisons across different implementation settings.
A method for positional encoding in transformer models that decomposes into frequency components, enhancing understanding of shared attention mechanisms in multimodal contexts.
A framework that regularizes transport plans for exotic derivatives pricing under sparse market data, addressing challenges posed by non-modelable risk factors.

A scheduling algorithm that assigns equal time slices to each process in circular order without priority. Round robin is used in CPU scheduling, DNS load balancing, and request distribution across servers.

A benchmark designed to evaluate the consistency of code understanding and execution in Large Language Models by testing their ability to maintain a one-to-one mapping during encoding and decoding operations.

A networking device that forwards data packets between computer networks using routing tables and protocols. Routers operate at the network layer of the OSI model and are essential infrastructure for the internet.
The process of determining the path that data or a request takes through a network or application. In web development, routing maps URL patterns to specific handler functions or components.

A retrieval-augmented generation architecture that dynamically adapts its retrieval strategy based on the complexity of the query and the sufficiency of evidence, designed for efficient operation on consumer-grade hardware.

A database storage format that stores data row by row, keeping all columns of a record together on disk. Row stores are optimized for transactional workloads where entire records are frequently read or written.

A database feature that restricts which rows a user can access in a table based on the user's identity or role. Row-level security policies enable fine-grained access control without modifying application queries.

Robotic Process Automation — the use of software robots to automate repetitive, rule-based business processes such as data entry, invoice processing, and report generation. RPA increases efficiency and reduces human error.

Remote Procedure Call — a protocol that allows a program to execute a procedure on a remote server as if it were a local call. Modern RPC frameworks like gRPC and tRPC provide type-safe, efficient communication between distributed services.
RPG-AE is a neuro-symbolic anomaly detection framework that combines Graph Autoencoders with rare pattern mining to detect Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) in system-level provenance data.
An event-only drone trajectory forecasting method that integrates propeller rotational speed information into a Kalman filtering framework to improve prediction accuracy.
A robust learning framework for regression neural networks that minimizes the impact of outliers and data contamination by utilizing β-divergences for training.

Rivest-Shamir-Adleman — one of the first public-key cryptosystems widely used for secure data transmission. RSA's security relies on the practical difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers.

RSHBench is a protocol-based benchmark for diagnosing factual and logical hallucinations in multimodal large language models, specifically targeting issues in remote sensing visual question-answering.

An application protocol providing reactive streams semantics for communication between services. RSocket supports request-response, fire-and-forget, request-stream, and channel interaction models over TCP or WebSocket.

Really Simple Syndication — a web feed format used to publish frequently updated content such as blog posts, news headlines, and podcasts. RSS allows users to subscribe to content and receive updates in a standardized format.

Real-Time Bidding — a programmatic advertising auction process where ad impressions are bought and sold in real-time as a web page loads. RTB enables advertisers to bid on individual impressions based on user data.
RTeAAL Sim reformulates RTL simulation as a sparse tensor algebra problem, allowing for decoupling of simulation behavior from binary size, thereby accelerating the simulation process.

RTLocating is an intent-aware RTL localization framework that formalizes the mapping of natural language change requests to affected Register Transfer Level (RTL) syntactic blocks, facilitating iterative hardware design updates.

Real-Time Operating System — an operating system designed to serve real-time applications that process data as it comes in, typically without buffer delays. RTOS is used in embedded systems, robotics, and industrial control.

A dynamic, open-source programming language with a focus on simplicity and productivity, known for its elegant syntax. Ruby is widely used in web development, particularly with the Ruby on Rails framework.

A server-side web application framework written in Ruby that follows the model-view-controller pattern and emphasizes convention over configuration. Rails prioritizes developer productivity and rapid application development.
