A modeling error that occurs when a machine learning model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data. Underfitting results in poor performance on both training and test datasets.
A graph in which edges have no orientation, meaning the connection between any two nodes is bidirectional. Undirected graphs model symmetric relationships like social connections or network topology.

A feature that reverses the most recent action or series of actions in an application. Implementing undo functionality typically uses the command pattern or a history stack to track and revert state changes.

A database log that stores previous versions of data before modifications, enabling transaction rollback if needed. It is essential for maintaining ACID properties and supporting multi-version concurrency control.
A data structure that stores a history of operations to enable undo and redo functionality. Each action is pushed onto the stack, and undo pops the most recent action and applies its inverse.

A software pattern that allows users to reverse and reapply actions, typically implemented using the Command pattern with a history stack. It is fundamental to text editors, design tools, and most interactive applications.

A Unified Skill-centric framework that supports skill-aware planning and facilitates automatic evolution of skill libraries in robotic manipulation tasks, enhancing adaptability without manual intervention.

A one-to-one network communication method where data is sent from a single source to a single destination. It is the most common form of data transmission on the internet, used for web browsing and email.
The process of forwarding network packets from a single source to a single destination using routing protocols like OSPF or BGP. It is the most common routing paradigm on the internet.

A universal character encoding standard that assigns a unique code point to every character in every writing system. Unicode enables consistent text representation across platforms and languages, supporting over 150,000 characters.

The process of converting Unicode text to a standard form so that equivalent strings have identical binary representations. Normalization forms like NFC and NFD handle composed and decomposed character representations.
A privately held startup company valued at over one billion dollars. The term was coined by venture capitalist Aileen Lee in 2013 to reflect the statistical rarity of such successful ventures.
A worker process in the Unicorn HTTP server for Ruby on Rails applications. The master-worker architecture allows zero-downtime deploys by forking new workers before killing old ones.

A process in the Unicorn HTTP server for Ruby that handles incoming web requests. Unicorn uses a pre-forking worker model where the master process manages multiple worker processes for concurrent request handling.
A unified framework for scientific document retrieval that integrates facet-aware retrieval methods to address mismatches between document-centric and question-driven retrieval approaches.

U.term: Unified AI-driven Data QC and DataOps Management
U.definition: A comprehensive framework that integrates various data quality control methods into a continuous governance layer, enhancing data integrity and management in regulated environments.

An API that provides a single interface to interact with multiple third-party services or data sources. Unified APIs abstract away differences between providers, simplifying integrations with CRMs, payment processors, or cloud services.

The integration of enterprise communication services such as instant messaging, voice, video, data sharing, and call control into a single platform. UC improves collaboration and productivity by providing a seamless user experience.

An architecture that combines data lake and data warehouse capabilities into a single platform for storing and analyzing structured and unstructured data. It eliminates data silos and reduces data duplication.

A standardized format for showing differences between two files, displaying context lines and marking additions with + and deletions with -. Unified diffs are the default output of git diff and patch utilities.
A framework for analyzing explanations of machine learning model predictions, encompassing sufficient and contrastive reasons across different probabilistic and non-probabilistic contexts.
A comprehensive framework that integrates learning-based argument mining with quantitative reasoning and ontology-based querying to enhance the analysis of debates and complex texts.
A comprehensive approach to analyze and derive sufficient and contrastive explanations for machine learning model predictions, integrating various computational complexity aspects.

A framework that maps various dexterous hand morphologies into a shared codebook, facilitating improved generalization and scalability in dexterous hand manipulation guided by free-form language commands.
A method for merging documents from multiple specialized retrieval channels in large-scale e-commerce search, optimizing for user conversion while adhering to latency constraints.
The UMQ framework jointly addresses both missing and noisy modalities in multimodal data, enhancing model robustness by treating these issues as a unified low-quality modality problem.

A standardized general-purpose modeling language used in software engineering to visualize, specify, construct, and document the artifacts of a software system. UML includes various diagram types such as class, sequence, and use case diagrams.
A motion planning strategy for Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships that ensures compliance with maritime regulations while navigating dynamic environments.
An architecture designed to enhance personal information retrieval by integrating temporal, spatial, and activity metadata, aligning with human memory recall mechanisms.
A computationally efficient framework for certifying optimality in sparse generalized linear models, reformulating relaxations as composite optimization problems that converge linearly.
A resource management strategy in aerial heterogeneous networks that aims to balance communication reliability and performance under diverse delay constraints and user mobility.
This paradigm focuses on region-of-interest (ROI) based image compression using a generalized Gaussian model to improve coding performance by adapting to distribution characteristics.

A search interface that queries multiple data sources and content types simultaneously, presenting results in a single unified view. It simplifies the user experience by eliminating the need to search different systems separately.

A network security appliance that combines multiple security functions including firewall, intrusion detection, antivirus, and content filtering into a single device. UTM simplifies security management for small and medium businesses.

A probability distribution where all outcomes are equally likely, such as rolling a fair die. In computing, uniform distributions are used for random number generation and initializing neural network weights.

An adaptation of Pitts' proof-theoretic technique for uniform interpolation to the intuitionistic multi-succedent setting, enabling the construction of uniform interpolants for intuitionistic modal logic KM.
UniG2U-Bench is a comprehensive benchmark that categorizes generation-to-understanding evaluation into multiple regimes and subtasks, assessing the effectiveness of unified multimodal models.

Specialized single-address-space machine images constructed by compiling only the OS components needed by a specific application. They offer minimal attack surfaces, tiny footprints, and fast boot times compared to traditional virtual machines.

A unified framework that integrates mesh and 3D Gaussian Splatting rendering with single-pass rasterization and proxy-based deformation, addressing occlusion and transparency challenges in graphics applications.

A disjoint-set data structure that tracks elements partitioned into non-overlapping subsets, supporting efficient union and find operations. It is commonly used in graph algorithms like Kruskal's minimum spanning tree.

A type system feature that allows a variable to hold values of multiple specified types. Union types are commonly used in TypeScript, Python, and Rust to express that a value could be one of several types.

The problem of approximating the volume of the union of multiple geometric objects, which can be solved in optimal time using specific query types. This estimation is crucial in various applications including computer graphics and spatial data analysis.

Uniqueness type systems allow for efficient mutation in programs while maintaining a semantics that supports value independence, facilitating easier reasoning about software systems and formal verification.
A unified framework for scale-aware 3D reconstruction from multi-view images, integrating geometric priors to enhance robotic perception and navigation tasks.

UniSkill is a dataset that pairs university curricula with professional competencies, providing both manually annotated and synthetic data based on the ESCO taxonomy to address skill extraction challenges.

The direct revenues and costs associated with a particular business model expressed on a per-unit basis. Key metrics include customer acquisition cost (CAC) and lifetime value (LTV), crucial for evaluating startup viability.

A design pattern that maintains a list of objects affected by a business transaction and coordinates writing out changes. It ensures data integrity by committing all changes as a single atomic operation.

A software testing method where individual components or functions are tested in isolation to verify they work correctly. Unit tests are fast, focused, and form the foundation of the testing pyramid, typically written using frameworks like Jest, PyTest, or JUnit.
A modular framework that integrates text-to-speech and audio-to-face modeling, facilitating internal feature transfer to improve consistency between audio and facial expressions.

A unified, end-to-end diffusion framework for generating high-fidelity speech and lip-synchronized video, leveraging Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks to model the temporal correspondence between audio and video.
A cross-platform game engine developed by Unity Technologies, widely used for creating 2D and 3D games, simulations, and AR/VR experiences. It supports C# scripting and deploys to over 25 platforms.

A mathematical theorem stating that a feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate any continuous function. This theoretical result underpins the expressiveness of neural networks.

An executable file that contains native code for multiple CPU architectures, allowing it to run natively on different processor types. Apple's Universal Binaries contain both x86-64 and ARM64 code for Intel and Apple Silicon Macs.
The UCP formalizes clustering as the maximization of a computable partition utility over metric spaces, proving NP-hardness and providing a unifying framework for various clustering paradigms.

UCL is a mathematical framework for systematic prompt optimization in AI models, transforming prompt engineering into a structured process that significantly reduces token usage and improves performance.

JavaScript code that can run in both server and client environments, also known as isomorphic JavaScript. Universal JavaScript enables server-side rendering with client-side hydration for improved performance and SEO.
A novel architecture designed to enhance wireless network performance by leveraging artificial intelligence to optimize spectral efficiency and reduce signaling overhead.
A framework aimed at restoring speech quality across various degradation conditions, focusing on training target selection and data quality to enhance signal fidelity.

U.term: Universality of Shallow and Deep Neural Networks on Non-Euclidean Spaces
U.definition: A framework that establishes the universal approximation property for neural networks operating over general topological spaces, extending results beyond traditional Euclidean settings.

Algorithms that are optimal for every input graph in the worst case, extending the concept of universal optimality to data structures, particularly in dynamic graph problems involving vertex-weighted trees.

An efficient continual multi-task training framework for universal audio representation that integrates various audio tasks into a unified instruction format, improving performance across diverse audio domains.

A family of multitasking, multi-user operating systems that originated at Bell Labs in the 1970s. Unix philosophy — small, composable tools that do one thing well — profoundly influenced Linux, macOS, and modern software development practices.

A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems that runs commands or scripts automatically at specified intervals. Cron uses a configuration file called crontab to define schedules using a five-field syntax.

An inter-process communication mechanism that allows data exchange between processes running on the same host using the file system as the address namespace. It is faster than TCP/IP loopback for local communication.

The point in time defined as January 1, 1970 00:00:00 UTC, used as the reference point for Unix timestamps. Time is represented as the number of seconds elapsed since this epoch in many programming languages and systems.

A set of software development principles originating from the Unix operating system that advocates building small, focused programs that do one thing well and work together through text streams. It emphasizes simplicity, modularity, and composability.

A mechanism for inter-process communication that connects the standard output of one process to the standard input of another. Pipes enable the chaining of simple commands to perform complex data transformations.

A software interrupt sent to a process to notify it of an event, such as SIGTERM for termination or SIGKILL for forced kill. Signal handling is essential for graceful shutdown and process management in Unix systems.

A system for tracking time as the number of seconds elapsed since January 1, 1970 UTC (the Unix epoch). Unix timestamps provide a simple, language-agnostic way to represent moments in time across distributed systems.
A decentralized Federated Learning framework that employs a peer-based mixnet for unlinkability, allowing for secure model sharing without revealing participant identities.

Code that executes directly on the operating system without a managed runtime environment like the CLR or JVM. C and C++ produce unmanaged code that handles its own memory management and system interaction.

A language feature that extracts values from data structures like arrays, tuples, or objects into individual variables. Known as destructuring in JavaScript and unpacking in Python, it enables concise and readable code.

A powerful game engine developed by Epic Games, known for high-fidelity graphics and used in AAA game development, film production, and architectural visualization. It uses C++ and a visual scripting system called Blueprints.

A variant of a linked list where each node stores an array of elements instead of a single element. Unrolled linked lists improve cache performance and reduce memory overhead from pointers compared to standard linked lists.

Code blocks in memory-safe languages like Rust and C# that bypass normal safety checks to perform low-level operations like raw pointer manipulation. Unsafe code requires explicit marking and careful review to avoid memory errors.

A method for synthesizing training data from code graphs to improve the performance of large language models in newly released software frameworks, addressing issues of hallucination and understanding codebase relationships.

An integer data type that can only represent non-negative values, using all bits for magnitude rather than reserving one for sign. Unsigned integers double the maximum positive value compared to their signed counterparts.

Data that does not conform to a predefined data model or schema, such as text, images, audio, and video. It constitutes the majority of enterprise data and requires specialized tools like NLP and computer vision to analyze.
A two-stage flow-feature-based pipeline for unsupervised IoT device traffic profiling that utilizes density-based clustering to adaptively update models as traffic evolves.

U.term: Unsupervised Continual Learning for Amortized Bayesian Inference
U.definition: A framework that enhances Amortized Bayesian Inference by decoupling simulation-based pre-training from unsupervised sequential self-consistency fine-tuning, enabling the model to adapt to sequentially arriving data and distribution shifts.
A two-stage pipeline for profiling IoT device traffic using unsupervised learning techniques, specifically density-based clustering, to adaptively update models as traffic patterns evolve.
A type of machine learning where models learn patterns from unlabeled data without explicit guidance. Common techniques include clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.
An unsupervised training strategy for hyperspectral image super-resolution that utilizes synthetic abundance data, decomposing images into abundances and endmembers through unmixing.

Unveiler is a framework designed for robotic manipulation in cluttered environments, which separates high-level spatial reasoning from low-level action execution, utilizing a Spatial Relationship Encoder to identify critical obstacles.

Universal Product Code is a barcode symbology widely used for tracking trade items in stores. In tech, barcode scanning and UPC databases are fundamental to inventory management and point-of-sale systems.

The process of treating a derived class object as an instance of its base class. Upcasting is implicit and safe in object-oriented programming, enabling polymorphism by allowing base class references to hold derived class objects.

Unsupervised Progressive Domain Adaptation (UPDA) is a framework for no-reference point cloud quality assessment that employs a two-stage coarse-to-fine alignment paradigm to mitigate performance degradation across domain shifts.
A data structure designed for efficient similarity search in large-scale datasets that supports real-time updates, addressing the challenges posed by frequent data shifts.

An inconsistency that arises when updating data in a poorly normalized database, requiring the same change in multiple places. Update anomalies are resolved through database normalization to eliminate redundancy.

A memory-efficient context parallelism technique that utilizes headwise chunking to enhance the processing of long sequences in Transformer models, optimizing memory usage while maintaining performance.
The process of transferring data from a local device to a remote server or cloud service. Upload speed is typically slower than download speed in consumer internet connections.

Universal Plug and Play is a set of networking protocols that allows devices to discover each other and establish network services automatically. While convenient, UPnP can pose security risks if exposed to the internet.

Internet Gateway Device is a UPnP profile that allows applications to automatically configure port forwarding on routers. It simplifies network configuration for applications like gaming and VoIP but poses security risks.
A structural result showing that online maximization of non-monotone Diminishing-Return submodular functions can be linearized under specific conditions, leading to improved regret bounds.

A technique used to increase the resolution of data, commonly applied in image processing and to address class imbalance in machine learning datasets. In neural networks, upsampling layers increase spatial dimensions of feature maps.

The process of increasing the resolution of images or video using algorithms, often powered by deep learning models like super-resolution networks. AI upscaling can generate realistic detail that wasn't present in the original low-resolution input.

A sales technique where a customer is encouraged to purchase a higher-end product or add-on to increase the transaction value. In SaaS, upselling often involves upgrading users to premium tiers with more features.

A database operation that inserts a new record if it does not exist, or updates the existing record if it does. This atomic operation simplifies data synchronization by combining insert and update logic into a single command.

In software development, the original source repository or project from which a fork or derivative is created. Upstream changes are typically pulled into downstream projects to stay current with improvements and fixes.

A component or service that another component depends on for its functionality. Understanding upstream dependencies is crucial for impact analysis, change management, and incident response in complex systems.

The process of integrating changes from a downstream fork or branch back into the original upstream repository. Upstream merges contribute improvements back to the source project in open-source development.

The amount of time a system or service is operational and available, typically expressed as a percentage. Five nines (99.999%) uptime allows only about 5 minutes of downtime per year, and is a key metric in service level agreements (SLAs).

A service level agreement that guarantees a minimum percentage of time a service will be operational, typically expressed as 99.9% or 99.99%. Violations of uptime SLAs usually entitle customers to service credits.
Urban Antifragility is a concept that describes how urban environments can benefit and grow stronger from shocks and stresses, as opposed to merely surviving them.

A decentralized personal server platform that provides a clean-slate software stack for peer-to-peer networking. It features its own programming language (Hoon) and aims to give users sovereignty over their digital identity and data.
UrgenGo is a non-intrusive, urgency-aware GPU scheduling system designed for autonomous driving applications, which prioritizes GPU executions without needing access to application source code.
Uniform Resource Identifier is a string that identifies a resource either by location (URL), name (URN), or both. URIs are the superset of URLs and URNs and form the foundation of web addressing.

Uniform Resource Locator — the address used to access resources on the internet, specifying the protocol, domain, path, and optional parameters. URLs are fundamental to web navigation and API design, with clean URL structures improving SEO and usability.

The process of converting characters in a URL into a format that can be transmitted over the internet, replacing unsafe characters with percent-encoded equivalents. For example, spaces become %20 or +.

The process of decomposing a URL into its component parts including scheme, host, port, path, query string, and fragment. URL parsing is fundamental to web servers, routers, and HTTP client libraries.

A server-side technique that translates a requested URL into a different URL internally, often used to create clean, SEO-friendly URLs. It is commonly configured in web servers like Apache and Nginx using rewrite rules.

A service that converts long URLs into abbreviated links that redirect to the original address. URL shorteners are useful for sharing links on character-limited platforms and for tracking click analytics.
A unified generative framework designed to improve performance in automatic speech recognition and speech enhancement across varying noise and channel conditions.

The ease with which users can learn and use a product to achieve their goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily. Usability testing involves observing real users interacting with a product to identify pain points and improvement opportunities.

A standardized measurement of a product's ease of use based on metrics like task completion rate, time on task, and error rate. Usability benchmarks enable comparison across product versions or against competitors.

A set of general principles for evaluating user interface design, most famously Jakob Nielsen's ten heuristics. They include visibility of system status, error prevention, and consistency, serving as guidelines for design review.

A user experience research technique where real users attempt tasks on a product while observers note difficulties and confusion. It reveals usability issues that may not be apparent to designers and developers.

Universal Serial Bus — a standard interface for connecting peripherals to computers, providing data transfer and power delivery. USB has evolved through versions 1.0 to 4.0 with increasing speed and capabilities.

A device that expands a single USB port into multiple ports, allowing several USB devices to connect to a computer simultaneously. Powered USB hubs provide additional electrical current for high-power devices.

USB On-The-Go allows mobile devices to act as a USB host, enabling them to connect directly to peripherals like keyboards, flash drives, and cameras. It eliminates the need for a computer as an intermediary.

A universal connector standard featuring a reversible plug design that supports data transfer, video output, and power delivery through a single cable. It has become the standard connector for modern laptops, phones, and peripherals.

A description of how a user interacts with a system to achieve a specific goal, documenting the sequence of actions and system responses. Use cases capture functional requirements and guide system design and testing.

A UML behavioral diagram that depicts the interactions between external actors and a system's use cases. It provides a high-level view of system functionality and is useful for requirements gathering.

The final phase of software testing where actual users validate the system against business requirements before go-live. UAT ensures the software meets the needs of end users and stakeholders in real-world scenarios.

A string sent by a web browser or HTTP client to identify itself to a server, including details about the browser type, version, and operating system. Servers use this information to deliver content optimized for the requesting client.
The process of verifying a user's identity before granting access to a system or resource. Common methods include passwords, biometrics, multi-factor authentication, and cryptographic certificates.

The process of determining what actions or resources an authenticated user is permitted to access. Authorization mechanisms include role-based access control (RBAC) and attribute-based access control (ABAC).
The rate at which users stop using a product or cancel their subscription over a given period. Reducing churn is critical for SaaS businesses as it directly impacts revenue and growth sustainability.

A core internet protocol that provides a simple, connectionless communication model with minimal protocol overhead. UDP is preferred for time-sensitive transmissions where occasional packet loss is acceptable over the delay of retransmission.

A measure of how actively users interact with a product, tracked through metrics like daily active users, session length, and feature adoption. High engagement correlates with retention and revenue growth.

The totality of a user's perceptions, responses, and satisfaction resulting from the use of a product or system. User experience design applies research, psychology, and design principles to create meaningful interactions.
A visual representation of the path a user takes through an application to complete a task. User flows help designers identify friction points and optimize the sequence of screens and interactions.

The point of interaction between a user and a computer program, including screens, pages, buttons, icons, and forms. User interface design creates visually coherent and accessible interfaces that guide user actions.

The process of designing the visual layout and interactive elements of software applications to maximize usability and user satisfaction. It encompasses typography, color, layout, and interaction patterns.
A visualization of the complete experience a user has with a product or service across all touchpoints over time. It maps emotions, actions, and pain points to identify opportunities for improvement.

The process of guiding new users through initial setup and key features of a product to drive adoption and activation. Effective onboarding reduces time-to-value and improves long-term user retention.

A fictional but data-driven archetype representing a segment of the target audience, used to guide design decisions. Personas include demographics, goals, pain points, and behavioral patterns based on real user research.

A user directory in Amazon Cognito that provides sign-up, sign-in, and access control functionality for web and mobile applications. User pools handle authentication flows including social and enterprise identity federation.

The process of creating, managing, and deactivating user accounts and access rights across IT systems. Automated provisioning using protocols like SCIM ensures consistent access management and compliance.

The systematic study of target users and their requirements to inform the design process with real context and insights. Methods include interviews, surveys, usability testing, card sorting, and contextual inquiry.
A metric measuring the percentage of users who continue using a product over a specific time period. High retention rates indicate product-market fit and are critical for sustainable growth.

The practice of dividing users into groups based on shared characteristics such as behavior, demographics, or usage patterns. Segmentation enables targeted marketing, personalized experiences, and more effective product decisions.

A networking architecture where the network stack runs in user space rather than the kernel, enabling higher throughput and lower latency. Technologies like DPDK and io_uring exemplify this approach for high-performance applications.
A prototype write-ahead log implemented in Rust that aims to provide reliability and high performance for fault-tolerance in user space applications.

A short, informal description of a feature from the perspective of an end user, typically written as: As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]. User stories are the primary unit of work in agile development.

A collaborative technique that arranges user stories along two dimensions — user activities and priority — to create a visual map of the product backlog. It helps teams understand the big picture and plan releases.

The practice of evaluating a product by having representative users attempt tasks while observing their behavior and gathering feedback. User testing validates design assumptions and reveals real-world usability issues.

A cryptographic string issued after authentication that represents a user's identity and permissions for subsequent requests. Common formats include JWT (JSON Web Tokens) and opaque tokens validated by an authorization server.

An iterative design framework that focuses on users and their needs at every phase of the design and development process. It involves users throughout and grounds decisions in user research rather than assumptions.

U.term: User-Centric Evaluation of Conversational Recommender Systems
U.definition: A paradigm for assessing Conversational Recommender Systems (CRS) that focuses on capturing subjective qualities such as user satisfaction, trust, and rapport through user-centric methods.
A technique for optimizing the prefill stage of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by selectively recomputing tokens based on user queries, improving efficiency in KV cache reuse.

U.term: User-Space Framework for Coordinating Multi-runtime Workloads
U.definition: A framework designed to manage and schedule multi-runtime and multi-process workloads in high-performance computing environments, addressing the challenges of oversubscription and interference.

The memory area where application software and some drivers execute, separated from kernel space by hardware-enforced protection. Programs in userspace must use system calls to request services from the operating system kernel.

Unstructured Supplementary Service Data is a communication protocol used by GSM cellular networks to send text-based messages between a mobile phone and network. It enables real-time, session-based communication used for mobile banking and service queries.

Coordinated Universal Time is the primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time. It is essential in computing for consistent timestamping, logging, and coordinating distributed systems across time zones.
The difference in hours and minutes between a local time zone and Coordinated Universal Time. Properly handling UTC offsets is critical for applications that serve users across multiple time zones.

A character encoding capable of encoding all Unicode code points using one or two 16-bit code units. It is used internally by Java, JavaScript, and Windows, though UTF-8 is more common for data interchange.

A fixed-width character encoding that uses exactly four bytes per character, allowing direct indexing into the string. While simple, its space inefficiency makes it impractical for storage and transmission compared to UTF-8.

A variable-width character encoding capable of encoding all Unicode characters. UTF-8 is the dominant encoding on the web, backward-compatible with ASCII, and supports every writing system in the world using 1 to 4 bytes per character.

A service model where computing resources are provided on demand and billed based on usage, similar to electricity or water utilities. This pay-per-use model underpins modern cloud computing, allowing organizations to scale costs with actual consumption.

A helper function that performs a common, frequently used task not specific to any particular business logic. Utility functions promote code reuse and are typically organized into utility modules or libraries.

A type of cryptocurrency token that provides access to a specific product or service within a blockchain ecosystem rather than representing ownership or investment. Utility tokens power decentralized applications and platform features.

A CSS methodology where small, single-purpose utility classes are composed directly in HTML to build custom designs without writing custom CSS. Tailwind CSS is the most popular utility-first CSS framework.
A forecasting approach that optimizes decision-making under constraints such as transaction costs and risk limits by incorporating a utility objective and a friction operator.
Utonia is a self-supervised point transformer encoder designed to unify point clouds from diverse domains, enabling consistent representation learning across various sensing geometries and densities.

Universally Unique Identifier — a 128-bit number used to uniquely identify resources in distributed systems without requiring a central authority. UUIDs are virtually guaranteed to be unique and are widely used as database primary keys and transaction identifiers.
A lightning-fast ASGI server implementation for Python using uvloop and httptools. Uvicorn serves async Python frameworks like FastAPI and Starlette with high concurrency and low latency.

Universal Windows Platform is a Microsoft application architecture that allows developers to create apps that run across all Windows 10 and later devices. UWP apps are distributed through the Microsoft Store and run in a sandboxed environment.

A full-stack application server and process manager that serves Python web applications using the WSGI protocol. It supports multiple worker models, load balancing, and is commonly deployed behind Nginx.

User Experience — the overall experience a person has when interacting with a product, system, or service, encompassing usability, accessibility, and satisfaction. UX design focuses on research, prototyping, and iterative testing.

A systematic evaluation of a product's user experience to identify usability issues and areas for improvement. It typically involves heuristic evaluation, analytics review, and comparison against UX best practices.
A baseline measurement of user experience quality against which future changes are compared. UX benchmarks use metrics like System Usability Scale scores, task success rates, and Net Promoter Scores.

The accumulation of suboptimal user experience decisions made for expediency that require future effort to correct. Similar to technical debt, UX debt grows when design shortcuts compromise usability.

User Experience Design — the process of creating products that provide meaningful, relevant, and enjoyable experiences to users. UX design encompasses research, information architecture, interaction design, usability testing, and user journey mapping.

A measure of how well an organization integrates user experience practices into its processes and culture. Higher UX maturity correlates with better product outcomes, as design becomes a strategic capability rather than an afterthought.

Quantitative measurements used to evaluate the quality of user experience, including task completion rate, time on task, error rate, and satisfaction scores. These metrics help teams make data-informed design decisions.

The systematic study of target users and their requirements to inform the design process. It employs methods like interviews, surveys, and usability testing to understand user behaviors, needs, and motivations.

The practice of crafting user-facing text in digital products, including microcopy, button labels, error messages, and onboarding flows. Good UX writing guides users through interfaces clearly and concisely.

The combined discipline of User Experience and User Interface design that creates products which are both visually appealing and easy to use. UX/UI professionals bridge the gap between user needs and visual design.
A hybrid user search simulation framework that integrates traditional user simulators with adaptive LLM agents to create a more dynamic and personalized user experience in interactive search systems.

A hybrid user search simulation framework that combines traditional user simulators with adaptive large language model agents, leveraging grounded data to create more dynamic and personalized user experiences in interactive search systems.

V.definition: V-FAT (Visual Fidelity Against Text-bias) is a benchmarking approach that investigates the reliance of multimodal large language models on linguistic shortcuts, assessing the balance between visual perception and linguistic priors in visual reasoning tasks.

A multimodal agent architecture that integrates visual grounding and an explicit memory stack to enhance autonomous web navigation by resolving ambiguous interactive elements and maintaining long-term context.

A software development methodology that extends the waterfall model by associating each development phase with a corresponding testing phase. It emphasizes verification and validation at every stage of the development lifecycle.

An extension of the MORALS method that utilizes visual Morse graphs for estimating Regions of Attraction in a learned latent space, aiming to improve reachability analysis without requiring full state knowledge.

V-Skip is a method for efficient multimodal reasoning that reformulates token pruning as a Visual-Anchored Information Bottleneck optimization, addressing issues of visual amnesia in Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
An extended vision-language embedding space that integrates text and speech languages, enabling improved performance in tasks like text-to-video retrieval through a post-hoc alignment pipeline.

A verifiable vector-search service that allows for audit-on-demand correctness checks of approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval, ensuring transparency and trust in the results provided by potentially untrusted service providers.

Google's open-source JavaScript and WebAssembly engine written in C++ that powers Chrome and Node.js. V8 compiles JavaScript to machine code using just-in-time compilation for high-performance execution.
Google's open-source high-performance JavaScript and WebAssembly engine, written in C++. V8 powers Chrome and Node.js, using just-in-time compilation to execute JavaScript at near-native speed.
VA-DAR is a keyed-discovery protocol designed for serverless wallet recovery that utilizes device-bound passkeys, enabling cross-device recovery while maintaining user privacy.

Variational Autoencoder is a generative model that learns a compressed latent representation of input data by encoding it into a probability distribution. VAEs can generate new data samples by sampling from the learned latent space.

An open-source tool for building and managing portable virtual development environments. Vagrant uses configuration files called Vagrantfiles to automate the setup of virtual machines with consistent, reproducible environments.
Valet is a standardized testbed comprising 21 traditional imperfect-information card games, designed to facilitate comparative research on algorithms for imperfect-information game-playing.

This framework focuses on translating code from languages like Go to memory-safe languages such as Rust, addressing challenges related to external library dependencies and semantic equivalence validation.

This framework facilitates the translation of Go projects with external dependencies to Rust, addressing challenges in semantic equivalence validation and API call generation during the translation process.

The process of checking that data meets specified requirements before processing. Input validation prevents errors and security vulnerabilities by ensuring data conforms to expected formats, types, ranges, and constraints at both client and server levels.

The full range of activities a company performs to deliver a product or service from conception to delivery. Value chain analysis identifies opportunities to create competitive advantage by optimizing each activity.

An immutable object in domain-driven design that represents a descriptive aspect of the domain with no conceptual identity. Value objects are compared by their attributes rather than by identity, like a Money or Address type.

A clear statement that explains how a product or service solves a customer problem, delivers benefits, and why a customer should choose it over competitors. Value propositions guide product development and marketing strategy.

A lean management technique that analyzes the flow of materials and information through a process to identify waste and optimize delivery. In software, it maps the steps from code commit to production deployment.

ValueFlow is a perturbation-based evaluation framework that measures the propagation of value perturbations in multi-agent large language model systems, quantifying agents' value orientations during interactions.
A reproducible training, deployment, and certification pipeline designed for aligning mulching models to ensure they represent diverse human values in high-stakes contexts.

An advanced physics-informed neural network architecture that integrates a vanishing-viscosity mechanism to improve the modeling of hyperbolic partial differential equations, addressing challenges like discontinuities and shocks.

Measurements that look impressive but do not provide actionable insights or correlate with business success, such as total page views or app downloads without retention data. Effective teams focus on actionable metrics instead.
A server-side Swift web framework that provides a clean API for building web applications, APIs, and HTTP servers. It leverages Swift's type safety and performance for backend development.
A named storage location in a program that holds a value which can be modified during execution. Variables have a type, scope, and lifetime that determine how and where they can be used in a program.
A framework that transforms volumetric fields into 3D Gaussian Splatting representations through wavelet-domain analysis, optimizing computational efficiency for real-time volumetric visualization.
A technique in randomized local search for multi-objective combinatorial optimization that adjusts the stepsize dynamically to improve search efficiency and solution quality.

An encoding scheme where different symbols are represented by codes of different lengths, with more frequent symbols using shorter codes. Huffman coding and UTF-8 are examples of variable-length encoding.
A method for representing items in recommender systems as sequences of low-cardinality tokens, addressing the challenges of high cardinality in item spaces and improving generative model training.
A function that accepts a variable number of arguments. Languages implement this differently—C uses va_list, Python uses *args, and JavaScript uses rest parameters (...args).

A statistical measure of how far data points are spread out from their mean, calculated as the average squared deviation. In machine learning, variance describes how much a model's predictions change with different training data.
A method for estimating calibration errors in machine learning predictions that extends variational frameworks beyond traditional divergence functions, particularly useful in multiclass settings.

A method that learns a smooth, differentiable representation of Green's functions for linear self-adjoint partial differential equations, facilitating efficient evaluation on arbitrary geometric discretizations.
A method for approximating complex probability distributions by finding the closest member of a simpler family of distributions. It converts intractable inference problems into optimization problems.

A quantum reinforcement learning approach utilizing variational quantum circuits to optimize dynamic portfolio management, achieving performance comparable to classical deep reinforcement learning models.
A quantum computing algorithm adapted for dynamic portfolio optimization problems, featuring enhancements like Ising Sample-based Quantum Configuration Recovery (ISQR).

A high-performance HTTP accelerator and reverse proxy cache designed for content-heavy dynamic websites. Varnish stores copies of web pages in memory, dramatically reducing backend server load and response times.

V.term: VaSST (Variational Inference for Symbolic Regression using Soft Symbolic Trees)
V.definition: A probabilistic approach to symbolic regression that employs variational inference to efficiently explore the space of symbolic expressions while quantifying uncertainty.

HashiCorp Vault is a secrets management tool that provides secure storage, dynamic secrets generation, encryption as a service, and identity-based access. It is widely used to manage API keys, passwords, and certificates in cloud-native environments.
A benchmark for the Video Connecting task, which focuses on generating smooth intermediate video content between clips, addressing the lack of standardized evaluation metrics in this area.
Version Control System tracks and manages changes to source code over time, enabling collaboration and history tracking. Common VCS types include centralized (SVN) and distributed (Git) systems.
Platforms that host version control repositories and provide collaboration features like pull requests, code review, and CI/CD integration. Major providers include GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket.
A benchmark for evaluating the capabilities of multimodal image editing models specifically for visual document image editing, focusing on the preservation of original text style and context.

Virtual Desktop Infrastructure hosts desktop environments on a centralized server, allowing users to access their full desktop remotely from any device. VDI provides consistent environments, centralized management, and enhanced security.
A novel framework that transforms large language models into proactive security experts capable of learning from analyses, thereby enhancing the discovery of unknown vulnerabilities in software.
A dynamic array data structure that automatically resizes as elements are added or removed. In machine learning, vectors are one-dimensional arrays of numbers representing features or embeddings.

A mechanism for tracking causality and ordering events in distributed systems by assigning each process a vector of logical timestamps. Vector clocks detect concurrent events and resolve conflicts in distributed databases.

A specialized database designed to store, index, and query high-dimensional vector embeddings efficiently. Vector databases like Pinecone, Weaviate, and Chroma enable semantic search, recommendation systems, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for AI applications.

A numerical representation of data (text, images, audio) as a dense vector in a high-dimensional space where similar items are positioned closer together. Embeddings enable semantic search, recommendation systems, and transfer learning.

A form of computer graphics in which visual images are created from geometric primitives like points, lines, curves, and shapes defined by mathematical expressions. Vector graphics scale without loss of quality.

A CPU instruction that operates on multiple data elements simultaneously using SIMD registers. Vector instructions like AVX and SSE accelerate numerical computations, media processing, and machine learning workloads.

A data compression technique that represents a large set of vectors by a smaller codebook of representative vectors. Vector quantization is used in image compression, speech coding, and efficient nearest-neighbor search.

A search technique that finds items similar to a query by comparing vector embeddings using distance metrics like cosine similarity. Vector search powers semantic search, recommendation systems, and RAG applications.
Vectorized Adaptive Histograms are an optimization technique for sparse oblique random forests, allowing dynamic switching between histograms and sorting to improve training speed and efficiency.

VectorMaton is an efficient vector search framework that incorporates pattern constraints via enhanced suffix automata, allowing for fine-grained searches that consider additional structured attributes alongside vector data.

V.term: Vehicle Routing Problem with Resource-Constrained Pickup and Delivery (VRP-RPD)
V.definition: A variant of the vehicle routing problem that allows different vehicles to handle drop-off and pickup operations at the same location, introducing inter-route dependencies for more practical autonomous robotics applications.

VeloANN is an optimized graph-based approximate nearest neighbor search method that utilizes a locality-aware data layout and coroutine-based asynchronous runtime to mitigate storage stalls and improve CPU utilization during vector searches on SSDs.

In Agile development, the total amount of work a team completes during a sprint, typically measured in story points. Velocity helps teams predict how much work they can commit to in future iterations.
The practice of measuring the amount of work a development team completes in each sprint or iteration. Velocity helps teams forecast future capacity, plan releases, and identify process improvements.

A situation where a customer becomes dependent on a specific vendor's products or services, making it difficult and costly to switch to a competitor. It is a key consideration when choosing cloud providers and proprietary tools.

The process of evaluating, selecting, and managing third-party suppliers and service providers to optimize value and mitigate risk. In tech, it includes managing SaaS subscriptions, cloud providers, and outsourced services.

An approach to technology selection that avoids dependence on a single vendor's proprietary products or standards. Vendor-neutral strategies use open standards and portable solutions to maintain flexibility and prevent lock-in.

The practice of copying third-party dependencies directly into a project's source tree rather than fetching them at build time. Vendoring ensures reproducible builds and removes external dependency on package registries.

VennFan is a method for generating n-set Venn diagrams using polar coordinate projections of trigonometric boundaries, resulting in fan-like diagrams that prioritize readability and customizability through shaped sinusoids and amplitude scaling.

A form of private equity financing provided to startups and early-stage companies with high growth potential in exchange for equity ownership. VC firms provide funding, mentorship, and industry connections.

VERAFI stands for Verified Agentic Financial Intelligence, a framework that combines neurosymbolic policy generation with dense retrieval and cross-encoder reranking to ensure compliance and accuracy in financial AI systems.
A framework that integrates verbal and latent reasoning to produce accurate and interpretable stock time-series forecasts by analyzing both historical price data and textual reports.
A component of a data-centric framework that utilizes reinforcement learning to convert structured user interaction logs into optimized natural language inputs for LLM-based recommendation systems.

A cloud platform optimized for deploying frontend frameworks and serverless functions, known for its seamless integration with Next.js. Vercel provides edge deployment, preview environments, and automatic scaling.

A multi-agent framework designed for Register-Transfer Level (RTL) code generation that incorporates contract-awareness, temporal tracing, and formal verification to enhance correctness in electronic design automation.
A certification protocol ensuring consistent communication among agents by verifying shared understanding of terms through empirical testing against observable events.

Two complementary quality assurance processes — verification checks that the product is built correctly according to specifications, while validation confirms the product meets the actual user needs and requirements.

V.term: Verification and Validation Framework for MASS
V.definition: An enhanced virtual simulation framework designed for the rigorous verification and validation of Maritime Autonomous Surface Ships under various environmental conditions.

The VERIFICATION ASSISTANT is a browser-based open-source tool designed to help journalists and fact-checkers rapidly verify digital media information against disinformation and false content generated by AI.
A systematic verification framework for event stream data that ensures data quality and logical consistency, particularly in sports analytics.

A framework that utilizes verifiable recursive decomposition for reinforcement learning, leveraging symbolic differentiation to ensure that subproblems are mathematically grounded and beneficial for solving complex mathematical tasks.

VeriLocc is a framework that integrates large language models with formal compiler techniques to achieve generalizable and verifiable register allocation across various GPU architectures, enhancing compiler efficiency.

A hardware description language used to model electronic systems at various levels of abstraction, from gate level to behavioral. Verilog is widely used in FPGA and ASIC design for digital circuit implementation and simulation.
VeriSoftBench is a benchmark comprising 500 Lean 4 proof obligations derived from open-source formal-methods developments, aimed at evaluating LLMs in the context of software verification.
The first dynamic benchmark for multimodal automated fact-checking, designed to evaluate systems against a diverse range of misinformation types while adapting to new data to prevent leakage.

A content delivery network service that distributes web content across globally distributed edge servers for faster delivery. CDN services reduce latency and server load by caching content closer to end users.
An algorithm-architecture co-design framework that enables feed-forward 3D reconstruction through versatile quantization, addressing challenges in deploying large models.

A system that records changes to files over time so you can recall specific versions later. Version control systems like Git enable collaboration, track history, manage branches, and provide a safety net for code changes across teams.

The practice of locking dependencies to specific versions to ensure reproducible builds and prevent unexpected breaking changes. Lock files in package managers like npm and pip implement version pinning.

An extension of vector clocks that tracks the version history of replicated data across multiple nodes. Version vectors detect conflicts and enable eventual consistency in distributed storage systems.

A polyglot event-driven application framework that runs on the JVM, supporting Java, Kotlin, Groovy, and JavaScript. It is designed for building reactive, high-performance microservices and web applications.

Google Cloud's machine learning platform that provides tools for building, deploying, and scaling ML models. Vertex AI offers AutoML, custom training, model management, and integration with Google's AI services including Gemini.

A database design technique that splits a table into multiple tables containing fewer columns. Vertical partitioning improves query performance by keeping frequently accessed columns together and separating rarely used data.
Increasing the capacity of a single server by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage resources. Also known as scaling up, it is simpler than horizontal scaling but has physical hardware limits.

A software architecture pattern where code is organized by feature rather than by technical layer. Each vertical slice contains all layers needed for a feature, from UI to database, promoting cohesion and independence.
A benchmark suite for studying agent-based verification of Rust systems, consisting of proof tasks extracted from open-source Verus-verified systems.

An unprecedentedly large-scale dataset designed to facilitate systematic studies in video reasoning, focusing on spatiotemporal consistency and enabling advanced reasoning capabilities in video models.
An advanced implementation of Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) that supports flexible training methods and optimizers, enhancing communication and memory efficiency for large-scale model training.

A native editing framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting that predicts attribute variations in a feedforward manner, overcoming cross-view inconsistencies associated with traditional indirect editing methods.

VHSIC Hardware Description Language is used for describing the behavior and structure of electronic systems. It is commonly used alongside Verilog for FPGA programming, ASIC design, and digital system simulation.

Vibe Coding refers to a programming workflow where novice programmers prioritize semantic intent over syntactic accuracy, potentially leading to issues in cognitive skill acquisition without proper pedagogical support.

A concept describing the use of AI agents to execute multi-step reasoning workflows in social science research, enabling autonomous execution of research pipelines with persistent state and domain-specific skills.
A method for enhancing generalization in evolutionary feature construction by minimizing the vicinal Jensen gap, which bounds empirical risk and regularization terms.
The recording, processing, storage, and playback of moving visual images. Digital video encompasses encoding formats, streaming protocols, compression algorithms, and rendering technologies.

The use of computer vision and machine learning to automatically analyze video content for extracting information, detecting events, or identifying objects. Applications include surveillance, traffic monitoring, and retail analytics.

Software or hardware that compresses and decompresses digital video data using algorithms like H.264, H.265/HEVC, VP9, and AV1. Codecs balance file size, quality, and encoding/decoding speed for efficient video delivery.

Technology that allows two or more people in different locations to communicate in real time using video and audio transmission. Video conferencing platforms like Zoom and Teams support remote collaboration.

The process of converting raw video data into a compressed digital format using codecs like H.264, H.265, VP9, or AV1. Video encoding balances file size, quality, and playback compatibility for streaming and storage.

A framework for retrieving specific moments from videos, which faces challenges in generalization when trained on caption-based queries and evaluated on search queries, highlighting the need for improved query representation.

The manipulation and analysis of video data using algorithms for tasks like encoding, decoding, transcoding, filtering, and computer vision. Video processing is used in streaming, surveillance, and content creation.

The continuous delivery of video content over the internet where playback begins before the entire file is downloaded. Adaptive bitrate streaming protocols like HLS and DASH adjust quality based on network conditions.

A novel framework for textual intent-guided multi-rate video communication that utilizes tokens as unified units of communication, incorporating Unequal Error Protection-based source-channel coding adaptation.

The process of converting a video file from one format or codec to another, often to optimize for different devices, bandwidths, or platforms. Transcoding is essential for adaptive streaming and content delivery.
A generic web component for WebRTC applications that allows for publishing and subscribing to media streams using a named stream abstraction, facilitating flexible application development.
A two-stage pipeline for extracting reusable neural material assets from video diffusion models, enhancing the creation of photorealistic materials for 3D rendering.

VideoSTF is a framework for measuring and stress-testing output repetition in Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs), formalizing repetition through complementary metrics to enhance model evaluation.
A framework designed for rendering video-native query results efficiently, addressing bottlenecks in interactive video data workloads by optimizing the rendering process.
A tool for authoring hybrid video stories that integrates captured and generated media through context-aware expansion, enhancing creative expression in video storytelling.
VidTune is a system that facilitates the creation of video soundtracks by generating diverse music options from text prompts and producing contextual thumbnails for efficient review and selection.

A city-scale open dataset of georeferenced LTE and 5G NR measurements collected across Vienna, Austria, combining passive wideband scanner observations with active handset logs for comprehensive mobile network analysis.

VietSuperSpeech is a large-scale Vietnamese automatic speech recognition dataset comprising 52,023 audio-text pairs focused on casual conversational speech, designed for fine-tuning ASR systems in real-world applications.
A design pattern component that exposes data and commands for the view layer while remaining independent of the UI framework. The View Model mediates between the model and view in MVVM architecture.

The visible area of a web page in the browser window, which varies by device and screen size. The viewport meta tag controls how content scales on mobile devices and is essential for responsive design.
An agent that reconstructs or edits scenes through interleaved multimodal reasoning, employing a closed-loop process of writing, running, rendering, comparing, and revising.
A reconfigurable accelerator that supports Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) with two-stage sparsity to improve real-time performance.

A highly configurable, modal text editor built for efficiency, available on virtually every Unix system. Its modal editing paradigm separates navigation, editing, and command modes for fast text manipulation.

The keyboard-based navigation commands in Vim that allow rapid cursor movement without a mouse, using keys like h/j/k/l, w/b, and search patterns. Many modern editors offer Vim motion plugins for efficient text navigation.

A software architecture pattern for iOS development that separates concerns into View, Interactor, Presenter, Entity, and Router components. VIPER provides better testability and modularity than the traditional MVC pattern.

VIRAASAT is a semi-automated multi-hop approach for generating cultural reasoning tasks focused on Indian culture, addressing the limitations of existing benchmarks by enabling scalable and context-rich assessments.

A growth pattern where a product or service spreads rapidly through user referrals and word-of-mouth, with each user bringing in multiple new users. Viral growth is measured by the viral coefficient and cycle time.
VIRGi is a novel method for view-dependent instant recoloring of 3D Gaussian splats, enabling rapid editing of scene appearance while preserving effects like specular highlights.

The set of memory addresses available to a process, mapped by the operating system to physical memory or disk. Virtual address spaces give each process the illusion of having its own contiguous memory block.

An AI-powered software agent that can perform tasks and answer questions through natural language interaction. Examples include Apple's Siri, Amazon's Alexa, and AI chatbots that handle customer support.
A geometrically defined construct in massive MIMO systems, created using multi-modal data to enhance beam alignment in various propagation environments.
An abstraction of a physical CPU core allocated to a virtual machine or container. vCPUs allow cloud providers to divide physical hardware resources among multiple tenants and workloads.

A lightweight in-memory representation of the actual DOM that frameworks like React use to optimize UI updates. Changes are first applied to the virtual DOM, then efficiently reconciled with the real DOM through a diffing algorithm.

An isolated Python runtime environment that maintains its own set of installed packages, independent of the system Python and other projects. Tools like venv and virtualenv create these environments to avoid dependency conflicts.

An abstraction layer in an operating system that provides a uniform interface to different types of file systems. The VFS allows applications to access local, network, and special-purpose file systems through the same API.

The Virtual Garbage Collector (VGC) is a zone-based memory management architecture for Python that enhances execution predictability and memory behavior by introducing a dual-layer model for managing object lifecycles and optimizing static allocation.

A technique that allows multiple websites to be served from a single web server, distinguished by domain name (name-based) or IP address (IP-based). It is how shared hosting providers serve thousands of sites from one machine.

A software emulation of a physical computer that runs an operating system and applications as if they were on dedicated hardware. VMs provide isolation and flexibility, though containers have largely replaced them for application deployment.

A memory management technique that provides each process with an idealized abstract view of storage, mapping virtual addresses to physical memory. Virtual memory enables running programs larger than available RAM.
A technique that utilizes virtual memory to enhance buffer management in tiered memory architectures, optimizing the use of local and remote memory resources.
A software-defined network that provides isolated network segments within a cloud environment, enabling resources to communicate securely. Examples include AWS VPC, Azure VNet, and GCP VPC networks.

A security policy rule applied at the web application firewall or intrusion prevention system level to block exploitation of a known vulnerability without modifying the application code. Virtual patches provide immediate protection while permanent fixes are developed.
A logically isolated section of a public cloud that provides private, configurable networking for cloud resources. VPCs allow users to define IP ranges, subnets, route tables, and security groups.

A computer-generated simulation of a three-dimensional environment that users can interact with using specialized hardware like headsets and controllers. VR is used in gaming, training, therapy, and architectural visualization.
A modeling approach for reconstructing channel gain maps in 3D space by characterizing channel power gain distribution through virtual scatterers, enhancing multi-path propagation analysis.

A software-based network switch that operates within a virtualization environment to manage traffic between virtual machines. Virtual switches like Open vSwitch provide network connectivity, VLAN tagging, and traffic monitoring.

Also known as a vtable, it is a mechanism used in C++ and similar languages to support dynamic dispatch of virtual functions. Each class with virtual methods has a vtable containing pointers to the appropriate function implementations.

A lightweight thread managed by the runtime rather than the operating system, enabling millions of concurrent threads with minimal overhead. Java's Project Loom introduced virtual threads to simplify concurrent programming.

The creation of virtual versions of physical resources including servers, storage, networks, and operating systems. Virtualization improves resource utilization, enables rapid provisioning, and forms the foundation of cloud computing infrastructure.

A two-stream segmentation architecture that integrates Vegetation-Index and Spectral Attention for accurate weed mapping in UAV imagery, addressing challenges in mixed crop and weed pixel classification.

A period during which a message retrieved from a queue is hidden from other consumers, preventing duplicate processing. If the consumer fails to delete the message within the timeout, it becomes visible again for retry.
A structural indicator of volatility clustering in financial markets, derived from the asymmetry of visibility graphs constructed on absolute returns, providing insights into market behavior.
A framework that combines multi-stage curriculum learning with domain randomization to train robust controllers for autonomous drone racing, particularly in environments with random obstacles.

A framework that enhances object manipulation in robotics by replacing verbose textual commands with direct visual interactions, allowing operators to select target objects through clicks in a robot's camera view.
Policies that integrate vision and language for action execution, evaluated for robustness against object-location correlations in multi-object manipulation tasks.
A model that estimates horizontal and vertical hand distances from RGB video to assess ergonomic risks associated with manual lifting tasks, enhancing traditional ergonomic assessment methods.

VLA models integrate visual, linguistic, and action-based inputs to enhance robotics and embodied AI applications, particularly in edge computing environments. They are characterized by significant performance improvements as they scale, although they face latency challenges during action generation.
A native visual-generation agentic model that integrates Understanding, Thinking, Planning, and Creation capabilities into a single end-to-end learnable framework for visual content creation.

The collection and analysis of data about website visitors including page views, session duration, bounce rates, and conversion paths. Tools like Google Analytics and Plausible provide insights to optimize user experience and marketing.

A behavioral design pattern that separates an algorithm from the object structure it operates on, allowing new operations to be added without modifying existing classes. It uses double dispatch to select the correct method at runtime.
A Virtual Skill Training system that utilizes augmented reality and AI coaching to provide feedback on athletic skills, following the Behavioral Skills Training framework.
A phenomenon where the performance of simulated users in reinforcement learning tasks is influenced by visual rendering artifacts, particularly luminance and contrast variations in task environments.
A measure of how independently visual channels, such as color and shape, can be perceived in multivariate data visualizations, particularly in symbol maps.
The use of imagery, color, shapes, typography, and form to enhance usability and improve the user experience. Visual design establishes brand identity and creates aesthetically pleasing interfaces.
A specialized retrieval method for visually rich documents that addresses the unique challenges posed by dense textual content and complex layouts in the era of multimodal large language models.
A visual and collaborative planning approach designed for agile practitioners, utilizing a milestone planning matrix to document and manipulate planning constructs in a hybrid development context.
A framework that integrates formal verification into deep learning-based image retrieval, addressing challenges related to complex queries and object relationships.

A programming approach where users create programs by manipulating graphical elements rather than writing text code. Visual programming tools like Scratch, Node-RED, and Unreal Blueprints lower the barrier to programming.

An automated testing approach that captures screenshots of UI components and compares them against baseline images to detect unintended visual changes. Tools like Percy and Chromatic integrate this into CI pipelines.

A comprehensive integrated development environment from Microsoft for building applications across multiple platforms and languages including C#, C++, Python, and JavaScript. It includes debugging, profiling, and design tools.

The graphical representation of data and information using charts, graphs, maps, and dashboards to make complex data more accessible and understandable. Tools like D3.js, Matplotlib, and Tableau are commonly used for data visualization.

Vision Transformer is a model architecture that applies transformer mechanisms, originally designed for NLP, to image classification by treating image patches as tokens. ViT has shown that pure transformer models can match or exceed CNNs on vision tasks.

A modern frontend build tool that provides fast development server startup and hot module replacement using native ES modules. Vite supports React, Vue, Svelte, and other frameworks, offering a significantly faster developer experience than traditional bundlers.

A fast Vite-native unit testing framework that provides Jest-compatible APIs with out-of-the-box TypeScript and ESM support. It shares Vite's configuration and transformation pipeline for a seamless development experience.
ViTex introduces a method for visual texture control in multi-track symbolic music generation using discrete diffusion models, allowing for direct human interaction in shaping musical compositions.
A framework for latency-critical mobile video analytics that employs vision transformers to enhance global context modeling and generalization capabilities in edge-assisted applications.
A human-agent collaborative system designed to generate interactive educational documents from a single topic input, utilizing a multi-agent pipeline for planning, execution, and evaluation.
An iterative platform for designing multiple-choice questions (MCQs) specifically tailored for visualization literacy, integrating multimodal elements to enhance educational assessments.

A framework that integrates Vision-Language Models with Knowledge Graph Embeddings to enhance cross-modal alignment and representation learning in multimodal knowledge graphs. It addresses the limitations of traditional KGE methods by processing diverse modalities in a unified manner.

Virtual Local Area Network — a logical subdivision of a physical network that groups devices together regardless of physical location. VLANs segment network traffic to improve security, reduce broadcast domains, and simplify management.
Process subunits that encapsulate sets of libraries and associated resources, designed to manage parallelism in software libraries that are not inherently composable.

Very Long Instruction Word is a processor architecture that packages multiple independent operations into a single wide instruction, relying on the compiler to find parallelism. It simplifies hardware design at the cost of compiler complexity.
A method utilizing Vision-Language Models (VLM) to estimate 3D object positions from monocular RGB images, enhancing human-robot interaction through natural language inputs.

VLMFusionOcc3D is a multimodal framework for dense 3D semantic occupancy prediction that utilizes Vision-Language Models to resolve semantic ambiguities in voxel-based occupancy models, particularly in challenging environments.

A spreadsheet function that searches for a value in the first column of a range and returns a value from a specified column in the same row. While widely used in Excel, it has been largely superseded by XLOOKUP and INDEX/MATCH.

A security exploit where an attacker breaks out of a virtual machine to interact with the host operating system or hypervisor. VM escape vulnerabilities are critical because they compromise the isolation that virtualization provides.

The process of moving a running virtual machine from one physical host to another with minimal or no downtime. Live VM migration enables hardware maintenance, load balancing, and disaster recovery without service interruption.

Virtual Machine Disk is a file format that describes containers for virtual hard disk drives used by VMware virtual machines. VMDK files store the contents of a VM's hard drive and can be converted to other formats.
A lightweight memory management architecture for cloud environments that supports online upgrades and efficient memory utilization, addressing the challenges of traditional memory management.

A leading provider of virtualization and cloud computing software, known for products like vSphere, ESXi, and vCenter. VMware's technology enables organizations to run multiple virtual machines on a single physical server.

Virtual Network Computing is a graphical desktop-sharing system that uses the RFB protocol to remotely control another computer's display. VNC transmits screen updates and keyboard/mouse events between client and server.
A non-autoregressive paradigm for speech large language models that utilizes Masked Diffusion Modeling to enhance streaming speech interaction efficiency.

An AI-powered software agent that can interpret and respond to voice commands, performing tasks like answering questions, controlling devices, and managing schedules. Examples include Alexa, Siri, and Google Assistant.

An AI technique that replicates a specific person's voice from audio samples, enabling text-to-speech synthesis that sounds like the original speaker. It raises ethical concerns around deepfakes and consent.

A technology that identifies and processes human speech, converting spoken words into text or commands. It differs from speaker recognition, which identifies who is speaking rather than what is being said.
